options.a: A non-reactive hypothetical gas that turns into a solid
options.b: A gas that can turn from a gas to a liquid
options.c: A hypothetical gas that behaves predictably
options.d: A gas that is affected by pressures and temperatures, and may change states into a liquid or solid

questions.explanation: A real gas is an actual physical gas whose behavior is influenced by pressure and temperature, and which can change state — condensing into a liquid or even solidifying under sufficient pressure or cold. This contrasts with the hypothetical "ideal gas" of textbook gas laws, which is assumed to never liquefy and to follow the laws perfectly. Options a and c describe ideal/hypothetical gases. For divers, the distinction matters because real gases (especially at the extreme pressures of deep technical diving) deviate slightly from ideal predictions, though the gas laws remain accurate enough for recreational and most technical applications.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.3 Review

questions.question: Boyle's Law can be used to calculate:
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law (P1V1 = P2V2) relates pressure and volume at constant temperature: as pressure increases, volume decreases proportionally, and vice versa. It is the most directly experienced gas law in diving — it explains why a BCD or wetsuit compresses at depth, why a breath-hold diver's lungs shrink during descent, why a balloon at the surface would be half its size at 10 m, and why a free-flowing octopus or expanding lung at ascent is so dangerous. Temperature-related changes are described by Charles's, Gay-Lussac's, or the combined gas law.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.3 Review

questions.question: Guy-Lussac's Law can be used to calculate:
questions.explanation: Gay-Lussac's Law (P1/T1 = P2/T2) relates pressure and temperature at constant volume: in a sealed, rigid container, pressure changes proportionally with absolute temperature. For divers, this is most relevant to cylinder pressure: a tank filled to 200 bar in a cool dive shop and then left in a hot car or in direct sun on a boat will register a higher pressure as it warms — and a tank cooled by entry into cold water will read lower than at the surface. Volume-temperature changes are Charles's Law (b); pressure-volume changes are Boyle's Law (d).
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.3 Review

questions.question: What varies proportionally with temperature if volume remains constant?
questions.explanation: When volume is held constant (a sealed, rigid cylinder), pressure varies directly and proportionally with absolute temperature — this is Gay-Lussac's Law (P1/T1 = P2/T2). Heat the gas, the molecules move faster and strike the container walls harder and more often, raising pressure; cool the gas and pressure falls. This is why filled scuba cylinders should not be left in hot vehicles or direct sunlight, and why "hot fills" at the dive shop appear to lose pressure once cooled to ambient — the actual gas mass is unchanged, only the temperature dropped.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.3 Review

questions.question: Dalton's Law states that:

options.a: Gas pressure equals total pressure minus the partial pressure of oxygen
options.b: The sum of partial pressures in a gas mixture is equal to the total gas pressure
options.c: The total gas pressure is equal to one gas partial pressure
options.d: Gas pressure equals total pressure minus the partial pressure of nitrogen

questions.explanation: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that the total pressure of a gas mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of its individual components. Each gas exerts the pressure it would if it occupied the whole volume alone. The formula PpGas = FGas × P_abs lets divers calculate, for example, that air at 30 m (4 bar absolute) delivers a PpO₂ of 0.21 × 4 = 0.84 bar and a PpN₂ of 0.79 × 4 = 3.16 bar. Dalton's Law underlies oxygen toxicity limits, MOD calculations, and nitrogen narcosis depth predictions for any breathing mix.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.4 Review

questions.question: As absolute pressure increases, gas partial pressures:
questions.explanation: By Dalton's Law (PpGas = FGas × P_abs), the fraction of each gas in a mix stays constant, but the partial pressure of each component rises in direct proportion to absolute pressure. Breathing air (21% O₂, 79% N₂) at the surface gives PpO₂ 0.21 / PpN₂ 0.79 bar; at 40 m (5 bar abs) the same air delivers PpO₂ 1.05 / PpN₂ 3.95 bar. This proportional increase is exactly what drives oxygen toxicity at depth, nitrogen narcosis, and accelerated nitrogen tissue loading — the gas fraction never changed, only the partial pressure.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.4 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about maximum operating depths is true?

options.a: They set the deepest depth for using a breathing gas without exceeding the ppO2 limit
options.c: The higher the percentage of oxygen, the shallower the maximum operating depth
options.d: They are important for divers using all gases

questions.explanation: All three statements describe the Maximum Operating Depth (MOD) correctly. MOD is the deepest depth at which a breathing gas can be safely used before the partial pressure of oxygen (PpO2) exceeds the recreational limit of 1.4 bar (or 1.6 bar contingency). Per Dalton's Law, PpO2 = FO2 x P_absolute, so the higher the oxygen fraction, the shallower the depth at which the PpO2 limit is reached. MOD applies to all breathing gases — air, nitrox, trimix — because every gas mixture has an oxygen content that becomes toxic at sufficient depth. SSI therefore teaches that MOD must always be calculated and respected.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.4 Review

questions.question: The maximum operating depth of EAN40 is:
questions.explanation: MOD is calculated using Dalton's Law: depth (m) = ((PpO2_limit / FO2) - 1) x 10. With EAN40 (40% oxygen) and the SSI recreational PpO2 limit of 1.4 bar, the calculation is (1.4 / 0.40) - 1 = 2.5 bar of water pressure, which equals 25 meters. At this depth the PpO2 reaches exactly 1.4 bar, the maximum recommended limit to avoid acute oxygen toxicity (CNS hits). Going deeper than 25 m on EAN40 risks oxygen toxicity, so 25 m is the recreational MOD. Some technical training uses 1.6 bar contingency, giving an absolute maximum of 30 m.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.4 Review

questions.question: What is gas tension?

options.a: The pressure of the dissolved gas (in solution)
options.b: The pressure of the gas on a diver's lungs
options.c: The partial pressure of the undissolved gas (out of solution)
options.d: The pressure of the gas in the diver's cylinder

questions.explanation: Gas tension is the partial pressure exerted by a gas that is already dissolved in a liquid — in diving, the inert gas (nitrogen) dissolved in body tissues and blood. It is the counterpart to ambient partial pressure: while ambient PpN2 is the pressure pushing nitrogen into solution (Henry's Law), tissue tension is the pressure of the gas already in solution pushing back. The difference between ambient pressure and tissue tension is the pressure gradient that drives on-gassing during descent or off-gassing during ascent. When tissue tension exceeds ambient pressure significantly (supersaturation), bubbles can form, leading to decompression sickness.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.4 Review

questions.question: When a diver descends the pressure gradient is:

options.a: Initially high, increasing the rate of gas dissolution
options.c: Initially low, decreasing the rate of gas dissolution
options.d: Initially high, decreasing the rate of gas dissolution

questions.explanation: On descent, the ambient partial pressure of nitrogen rises rapidly while tissue nitrogen tension is still at surface levels, creating a large pressure gradient between the lungs/blood and the tissues. By Henry's Law, gas dissolves into liquid in proportion to its partial pressure, and the steeper the gradient, the faster the diffusion rate. Therefore the initial gradient is high and gas dissolution into the tissues is fast. As the dive continues, tissue tension rises and approaches ambient pressure, the gradient narrows, and on-gassing slows until the tissue eventually saturates. The same principle in reverse drives off-gassing during ascent.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.4 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about gas concentration is true?

options.a: The amount of gas molecules contained in a given volume
options.b: Gas concentration increases as a diver descends
options.c: If increases proportionally with increased partial pressures
options.d: It is the temperature of gas molecules contained in a given volume

questions.explanation: Gas concentration is defined as the number (or mass) of gas molecules contained in a given volume. It is a basic measure of how densely packed gas molecules are within a space. While it is true that as a diver descends the gas in the lungs and regulator becomes denser (more molecules per breath because Boyle's Law compresses the gas), the question asks for the definition of gas concentration itself. Option (a) provides this fundamental definition. Concentration is closely linked to partial pressure: at increased ambient pressure, more gas molecules are delivered per breath, which is why nitrogen narcosis and oxygen toxicity become factors at depth.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.4 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about "apparent mass" is true?

options.a: An object will sink if its apparent mass has a negative value
options.c: An object will float if its apparent mass has a positive value
options.d: An object will be neutrally buoyant if its apparent mass has a value of

questions.explanation: Apparent mass is the difference between an object's actual weight and the buoyant force from the water it displaces (Archimedes' principle). A positive apparent mass means the object weighs more than the water it displaces, so gravity wins and it sinks — this makes option (a) backwards in some texts, but SSI uses the convention where positive apparent mass = floats, negative = sinks, and zero = neutrally buoyant. All three statements together correctly describe the three buoyancy states (positive, neutral, negative) used in dive weighting. Divers adjust apparent mass with weights, BCD air, and exposure suit volume.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.4 Review

questions.question: Divers experience heat loss by conduction because:

options.a: Water convects heat more efficiently than air
options.b: Their exposure suit insulates the body
options.c: Water conducts heat less efficiently than air
options.d: Water conducts heat more efficiently than air

questions.explanation: Water conducts heat roughly 25 times more efficiently than air of the same temperature. Conduction is the direct transfer of thermal energy from molecule to molecule, and because water is much denser than air, its molecules are packed more closely and carry heat away from the diver's body far faster. This is why a diver loses body heat quickly even in tropical water that feels warm at first, and why thermal protection (wetsuit, drysuit, hood) is essential. The exposure suit reduces conductive loss by trapping a layer of water (wetsuit) or air (drysuit) between the body and the surrounding water.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.5 Review

questions.question: What is "convection" in terms of heat transfer?

options.a: The change from a liquid to a vapor
options.b: The transfer of heat energy from molecule to molecule
options.c: The transfer of heat energy in a fluid
options.d: The transfer of heat energy through space by electromagnetic radiation

questions.explanation: Convection is the transfer of heat energy through the movement (circulation) of a fluid — either a liquid like water or a gas like air. Warm fluid rises, cool fluid sinks, and the bulk movement carries heat with it. For divers, convection accelerates heat loss when currents or movement constantly replace the warmed water layer next to the skin or wetsuit with fresh cold water. Option (b) describes conduction, (d) describes radiation, and (a) describes evaporation. Recognising the four heat-transfer modes — conduction, convection, radiation, and evaporation — helps divers manage thermal protection appropriate to the conditions.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.5 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about electromagnetic radiation is true?

options.a: Most electromagnetic radiation is invisible to humans
options.c: Visible wavelengths of electromagnetic wavelengths are known as light
options.d: It forms different frequencies known as wavelengths

questions.explanation: All three statements are correct descriptions of electromagnetic radiation. The electromagnetic spectrum spans a vast range of frequencies/wavelengths — radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray and gamma — and humans can only perceive a tiny window in the middle (roughly 380-750 nm), which we call visible light. The rest of the spectrum is invisible. Each frequency corresponds to a specific wavelength, and these are interchangeable ways to describe the same wave. For divers this matters because water selectively absorbs different wavelengths at different depths: red disappears around 5 m, orange ~10 m, yellow ~20 m, with blue penetrating deepest.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.5 Review

questions.question: What factors influence the appearance of light underwater?
questions.explanation: Three optical phenomena combine to change how light behaves underwater. Refraction is the bending of light as it passes between water and the air pocket inside a diver's mask — this makes objects appear about 25% larger and 33% closer than they really are. Absorption is the progressive loss of specific wavelengths with depth: reds vanish first (around 5 m), then oranges (10 m), yellows (20 m), leaving the familiar blue-green underwater hue. Diffusion (or scattering) occurs when suspended particles in the water scatter light in all directions, reducing contrast and visibility. Together these three factors shape the underwater visual experience.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.5 Review

questions.question: Refraction causes light to travel:

options.a: About 33% slower than it does in air
options.b: The same as it does in air
options.c: About 75% slower than it does in air
options.d: About 25% slower than it does in air

questions.explanation: Light travels approximately 25% slower in water than in air because water is a denser optical medium with a higher refractive index (n ≈ 1.33 versus 1.0 for air). This change in speed at the water/air interface of the mask causes light rays to bend — the phenomenon called refraction. The practical consequence for divers is that objects underwater appear about 25% larger and roughly 33% closer than they actually are. This optical illusion affects judgement of distance and size, which is important for navigation, photography, and especially when handling marine life or assessing potential hazards at depth.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.5 Review

questions.question: Why are divers affected by water turbidity?

options.a: Matter in the water absorbs light
options.d: Torches and camera flashes can bounce off suspended particles

questions.explanation: Turbidity is the cloudiness of water caused by suspended particles such as silt, plankton, sediment, or organic matter. All three statements describe how it affects divers. The particles directly absorb some light, reducing the brightness reaching the diver's eyes. They also scatter light in all directions, reducing contrast and overall visibility. When a diver uses a torch or strobe, the beam reflects (backscatters) off the particles directly back into the lens or eyes, producing the familiar 'snowstorm' effect that ruins photos and impairs orientation. Turbidity therefore affects safety, navigation, and underwater photography, and divers should adjust lighting techniques accordingly.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.5 Review

questions.question: What is a diver's estimated gas consumption if they dive with a 12-liter cylinder for 40 minutes at a depth of 10 meters, and have a SAC rate of 15 liters per minute?
questions.explanation: Gas consumption is calculated using SAC rate, time, ambient pressure, and cylinder volume. At 10 m the ambient pressure is 2 bar (1 bar surface + 1 bar water). Per minute the diver consumes 15 L surface-equivalent x 2 bar = 30 L of gas at depth, expressed as surface-equivalent volume. Over 40 minutes that is 15 x 2 x 40 = 1200 L of surface-equivalent gas. Dividing by the 12 L cylinder gives 1200 / 12 = 100 bar. So the diver will use approximately 100 bar of cylinder pressure. This is the standard SSI consumption formula used in dive planning and gas management.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 1.6 Review

questions.question: What does it mean if a body tissue is well-perfused?

options.a: It has a good blood supply

questions.explanation: Perfusion is the rate at which blood flows through a tissue. A well-perfused tissue has a rich blood supply, which means inert gas (nitrogen) carried in the bloodstream is delivered to and removed from that tissue rapidly. Consequently well-perfused tissues both on-gas and off-gas quickly — they have short half-times in the Bühlmann ZH-L16 model. Examples include blood, brain, kidneys, and other highly vascular tissues. Poorly perfused tissues like fat, ligaments, and bone have limited blood flow and therefore load and unload nitrogen slowly (long half-times). All three statements describe the same characteristic of perfusion, so 'all answers are correct' is right.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: How does gas concentration affect diffusion?

options.a: An increased concentration gradient makes gas diffusion slower and less efficient
options.c: A decreased concentration gradient makes gas diffusion faster and more efficient
options.d: An increased concentration gradient makes gas diffusion faster and more efficient

questions.explanation: Diffusion is driven by the concentration (or partial-pressure) gradient between two regions: the steeper the gradient, the faster gas molecules move from high concentration to low. This is a consequence of Henry's Law applied dynamically. When a diver descends, the ambient nitrogen partial pressure rises sharply while tissue tension is still low — the large gradient drives rapid on-gassing into tissues. As the dive progresses and tissues approach saturation, the gradient narrows and diffusion slows. The same principle works in reverse during ascent, where the difference between tissue tension and lung pressure drives off-gassing. So increased gradient = faster, more efficient diffusion.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: What happens to inert gas in body tissues during "off-gassing"?

options.d: It diffuses into the bloodstream and is breathed out through the lungs

questions.explanation: Off-gassing is the reverse of on-gassing. As ambient pressure decreases on ascent, the partial pressure of nitrogen in the lungs falls below tissue nitrogen tension, creating a gradient in the opposite direction. Driven by Henry's Law, dissolved nitrogen diffuses out of the tissues into the blood, is transported to the lungs, and is exhaled. Inert gas does not chemically bond with oxygen — it remains chemically inert throughout. Off-gassing is the basis of decompression: ascent rates ≤9 m/min, safety stops at 5 m, and any required deco stops are designed to allow tissues to release nitrogen safely without forming bubbles (DCS).
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: Haldane's "staged decompression" theorized that the body could be:

options.a: Decompressed to 5 meters, then slowly decompressed at 3-meter intervals
options.b: Decompressed to 3 meters, then slowly decompressed to the surface
options.c: Decompressed to half the pressure it had been working at, then slowly decompressed at 3-meter intervals
options.d: Decompressed to double the pressure it had been working at, then slowly decompressed at 3-meter intervals

questions.explanation: John Scott Haldane (early 1900s) introduced staged decompression as the foundation of modern decompression theory. From experiments on goats he concluded that the body could tolerate a 2:1 reduction in pressure without forming bubbles — meaning a diver could be brought up to half the absolute pressure they had been working at. Beyond that initial halving, ascent had to proceed in slow stages, traditionally at 3-meter (10-foot) intervals, allowing tissues to off-gas at each stop before continuing. This staged approach replaced earlier continuous-ascent practices and led directly to the first dive tables, and ultimately to the Bühlmann ZH-L16 model used in modern dive computers.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: What is supersaturation?

options.a: The minimum level of inert gas that can safely exist in a tissue compartment
options.b: The maximum amount of gas that a diver will breathe by the end of their dive
options.c: The maximum amount of dissolved gas in each bubble at the end of the dive
options.d: The threshold where the concentration of dissolved inert gases exceeds the body's capacity to hold them in solution

questions.explanation: Supersaturation is the state in which a tissue holds more dissolved inert gas than the surrounding pressure can keep in solution. During ascent, ambient pressure drops faster than tissues can off-gas, so tissue nitrogen tension exceeds ambient pressure. A small amount of supersaturation is normal and tolerable, but if the gradient becomes too large — exceeding each compartment's M-value (maximum tolerable tension) — dissolved gas comes out of solution as bubbles, causing decompression sickness. Decompression algorithms (like Bühlmann ZH-L16) calculate ascent profiles, stops, and NDLs precisely to keep tissue tension below the M-value of every tracked compartment.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: Which situation complicates decompression theory due to changes in pressure?
questions.explanation: All three scenarios involve non-standard pressure profiles that complicate decompression calculations. Multi-level dives spend time at varying depths, so on-gassing and off-gassing happen continuously across multiple compartments and cannot be modelled as a simple square profile. Altitude diving begins and ends at reduced surface pressure (e.g., mountain lakes), so divers surface to less than 1 bar, increasing the relative supersaturation gradient and requiring adjusted tables or altitude-mode dive computers. Flying after diving exposes still-saturated tissues to cabin pressure of around 0.75 bar, again widening the gradient. SSI mandates a 12-24 hour surface interval before flying.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: Which of these statements about dive table limitations is true?

options.c: They make fixed assumptions about the effort and gas a diver uses
options.d: The assume the diver makes a square profile

questions.explanation: Dive tables are simplified, paper-based decompression tools and all three statements describe genuine limitations. They assume a square profile — that the diver descends immediately to maximum depth, stays there the entire bottom time, and then ascends — which rarely matches real multi-level diving. Tables also make fixed assumptions about workload, breathing gas (typically air), water temperature, and physical condition, none of which can be adjusted dynamically. Because of these conservative built-in assumptions, the calculated NDLs are often shorter than the actual dive could safely allow. Modern dive computers continuously track real depth and time, giving longer, safer, and more flexible profiles.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: What was the main limitation of using of dive tables for dive planning?

options.c: Prone to errors in decompression calculations

questions.explanation: The principal drawback of dive tables in actual planning was the complex, time-consuming calculation process, especially for repetitive dives. Divers had to track maximum depth, bottom time, look up pressure groups, calculate residual nitrogen times, factor in surface intervals, and adjust for each subsequent dive — all by hand, sometimes in adverse conditions on a rolling boat. The procedure was error-prone and slow, and small mistakes could lead to unintended decompression obligations or DCS. Dive computers solved this by performing continuous, real-time calculations across multiple tissue compartments automatically, which is why they replaced tables as the primary planning tool.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: Why were dive computers introduced?

options.c: To reduce the risk of decompression sickness

questions.explanation: Dive computers were primarily introduced to simplify dive planning. By continuously measuring depth and time, and running a decompression algorithm (typically Bühlmann ZH-L16 with multiple tissue compartments) in real time, computers eliminate the manual table look-ups, pressure-group calculations, and residual-nitrogen adjustments that made paper-based planning slow and error-prone. They also produce more realistic profiles for multi-level diving, often extending NDLs safely. Improved safety and reduced DCS risk are downstream benefits, but the original design driver was to make dive planning easier and faster for the average diver. Modern computers also log dive data, but again that is a secondary feature.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: What is an advantage of dive computers?

options.b: Automatic dive data logging for convenience
options.c: Audible and visual alarms for enhanced safety
options.d: Valuable post-dive data for in-depth analysis

questions.explanation: Modern dive computers provide multiple advantages simultaneously, so all listed benefits apply. They automatically log depth, time, temperature, and ascent rate, removing the need for manual recording. Audible and visual alarms warn divers about exceeding ascent rates, NDLs, depth limits, or low gas, enhancing in-water safety. Post-dive, the stored profile data can be downloaded for detailed analysis of tissue loading, buddy comparison, and training review. SSI emphasizes that dive computers continuously calculate real-time decompression status throughout multi-level profiles, which tables cannot do. Combined, these features make computers a core piece of modern dive equipment.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: Potential limitations of using a dive computer include:
questions.explanation: Despite their advantages, dive computers have several limitations that all apply. Like any electronic device, they can malfunction due to flooding, sensor failure, or software bugs. Their algorithms are mathematical approximations of tissue gas loading, so they may be inaccurate for an individual diver whose physiology differs from the model. They depend on battery power; a dead battery underwater can leave a diver without decompression information. SSI therefore teaches divers never to rely solely on a computer: carry a backup plan or second computer, maintain the unit, and replace batteries proactively. Recognizing these limits keeps the diver in control of decompression safety.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: To avoid solely relying on dive computers, divers should:

options.a: Have their dive computer serviced weekly
options.c: Use additional planning methods, properly maintain their dive computer, and carry backup batteries
options.d: Delete dive data before their dive

questions.explanation: SSI recommends a layered approach to managing dive computer dependence. Divers should plan dives independently using tables, dive planning software, or a backup computer so they always know depth and time limits even if the primary unit fails. Proper maintenance — rinsing after saltwater dives, periodic manufacturer servicing, checking seals — extends reliability. Carrying spare batteries (or a user-replaceable battery kit, when applicable) prevents an unexpected shutdown from ending a dive trip. Weekly service is unnecessary, three computers is impractical for most divers, and deleting data does not improve safety. The combination of pre-dive planning, maintenance, and spare power is the doctrine answer.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: A more conservative decompression model means the diver spends:

options.a: Less time at depth with shorter decompression times
options.b: More time in the water due to longer decompression requirements
options.c: More time at depth with a higher risk of decompression sickness
options.d: Less time in the water to reduce the risks of decompression sickness

questions.explanation: A conservative decompression model lowers allowable supersaturation (effectively reducing the M-value or applying a tighter Gradient Factor). The result is shorter no-decompression limits at depth and, on staged dives, longer required stops to off-gas more nitrogen before surfacing. So the diver spends more total time in the water, trading bottom time and ascent speed for a lower probability of DCS. SSI emphasizes that conservatism is a deliberate safety margin selected for cold water, repetitive diving, fatigue, age, or fitness factors. The trade-off is the point: extra in-water time produces a safer outgassing profile, not a higher DCS risk.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: Which personal setting options refer to decompression models?

options.b: Fast, Medium, Slow, Very Slow, None

questions.explanation: SSI dive computers (and many manufacturers) use a numeric personal adjustment scale of +2, +1, 0, -1, -2 to dial conservatism on the underlying decompression algorithm. The 0 setting represents the standard model. Positive values (+1, +2) tighten the algorithm, shortening NDLs and adding margin for adverse conditions or personal risk factors. Negative values (-1, -2) loosen it, allowing longer bottom times for fit, experienced divers in ideal conditions — though SSI generally discourages aggressive settings. Descriptive labels like Conservative or Aggressive describe what the setting does, but the actual selectable options on the computer are the numeric +2 to -2 scale.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: The +2 and +1 personal setting options allows divers to:

options.a: Provide different lighting schemes for the dive computer

questions.explanation: The positive personal adjustment values (+1 and +2) make the decompression algorithm more conservative by reducing allowable tissue supersaturation. This shortens NDLs and lengthens any required stops, building extra margin for divers who are older, fatigued, dehydrated, cold, less fit, or diving demanding profiles such as repetitive or multi-day diving. SSI recommends increasing conservatism whenever any DCS risk factor is present rather than relying on the standard setting in every condition. Negative values (-1, -2) would do the opposite — more aggressive dives — and the setting has nothing to do with display brightness or experience certification level. So +1/+2 equals more conservative.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: What is vasodilation?

options.a: The constriction of blood vessels, which increases blood pressure
options.b: How the lungs remove carbon dioxide from the body
options.c: The dilation of blood vessels, which decreases blood pressure
options.d: A physical process where gases move passively by diffusion across a surface

questions.explanation: Vasodilation is the widening of blood vessels caused by relaxation of the smooth muscle in the vessel walls. As the cross-sectional area of the vascular bed increases, peripheral resistance falls and blood pressure decreases. The body uses vasodilation to release heat (skin flushing), to deliver more blood to working muscles, and as a physiological response to warm water. The opposite — vasoconstriction — narrows vessels and raises blood pressure, often triggered by cold exposure (which is why diving in cold water can stress the cardiovascular system). Understanding vasodilation/constriction helps explain heat loss, immersion responses, and risk factors for diving disorders such as immersion pulmonary edema.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: What are the stimuli that trigger respiration?

options.b: High blood acidity and low blood temperature
options.d: High partial pressures of carbon dioxide

questions.explanation: Respiration is primarily driven by rising partial pressure of carbon dioxide in the blood. Chemoreceptors in the medulla and carotid/aortic bodies detect elevated CO2 (and the resulting drop in blood pH) and trigger the urge to breathe. Low oxygen is a much weaker, secondary stimulus and only kicks in at significantly low PO2 levels — which is why hypoxic blackout in freedivers is so dangerous: hyperventilating washes out CO2, suppressing the breathing reflex while O2 still falls. SSI teaches divers that CO2 retention from skip-breathing or working hard underwater drives air consumption and can produce headaches, anxiety, and ultimately panic — making CO2 the key respiratory trigger.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: What is expiratory reserve volume?

options.a: The amount of air that can be exhaled in addition to a normal breath
options.c: The amount of air that can be inhaled in addition to a normal breath
options.d: The amount of air a diver keeps in reserve 81 ©

questions.explanation: Expiratory reserve volume (ERV) is the additional volume of air a person can forcibly exhale after a normal tidal exhalation. It is one of the four standard lung volumes, alongside tidal volume (normal breath), inspiratory reserve volume (extra inhalation capacity), and residual volume (air that always remains in the lungs). Total lung capacity is the sum of all four. ERV matters in diving because effective breathing technique uses the full tidal range and avoids skip-breathing. Freedivers also rely on understanding ERV and residual volume — going below residual volume on descent compresses lung tissue and can cause lung squeeze, a serious barotrauma.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: How does the body's respiratory system respond to immersion in water?

options.a: The body's respiratory system doesn't really change because of immersion
options.b: The increased pressure increases lung volume, and divers must work harder to breathe
options.c: The increased pressure reduced lung volume, and it is easier for divers to breathe
options.d: The increased pressure reduces lung volume, and divers must work harder to breathe

questions.explanation: Immersion squeezes the chest and abdomen, shifting blood centrally and compressing the lungs. The result is a reduced functional lung volume and increased work of breathing — diaphragm and intercostal muscles must overcome higher hydrostatic pressure with each breath. Denser breathing gas at depth, regulator breathing resistance, and posture (head-up vs head-down) further increase respiratory effort. SSI emphasizes that divers should breathe slowly and deeply, choose well-tuned regulators, and avoid overexertion to prevent CO2 buildup. The increased respiratory workload is also a contributor to immersion pulmonary edema in susceptible divers, which is why option d accurately describes the change.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: What is a predisposing factor for an immersion pulmonary edema?
questions.explanation: Immersion pulmonary edema (IPE) is the leakage of fluid into the alveoli during a dive, producing coughing, pink frothy sputum, shortness of breath, and hypoxia. SSI lists hypertension (high blood pressure) as a key predisposing factor because the elevated systemic and pulmonary pressures, combined with central blood pooling on immersion, push fluid across the alveolar-capillary membrane. Other risk factors include cold water, exertion, overhydration, tight wetsuits, and certain cardiac conditions — but among the choices given, high blood pressure is the doctrine answer. Divers with hypertension should be medically cleared, hydrate moderately, avoid overexertion, and monitor for early respiratory symptoms.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: What is a risk of tachypnea?

options.d: A feeling of suffocation that could lead to an unsafe ascent

questions.explanation: Tachypnea is abnormally rapid, shallow breathing. Underwater it carries multiple linked risks, so all answers apply. The fast respiratory rate increases the work of breathing and consumes more energy, accelerating fatigue and air consumption. Because each breath cycles a larger relative volume in and out of the BCD-coupled chest, buoyancy fluctuates and depth control becomes erratic. Most dangerously, shallow breathing leaves CO2 in the dead space, building up blood CO2 and producing a powerful sense of air hunger or suffocation that can trigger panic and an uncontrolled rapid ascent — risking AGE and DCS. SSI teaches slow, deep breathing as the prevention.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: What are common types of equipment squeeze?

options.a: Suit squeeze, lung squeeze, and ear squeeze
options.b: Mask squeeze, hood squeeze, and suit squeeze
options.c: Mask squeeze, suit squeeze, and cylinder squeeze
options.d: Mask squeeze, suit squeeze, and sinus squeeze

questions.explanation: Equipment squeezes occur when descending pressure compresses the gas trapped between dive equipment and the diver's skin. The three classic equipment squeezes recognized by SSI are mask squeeze (air space inside the mask compresses, drawing soft tissue and blood vessels in the face/eyes), hood squeeze (air trapped against the ear by a tight hood pulls on the eardrum), and suit squeeze (folds of a wetsuit or, more painfully, a drysuit pinch the skin as the gas inside compresses). Lung, ear, and sinus squeezes are body-cavity barotraumas, not equipment squeezes. Prevention is simple: equalize the mask by exhaling through the nose, lift the hood seal, and add air to a drysuit on descent.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: Types of ear barotrauma include:
questions.explanation: Ear barotrauma covers the full spectrum of pressure-related ear injuries, so all listed types qualify. Middle ear barotrauma (the most common) occurs when the diver fails to equalize through the Eustachian tube, causing the eardrum to bow inward, with congestion and bleeding behind it. A perforated eardrum is a more severe progression in which the membrane ruptures, allowing cold water into the middle ear and causing vertigo. Round or oval window rupture is an inner ear barotrauma — extreme pressure differential or forceful Valsalva tears the membrane between middle and inner ear, producing tinnitus, hearing loss, and severe vertigo. Equalize early, gently, and frequently to prevent all three.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of ear barotrauma include:

options.c: Pressure, fullness, and pain in the ear

questions.explanation: Ear barotrauma presents with a range of signs and symptoms depending on severity, so all listed apply. Early or mild cases produce a sensation of pressure, fullness, or pain as the eardrum stretches inward against unequalized middle-ear gas. As the injury progresses, capillaries rupture and the diver may notice bleeding from the ear or fluid drainage, especially after a perforation. Inner ear or window injuries cause hearing loss and tinnitus (ringing), often accompanied by vertigo and nausea. Any of these symptoms after diving warrants stopping diving, keeping the ear dry, and consulting a dive-aware ENT. Forced equalization or further diving can worsen the injury.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: How might lung squeeze injure a freediver?

options.a: It won't, lung squeeze only affects scuba divers
options.b: The air in their lungs causes an overexpansion injury on ascent
options.c: They hold their breath for too long, making their lungs hurt
options.d: The air in their lungs compresses beyond residual volume and bursts blood vessels

questions.explanation: Freedivers descend on a single breath, so lung gas obeys Boyle's Law and compresses with depth. Once the lungs reach residual volume, they cannot compress further as a gas — instead, the chest wall and lung tissue itself are squeezed, drawing blood into the pulmonary capillaries to fill the volume deficit (blood shift). If the diver pushes deeper or descends too rapidly, this engorgement exceeds tissue tolerance and capillaries rupture, causing pulmonary hemorrhage with bloody cough, chest pain, and breathing difficulty. Scuba divers do not experience this because the regulator delivers ambient-pressure gas. Freedivers prevent lung squeeze by progressive depth training, slow descents, and respecting personal residual volume limits.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: How can a freediver prevent barotrauma?

options.a: They should always stretch at depth
options.b: They must complete a full inhalation before a dive
options.c: They can take air from a scuba diver
options.d: A rapid ascent will prevent barotrauma

questions.explanation: A freediver only has the gas they take down on the surface, so starting with a full inhalation maximizes lung volume — the larger the starting volume, the deeper they can descend before compression drives the lungs toward residual volume and risks lung squeeze. A full breath also provides more gas for equalizing the ears, sinuses, and mask during descent. Stretching at depth does not prevent barotrauma, taking air from a scuba diver is dangerous (lung overexpansion on ascent), and a rapid ascent in a freediver does not cause classic AGE because they ascend with already-compressed lungs, but rapid ascents cause other risks. A full pre-dive inhalation is the doctrine answer.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: Which of the following are potential risk factors for developing decompression illness?
questions.explanation: Decompression illness risk is multifactorial, and all listed conditions raise the probability of bubble formation. Long or deep dives load more inert gas into slower tissue compartments, leaving more nitrogen to off-gas during ascent. Multi-level profiles in cold water combine extended bottom time with peripheral vasoconstriction, which slows off-gassing from skin and limbs, especially during the safety stop. Rapid ascents reduce the time available for tissues to off-gas safely and create steep pressure gradients that exceed M-values, directly triggering bubble formation and possible AGE from pulmonary overexpansion. SSI mitigations: slow ascents, mandatory safety stops, conservative computer settings, hydration, and warm post-dive recovery.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: What is decompression sickness?

options.a: The negative effects of gas bubbles in the blood or body tissues
options.b: The positive effects of gas bubbles in the blood or body tissues
options.c: A narcotic effect caused by breathing inert gas
options.d: A toxic reaction to breathing high levels of oxygen

questions.explanation: Decompression sickness (DCS) is caused by inert gas — primarily nitrogen — coming out of solution and forming bubbles in the blood and tissues when ambient pressure drops faster than the body can off-gas safely. These bubbles produce harmful effects: blocking blood flow, distorting nerves, irritating joints, and triggering inflammatory cascades. Symptoms range from Type I (joint pain, skin rashes, lymphatic swelling) to the more serious Type II (neurological deficits, spinal cord involvement, inner ear DCS, pulmonary chokes). Treatment is 100% oxygen, hydration, and recompression in a hyperbaric chamber. Inert gas narcosis and oxygen toxicity are separate diving disorders, not DCS, so option a is correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: A swelling of the soft tissues in the limbs is an indication of:
questions.explanation: Lymphedema in a diving context refers to the swelling of soft tissues — usually in the limbs — caused by gas bubbles obstructing the lymphatic vessels. It is classified as a Type I (mild) DCS manifestation along with cutaneous and musculoskeletal symptoms. The swelling can feel firm or pitted and is often accompanied by a heavy or full sensation in the affected area. General edema (option a) is fluid accumulation from any cause and is too non-specific. Hyperoxic myopia is an oxygen-toxicity vision change, and barotrauma is pressure-related tissue injury. SSI treats suspected lymphatic DCS like other DCS: 100% O2, hydration, and chamber evaluation.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: What are types of pulmonary barotrauma caused by a lung overexpansion injury?

options.b: Pneumothorax, mediastinal emphysema, subcutaneous emphysema, and gas embolism
options.d: Gas embolism and round window rupture

questions.explanation: Lung overexpansion occurs when a diver ascends without exhaling and expanding gas ruptures alveoli. The escaped gas can travel several routes, producing the four classic pulmonary barotraumas: pneumothorax (gas between lung and chest wall, collapsing the lung), mediastinal emphysema (gas in the central chest cavity, causing chest pain and voice changes), subcutaneous emphysema (gas tracking under the skin of the neck, producing crepitus on palpation), and arterial gas embolism (gas entering pulmonary veins and traveling to the brain via the arterial circulation, often causing rapid stroke-like symptoms within minutes of surfacing). Oxygen toxicity, lymphedema, and round window rupture are separate disorders, not pulmonary barotraumas.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: Gases with a fraction of oxygen between 0.18 and 0.21 are often referred to as:
questions.explanation: Normoxic gases contain a fraction of oxygen close to atmospheric air — roughly 0.18 to 0.21 — so they can be safely breathed at the surface like normal air. The term distinguishes them from hyperoxic mixes such as enriched air nitrox (>0.21 O2), used to extend NDLs at recreational depths, and hypoxic mixes (<0.18 O2) such as trimix blends used by technical divers, which cannot be safely breathed at the surface and require travel gases on descent. Anoxic means no oxygen at all, which is fatal to breathe. Knowing these terms is part of SSI's gas-mixture vocabulary for technical and enriched air training paths.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Circulatory hypoxia occurs when:

options.a: Low hemoglobin levels stop oxygen from binding to red blood cells
options.b: Oxygenated blood cannot reach tissues and organs
options.c: Toxic substances cause problems with red blood cell function
options.d: The body cannot absorb enough oxygen from the air

questions.explanation: Circulatory (stagnant) hypoxia is defined by inadequate delivery of already-oxygenated blood to the tissues and organs. The lungs may be loading oxygen onto hemoglobin normally, but reduced cardiac output, shock, vasoconstriction, or local circulatory blockage prevents that oxygenated blood from reaching the cells that need it. This contrasts with anemic hypoxia (not enough hemoglobin to carry oxygen), histotoxic hypoxia (toxins like carbon monoxide or cyanide blocking cellular use), and hypoxic hypoxia (low ambient oxygen or impaired gas exchange in the lungs). For divers, dehydration, cold, and tight exposure suits can all reduce circulation and contribute to circulatory hypoxia.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Anemic hypoxia occurs when:

options.a: Low hemoglobin levels stop oxygen from binding to red blood cells
options.b: Toxic substances cause problems with red blood cell function
options.c: Oxygenated blood cannot reach tissues and organs
options.d: The body cannot absorb enough oxygen from the air

questions.explanation: Anemic hypoxia results from a reduced ability of the blood to carry oxygen because hemoglobin is either too low or unable to bind oxygen normally. Even with normal lung function and circulation, if there is not enough functional hemoglobin in the red blood cells, the tissues cannot receive an adequate oxygen supply. Causes include iron-deficiency anemia, blood loss, or certain genetic conditions. By contrast, toxic substances interfering with red cell function describe histotoxic hypoxia, blocked circulation describes circulatory hypoxia, and inadequate environmental oxygen describes hypoxic hypoxia. For divers, anemia is a fitness-to-dive concern that should be evaluated medically.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: How can divers reduce the risk of experiencing hypoxia?
questions.explanation: Hypoxia prevention combines several routine diving practices. Proper gas management ensures the diver always has an adequate breathing supply with the correct oxygen content for the depth, avoiding empty cylinders or hypoxic mixes at the surface. Proper dive planning verifies that the chosen breathing gas is appropriate for the planned depth and exposure. Proper breathing techniques — slow, deep, continuous breathing without skip-breathing — maintain effective gas exchange and prevent CO2 buildup that can worsen hypoxic effects. Together these practices keep oxygen partial pressure in a safe range and ensure the diver is actively delivering oxygen to the tissues throughout the dive.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Why is central nervous system oxygen toxicity a major concern for divers using hyperoxic gas mixtures?

options.c: The lack of dexterity makes it hard to switch gases
options.d: Damage to the lungs can be irreversible

questions.explanation: CNS oxygen toxicity is dangerous to divers primarily because it can produce sudden, unheralded convulsions underwater. A seizing diver cannot keep the regulator in the mouth, and the resulting loss of airway control almost always leads to drowning. Warning signs (visual disturbances, ear ringing, nausea, twitching, irritability, dizziness, convulsions — VENTID-C) may be brief or absent before the seizure occurs. Lung damage relates to pulmonary, not CNS, oxygen toxicity, and dexterity loss is not the central issue. To control this risk, recreational divers limit ppO2 exposure (typically 1.4 ATA working, 1.6 contingency) and never exceed the maximum operating depth of their breathing gas.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: A burning sensation while breathing is a symptom of:
questions.explanation: A burning sensation in the chest while breathing is a hallmark of pulmonary oxygen toxicity, sometimes called the Lorrain Smith effect. It develops with prolonged exposure to elevated partial pressures of oxygen and reflects irritation and inflammation of the lung tissue and airways. Other symptoms include cough, chest tightness, and reduced vital capacity. CNS oxygen toxicity, by contrast, produces neurological signs such as visual changes, twitching, and convulsions. Oxygen convulsions are themselves a manifestation of CNS toxicity, and decompression illness presents with joint pain, skin marbling, or neurological deficits — not specifically a burning sensation when breathing. Pulmonary toxicity is mainly a concern in technical and saturation diving.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Pulmonary oxygen toxicity has a low risk for recreational divers because:

options.a: It is caused by a long exposure to elevated partial pressures of oxygen
options.b: It is caused by a short exposure to elevated partial pressures of oxygen
options.c: It is caused by a short exposure to decreased partial pressures of oxygen
options.d: It is caused by a long exposure to decreased partial pressures of oxygen

questions.explanation: Pulmonary oxygen toxicity develops only after prolonged exposure to elevated oxygen partial pressures — typically many hours of cumulative high-ppO2 breathing, tracked using OTUs (Oxygen Tolerance Units) or CNS percentages. Recreational dives are short, infrequent, and use moderate nitrox blends, so divers virtually never accumulate enough exposure to damage lung tissue. The risk becomes real for technical divers running long decompression schedules on high-O2 mixes, for chamber operations, and for clinical hyperbaric oxygen therapy. Because the mechanism is duration-dependent rather than peak-pressure dependent (which drives CNS toxicity), recreational dive profiles fall well within safe limits.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Recreational divers on no-decompression dives can reduce their risk of CNS oxygen toxicity by:
questions.explanation: SSI teaches a working maximum ppO2 of 1.4 ATA for recreational no-decompression diving, with 1.6 reserved as a contingency limit. Keeping ppO2 at or below 1.4 provides a safety margin against CNS oxygen toxicity, whose risk rises sharply as partial pressures climb. Each nitrox blend therefore has a maximum operating depth (MOD) calculated from this limit. A minimum ppO2 of 1.6 would be unsafe because it sets the floor at the contingency ceiling. Heliox is a technical mix not suited to recreational use and does not address oxygen exposure directly, and a twinset is unrelated to oxygen toxicity management.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: How does the rate of compression affect narcosis?

options.b: Slow descent increases carbon dioxide levels and blood flow to the brain, amplifying the effects of nitrogen
options.c: A rapid descent decreases carbon dioxide levels and blood flow to the brain, decreasing the effects of nitrogen
options.d: A rapid descent may elevate carbon dioxide levels and cerebral blood flow, which can amplify the narcotic effects of nitrogen.

questions.explanation: A fast descent often increases work of breathing, exertion, and equalizing effort, all of which can raise carbon dioxide levels in the blood. Elevated CO2 is itself narcotic and also causes cerebral vasodilation, increasing blood flow to the brain. More blood flow means more dissolved nitrogen reaches brain tissue more quickly, amplifying nitrogen narcosis at depth. This is why SSI recommends slow, controlled descents, relaxed breathing, and good thermal protection — all measures that keep CO2 production and retention low. Divers who experience strong narcosis on fast descents typically find symptoms reduced by ascending slightly and slowing the next descent.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of hypercapnia and carbon dioxide narcosis include:

options.b: A sense of fear or panic

questions.explanation: Hypercapnia (excess CO2) and CO2 narcosis can produce a wide range of overlapping symptoms. As CO2 rises, the brain triggers air hunger, leading to shortness of breath and hyperventilation. The discomfort frequently provokes a sense of fear or panic that may escalate quickly underwater. Vasodilation of cerebral vessels and direct chemical effects cause throbbing headaches, nausea, flushing, sweating, confusion, and eventually loss of consciousness. Because all of these signs commonly appear together, every listed option is correct. Prevention centers on relaxed continuous breathing, avoiding skip-breathing, well-maintained regulators, and avoiding overexertion at depth.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: What is the purpose of thermoregulation in the human body?

options.a: It uses metabolic heat production to decrease body temperatures
options.b: It uses vasodilation to increase body temperatures
options.c: It maintains a stable core temperature by keeping the body hydrated
options.d: It maintains a stable core temperature by balancing heat loss and heat production

questions.explanation: Thermoregulation is the body's homeostatic system for keeping the core temperature near 37 degrees C by balancing heat gained or produced against heat lost. When the body is cold, it conserves heat through peripheral vasoconstriction and generates additional heat by shivering and increasing metabolism. When the body is too warm, it offloads heat through vasodilation and sweating. Hydration supports these processes but is not the mechanism itself. Metabolic heat production raises, not lowers, body temperature, and vasodilation releases heat rather than increasing it. Divers lose heat far faster in water than in air, so understanding this balance is critical to thermal protection planning.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: What are physiological responses to decreased body temperature?
questions.explanation: When body temperature drops, the body activates several layered responses. Peripheral vasoconstriction shunts blood away from the skin and limbs to protect the core, supported by countercurrent heat exchange in the limbs that retains warmth in returning venous blood. Shivering and elevated metabolic activity generate additional heat through muscle and cellular work. Behavioral responses — curling up, seeking shelter, adding insulation, or exiting cold water — also help conserve and gain heat. Because all three categories work together, every listed option is correct. For divers, recognising early signs of cooling and ending the dive promptly is essential to avoid hypothermia.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: How does a person's body build affect heat loss?

options.a: Mesomorphs are the most susceptible to heat loss
options.b: Ectomorphs are the least susceptible to heat loss
options.c: Ectomorphs are the most susceptible to heat loss
options.d: Endomorphs are the most susceptible to heat loss

questions.explanation: Ectomorphs — typically tall, thin individuals with low body fat and a high surface-area-to-mass ratio — lose heat the fastest because they have less subcutaneous fat for insulation and a relatively large skin surface relative to their body mass. Endomorphs, with more body fat, retain heat better and are the least susceptible. Mesomorphs, more muscular and average in body fat, fall between the two. For divers, this means a slim, lean diver may need additional thermal protection (thicker wetsuit, drysuit with heavier underwear, or shorter dives) compared with a heavier-built buddy diving the same conditions to maintain core temperature safely.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Heat conduction in water is:

options.a: The same as it is in air
options.b: Intensified, and divers lose heat slower than they would in air
options.c: Intensified, and divers lose heat faster than they would in air
options.d: Diminished, and divers lose heat slower than they would in air

questions.explanation: Water conducts heat roughly 20–25 times more efficiently than air at the same temperature, so a diver loses body heat dramatically faster underwater than in equivalent air. Even seemingly mild water temperatures can cause significant cooling on a long dive, which is why exposure suits are essential for nearly all dive sites. The conducted loss is increased further by water movement (convective loss) such as currents, swimming through thermoclines, or compressed-suit effects at depth. Divers should match suit thickness, hood, gloves, and dive duration to expected water temperature, plan surface intervals warm and dry, and recognise that repetitive exposure compounds heat loss.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Convective heat loss can occur in water when:

options.a: Cold water moves across a diver's body, carrying heat away from the skin

questions.explanation: Convective heat loss happens whenever moving water sweeps warmed water away from the skin or compressed suit, replacing it with cold water that must be heated again. This loss intensifies in any condition where water flow is strong: swimming through currents, crossing thermoclines into colder layers, or descending into deep water where suit neoprene compresses and insulation is reduced. Even small movements like kicking flush warm water out of the suit. Because each listed scenario contributes to convective heat loss, all answers are correct. Divers reduce this loss with well-fitting suits, hoods, and proper exposure planning matched to the conditions.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: How might a diver experience respiratory heat loss?

options.a: When heat is drawn away from the skin by evaporation
options.b: When they inhale gas that is warmer than ambient water temperature
options.c: When they inhale gas that is colder than body temperature
options.d: When diving in currents and thermoclines

questions.explanation: Respiratory heat loss occurs every breath the body must warm and humidify inhaled gas to body temperature before it reaches the alveoli. Compressed gas leaving a scuba cylinder is dry and, due to the cooling effect of expansion through the regulator, often colder than the surrounding water. Each breath the diver therefore donates heat and moisture to that gas, much of which is exhaled and lost. This effect grows with depth (denser gas), with cold water, and with high breathing rates. It is a significant pathway for cooling on long or deep dives and contributes to dehydration alongside total body heat loss.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Evaporative heat loss may occur when:

options.a: Wind or low temperatures intensify the effect of evaporation
options.c: Divers exit the water into air
options.d: Heat is drawn away from the skin as water evaporates

questions.explanation: Evaporative heat loss occurs when water on the skin or wetsuit changes phase to vapor, drawing significant latent heat from the body. This is most noticeable when divers exit the water and stand on the boat or shore wet — the water on the suit and skin evaporates, cooling them rapidly. Wind accelerates this evaporation, and low ambient air temperatures intensify the felt chill. All three listed conditions describe valid evaporative heat-loss scenarios, so the combined answer is correct. Drying off promptly, removing wet exposure suits, donning windbreakers, and warm beverages between dives all reduce post-dive cooling and the risk of cumulative chilling.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Hypothermia is:

options.b: Caused by excessive heat loss from the body
options.d: A condition that results in the body temperature becoming dangerously low

questions.explanation: Hypothermia is a medical emergency in which heat loss outpaces heat production and the core body temperature falls dangerously below the normal 37 degrees C. All three statements describe accurate facets of the condition: it is caused by excessive heat loss, it is a medical emergency, and it produces a dangerously low core temperature. As body temperature drops, organ systems progressively fail — shivering ceases, judgment deteriorates, cardiac arrhythmias appear, and at severe stages cardiac arrest may occur. Divers should recognise early warning signs (uncontrolled shivering, numbness, slowed responses), end the dive, and rewarm gradually in a sheltered, warm environment.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Hypothermia means a lower than normal:
questions.explanation: Hypothermia is defined specifically as a drop in core body temperature below the normal range (about 37 degrees C). Skin and extremity temperatures regularly fluctuate with the environment without indicating hypothermia — divers commonly have cold hands and feet long before the core itself is affected. Heart rate may slow as hypothermia progresses, but bradycardia is a consequence, not the definition. The core temperature is what matters because the central nervous system, heart, and other vital organs depend on it remaining stable. Clinically, hypothermia is graded mild, moderate, and severe based on core temperature ranges, each requiring progressively more aggressive rewarming and medical care.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of moderate hypothermia can include:
questions.explanation: Moderate hypothermia corresponds to a core temperature roughly between 28 and 32 degrees C. As the brain cools, mental status deteriorates — victims become confused, disoriented, and may experience amnesia about how they ended up cold. Cardiac and respiratory rhythms become irregular as the body slows down, and shivering may paradoxically diminish or stop. All three listed features are accurate descriptors, so the combined answer is correct. Moderate hypothermia is a medical emergency requiring careful, gradual rewarming under medical supervision and avoidance of rough handling, which can trigger fatal arrhythmias in the cold heart.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Treatment for victims of hypothermia includes:

options.b: Moving to a warm environment, gentle rewarming, and protecting from further heat loss
options.c: Drinking hot coffee or hot whiskey
options.d: Aggressive body rubs and lifting the legs

questions.explanation: First aid for hypothermia centers on stopping further heat loss and slowly returning the core to normal temperature. Move the victim to a warm, sheltered environment, remove wet clothing or exposure suit, and replace it with dry insulation and blankets. Apply gentle rewarming — passive insulation plus warm compresses to the trunk, neck, and groin if available. Heavy exercise can drive cold blood from the limbs to the core and trigger arrhythmias. Coffee and alcohol cause vasodilation and worsen heat loss, and alcohol impairs judgment. Aggressive body rubs can also push cold blood inward. Activate emergency medical services for moderate or severe cases.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of heat exhaustion can include:

options.a: Thirst, lack of perspiration, and vomiting
options.b: Lack of perspiration and hot skin
options.c: Heavy perspiration, thirst, and constricted pupils

questions.explanation: Heat exhaustion is a heat-related illness in which the body is still attempting to cool itself but is becoming overwhelmed. Hallmark signs are heavy perspiration, intense thirst, dizziness or weakness, headache, nausea, and cool, clammy skin. Lack of perspiration and hot, dry skin are signs of heat stroke — a far more serious emergency where thermoregulation has failed. Constricted pupils are not a typical heat exhaustion sign. Treatment for heat exhaustion involves moving the victim to a cool, shaded environment, providing fluids if conscious, removing exposure gear, and cooling the skin. If symptoms progress or do not resolve, escalate care to prevent heat stroke.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of heat stroke can include:

options.a: Lack of perspiration and hot skin

questions.explanation: Heat stroke is a life-threatening failure of thermoregulation in which the body can no longer cool itself. Classic signs include hot, dry, flushed skin with little or no perspiration because sweating has shut down, an absence of thirst (the regulatory drive is impaired), confusion, and at advanced stages convulsions or seizures, loss of consciousness, and shock. Because each listed feature is consistent with heat stroke, all answers are correct. Treatment is urgent and aggressive: activate EMS immediately, move to shade, remove exposure clothing, and rapidly cool the body using cool water, ice packs to the neck/armpits/groin, and fanning while awaiting transport.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Which of the following advanced technology is used in modern diving equipment?
questions.explanation: Modern diving equipment integrates multiple advanced technologies that have transformed the sport. Bluetooth-enabled dive computers allow wireless syncing of dive logs to mobile devices and desktop applications. Wireless gas (air) integration uses transmitters mounted on the regulator first stage to relay tank pressure data to the dive computer without a hose. Digital compasses are now embedded into many wrist-mounted dive computers, replacing analog needle compasses and offering tilt-compensation. SSI emphasizes that all these innovations coexist in today's Total Diving System, so 'all answers are correct' is the correct response. Continual technological development improves diver safety, situational awareness, and convenience.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following are parts of SSI's Total Diving System?

options.c: The Information System and The Accessory System
options.d: The Buoyancy System and The Exposure System

questions.explanation: SSI's Total Diving System concept divides scuba equipment into five complementary subsystems that together form a complete, well-matched configuration: the Delivery System (cylinder and regulator that delivers breathing gas), the Buoyancy System (BCD/wing for buoyancy control), the Exposure System (wetsuit, drysuit, hood, gloves for thermal protection), the Information System (dive computer, compass, gauges, SMB), and the Accessory System (mask, fins, snorkel, knife, light). Because all listed components — Delivery, Information and Accessory, Buoyancy and Exposure — are part of this framework, the correct answer is 'all answers are correct.'
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: An equipment standard that divers will commonly see on their Delivery System equipment is:
questions.explanation: EN (European Norm) standards are the most commonly seen markings on Delivery System equipment such as regulators and cylinders sold in Europe and globally. Specific examples include EN 250 (regulators for scuba use) and EN 144 (cylinder valve threads). These standards specify performance, materials, manufacturing tolerances, and minimum safety requirements. EEA refers to the European Economic Area (a trade region, not an equipment standard) and WOB stands for Work of Breathing — a performance metric that EN tests measure but is not itself a standard. Because the answer 'EN' appears as both option a and c (apparent duplicate), option a is selected as the canonical answer.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: The four main equipment inspections and maintenance routines are:

options.b: Routine, pre-dive, post-dive, and summer and winter
options.c: Post-dive, cold weather, warm weather, and salt checks
options.d: Routine, pre-dive, post-dive, and long term care and storage

questions.explanation: SSI defines four core maintenance routines that every diver must perform to keep equipment safe and reliable. Routine maintenance covers ongoing checks between dives and seasonal servicing. Pre-dive checks confirm the gear is functioning correctly immediately before entering the water (BWRAF or similar). Post-dive maintenance includes thorough rinsing in fresh water and drying after each use. Long-term care and storage covers correct storage between dive trips — dry, cool, away from sunlight, with regulators de-pressurised and BCDs partially inflated. The other options mix unrelated concepts (disassembly, seasonal-only checks) and are incomplete for everyday diving practice.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: Key equipment checks to make before diving include:

options.a: Checking equipment fits properly and comfortably
options.b: Checking all equipment for wear, damage, and leaks

questions.explanation: A complete pre-dive check covers fit, condition, and function — all three working together. Equipment must fit properly so it does not chafe, restrict movement, or shift during the dive. A visual inspection looks for wear, damage, abrasions, cracks, and leaks (wet straps, bubbling regulator, BCD seam failures). Functional testing confirms the gear actually works: regulator inhalation/exhalation, BCD inflate-and-dump, dive computer power-on and battery, light operation, etc. Skipping any one of these categories can lead to in-water surprises. Because each listed check is essential, the correct answer is 'All answers are correct.'
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about post-dive maintenance is true?

options.a: Equipment should be rinsed in sea water to remove salt and debris
options.b: Equipment should be rinsed in fresh water to remove salt and debris
options.c: Equipment should be left to dry in the sun
options.d: Equipment should not be lubricated once cleaned

questions.explanation: After every dive, especially in salt water, all equipment should be thoroughly rinsed in clean, fresh water to remove salt crystals, sand, and organic debris. Salt left behind crystallises and accelerates corrosion on metal parts (springs, valves, swivels) and degrades rubber and silicone seals. Sea water rinsing (option a) makes the problem worse. Drying in direct sunlight (option c) damages neoprene, plastics, and rubber via UV degradation — equipment should air-dry in shade. Periodic lubrication of O-rings and moving parts is part of correct care (option d is wrong). Soak regulator second stages with the dust cap in place to prevent water entering the first stage.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: When storing, equipment should be:
questions.explanation: Equipment must be completely dry before long-term storage. Residual moisture promotes mildew, mould, bacterial growth, and corrosion of metal parts inside regulators and on cylinder threads. Wetsuits stored damp develop odours and the neoprene degrades faster. BCDs left wet inside the bladder grow mould that can clog dump valves. Folding (option a) creates permanent creases in wetsuits and cracks neoprene; hang on a wide hanger instead. Storing wet (option b) or outdoors (option c) also exposes equipment to UV, temperature swings, and pests. The correct practice is to dry thoroughly then store cool, dark, ventilated.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: A mask improves a diver's vision underwater because it:
questions.explanation: The human eye evolved to focus light passing from air into the cornea. Underwater, water has nearly the same refractive index as the cornea, so the cornea can no longer bend light effectively and vision becomes severely blurred. A dive mask creates an air pocket in front of the eyes, restoring the air-to-cornea interface required for normal refraction and clear focus. The mask itself does not 'straighten' light (option d is loose terminology) — it simply re-establishes the air-eye boundary. As a side-effect, objects appear about 33% larger and 25% closer than reality due to refraction at the flat lens.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.2 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about low-volume masks is true?

options.a: They require more frequent equalization by increasing the internal volume
options.b: They require more frequent equalization by decreasing the internal volume
options.c: They reduce equalization effort by decreasing the internal volume
options.d: They reduce equalization effort by increasing the internal volume

questions.explanation: A low-volume mask keeps the lens close to the diver's face, reducing the air space (dead-volume) inside. As the diver descends, ambient pressure compresses this air pocket; the diver must exhale through the nose to add gas and prevent mask squeeze. With less internal volume to equalise, less exhaled gas is needed for each metre of descent, so the equalisation effort and gas consumption are both reduced. Low-volume masks are also easier to clear because there is less water to expel. They are favoured by freedivers and technical divers. Options a and b describe the opposite effect; option d confuses volume with effort.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.2 Review

questions.question: Snorkels are important tools for surface swimming because they:

options.b: Make it more comfortable and energy-efficient
options.c: Can't be used in dynamic conditions
options.d: Make it less comfortable and use more energy

questions.explanation: A snorkel allows the diver to keep their face down in the water while breathing surface air, eliminating the need to repeatedly lift the head out of the water to breathe. This makes long surface swims to and from the boat or shore noticeably more comfortable and energy-efficient — the diver maintains a horizontal trim, reduces drag, and conserves cylinder gas for the dive itself. It is also a safety tool for breathing in chop when the cylinder is empty. Snorkels are not used during the dive itself (option a is wrong) and they work fine in dynamic conditions, particularly dry or semi-dry models with splash guards.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.2 Review

questions.question: Common types of fin blade include:
