questions.explanation: SSI lists four common fin blade designs. Paddle fins use a flat, stiff blade and deliver strong, traditional thrust suitable for currents and frog-kick. Channel fins have ribs along the blade that 'channel' water along the surface for improved efficiency. Split fins have a longitudinal slit that creates two foil-like halves, generating thrust through a propeller-like effect with reduced effort. Monofins join both feet onto a single large blade and are used primarily by freedivers and underwater hockey players for maximum dolphin-kick propulsion. 'Semi-dry' (option a) refers to snorkels, not fins, and 'high-volume' (option b) is not a recognised fin category.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.2 Review

questions.question: The Delivery System includes:
questions.explanation: Within SSI's Total Diving System framework, the Delivery System is the subsystem that stores breathing gas and delivers it to the diver on demand. It comprises the cylinder (high-pressure gas storage) and the regulator (which reduces the high cylinder pressure first to an intermediate pressure and then to ambient pressure for breathing). The buoyancy compensator belongs to the Buoyancy System, the mask to the Accessory System, and the wetsuit to the Exposure System. Recognising the correct grouping is important because matching components within a subsystem (e.g., DIN regulator to DIN cylinder valve, nitrox-rated regulator to nitrox cylinder) is essential for safe diving.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: How does steel differ from aluminum?

options.c: It is less dense and weaker

questions.explanation: Steel is approximately three times denser than aluminium and has roughly three times the tensile strength. Because steel is stronger, manufacturers can build steel cylinders with thinner walls, producing a smaller external profile for the same gas volume — so steel cylinders are typically more compact than aluminium ones of equal capacity. Higher density means a steel cylinder is also heavier and tends to be more negatively buoyant in water, which influences how much lead a diver needs. Option a wrongly says 'larger' (steel is generally smaller); option c reverses the physical reality. The correct distinction is 'denser and stronger.'
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: When compared with a steel cylinder of the same size, an aluminum cylinder may be:
questions.explanation: Aluminium has roughly one-third the density of steel, but aluminium cylinders need thicker walls because the metal is weaker. The thicker walls add bulk so the overall mass is still lower than the equivalent steel cylinder — aluminium cylinders are lighter on land. Because aluminium is less dense, it also displaces relatively more water for its weight, making aluminium cylinders generally more positively buoyant in the water, particularly when nearly empty (a common AL80 swings from slightly negative full to about +1.5 to +2 kg positive empty). Steel cylinders by contrast remain negatively buoyant throughout the dive. The correct answer is therefore 'lighter but more positively buoyant.'
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: Four types of cylinders used by divers include:
questions.explanation: SSI identifies four common cylinder configurations divers will encounter. Single cylinders are the standard recreational setup (one tank on the back). Twinsets (doubles) join two cylinders with a manifold to provide redundant gas and twice the capacity, used in technical, cave, and wreck diving. Pony cylinders are small bail-out tanks (usually 2-3 litres) carried as a fully redundant emergency air source. Stage cylinders are larger separate tanks (often slung on the side) used to extend bottom time, carry decompression gas, or stage gas for cave penetration. Because all three listed options describe legitimate types, the correct answer is 'all answers are correct.'
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: What criteria should a diver include when selecting a cylinder?

options.a: It must be very light so they can easily carry it
options.b: It should not have a high volume
options.c: It must have capacity for gas for their planned dive and an emergency reserve in a pony cylinder
options.d: It must have capacity for gas for their planned dive and an emergency reserve

questions.explanation: When selecting a cylinder a diver must ensure it carries enough gas for the entire planned dive plus a sufficient emergency reserve to handle problems such as a delayed ascent, buddy share, or unplanned decompression. The reserve does not have to be in a separate pony cylinder (option c is too narrow — the reserve can simply be the rock-bottom or 1/3 turn-pressure portion of the same cylinder). Lightness alone (option a) is not a primary selection criterion, and minimising volume (option b) contradicts the gas-supply requirement. Correct selection therefore matches dive plan + reserve to cylinder capacity, factoring in depth, duration, SAC rate, and a safety margin.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about twinsets is true?

options.b: It is very light and can be carried easily
options.c: It contains double the gas supply of a single
options.d: It should only be used for shallow dives

questions.explanation: A twinset (doubles) consists of two matched cylinders joined by an isolator manifold, effectively giving the diver double the gas capacity of a single cylinder. This makes twinsets the standard configuration for technical, cave, decompression, and deep dives where extended bottom times and full redundancy are required. They are explicitly NOT light or easy to carry (option b is wrong — a steel twinset can weigh 30 kg or more on land), and they are designed for deep or long dives, not shallow ones (option d is wrong). The defining characteristic SSI emphasises is the doubled gas supply and built-in redundancy via the manifold.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: Where might divers place decompression gas in stage cylinders?

options.b: Hung from the side of the boat

questions.explanation: Decompression gas in stage cylinders is typically slung on the diver's side using bolt-snap clips on the harness D-rings — one snap at the regulator yoke and another at the bottom of the cylinder. This 'side-mount' or 'stage' carry positions the cylinder along the diver's torso for streamlined trim and easy access to the regulator and SPG when switching gases at decompression stops. The bottom gas remains in the back-mounted twinset, not the deco gas (option a is wrong). Hanging cylinders from the boat (option b) is sometimes done for emergency oxygen but is not where 'stage cylinders' are placed during the dive. Option d is incorrect because each diver carries their own gas.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: A K-valve can accommodate:

options.b: Two regulator first stages on one cylinder
options.c: Two regulator first stages on two cylinders using a manifold
options.d: One regulator first stage on one cylinder

questions.explanation: A K-valve is the simplest cylinder valve design — a single on/off knob with a single outlet port that accepts one regulator first stage on a single cylinder. It has no provision for a second regulator and no manifold connection, so it cannot mount redundant first stages or join twin cylinders. To mount two regulators on one cylinder a diver needs an H-valve (two outlets), and to join two cylinders into a twinset a manifold-equipped valve set is required. The K-valve remains the standard for most single-cylinder recreational setups due to its simplicity, low weight, and low cost. The correct answer is therefore 'one regulator first stage on one cylinder.'
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: How many regulators can an H-valve accommodate on one cylinder?

options.a: One regulator first stage on two cylinders using a manifold
options.b: Two regulator first stages on one cylinder
options.c: One regulator first stage on one cylinder
options.d: Three regulator first stages on one cylinder

questions.explanation: An H-valve has two completely independent outlets on a single cylinder, each with its own on/off knob. This allows two regulator first stages to be mounted on one cylinder, providing full first-stage redundancy. If one regulator free-flows or fails, the diver shuts down that side and breathes from the other regulator — without losing any gas supply, since both regulators draw from the same cylinder. H-valves are popular with technical divers using single cylinders, solo divers, and ice or overhead divers seeking redundancy without the bulk of twinsets. Option a describes a manifold (twinset), option c describes a K-valve, and option d is not a standard configuration.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about DIN connections is true?

options.a: It is the standard connection on most U.S.-made cylinders.
options.b: It operates at lower working pressures compared to yoke connections.
options.c: The first stage fits like a clamp and tightens over the cylinder valve
options.d: The first stage screws into the cylinder valve

questions.explanation: On a DIN (Deutsches Institut für Normung) connection the regulator first stage has a male threaded fitting that screws directly into the female threaded port of the cylinder valve, capturing the O-ring inside the valve body. This is mechanically more secure and rated for higher working pressures (up to 300 bar/4350 psi) than the yoke (INT/A-clamp) connection (option b is wrong — DIN handles higher pressures). The yoke is the design that fits 'like a clamp' over the valve (option c describes yoke). Yoke is the U.S. standard (option a is wrong); DIN is dominant in Europe and technical diving worldwide. The correct statement is that the DIN first stage screws into the cylinder valve.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: What cylinder markings and labels must a diver check before diving?
questions.explanation: Before every dive the diver must verify all cylinder markings and labels. The gas contents label (e.g., 'Air,' 'EAN32,' 'Trimix 18/45') confirms what gas is inside and must be analysed and signed for. The Maximum Operating Depth (MOD) is calculated from the FO₂ and the maximum allowed PpO₂ (typically 1.4 bar) and is marked on a contents tag — exceeding it risks CNS oxygen toxicity. The Visual Inspection (VIP) date confirms the cylinder has been internally inspected within the past year, and the hydrostatic test stamp confirms structural integrity (typically every 5 years). All three checks are mandatory, so the correct answer is 'all answers are correct.'
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: When using gas blends other than air, what must a diver do before diving?

options.a: Ask their divemaster what they gas analysis is
options.b: They do not need to analyze the gas--their dive team will check this for them
options.c: Get a buddy to analyze their gas while they get a coffee
options.d: Personally analyze the gas or witness the analysis

questions.explanation: Whenever a cylinder is filled with anything other than standard air — such as Nitrox, Trimix, or any enriched blend — the diver who will breathe that gas is personally responsible for verifying its content. SSI doctrine requires that the diver either analyze the gas themselves with a calibrated oxygen (and, if applicable, helium) analyzer, or directly witness the analysis being performed. The diver then logs the fraction, MOD, and signs the cylinder tag. Delegating this step to a divemaster, buddy, or fill operator is unacceptable, because breathing an unverified blend can cause oxygen toxicity or hypoxia at depth.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of automatic water drainage systems in breathing compressors?
questions.explanation: Compressing atmospheric air concentrates the water vapor it contains, and as the gas cools between compression stages this moisture condenses into liquid water inside the separators. If that condensate is not removed it will saturate the filter media, accelerate internal corrosion of the cylinder, and ultimately reach the diver as wet, contaminated breathing gas. Automatic condensate drains periodically purge accumulated water from the inter-stage separators, keeping the gas dry and meeting CGA Grade E / EN 12021 air-quality standards. While filters remove hydrocarbons and oil mist, the drains exist specifically to manage moisture buildup in the compression train.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: How could a breathing gas compressor cause carbon monoxide poisoning?

options.b: Compressors could draw exhaust fumes into breathing gas
options.c: Improper maintenance could introduce combustion vapors into cylinders
options.d: Filter failure could allow contaminants into the cylinder

questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide can enter scuba cylinders through several distinct compressor failure modes, and SSI lists all of them as legitimate causes. A poorly positioned intake may draw exhaust fumes from a generator, vehicle, or boat engine directly into the air supply. An overheating or oil-burning compressor with worn rings can produce CO from partially combusted lubricant inside the compression stages. And a saturated, expired, or bypassed catalyst/filter cartridge can fail to convert or trap residual CO before the gas reaches the cylinder. Because all three pathways are realistic, the correct answer is that all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: Each cylinder must be visually inspected:
questions.explanation: SSI doctrine, mirroring industry standards such as PCI/PSI in North America and IDEST in Europe, requires that every scuba cylinder undergo a Visual Inspection Procedure (VIP) once a year. A trained inspector removes the valve and examines the interior and exterior for corrosion, pitting, cracks, neck damage, and contamination, then affixes a dated VIP sticker if the cylinder passes. This annual visual check is separate from, and more frequent than, the hydrostatic pressure test, which is performed every five years in the US (DOT) or on a 2.5/5-year schedule under EU PED. Cylinders without a current VIP should not be filled.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: What should divers do with cylinders after diving?

options.a: Rinse them and vent the valve
options.b: Make sure the valve is removed and rinsed in water
options.c: Nothing, they can be left to dry
options.d: Vent the valve before exiting the water

questions.explanation: Standard post-dive cylinder care has two parts. First, rinse the cylinder, valve, and boot with fresh water to flush away salt, sand, and contaminants that accelerate corrosion of the metal body and seize the valve threads. Second, vent the valve briefly — open it for a moment after rinsing — to clear any water that may have entered the dust cap area or the valve outlet, preventing moisture from being driven into the cylinder during the next fill. Removing the valve entirely (option b) is a service-shop task, not a diver task, and is only done during annual VIP.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: How can divers identify a possible cylinder leak?

options.b: By the presence of hissing sounds or bubbles when the valve is open
options.c: When the valve sticks on the cylinder
options.d: If no gas comes out of the cylinder

questions.explanation: A leaking cylinder, valve, or regulator connection reveals itself the same way any pressurized gas leak does: by an audible hiss in air, or by a steady stream of small bubbles when the assembly is submerged or wetted. Divers should perform this check during pre-dive setup by pressurizing the regulator, listening near the yoke or DIN fitting, the burst-disk port, and the o-ring seat, and watching for bubbles once in the water. A free-flowing regulator (option a) indicates a second-stage problem, a stuck valve indicates corrosion, and no gas flow points to a closed valve — none of those signal a leak.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.3 Review

questions.question: When a cylinder is first connected and turned on, the whole regulator becomes:
questions.explanation: Opening the cylinder valve admits high-pressure gas through the first stage, which steps it down to intermediate pressure (typically 9-11 bar above ambient) and feeds it through every low-pressure hose to the second stages, BCD inflator, and drysuit hose, while the SPG hose carries full cylinder pressure to the gauge. At that moment the entire regulator system — first stage, hoses, and second stages — is pressurized, and a leak anywhere in the assembly will become audible or visible. Divers should always open the valve slowly while watching the SPG, and confirm the regulator holds pressure before donning the unit.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.4 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about unbalanced first stages is true?

options.a: Breathing gas pressure decreases as cylinder pressure drops
options.b: Breathing gas pressure decreases as cylinder pressure increases
options.c: Breathing gas is delivered at a consistent pressure as cylinder pressure drops
options.d: Breathing gas pressure increases as cylinder pressure drops

questions.explanation: An unbalanced first stage uses high-pressure gas acting directly on one face of the valve seat to help close the valve. As the cylinder empties and supply pressure falls, that closing force weakens relative to the spring, so the intermediate pressure delivered to the second stage drifts slightly downward as well. The diver experiences this as gradually increasing breathing effort late in the dive, especially at depth. Balanced first stages use a vented or compensated piston/diaphragm design that isolates the valve from supply pressure, holding intermediate pressure constant from full to nearly empty — which is why most modern regulators are balanced.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.4 Review

questions.question: The basic mechanisms of a piston first stage use a:

options.a: Flexible diaphragm to control the valve allowing gas flow to the second stage
options.b: Spring-loaded piston to keep the valve from the cylinder continually open
options.c: Flexible piston to open the second stage valve
options.d: Spring-loaded piston to regulate pressure between the high- and intermediate-pressure chambers

questions.explanation: In a piston first stage, a metal piston rides between the high-pressure side and the intermediate-pressure (IP) chamber, with a main spring pushing it toward the open position. When IP in the chamber rises high enough — combined with ambient water pressure on the spring side — the piston shifts and seals against the high-pressure seat, stopping flow. As the diver inhales and IP drops, the spring re-opens the piston and lets more gas through. The piston therefore regulates pressure between the HP and IP chambers; a diaphragm (option a) describes a different first-stage architecture, not a piston.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.4 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about balanced diaphragm first stages is true?

options.a: They provide a reliable and smooth breathing effort
options.c: Most diaphragm first stages use balancing technology

questions.explanation: A balanced diaphragm first stage combines two desirable traits. Because the valve is isolated from cylinder pressure by the balancing chamber, intermediate pressure stays constant from a full tank down to near-empty, which gives a smooth, consistent breathing effort throughout the dive. The sealed diaphragm design also keeps water and contaminants out of the spring chamber, making it a popular choice for cold-water and silty environments. Today the overwhelming majority of diaphragm first stages on the market are balanced — manufacturers rarely sell unbalanced diaphragm models. All three statements are therefore correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.4 Review

questions.question: Common types of second stage mechanisms include the:

options.d: Classic downstream piston and air-balanced flow-through piston

questions.explanation: Modern second stages are built around two common valve architectures. The downstream valve is the classic design: intermediate pressure pushes the valve open in the direction of gas flow, and a spring plus the diaphragm hold it closed; this is simple, reliable, and inherently fail-safe because over-pressurization vents through the mouthpiece rather than rupturing a hose. The pilot valve uses a small servo-style valve to actuate a larger main valve, dramatically reducing cracking effort and giving very smooth breathing on demand. Pistons (options a, c, d) are first-stage mechanisms, not second-stage mechanisms.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.4 Review

questions.question: As a diver consumes breathing gas with an unbalanced second stage, "cracking" effort may:
questions.explanation: Cracking effort is the small inhalation pressure (typically 1-2 inches of water) the diver must generate to flex the second-stage diaphragm and open the demand valve. In an unbalanced second stage, intermediate pressure itself helps lift the valve off the seat, so as the cylinder empties and IP sags, less assist is available and the diver must pull harder to start each breath. Combined with increased gas density at depth, this makes the regulator feel noticeably stiffer late in the dive. A balanced second stage compensates for IP variation, keeping cracking effort essentially constant from a full to a nearly empty cylinder.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.4 Review

questions.question: External icing may be caused by:

options.c: Rapidly inflating a dry suit while breathing heavily
options.d: Moving into cold water through a thermocline

questions.explanation: External icing forms on the outside of a regulator when ambient water is already near freezing and the metal body is chilled further by the Joule-Thomson effect of gas expanding through the first stage. Three common situations all promote it: simply diving in extremely cold water (under roughly 5 °C); high gas-flow events such as rapid drysuit inflation or BCD inflation while the diver is also breathing heavily, which multiply the cooling effect; and crossing a thermocline into significantly colder water, where the regulator suddenly meets a much lower temperature. Because all three scenarios are valid triggers, the correct answer is all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.4 Review

questions.question: The key purpose of the Buoyancy System is to achieve:

options.a: Neutral buoyancy at depth and controlled ascents
options.b: Positive surface flotation and controlled descents

questions.explanation: SSI describes the Buoyancy Compensator Device (BCD) as a multi-purpose tool that fulfils every phase of the dive. On the surface it provides positive flotation so the diver can rest, signal, or assist a buddy. During descent the diver vents it to achieve a controlled, head-up entry into the water column. At depth the BCD is fine-tuned to neutral buoyancy, allowing effortless trim and precise positioning. On the way up it is gradually vented to maintain a controlled, slow ascent and to hold a stable safety stop. Because all of those functions are core to the system, the correct answer is all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.6 Review

questions.question: Deflation mechanisms on a buoyancy compensator include:

options.c: A deflator or exhaust button on the power inflator

questions.explanation: A modern BCD has multiple ways of releasing gas, each suited to a different orientation. The exhaust button on the power inflator dumps gas out of the corrugated hose when the diver holds it overhead — the standard method for a head-up vertical posture. Dedicated dump valves on the upper rear shoulder and the lower rear or right hip allow the diver to vent quickly while horizontal or feet-up, with no need to reach for the inflator. Over-pressure relief valves open automatically if the bladder is over-filled or if expanding gas during ascent exceeds a safe internal pressure, preventing rupture. All three are deflation mechanisms.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.6 Review

questions.question: Buoyancy compensator attachment and adjustment features include:

options.a: Straps, buckles, clips, and dump valves

questions.explanation: The fastening and adjustment hardware on a BCD consists of webbing straps (shoulder, waist, sternum, and crotch) that adjust the fit, buckles and quick-releases that allow the diver to don and doff the unit, plastic or metal clips for securing accessories, and stainless-steel D-rings for clipping off lights, SMBs, lift bags, or stage bottles. Dump valves and low-pressure hoses are functional pneumatic components rather than attachment points, and instrument consoles are themselves accessories that get clipped to the BCD. The combination of straps, buckles, clips, and D-rings is therefore the correct grouping.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.6 Review

questions.question: What are buoyancy compensators usually made of?
questions.explanation: Modern BCD bladders and outer shells are fabricated from high-denier nylon (typically 420D, 1000D, or 1680D Cordura) bonded to or laminated with a polyurethane (TPU) coating. The nylon provides abrasion resistance, structural integrity, and resistance to UV; the polyurethane gives the assembly its airtight, watertight membrane and is welded at the seams. Some technical wings use a separate inner urethane bladder inside an outer nylon shell, but the materials remain the same. Cotton is unsuitable for prolonged immersion, and thermoplastic shells alone would lack the flexibility and tear resistance that nylon-PU laminates provide.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.6 Review

questions.question: A jacket-style buoyancy compensator is:

options.a: Difficult to put on and remove, and disperses cylinder load unevenly
options.b: Easy to put on and remove, and disperses cylinder load evenly

questions.explanation: A jacket-style BCD wraps air cells around the diver's torso, with bladder volume distributed across the sides, back, and lower abdomen. Because the harness, backplate, and cylinder band are integrated into a single padded vest, the assembly slips on like a jacket and the cylinder weight is spread evenly across the shoulders, back, and waist via the cummerbund — making it a comfortable, beginner-friendly platform. It is not restricted to a particular color, and most modern jackets accept integrated weight pockets in addition to or instead of a weight belt, so options c and d are simply false.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.6 Review

questions.question: Wing-style buoyancy compensators are:

options.d: Customizable and popular with extended range divers

questions.explanation: A wing BCD consists of a separate inflatable bladder mounted on a rigid backplate with a one-piece harness — a true modular system in which the wing, plate, harness, and accessories can be mixed and matched to suit single or twin cylinders, fresh or salt water, and recreational or technical configurations. Because all the lift sits behind the diver, wings inherently provide back-inflation only, which produces a clean, horizontal trim and an uncluttered chest area. This combination of customization, streamlined trim, and load-carrying capability has made wings the default choice for extended-range and technical divers. All three statements apply.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.6 Review

questions.question: Types of wetsuit divers can choose from include:
questions.explanation: Wetsuits are produced in a range of cuts so the diver can match insulation to the planned water temperature and personal thermal tolerance. A full wetsuit (steamer) covers the legs, arms, and torso and is the standard choice for moderate to cool water. A short wetsuit, or shorty, has short sleeves and short legs and is intended for warm tropical water where minimal insulation is needed. A sleeveless wetsuit, sometimes called a Farmer John bottom or vest, can be worn alone in warm water or layered under a jacket for two-piece systems. Because all three styles are legitimate options, all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.7 Review

questions.question: A diver wearing a shorty with large steel cylinders may be:
questions.explanation: A shorty is the thinnest standard wetsuit, providing relatively little neoprene volume and therefore relatively little inherent buoyancy. Large steel cylinders, by contrast, are negatively buoyant both full and empty — far more so than aluminum 80s. A diver who combines minimal suit buoyancy with heavy negative steel cylinders has very little positive buoyancy to offset, so adding the same amount of lead they would carry with a full 7 mm suit and an aluminum tank will leave them significantly overweighted. The correct fix is to remove lead, redo a proper weight check at the surface, and re-balance for the actual exposure-suit and cylinder combination being used.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.7 Review

questions.question: Full wetsuits are advised in water temperatures between:
questions.explanation: SSI maps exposure-suit choice to water temperature on a sliding scale. Above roughly 27 °C a shorty or even a dive skin is generally adequate. Between about 10 °C and 27 °C the diver needs a full wetsuit, with thickness scaled to temperature: 3 mm for the warm end of the range, 5 mm in the middle, and 7 mm or a semi-dry as the water drops toward 10 °C. Below 10 °C the heat loss is too great for any wetsuit to handle on a multi-dive day, so a drysuit becomes the recommended choice. Range 10-27 °C therefore matches the full-wetsuit window.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.7 Review

questions.question: What is a common type of wetsuit thickness?
questions.explanation: Wetsuits are manufactured in a range of thicknesses to match different water temperatures and exposure conditions. The 3 mm, 5 mm, and 7 mm thicknesses are all common standards in recreational diving — 3 mm for warm tropical water, 5 mm for temperate conditions, and 7 mm for colder water. Selecting the correct thickness is essential because too thin a suit risks hypothermia, while too thick a suit can cause hyperthermia and limit mobility. Because each listed option represents a standard recreational thickness, the correct answer is that all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.7 Review

questions.question: Dry suit insulating gas:
questions.explanation: A drysuit relies on a layer of trapped gas — typically air — between the diver and the suit to provide insulation, working much like a thermos. As the diver descends, gas must be added to the suit to prevent suit squeeze caused by increasing ambient pressure compressing the suit against the body. On ascent, that same gas expands and must be vented through the exhaust valve to maintain proper buoyancy and prevent an uncontrolled rise. Because the insulating gas serves all three functions — adding warmth, preventing squeeze, and requiring controlled release on ascent — the correct answer is that all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.7 Review

questions.question: Dive boots count as exposure protection because they protect the feet from:
questions.explanation: Dive boots are part of the diver's overall exposure protection system. Like the wetsuit or drysuit, an exposure system shields the body from cold, irritants, and abrasions. Boots provide thermal insulation for the feet in cool water, cushion the foot inside hard fin pockets to prevent chafing, and protect against cuts, scrapes, and abrasions when walking on rocky shores, boat decks, or coral substrates. They also offer support for fin straps and reduce hot spots. Because boots address all three protective categories — cold, chafing, and abrasions — the correct answer is that all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.7 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about gloves is true?

options.a: There is only one type of glove
options.d: They do not provide thermal protection 168 ©

questions.explanation: Dive gloves are an accessory component of the exposure protection system, designed primarily to protect the diver's hands from cold water, abrasions, cuts, and irritants such as stinging organisms. They come in many styles and thicknesses — from thin tropical gloves for abrasion protection to thick neoprene or dry gloves for cold-water thermal insulation — so it is incorrect to say there is only one type or that they offer no thermal protection. They are not mandatory in every environment; some destinations restrict them to discourage touching marine life. The accurate statement is that gloves protect the diver's hands.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.7 Review

questions.question: Digital submersible pressure gauges use:

options.a: An air-filled tube that responds to pressure changes
options.b: A flexible diaphragm that responds to pressure changes
options.c: Electronic sensors that measure cylinder pressure
options.d: A Bourdon tube that responds to gas consumption

questions.explanation: A digital submersible pressure gauge (SPG) measures the remaining gas in the cylinder using electronic pressure transducers rather than the mechanical Bourdon tube found in analog SPGs. The sensor converts cylinder pressure into an electrical signal that is processed and shown on a digital display, often integrated into a dive computer either through a high-pressure hose or via a wireless transmitter. Bourdon tubes and flexible diaphragms are mechanical sensing elements typical of analog gauges, not digital ones. The correct answer is that digital SPGs use electronic sensors that measure cylinder pressure.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: Analog depth gauges may measure pressure changes with:
questions.explanation: Analog depth gauges sense ambient water pressure and convert it into a depth reading using a mechanical sensing element. The most common is a Bourdon tube — a curved metal tube that flexes as ambient pressure changes, moving a needle across the dial — though some designs use flexible (not rigid) diaphragms or capillary tubes. A rigid diaphragm cannot deflect, and a low-pressure hose is part of the regulator system, not a sensing mechanism for depth. The correct mechanical sensing element listed is the Bourdon tube.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: Diving with a compass benefits divers because it:

options.b: Can only be worn on the wrist
options.c: Helps them navigate confidently and avoid disorientation

questions.explanation: A compass is a fundamental underwater navigation tool. By indicating direction relative to magnetic north, it allows divers to follow planned headings, return reliably to an exit point, and orient themselves when visibility drops or landmarks disappear. This builds confidence and helps prevent disorientation, lost-diver situations, and unnecessary gas consumption from wandering. Compasses are not always digital — most are still reliable analog instruments — and they can be wrist-mounted, console-mounted, or integrated into a dive computer, so they are not limited to wrist wear. The correct benefit is that they help divers navigate confidently and avoid disorientation.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: Analog compasses are dependable because they:

options.a: Operate without batteries or electronic components
options.d: Integrate with the Global Positioning System for location tracking

questions.explanation: An analog compass uses a magnetized needle floating in a fluid-filled housing that aligns with Earth's magnetic field. Because it is purely mechanical, it requires no batteries, software, or electronics to function, making it extremely reliable in cold water, at depth, and during long dives where electronic devices could fail. It does not provide graphical readouts, depth updates, or GPS integration — those are functions of dive computers or surface-based electronics. The correct answer is that analog compasses operate without batteries or electronic components.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: Which basic features do all dive computers perform?
questions.explanation: Tracking depth and elapsed bottom time are the foundational functions every dive computer performs, because these two values feed the decompression algorithm that calculates no-decompression limits, ascent rates, and surface intervals. More advanced features — such as multi-gas switching, gas integration, mask-mounted displays, or high-FO₂ nitrox handling — are optional extras found only on certain models, not on every computer. Selecting 60% oxygen is also outside standard recreational use. The basic, universal function of all dive computers is tracking depth and time.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: What is the key feature of a gas-integrated dive computer?

options.d: Monitoring and displaying remaining gas supply

questions.explanation: A gas-integrated dive computer connects to the cylinder either through a high-pressure hose or, more commonly, via a wireless transmitter mounted on the first stage. This integration allows the computer to continuously read tank pressure and display the remaining gas supply, plus calculated values such as breathing rate and remaining bottom time based on actual consumption. Temperature display, dive logging, and depth monitoring are general features available on most dive computers, integrated or not. The defining, key feature of a gas-integrated unit is monitoring and displaying remaining gas supply.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: Dive computer designs include:
questions.explanation: Modern dive computers come in several form factors to suit diver preference and diving style. Wrist-mounted units are the most popular and easy to read at a glance. Watch- and smartwatch-style computers double as everyday timepieces while still providing full dive functionality. Mask-mounted computers project the display inside the diver's field of view through the mask, keeping the hands free for tasks. Console-mounted models combine the computer with the SPG. Because watches/smartwatches, wrist-mounted, and mask-mounted are all valid designs, the correct answer is that all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: What is one benefit of console-mounted dive computers?

options.a: They may be bulkier and more durable
options.b: They are popular among recreational divers who only want a single gauge
options.c: They are difficult to misplace or lose
options.d: They are lightweight and come with a strap attachment that is adjustable to fit most wrist sizes

questions.explanation: A console-mounted dive computer is built into the same housing as the submersible pressure gauge, attached to the regulator's high-pressure hose. Because it remains physically connected to the regulator and cylinder at all times, it is very difficult to misplace or lose between dives, unlike a small wrist unit that can be left behind. The bulkier-and-more-durable description and the lightweight wrist-strap description fit other designs, and console computers are not limited to single-gauge users. The correct benefit listed is that they are difficult to misplace or lose.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: What is the main advantage of a mask-mounted dive computer?

options.a: It displays real-time video footage of the dive
options.b: It allows for automatic adjustment of the diver's breathing rate
options.c: It provides a dive log for later review
options.d: It keeps the hands free for other tasks

questions.explanation: A mask-mounted dive computer projects key information — depth, time, no-deco status, and gas data — into a small heads-up display visible inside the mask. Because the data is always in the diver's field of view, there is no need to lift a wrist or pick up a console to check it, leaving both hands free for handling lights, cameras, reels, or task-loaded activities such as photography and technical work. Video footage, automatic breathing-rate adjustment, and dive logging are not unique mask-mounted features. The main advantage is that it keeps the hands free for other tasks.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: What is the main function of gauge mode on a dive computer?

options.c: Reef Hook a. Overview A reef hook is a specialized diving tool that secures divers to underwater structures in strong currents. b. Proper Use of a Reef Hook Divers must attach the hook to a suitable point, avoiding live corals to prevent damage. Improper use of a reef hook can cause long-term, irreparable damage to reefs and habitats. Using a reef hook does not replace the need for diving skills. Using a reef hook creates a unique experience and reduces some current-related risks. CI. Diver Propulsion Vehicle a. Overview A diver propulsion vehicle (DPV) propels the diver through water using a battery-powered motor. b. Drift Diving DPVs can help divers hold their position in currents or maintain their depth in vertical water flows. When diving with a DPV, each diver in a buddy team should have one so they can keep together. CII. Audible Signaling Devices a. Overview Many circumstances could cause a diver to become separated from their dive boat or lost at sea. 177 ©
options.d: It measures only depth and time 174 ©

questions.explanation: Gauge mode (sometimes called bottom-timer mode) turns the dive computer into a basic instrument that records and displays only depth and elapsed time, without running its decompression algorithm or tracking tissue loading. This mode is used by technical divers who plan their decompression separately using tables or external software, or as a backup when the computer is in a planned-decompression scenario. It does not measure gas consumption or analyze decompression status. The correct main function is that it measures only depth and time.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.8 Review

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a dive flag?

options.a: To identify there is sea life present in the area
options.b: To signal the completion of a dive to the crew
options.c: To notify boaters of divers in the water
options.d: To indicate the depth of the dive site

questions.explanation: A dive flag — typically the red-and-white diagonal-stripe sport-diver flag or the international blue-and-white alpha flag — is displayed on a boat or surface float to alert other vessels that divers are below in the immediate area. By law or convention in most jurisdictions, boaters must slow down and keep a safe distance from a displayed flag, helping prevent serious injury from propellers and collisions. It does not indicate sea life, dive completion, or site depth. The primary purpose is to notify boaters of divers in the water.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: What is one advantage of using a surface marker buoy?

options.a: It can detect and repel large marine predators
options.b: It can support a raised dive flag for added visibility
options.c: It automatically guides divers back to their starting point
options.d: It can communicate with boats via radio signals

questions.explanation: A surface marker buoy (SMB) is a floating marker towed by the diver throughout the dive to mark their position to surface traffic and the dive boat. Many SMBs are equipped with a small mast or are designed to fly a dive flag mounted above the float, raising it above the water surface so it is highly visible to passing boats from a greater distance. SMBs do not detect predators, navigate automatically, or communicate by radio. The correct advantage is that an SMB can support a raised dive flag for added visibility.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: What is one advantage of using a delayed surface marker buoy?

options.a: It allows divers to communicate verbally with the surface team
options.b: It automatically adjusts buoyancy for divers during descent
options.c: It minimizes entanglement risks during the dive as it is deployed towards the end of the dive
options.d: It provides propulsion for divers during ascent

questions.explanation: A delayed surface marker buoy (DSMB) is carried rolled up in a pocket or pouch and only inflated and deployed near the end of the dive, typically before the safety stop. Because it is not towed throughout the dive on a long line like a standard SMB, there is no trailing reel line in the water for most of the dive, dramatically reducing the risk of entanglement on wrecks, kelp, or other structures. It does not enable verbal communication, automatic buoyancy, or propulsion. The correct advantage is the reduced entanglement risk from late deployment.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: If there is slack in the line of an open-ended delayed surface marker buoy:

options.a: The buoy will overinflate and cause a rapid ascent
options.b: The buoy may become detached and fail to reach the surface
options.d: The buoy may deflate at the surface, reducing its effectiveness

questions.explanation: An open-ended DSMB has an unsealed bottom opening so that excess gas can vent as the buoy ascends and the gas inside expands. This design relies on tension in the line to hold the buoy upright at the surface. If there is slack in the line, the buoy can tip sideways or invert, allowing trapped gas to escape through the open bottom. As gas is lost, the buoy partially deflates at the surface and becomes less visible to surface support. It will not overinflate or detach because of slack. The correct answer is that the buoy may deflate at the surface, reducing its effectiveness.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: What is type of delayed surface marker buoy?
questions.explanation: Delayed surface marker buoys are produced in several configurations to suit different diving styles. Open-ended DSMBs have an unsealed bottom that allows expanding gas to vent during ascent. Sealed DSMBs are closed and use an over-pressure relief valve to manage expanding gas. Self-sealing DSMBs use a one-way valve at the inflation point, allowing gas to enter but not escape, keeping the buoy fully inflated and stable on the surface. Because all three — self-sealing, sealed, and open-ended — are recognized DSMB types, the correct answer is that all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: What is a unique characteristic of an open-ended delayed surface marker buoy?

options.a: It is equipped with an electronic pressure monitoring system
options.b: It has a complex design with multiple compartments for stability
options.c: The bottom is unsealed so excess gas can escape
options.d: It includes a fully sealed structure preventing any gas leakage

questions.explanation: The defining feature of an open-ended DSMB is exactly what its name implies — the bottom of the buoy is left unsealed. As the buoy is inflated underwater and ascends, the gas inside expands due to decreasing ambient pressure, and the open bottom allows that excess gas to escape harmlessly. This simple design avoids the need for an over-pressure relief valve found on sealed DSMBs and keeps the buoy lightweight and inexpensive. It does not include electronic monitoring, multiple compartments, or a fully sealed structure. The correct unique characteristic is that the bottom is unsealed so excess gas can escape.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: Which of the following are types of cutting device?
questions.explanation: Cutting devices are essential safety accessories used to free a diver from entanglement in monofilament fishing line, kelp, nets, or other underwater hazards. Recognized types include dive knives, which are versatile and can pry as well as cut; line cutters, small hook-shaped tools with a recessed blade designed to slice through line without exposed edges; and dive shears, similar to heavy-duty scissors that easily cut through thick line, webbing, and even small wires. Because dive knives, line cutters, and dive shears are all valid cutting devices, the correct answer is that all answers are correct.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: The type of cutting device selected depends on the:

options.a: Types of hazards that may be encountered
options.b: Type of current you are going to dive
options.c: Size of the sharks in the water
options.d: Size of the wreck which you want to enter

questions.explanation: The choice of cutting device should match the potential entanglement hazards expected at the dive site. Thin monofilament fishing line is best handled by a small line cutter, while heavy commercial netting, kelp, or webbing may require dive shears with strong cutting power. A general-purpose dive knife may suit varied environments such as wreck or low-viz dives. Currents, shark size, and wreck size do not determine the cutting tool selection — the actual entanglement risks do. The correct answer is that the type of cutting device depends on the types of hazards that may be encountered.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: Why might a diver prefer a line cutter over other cutting devices?

options.a: It can be used for prying, digging, or cutting through vegetation
options.b: Its compact size for easy carrying and accessibility
options.c: It has blunt tips to prevent accidental punctures
options.d: It has long, flexible blades for increased reach

questions.explanation: Line cutters are favored for their compact size, which allows divers to attach them to a BCD, harness, or wrist where they can be quickly accessed during an entanglement emergency. Unlike full-size dive knives or shears, line cutters are small, lightweight, and unobtrusive, yet feature a recessed razor blade specifically designed to slice through monofilament, fishing line, kelp, and other entangling materials without risking injury to the diver or damage to surrounding equipment. Every diver should carry at least one cutting device, and the line cutter's accessibility makes it ideal as a primary or backup tool.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: A reef hook is used to:

options.c: Secure divers to an underwater structure

questions.explanation: A reef hook is a curved metal hook attached to a short line and clipped to the diver's BCD, used to secure the diver to dead substrate such as bare rock or rubble in strong current environments. This allows the diver to hold position effortlessly while observing pelagic life like sharks or schooling fish, conserving gas and energy that would otherwise be expended fighting the current. Per SSI doctrine and reef-conservation ethics, divers must hook only into dead substrate and never into living coral, sponges, or other delicate organisms, as doing so causes lasting environmental damage.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: While drift diving, a diver can use a diver propulsion vehicle to:

options.b: Hold their position in the current

questions.explanation: A diver propulsion vehicle (DPV) provides controlled thrust that allows a drift diver to hold position against a current, reverse direction to investigate features, or simply reduce workload by counteracting drift. DPVs should never be used for rapid ascents, which violate safe ascent-rate limits and can cause decompression issues or barotrauma. By holding position, divers can stop to observe marine life or wait for a buddy without being swept away. Other listed options describe unsafe or impractical uses, while station-keeping in current is a recognized, beneficial application of a DPV during drift dives.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: A whistle should be regularly inspected to verify:

options.a: It remains securely attached to the buoyancy compensator
options.c: Its color has not worn off and it is highly visible
options.d: It can be easily accessed by a buddy

questions.explanation: A whistle is a surface signaling device used to attract attention from boats or shore in case of emergency, and it must remain securely fastened to the BCD inflator hose or shoulder strap so it is available when needed. Regular inspection of the attachment point ensures the whistle is not lost during transit, entry, or the dive itself. Whistles do not function underwater, so subsurface use is not relevant. Color and buddy access are secondary considerations; the critical maintenance check per SSI doctrine is verifying the whistle stays attached and accessible to the diver who will need it at the surface.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: What must divers consider when choosing a signal horn?

options.a: The horn's size compared to other signaling devices
options.b: If the horn has adjustable sound levels for different distances
options.c: If the horn is brightly colored for easy visibility underwater
options.d: The horn's compatibility with their low-pressure inflator hose connections

questions.explanation: A signal horn is powered by air drawn from the BCD's low-pressure inflator hose, producing a loud audible signal far more powerful than a whistle. When selecting a horn, the diver must verify it is compatible with the specific quick-disconnect fitting on their LP inflator hose, because non-matching connectors will not seal properly and the horn will fail to function. Size, color, and adjustable volume are not standard horn features. Ensuring proper LP hose compatibility guarantees the horn can be deployed reliably to alert nearby boats or shore personnel during a surface emergency.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: Which of the following are classed as visual signaling devices?

options.a: Strobes, lights, signaling mirrors, and chemical light sticks
options.b: Strobes, lights, signaling mirrors, and personal locator beacons
options.c: Strobes, lights, signaling mirrors, and signal horns
options.d: Strobes, lights, whistles, and chemical light sticks

questions.explanation: Visual signaling devices are those that attract attention through light or reflection, and include strobes, dive lights, signaling mirrors that reflect sunlight over long distances, and chemical light sticks for low-light or night conditions. Whistles and signal horns are audible (acoustic) devices, while personal locator beacons are electronic devices that transmit distress signals via satellite. SSI recommends carrying multiple types of signaling equipment so the diver is prepared for different situations: day or night, surface or underwater, close-range or long-distance. Grouping visual tools together helps divers build a balanced signaling kit.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: What is one disadvantage of using chemical light sticks for signaling?

options.a: They are not bright and have minimal impact in daylight
options.b: They require more frequent maintenance than strobes and lights
options.c: They can only be activated in complete darkness
options.d: They fail to remain waterproof under pressure

questions.explanation: Chemical light sticks (cyalume glow sticks) produce a dim chemical luminescence that is highly visible at night but easily washed out by daylight, making them ineffective as a daytime signaling device. They are sealed, single-use, and require no maintenance, and they activate fine in any light condition once snapped. Their primary value is as a night marker for buddy identification, surface position, or boat recovery in darkness. Divers should pair light sticks with daylight-effective tools such as SMBs, signal mirrors, or audible devices to ensure they are signaling-ready around the clock.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: An Automatic Identification System beacon transmits signals via the:

options.c: Search and Rescue Satellite Aided Tracking network

questions.explanation: An Automatic Identification System (AIS) beacon transmits a distress signal that is relayed through the Search and Rescue Satellite Aided Tracking (SARSAT/COSPAS) network, which is monitored internationally by maritime rescue authorities. AIS beacons broadcast the diver's GPS position directly to nearby vessels equipped with AIS receivers, dramatically reducing search time. Although GPS provides positioning data, it is not the transmission network. EPIRBs and PLBs are separate beacon types that also use SARSAT but operate on different protocols. AIS is particularly valued by divers because it alerts ships in the immediate vicinity rather than relying solely on shore-based rescue coordination.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: Slates are commonly used by divers because they:

options.a: Attach to the forearm or buoyancy compensator
options.b: Allow more complex communication than hand signals
options.d: Can be used for quick notes or detailed information

questions.explanation: Underwater slates are a versatile communication tool valued for several reasons: they attach conveniently to the forearm or BCD with retractors or clips for quick deployment; they enable complex written messages that exceed the capabilities of standardized hand signals; and they can be used to record dive data, sketch sites, take notes, or convey detailed instructions to a buddy. Because all three statements are accurate descriptions of slate use, the correct answer is the inclusive option. Slates are especially useful for instructors, technical divers, surveyors, and any team needing precise underwater information exchange.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: Full face masks provide divers with:

options.d: A wider field of vision and verbal communication 182 ©

questions.explanation: A full face mask covers the entire face, integrating the regulator second stage with the mask itself. This design provides a noticeably wider field of vision than a standard mask and, when paired with a through-water communication unit, allows verbal communication between divers or with the surface. Full face masks also offer protection from cold water and contaminated environments, which is why they are standard equipment for public-safety, commercial, and scientific diving. While some advanced models support comm systems, the foundational benefits in the SSI manual are improved visibility and the ability to speak underwater clearly.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 4.9 Review

questions.question: Marine geology is the study of:

options.a: The ocean's geological features and processes
options.b: Atmospheric weather patterns over the ocean

questions.explanation: Marine geology is the branch of earth science focused on the geological features and processes of the ocean floor and underlying crust. It includes the study of mid-ocean ridges, abyssal plains, seamounts, ocean trenches, continental shelves, hydrothermal vents, and the plate-tectonic activity that shapes them. Atmospheric study falls under meteorology, currents and tides under physical oceanography, and the study of marine organisms under marine biology. Understanding marine geology helps divers interpret underwater terrain, recognize how dive sites were formed, and appreciate the geological forces that continue to reshape coastlines and reefs.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.1 Review

questions.question: Ocean basins expand and shrink due to:
questions.explanation: Ocean basins change size over geologic time through plate-tectonic processes. At divergent boundaries, such as mid-ocean ridges, plates pull apart and new oceanic crust forms, causing basins to expand. At convergent boundaries, one plate is subducted beneath another, consuming crust and causing basins to shrink, often forming deep ocean trenches. Together, these two opposing forces drive the cycle of seafloor spreading and recycling. Transform boundaries, where plates slide past each other, do not create or consume crust, and volcanic activity is a result rather than a category of plate motion. Therefore convergent and divergent activity is the correct pairing.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.1 Review

questions.question: Landforms that feature in ocean terrain include:

options.a: Abyssal plains including mid-ocean ridges, seamounts, volcanic islands, and hydrothermal vents

questions.explanation: Ocean terrain encompasses a wide variety of landforms shaped by tectonic and volcanic activity. Abyssal plains host mid-ocean ridges, seamounts, volcanic islands, and hydrothermal vents; continental margins include the gently sloping continental shelves and steeper continental slopes; and convergent boundaries form deep ocean trenches such as the Mariana Trench. Because each option lists a legitimate category of ocean landform, the inclusive answer is correct. Familiarity with this terrain helps divers understand why certain sites attract specific marine life and how depth, current, and substrate vary across geologic features.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following are found in the littoral zone?

options.b: The hadal zone and deep-sea trenches
options.c: Estuaries, intertidal habitats, and the surf zone

questions.explanation: The littoral zone is the coastal region between the high-tide and low-tide marks, encompassing the dynamic interface between land and sea. It includes estuaries where rivers meet the ocean, intertidal habitats exposed and submerged with the tides, and the surf zone where waves shoal and break. Open-ocean currents and gyres belong to the pelagic environment, the hadal zone refers to extreme deep-sea trenches, and hydrothermal vents are deep-sea features. Understanding the littoral zone is important for divers because these productive but turbulent environments host diverse life and present unique entry, exit, and surge considerations.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.1 Review

questions.question: What is one role coral reefs play in the ecosystem?

options.a: They prevent all types of pollutions in oceans
options.b: They generate freshwater resources for coastal areas
options.c: They increase landmass by adding new territories
options.d: They provide shelter and breeding grounds for marine life

questions.explanation: Coral reefs are among the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth, providing essential shelter, feeding areas, and breeding grounds for an enormous variety of marine species including fish, invertebrates, and turtles. Their three-dimensional structure offers refuge from predators and nursery habitat for juvenile fish, supporting both reef and pelagic populations. Reefs do not prevent all pollution, generate freshwater, or meaningfully increase landmass beyond slow biogenic accumulation. Because coral reefs underpin coastal fisheries and global marine biodiversity, divers have a particular responsibility to practice good buoyancy, avoid contact, and follow conservation ethics whenever diving on or near them.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.1 Review

questions.question: Organisms that bury themselves within the soft substrates of aquatic environments are called:
questions.explanation: Infauna are benthic organisms that live buried within soft substrates such as sand, mud, or silt on the seafloor. Examples include many species of clams, worms, crustaceans, and sea pens that burrow for protection, feeding, or reproduction. Epifauna, by contrast, live on top of the substrate, plankton drift in the water column, and sea urchins are typically epifaunal grazers. Recognizing infauna helps divers understand soft-bottom ecosystems and approach them carefully, because finning too close to sand or silt can disturb burrows, damage delicate organisms, and reduce visibility through stirred sediment.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.1 Review

questions.question: What is the pelagic zone?

options.a: It is the area where all deep sea organisms live
options.b: It is the area between the beach and sea where waves break
options.c: It is the shallow coastal waters near rivers
options.d: It is the open ocean beyond the continental shelf

questions.explanation: The pelagic zone is the open ocean water column extending from the surface down through all depths, located beyond the continental shelf. It is the habitat of free-swimming and drifting organisms such as sharks, dolphins, whales, tuna, billfish, and many species of plankton and jellyfish, which are collectively known as pelagic species. The surf zone refers to where waves break, the littoral zone is shallow coastal water near shore, and deep-sea organisms occupy specific subzones within the broader pelagic and benthic realms. Pelagic dives often rely on drift techniques and offer encounters with large open-ocean wildlife.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.1 Review

questions.question: Seawater is mainly composed of:
questions.explanation: Seawater is mainly composed of water, with H2O accounting for roughly 96.5 percent of its mass. Although seawater contains dissolved salts, gases such as nitrogen and oxygen, and trace minerals, these constituents represent only a small fraction of the total. Salt averages about 3.5 percent by weight, and dissolved gases are present in much smaller amounts. Recognizing that seawater is fundamentally water with a salinity component helps divers understand related concepts such as buoyancy differences between fresh and salt water, osmosis, and the formation of haloclines where waters of different salinities meet.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: What is the main difference between marine environments and fresh water environments?
questions.explanation: The defining difference between marine and freshwater environments is salinity. Marine waters average about 3.5 percent dissolved salts, while freshwater bodies such as lakes and rivers contain less than 0.05 percent. This contrast affects density, buoyancy, the species that can survive there, and even how divers must weight themselves. Temperature, body size, and presence of aquatic life vary widely within both categories and therefore do not characterize the fundamental distinction. Understanding salinity helps divers anticipate buoyancy adjustments and recognize transitional environments such as estuaries and haloclines where fresh and salt water layers meet.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: The average amount of salt in a sample of seawater is:
questions.explanation: The average salinity of seawater is approximately 3.5 percent by weight, often expressed as 35 parts per thousand (35 ppt or 35 g/kg). This means that one kilogram of seawater contains about 35 grams of dissolved salts, with sodium chloride dominating but also including magnesium, sulfate, calcium, and potassium compounds. Salinity varies regionally, being lower in polar and estuarine waters and higher in enclosed seas like the Red Sea or Mediterranean. Divers should remember the 3.5 percent figure because it directly explains why saltwater is denser than freshwater and requires additional weight for proper buoyancy.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: Saline water is denser than fresh water because:

options.a: Dissolved salt particles decrease its mass and increase its volume
options.b: Dissolved salt particles increase its mass without increasing water volume
options.d: Dissolved salt particles increase its mass and increase its volume

questions.explanation: Saline water is denser than freshwater because dissolved salt ions add mass to the water without significantly increasing its volume. The salt ions occupy spaces between water molecules rather than displacing them proportionally, so a given volume of seawater contains more total mass than the same volume of freshwater. This is why divers experience more buoyant lift in the ocean than in a lake and must add weight when transitioning from freshwater to saltwater. The simple statement that salt is heavier than water misses the underlying mechanism, which is that mass increases while volume remains essentially the same.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: A halocline is a water layer where:

options.a: A water layer where temperature changes rapidly
options.b: A water layer where salinity increases rapidly with depth
options.d: A water layer where salinity changes rapidly with depth

questions.explanation: A halocline is a distinct water layer in which salinity changes rapidly with depth, marking the boundary between waters of differing salt concentrations. The change can be an increase or a decrease, so the more accurate definition simply describes a rapid change rather than only an increase. Haloclines are common in cenotes, fjords, estuaries, and where freshwater rivers meet the sea. Visually they often produce a shimmering, blurry effect because the two layers refract light differently. Rapid temperature change defines a thermocline, a separate phenomenon. Divers crossing a halocline may notice buoyancy shifts due to the density change between layers.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: Gas concentration in seawater is affected by:
questions.explanation: All three factors affect gas concentration in seawater. Water pressure influences solubility — higher pressure pushes more gas into solution, which is why the deep ocean holds dissolved gases differently than surface water. Temperature has an inverse relationship with gas solubility: cold water holds more dissolved gas (oxygen, CO₂) than warm water, which is why polar seas are highly oxygenated. Salinity also matters — saltier water holds less dissolved gas because dissolved ions reduce gas-holding capacity. Together these three variables determine how much oxygen, CO₂, and nitrogen seawater contains, shaping marine productivity and climate-regulating ocean chemistry.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: Diving in water with higher salinity levels may require divers to:
questions.explanation: Higher salinity makes water denser, which increases buoyancy on a submerged diver. To compensate for the extra lift and achieve neutral buoyancy at depth, divers must add weight when transitioning from fresher water to saltier water. The exact amount varies by exposure suit, body composition, and equipment, but a common rule is roughly 2–3% of total body+gear weight when moving from fresh to typical seawater. The reverse applies in low-salinity environments such as the Baltic or freshwater lakes, where divers remove weight. Failing to adjust weighting causes excessive positive buoyancy, difficulty descending, and poor trim throughout the dive.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: The Earth's spherical shape and tilt mean that:

options.b: Ocean surface temperatures are warmer in the tropics
options.c: More solar radiation is absorbed at the equator than the poles
options.d: Ocean surface temperatures are cooler in polar regions

questions.explanation: All three statements are correct consequences of Earth's geometry. Because the planet is a tilted sphere, sunlight strikes the equator nearly perpendicular and concentrates energy on a small surface area, while the same beam at the poles spreads across a much larger area at a low angle, depositing far less energy per square meter. This uneven solar absorption directly produces warm tropical ocean surfaces and cold polar surfaces. The resulting temperature gradient drives global atmospheric circulation, ocean currents, weather patterns, and climate zones. It is the foundation reason why tropical reefs and polar ecosystems differ so dramatically in conditions and biodiversity.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: What process drives atmospheric circulation?
questions.explanation: Convection is the engine of atmospheric circulation. Solar heating warms equatorial air, which becomes less dense and rises; cooler, denser air sinks at higher latitudes, creating large convective cells (Hadley, Ferrel, Polar) that move heat poleward. As air rises and falls, it generates the trade winds, westerlies, and polar easterlies, modified by Earth's rotation through the Coriolis effect. This same convective mechanism also drives ocean surface currents and transfers heat between equator and poles, regulating global climate. Conduction transfers heat through direct contact and is far too slow over atmospheric scales, while precipitation is a result, not a cause.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: What effects can strong winds and heavy rainfall have on diving conditions?

options.a: Choppy water conditions, rough seas, and strong currents

questions.explanation: Strong winds and heavy rain affect dive conditions in all three ways. Wind builds waves, generates swell and surface chop, and drives stronger currents — making entries, exits, and surface swims more demanding. Heavy rainfall and runoff reduce salinity at the surface and near river mouths, creating haloclines that distort vision and altering buoyancy. Both rain and wave action stir up sediment, wash terrestrial particulates into the sea, and trigger plankton blooms — all of which sharply reduce underwater visibility. Together these factors compound risk, which is why divers consult weather forecasts and avoid diving during or shortly after storms.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.2 Review

questions.question: What is "wavelength" when referring to ocean waves?

options.a: The distance between two consecutive crests or troughs
options.b: The time it takes for one complete wave cycle to pass a fixed point
options.c: The vertical height of a wave from its middle point to its peak
options.d: The vertical height of a wave from its middle point to its trough

questions.explanation: Wavelength is the horizontal distance between two consecutive crests (or two consecutive troughs) of a wave — the spatial length of one full wave cycle. It is distinct from wave period (the time for one cycle to pass a fixed point) and from amplitude (the vertical distance from the still-water midpoint to the crest or trough). Wavelength is a key descriptor for predicting how waves behave: long-wavelength swells travel far with little energy loss, while short-wavelength wind chop dissipates quickly. As waves approach shore and water becomes shallower than half the wavelength, they slow, shorten, and steepen, eventually breaking in the surf zone.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.3 Review

questions.question: How does prolonged wind exposure affect wave characteristics?

options.a: It builds waves in size and intensity
options.b: It decreases wave height but increases wave frequency
options.c: It creates choppy waves and reduces surface smoothness

questions.explanation: The longer wind blows over a given stretch of water (the fetch), and the stronger it blows, the more energy is transferred to the sea surface. This sustained energy input builds waves progressively larger, steeper, and more powerful — increasing both wave height and intensity. Brief gusts produce only short, choppy local waves, but extended exposure organizes that energy into longer wavelengths and ultimately into long-traveling swells that can cross entire ocean basins. For divers this matters at the planning stage: a long-duration wind forecast usually means rough surface conditions, larger surf, and more demanding entries and exits, even if the wind itself eases by dive time.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.3 Review

questions.question: How does water depth affect wave behavior?

options.a: Waves cannot form in deep areas
options.b: Waves become tsunamis in deep areas
options.c: Waves flatten completely in shallow areas
options.d: Shallow areas amplify wave height and steepness

questions.explanation: As waves move from deep water into shallower water, the orbital motion of water particles begins interacting with the seabed. This friction slows the lower portion of the wave while the upper portion continues at speed, causing the wave to compress: wavelength shortens, height increases, and the wave steepens dramatically. Eventually the crest outruns its base and the wave breaks, forming the surf zone. This is why even modest offshore swells can produce powerful breakers along shallow coastlines and reefs. Divers entering or exiting from shore must time their movements to avoid these amplified, steep, breaking waves which are the most hazardous part of a shore dive.
questions.source: SSI: Science of Diving Manual — Lesson 5.3 Review

questions.question: When shore diving, divers usually try to:

options.a: Use boats to get beyond the surf zone
options.b: Rest in the surf zone and swim hard to the point beyond it
options.c: Get through the surf zone and into calmer water before resting
