(a) is nonsensical — they already hold the OWD cert, no new one issued (mssi-inst-287 — only a recognition card). (d) specialty skills live in specialty programs (mssi-inst-160–166), not SSU. (b) all-correct sweeps in the wrong claims.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.2 Review

questions.question: ____ training dive(s) must be ____ during specialty programs.

options.c: The first open water, indirectly supervised

questions.explanation: Specialty programs split dives into MANDATORY (core curriculum, must be direct-supervised by an active Specialty Instructor) and OPTIONAL (additional / fun dives that can run with looser supervision). (a) over-restricts — applying direct supervision to all OW dives, including optional ones, isn't the rule. (b) inverts: optional dives don't need direct supervision by definition. (c) wrong combination of attributes.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.3 Review

questions.question: The Ecology specialty programs:

options.b: Can be taught by an Assistant Instructor with the applicable certification
options.c: Cannot be combined with Open Water Diver training
options.d: Require at least two open water dives

questions.explanation: Ecology specialties (Marine Ecology, Shark Ecology, Sea Turtle Ecology, etc.) are knowledge-domain programs without depth/penetration risk — they fall in the lower-risk band that AIs can earn Specialty Instructor ratings for (consistent with mssi-inst-163, Navigation). (c) is wrong — Ecology specialties combine well with OWD training. (d) dive counts vary by specialty; 'at least two' isn't universal.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.3 Review

questions.question: Pool/Confined Water specialties:

options.a: Must only be taught in the pool/confined water
options.b: Must be taught by an Open Water Instructor or higher
options.d: Require at least two in-water training sessions

questions.explanation: Universal property of Pool/Confined Water specialties: minimum two in-water training sessions to allow proper skill acquisition (Perfect Buoyancy, Snorkeling, etc. need that practice volume). (a) 'must ONLY' is too rigid — some Pool/Confined specialties allow demonstrations to bridge into OW. (b) Pro-rating requirement varies by specialty (some are AI-eligible, mssi-inst-163). (c) all-correct sweeps in the over-restrictive answers.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.3 Review

questions.question: Enriched Air Nitrox programs:

options.a: Do not require any in-water training
options.b: Can be taught by an Assistant Instructor
options.c: Can be taught by a Divemaster
options.d: Requires at least two open water dives

questions.explanation: SSI Enriched Air Nitrox is a knowledge-only specialty: theory (gas physics, oxygen toxicity, MOD calculation) + practical gas analysis with an oxygen analyser — no in-water requirement. The diver demonstrates competence on the bench, not underwater. (c) DMs have no Specialty Instructor authority. (d) two OW dives contradicts the knowledge-only nature. (b) AI authority for EAN varies by region/standard, but the universal truth is the no-water property.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.3 Review

questions.question: If a diver can show proof of prior experience dives for a specialty program:

options.a: You can credit all prior experience dives

questions.explanation: SSI prior-experience credit cap = ONE dive per specialty when documented (logbook entry, prior cert). The cap exists so the specialty still requires meaningful new training. (a) crediting all would gut the program. (b) two exceeds the standard. (d) refusing all credit ignores SSI's flexibility. Memory hook: 'one and done' for prior credit.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.3 Review

questions.question: Open Water specialties:

options.a: Can be promoted to both new and experienced divers
options.b: Require at least two open water training dives for certification
options.d: Focus on real-world, open water skills

questions.explanation: Three properties of OW specialties, all true: (a) marketable to both new divers (Boat Diving, Navigation right after OWD) and veterans adding to their portfolio; (b) minimum two OW training dives for cert — that's the SSI standard for OW-domain specialties; (d) focused on real-world OW skills (Deep, Wreck, Drift, Night, Photography in the actual environment, not simulated).
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.3 Review

questions.question: Science of Diving is a ____ specialty program.
questions.explanation: Science of Diving is the academic deep-dive into physics, physiology, equipment, environment and decompression theory — pure theory, zero in-water requirement. That places it in the Diving Knowledge specialty category alongside EAN, Marine Ecology and similar knowledge-only programs. (a), (b) and (c) require water or equipment-handling training the SoD curriculum doesn't include.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.3 Review

questions.question: How many categories of Explorer programs are there?
questions.explanation: SSI Explorer family covers 6 categories targeted at youth/intro diving across different age groups and progression levels (Snorkeling, Scuba Rangers, Scuba Explorer pathways, etc.). The structure lets a TC offer age-appropriate intros from very young children up through late teens transitioning into entry-level adult programs.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.4 Review

questions.question: If a student has trouble performing a skill:

options.a: Be encouraging, and do not force them to complete the skill
options.b: Do not stop until they have mastered the skill
options.c: End the session and have them leave the water
options.d: Have them practice on their own

questions.explanation: Positive support is the SSI way, especially in Explorer / youth programs where forcing kills enjoyment and trust. If a skill isn't clicking now, move on and revisit it later — the brain needs incubation time. (b) drilling to mastery without breaks creates frustration and locks in tension. (c) ending the session is a punishment response that destroys the learning relationship. (d) unsupervised practice is unsafe and counter to SSI standards.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.4 Review

questions.question: The intent of the SSI Explorers program is to provide:

options.a: The knowledge and skills to safely participate in snorkeling, mermaid, freediving, or scuba experiences
options.b: A pool or confined water environment experience

questions.explanation: Three aligned facets of Explorer programs: (a) age-appropriate knowledge and skills across multiple aquatic disciplines (snorkeling, mermaid, freediving, scuba); (b) controlled pool / confined water environment, never open water at this level — keeps risk minimal; (c) the target demographic is children — youth-friendly pacing, language and game-based methodology. The trio defines the program's whole value proposition.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.4 Review

questions.question: The SSI Explorers program:

options.a: Is intended for children 6-10 years old
options.c: Is a pool/confined water program only
options.d: Does not certify students to dive without a dive professional

questions.explanation: Three consistent facts about Explorers: (a) target age 6-10 — the sweet-spot demographic for Scuba Rangers / Explorer level; (c) pool / confined water only — no open water at this age; (d) no autonomous-diver certification — children always dive under direct Pro supervision regardless of program completion. All three together define the safety envelope of the program.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.4 Review

questions.question: Using games and fun activities to teach:

options.c: Allows students to experience deep water

questions.explanation: Gamification: kids learn through play, retention rides on the pleasure association. Linking new knowledge to a game makes it stick. (b) unsupervised fun is absolutely forbidden in youth programs — supervision never lapses. (c) deep water and Explorer-age students never mix; programs stay shallow. (a) all-correct sweeps in the dangerous claims.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.4 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is an Explorer program?
questions.explanation: Blue Oceans Explorer is the conservation-themed Explorer program — SSI's Blue Oceans environmental philosophy delivered to children in age-appropriate format. (b) 'Lifeguard Explorer' isn't an SSI program name. (d) 'Sidemount Explorer' is nonsensical — Sidemount is a technical configuration for advanced divers, never youth. (a) all-correct sweeps in the fake names.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.4 Review

questions.question: How is the Specialty Explorer recognition level earned?

options.a: By completing at least ten in-water sessions
options.c: By completing all the Ecology specialty experiences

questions.explanation: Specialty Explorer mirrors the adult Specialty Diver (12 dives + 2 specialties / mssi-inst-131) but scaled into the Explorer family — completing two specialty experiences within Explorer programs earns the recognition. (a) ten sessions is an invented count. (b) any single Explorer doesn't reach Specialty Explorer tier. (c) requiring ALL Ecology specialties is over-prescriptive — the rule is two of any specialties.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.4 Review

questions.question: What are the Explorers recognition levels?
questions.explanation: SSI Explorer progression mirrors the adult Specialty / Advanced / Master Diver ladder: Specialty Explorer (2 specialty experiences — mssi-inst-306), Advanced Explorer (more specialties), Master Explorer (top tier). (a) sneaks in 'Professional' which isn't an Explorer level — Pros aren't kids. (d) 'Rookie / Intermediate / Advanced' is invented language. (b) is the standard mySSI distractor.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.4 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a real-world application for Section 4 | The Value of a Dive Buddy and Executing Your Dives?

options.a: Emphasizing the difference between rental and personal equipment
options.b: Calculating gas management and dive plans for various depths at your local dive site
options.c: Describing interesting or unique animals seen at your favorite dive site
options.d: Discussing the dive schedule for a trip your training center takes that involves plane flights

questions.explanation: Section 4 covers buddy system + executing dives — practical pre-dive planning: gas management (SAC/RMV consumption), depth-time profiles, NDL tracking, buddy coordination. Real-world application = applying those calculations to a real local site. (a) equipment topics live in earlier sections; (c) marine life identification is ecology / Section 5+; (d) trip logistics around flying is more relevant to flying-after-diving topics, not Section 4.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.5 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a real-world application for Section 5 | The Ocean and Your Diving Opportunities?

options.a: Providing real-world examples of no-decompression limit dive planning
options.b: Teaching students how proper buoyancy prevents accidental encounters with sea urchins
options.c: Describing the process for assembling their Total Diving System
options.d: Emphasizing the difference between rental and personal equipment

questions.explanation: Section 5 covers ocean environment + diving opportunities — marine life awareness, safe interaction with the underwater world, environmental responsibility. Linking buoyancy control to avoiding contact with marine life (sea urchins, fire coral, stinging hydroids) is exactly the ecology-meets-skill real-world tie-in. (a) NDL planning is Section 4 (mssi-inst-308). (c) and (d) are equipment-section topics.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.5 Review

questions.question: For the Open Water Diver program, Open Water Training Dive 1 can be conducted:

options.a: Prior to completing the academic and pool/confined water requirements
options.b: Only after completing the first three academic and pool/confined water sessions
options.c: Only after completing the first three pool/confined water sessions
options.d: Only after Academic Sessions 1-3 are completed

questions.explanation: Flexibility Rule (mssi-inst-122, 125) in action: OW1 can run before ALL academic + pool requirements are finished. Pro just needs to ensure the specific skills evaluated in OW1 have been mastered in pool first — they can interleave (pool morning, OW1 afternoon, more pool the next day). The other options impose rigid 'must finish first three' gates that aren't in the standard.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.5 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a real-world application for Section 6 | Your Scuba Diving Experiences and Beyond?

options.a: Connecting their academic knowledge to the open water training dives that they are about to complete
options.b: Describing the process for assembling their Total Diving System
options.c: Emphasizing the difference between rental and personal equipment
options.d: Providing real-world examples of no-decompression limit dive planning

questions.explanation: Section 6 = the wrap-up + transition section: it's where the theory clicks together and the student looks forward to actual diving + the continuing-ed pathway beyond OWD. Connecting academic knowledge to upcoming OW training dives fits that 'ready to dive' framing perfectly. (b) and (c) belong in equipment sections; (d) NDL planning lives in Section 4 (mssi-inst-308).
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.5 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a real-world application for Section 2 | Using Your Life Support Equipment?

options.a: Promoting the Perfect Buoyancy program to students who want to learn more about safe diving practices
options.b: Explaining the importance of logging dives
options.c: Explaining the effects of nitrogen narcosis on a diver
options.d: Promoting the Science of Diving program to students who are interested in the effects of pressure

questions.explanation: Section 2 covers life support equipment — BCD, regulator, buoyancy control. Promoting Perfect Buoyancy specialty is a natural cross-sell tied directly to that content (BCD use → buoyancy mastery → next program). (b) logbook is generic to any section. (c) narcosis lives in Section 4 (depth/gas physiology). (d) Science of Diving is a separate knowledge specialty, not the equipment-focused sell.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.5 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a real-world application for Section 3 | Breathing Gas and the Effects on Your Body?

options.a: Discussing the dive schedule for a trip your training center takes that involves plane flights
options.c: Encouraging students to take an Ecology specialty program
options.d: Describing interesting or unique animals seen at your favorite dive site

questions.explanation: Section 3 covers breathing gas + physiological effects: decompression, residual nitrogen, no-fly times. A dive trip itinerary that involves plane flights anchors that abstractly-taught no-fly window in something tangible (18-24h pre-flight depending on dive profile). (c) and (d) are ecology / Section 5 territory. (b) all-correct sweeps in irrelevant content.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.5 Review

questions.question: Open Water Training Dive 2 may only be conducted:

options.b: After completing Academic Sessions 1-3, and Pool/Confined Water Sessions 2-3
options.c: After completing all academic sessions and Pool/Confined Water Sessions 1-3
options.d: After completing all academic and pool/confined water sessions

questions.explanation: OWD program gating: OW2 needs Academic Sessions 1-3 + Pool/Confined Sessions 2-3 done — specific milestone targeting, not over-prescriptive 'finish everything'. (a) drops the pool prereq entirely. (c) and (d) over-restrict by requiring more sessions than the standard mandates. The Flexibility Rule still applies: this is the SOONEST OW2 can run, not the only allowed sequence.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.5 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a real-world application of Section 1 | Your Body and the Aquatic Environment?

options.a: Providing real-world examples of no-decompression limit dive planning
options.b: Explaining the process for dealing with a suspected decompression illness or diving injury
options.c: Explaining the effects of nitrogen narcosis on a diver
options.d: Describing how a high-quality mask improves a student's comfort and ability to see underwater

questions.explanation: Section 1 covers body adaptation to underwater environment — vision (mask physics), hearing, pressure on ears/sinuses, thermal regulation. Mask quality and underwater vision is a direct connect-to-section application. (a) NDL = Section 4. (b) DCI response = Section 3 / emergency procedures. (c) narcosis = Section 4 (depth / gas physiology).
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 6.5 Review

questions.question: When must a certification card be issued?

options.a: Only if the student requests a card
options.b: Only if the Open Water Instructor decides that the student is qualified
options.c: When the student completes the program requirements outlined in the SSI Training Standards
options.d: Once the training center decides that the student is qualified

questions.explanation: Cert issuance is standards-driven, not discretionary. Once the student has met the requirements set out in SSI Training Standards (skills mastered, exam passed, paperwork complete), the cert is issued — full stop. (a) student request shouldn't gate the cert (some students never ask but still earned it). (b) and (d) frame it as a judgement call by Pro / TC, but Standards remove the arbitrariness — the same competence yields the same cert anywhere in the SSI network.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.1 Review

questions.question: Why should you learn about your students' motivations for taking the Open Water Diver program?

options.a: You can reduce the open water training dives
options.b: You can eliminate skills that they will not need
options.c: You can adapt your training to meet their goals
options.d: You do not need to learn about their motivations

questions.explanation: Adult-learner principle echoed across mssi-inst-108, 116, 210: knowing the student's goals lets you tune emphasis and examples to what they personally care about (destination diving, photography, drysuit work). Standards content stays fixed — but the framing flexes. (a) and (b) would breach Training Standards (you cannot reduce required dives or skip required skills based on goals). (d) inverts adult-learner methodology entirely.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.1 Review

questions.question: The Open Water Diver program is designed to:

options.b: Train students for every diving environment
options.c: Teach advanced skills, like lift bags and deep descents

questions.explanation: OWD mission statement: produce a diver who can plan and execute a safe, well-managed dive within OWD limits without instructor supervision. (b) 'every environment' over-reaches — cold water, altitude, overhead environments need separate specialty training. (c) lift bags and deep descents live in Search & Recovery, Deep Diving, and similar specialties — not OWD core. (a) all-correct sweeps in those wrong scopes.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.1 Review

questions.question: Divers taking the Open Water Diver program:

options.a: All share the same reason for becoming divers
options.b: Have a variety of reasons for becoming divers

questions.explanation: Diverse motivations across the OWD population: dream destinations, fitness and sport, family activity, career change, lifelong bucket-list, peer pressure from a diver friend. The Pro adapts language and examples to each student's reason. (a) is the generalisation trap. (c) and (d) absolute 'always' claims ignore the spectrum — many students mix internal and external motivation.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.1 Review

questions.question: The purpose of evaluating Open Water Diver students is to:

options.a: Make them feel bad about their abilities
options.b: Determine whether they can continue with advanced training
options.c: Determine if they have the knowledge, skills, and abilities to earn the Open Water Diver certification
options.d: Require them to take remedial training

questions.explanation: Evaluation = certification gate: does the student meet the Training Standards? Yes → cert. No → more training before re-evaluation. (a) is negative pedagogy and breaks SSI ethics. (b) advanced-training readiness is a downstream consequence, not the evaluation's purpose. (d) remedial training is one possible RESPONSE to evaluation results, not the purpose.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is true about Open Water Diver students?

options.a: They only need to be supervised in deep water
options.b: They only need to be supervised in open water
options.c: They must never be under water without supervision
options.d: They only need to be supervised during skill evaluations

questions.explanation: Until certification, an OWD student is never under water without Pro supervision — period. Pool, shallow, deep, OW, evaluation, practice — every minute submerged needs eyes on. Post-cert they become autonomous within OWD limits, but the cert is the gate. (a), (b) and (d) all create false safe zones where supervision could lapse — none exist for students.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.1 Review

questions.question: The Open Water Diver certification trains divers to a maximum depth of:
questions.explanation: OWD = 18 m / 60 ft maximum depth. Memory hooks for the wider tier ladder: Scuba Diver 12 m, Open Water Diver 18 m, Advanced Open Water Diver 30 m (often cited as 27 m for older curricula), Deep Diving Specialty 40 m. (a) 15 m falls between Scuba Diver and OWD — not a standard limit. (b) 32 m and (c) 27 m are higher-tier or older limits.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.1 Review

questions.question: The Indoor Diver program may be completed in _____ as outlined in the SSI Training Standards.

options.c: Any indoor pool deeper than 10 meters

questions.explanation: Indoor Diver is a specialty designed for purpose-built indoor deep-water facilities — Deepspot in Poland, Y-40 in Italy, NEMO 33 in Belgium, Deep Joy in Belgium. The Standards specify 'an indoor diving facility' as the qualifying environment — recognised facilities, not just any indoor pool. (a) inverts (open water is the antonym). (c) depth alone doesn't qualify a pool as a recognised facility. (d) too broad — must be the certified facility class.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.2 Review

questions.question: Where can you find more information on the standards requirements for the Scuba Diver program?

options.a: In the Open Water Diver Standard
options.b: In the Open Water Instructor Standards

questions.explanation: Scuba Diver = entry-level certification, so its standards live under Entry-Level Program Standards alongside OWD. (a) OWD has its own dedicated section, separate from Scuba Diver. (b) OWI Standards covers the Pro side (what an OWI may do), not the program itself. (d) General Training Standards covers cross-program rules — ethics, active status, the Flexibility Rule (mssi-inst-121, 171) — not specific program content.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.2 Review

questions.question: Students must complete ____ from the Open Water Diver program to receive the Referral Diver certification.

options.a: The academic training and all pool/confined water requirements
options.c: The academic training, pool/confined water sessions, and the first two open water training dives
options.d: The academic training and the first two pool/confined water sessions

questions.explanation: Referral Diver = the classic 'finish at home, OW on vacation' scenario: student completes ALL academic + ALL pool/confined water training at home TC, then flies to a destination and completes the OW dives with another SSI TC. Pre-departure milestone = academic + pool/confined complete, no OW yet. (b) and (d) leave pool training incomplete; (c) implies partial OW already, which means they're past referral status.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.2 Review

questions.question: Which academic training sessions must be completed for the Scuba Diver certification?
questions.explanation: Scuba Diver is the lighter entry-level cert — covers fundamentals: Section 1 (Body & Aquatic Environment), Section 2 (Life Support Equipment), Section 3 (Breathing Gas / Effects on Body). Sections 4-6 (buddy + executing dives, ocean, future) are OWD-only extension content. (a) 1-4 over-includes; (b) 1-6 is full OWD scope; (c) 3-6 inverts the structure.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.2 Review

questions.question: An Indoor Diver must complete _____ to upgrade to an Open Water Diver certification.

options.a: Two open water training dives, and all skill evaluations
options.b: At least one open water training dive, and all skill evaluations
options.c: Two pool/confined water sessions, and two open water training dives
options.d: All four open water training dives, and all skill evaluations

questions.explanation: Indoor Diver already has the academic + skill mastery completed in an indoor facility, so the upgrade gap is the open-water transfer: 2 OW training dives + skill evaluation in actual open water. (d) 'all four' would duplicate work the Indoor cert already validates. (b) one dive isn't enough open-water exposure. (c) more pool sessions don't add anything — they already have those.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.2 Review

questions.question: A Scuba Diver must complete _____ to upgrade to an Open Water Diver certification.

options.b: The remaining academic and pool/confined water sessions and four open water training dives.
options.c: The remaining academic and pool/confined water sessions and two open water training dives.
options.d: Only the open water training dives

questions.explanation: Scuba Diver already covers Academic Sessions 1-3 + corresponding pool/confined work + 2 OW dives (12 m limit). The OWD upgrade gap = Academic Sessions 4-6 + remaining pool/confined sessions + 2 ADDITIONAL OW dives (to reach the OWD total of 4). (b) 'four OW dives' would duplicate the two already done. (a) and (d) ignore the academic and pool gap.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.2 Review

questions.question: A Referral Diver must complete ____ to upgrade to an Open Water Diver certification.

options.a: At least two open water training dives and all skill evaluations
options.b: All open water training dives, and all skill evaluations
options.c: At least two open water training dives, and the skill evaluations from the first two open water training dives
options.d: At least four open water training dives

questions.explanation: Referral Diver has academic + all pool/confined done at the home TC (mssi-inst-325) but ZERO OW dives — that's the whole point of referral. The destination TC therefore runs all four OW training dives + all skill evaluations to finish the cert. (a) and (c) leave OW work incomplete with only two dives. (d) drops the skill evaluations, which are required.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.2 Review

questions.question: Referral Divers may dive in:

options.a: Open water environments shallower than 12 meters with a buddy
options.b: Any open water environment with a certified assistant
options.c: A pool/confined water environment with a buddy
options.d: A pool/confined water environment without a buddy

questions.explanation: Referral Diver hasn't completed OW training yet, so they cannot dive in open water — only pool / confined water, and always with a buddy (no solo, since they're not even certified yet). (a) any OW depth is forbidden until OW dives are complete. (b) is too permissive — assistants don't unlock OW for a referral. (d) solo without buddy is unsafe and never allowed at this stage.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 7.2 Review

questions.question: The Advanced Open Water Diver program is designed to:
questions.explanation: AOW program = a sampler / tasting menu of five specialty intros — one dive per specialty, 5 dives total. The student gets exposure but does NOT earn the individual specialty certs. Distinct from Specialty Diver (2 specialties completed + 12 dives) and Advanced Specialty Diver (4 specialties completed + 24 dives — mssi-inst-135). The 'introduce' wording is the key — full cert in a specialty needs that specialty's full program.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.1 Review

questions.question: Teaching the Advanced Open Water Diver program allows an Open Water instructor to:

options.b: Become familiar with various SSI specialty programs
options.c: Qualify for the Master Instructor rating
options.d: Certify students as SSI Specialty Divers

questions.explanation: AOW is a low-friction way for an OWI to get exposure to several specialty curricula — running the intro dives builds familiarity that helps when the OWI later pursues full Specialty Instructor ratings. (c) Master Instructor is a separate rating path with its own prerequisites. (d) the AOW intro doesn't certify students as Specialty Divers — full cert needs the complete specialty program (mssi-inst-331). (a) all-correct sweeps in the wrong claims.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.1 Review

questions.question: Students must complete the digital learning materials for:

options.a: At least three open water training dives
options.b: No digital learning materials are available for Advanced Open Water Diver
options.c: At least five open water training dives
options.d: All of the open water training dives

questions.explanation: AOW = 5 specialty intros = 5 OW dives (mssi-inst-331), each one requires the student to complete the corresponding specialty's digital learning materials before the dive. So the minimum is 5; can be more if the student adds extra specialties. (a) three is below the AOW minimum. (b) is false — every AOW specialty has digital materials. (d) 'all' is vague vs the precise 'at least five' floor.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.1 Review

questions.question: Pool/confined water sessions are:

options.b: Required before some Dedicated Equipment Specialty open water training dives
options.c: Required before all open water training dives
options.d: Only required for Junior Advanced Open Water Diver students

questions.explanation: Pool/confined sessions in AOW are conditional — required before SOME Dedicated Equipment Specialty intros where equipment-specific risks need controlled-environment familiarity first (Sidemount, Rebreather, Drysuit, Twin Tank). Knowledge specialties and Open Water specialties don't need pool prep. (a) 'recommended' understates the rule for the equipment cases. (c) and (d) over-generalise.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.1 Review

questions.question: After completing the open water training dives, you should always:

options.a: Issue the Advanced Open Water Diver recognition card
options.c: Administer the Advanced Open Water Diver final exam

questions.explanation: Debriefing is the universal post-dive practice — every dive, every time. (a) issuing the recognition card is conditional on the entire program meeting Standards (mssi-inst-316) — not 'always'. (c) AOW typically doesn't have a single program-wide final exam (each specialty has its own knowledge component). (b) all-correct sweeps in the conditionals.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.1 Review

questions.question: How does a diver upgrade to a specialty certification after completing the associated Advanced Open Water Dive?

options.a: Only complete at least one additional open water training dive
options.b: Complete the specialty program and at least two open water training dives
options.c: Nothing, the specialty certification is issued with the Advanced Open Water Diver certification
options.d: Complete the specialty program and at least one additional in-water dive (as required by standards)

questions.explanation: AOW intro dive can count as one dive of the corresponding specialty's curriculum, but the upgrade to full Specialty cert requires completing the rest: academic content + remaining required dives (at least one additional, as defined by that specialty's standards). (a) one extra dive ignores the academic and remaining curriculum gaps. (c) is the trap — AOW is intro, NOT cert (mssi-inst-331). (b) 'two extra dives' over-prescribes — the standard says at least one additional, not always two.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is a benefit for professionals who teach Enriched Air Nitrox?

options.a: It decreases the earning and marketing opportunities for the professional
options.b: There is no benefit to teaching Enriched Air Nitrox
options.c: It certifies the instructor to teach Extended Range Nitrox Diving
options.d: It is a prerequisite for Decompression Diving

questions.explanation: EAN Instructor rating is the gateway into the Tec instructor pathway — Decompression Diving and similar Extended Range programs require Nitrox knowledge as the gas-management foundation. Teaching EAN earns relevant competence and unlocks higher specialty teaching ratings. (a) and (b) invert the value proposition. (c) Extended Range Nitrox is a separate specialty rating, not automatically granted by EAN.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.2 Review

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Enriched Air Nitrox 40 specialty program?

options.a: To certify students to dive with nitrox blends up to 40% oxygen
options.b: To certify students to dive with nitrox blends up to 60% oxygen
options.c: To certify students to dive with a stage cylinder blend up to 50% oxygen
options.d: To certify students to dive with nitrox blends up to 100% nitrogen

questions.explanation: The number in the name = the maximum O2 percentage. EAN 40 = blends up to 40% O2, EAN 32 = up to 32%, EAN 36 = up to 36%. (b) 60% is beyond standard recreational Nitrox into Tec range. (c) 50% stage-cylinder gases live in Decompression Diving territory, not EAN 40. (d) '100% nitrogen' is nonsense — air is already 78% N2 and pure nitrogen is unbreathable.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.2 Review

questions.question: You can issue the Enriched Air Nitrox 40 certification card only after the student completes:

options.a: All required academic sessions, and at least two pool/confined water sessions
options.b: All required academic sessions, the practical application sessions, and at least one open water training dive
options.c: All required academic sessions, the final exam, and the practical application sessions
options.d: All required academic sessions and at least two in-water training dives

questions.explanation: EAN is a knowledge-only specialty (mssi-inst-296) — no in-water requirement. The three cert components are: academic sessions, final exam, and practical application (oxygen analyser use, MOD calculation, gas labelling). (a), (b) and (d) all incorrectly add in-water requirements that don't exist for EAN — competence is verified on the bench, not underwater.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.2 Review

questions.question: Enriched Air Nitrox certification allows a student to _____ compared to equivalent air depths.

options.a: Reduce surface intervals, increase bottom times, and increase depths
options.b: Increase surface intervals, and increase bottom times
options.c: Reduce their surface intervals, and increase maximum depths
options.d: Reduce their surface intervals, and increase bottom times

questions.explanation: EAN's two real advantages: less nitrogen → shorter surface intervals + longer bottom times. Important trap: max depth is REDUCED with EAN (oxygen toxicity caps PPO2 ≤ 1.4 ATA, so MOD shrinks as O2% rises) — so (a) and (c) 'increase depths' are explicitly wrong. (b) 'increase surface intervals' is the opposite of the benefit.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.2 Review

questions.question: Students with the Enriched Air Nitrox certification should use a dive computer to:

options.a: Maximize the benefits of their training

questions.explanation: Nitrox-capable dive computer tracks the actual N2 loading on whatever blend the diver is on, so they get the full extended-bottom-time + reduced-SI benefit (mssi-inst-340) safely. (b) inverts the truth — EAN REDUCES max depth via oxygen toxicity. (d) NDL is a hard boundary in recreational diving; 'safely exceed' is an oxymoron — that requires Decompression Diving training and gear. (c) all-correct sweeps in dangerous claims.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.2 Review

questions.question: Dedicated Equipment specialties:

options.a: Have a required pool/confined water session
options.b: Require at least four open water training dives
options.c: Can be taught by an Assistant Instructor or higher
options.d: Require at least two pool/confined water sessions

questions.explanation: Universal property of Dedicated Equipment specialties (Sidemount, Drysuit, Twinset, Rebreather): a required pool/confined session before OW, so the diver gets familiar with the equipment in a controlled environment first (consistent with mssi-inst-334). (b) OW dive count varies by specialty. (c) most equipment specialties cap teaching at OWI or higher due to equipment + depth risk profile (mssi-inst-163). (d) two-session requirement is over-specific.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.3 Review

questions.question: How does a diver upgrade from Wreck Diving to Advanced Wreck Diving?

options.a: Complete two pool/confined water sessions, and one open water training dive
options.b: Complete a dry-land session and two additional open water training dives
options.d: Complete the additional academics, the Advanced Wreck Diving final exam, and at least one additional open water training dive

questions.explanation: Advanced Wreck = penetration territory. Upgrade gap = a dry-land session for line + reel handling drills (rehearse the rope work where you can see and breathe), plus two additional OW penetration training dives to apply the technique inside an actual wreck under direct supervision. (a) pool sessions + one dive understates the OW exposure needed. (c) no additional requirements is wrong — penetration is a step up. (d) drops the dry-land line drill, which is the safety-critical pre-OW prep.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.3 Review

questions.question: Training environments and conditions for these programs should be ______ typical training sites.
questions.explanation: Pedagogical principle: training site < real-world conditions. Pick shallower depths and simpler conditions so students can focus mental bandwidth on the new skill, not on managing the environment. After mastery, they can transfer the skill to harder conditions on their own dives. (a) and (b) raise cognitive load during learning, hurting retention. (d) 'same as' removes the safety margin needed for first-attempt skills.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.3 Review

questions.question: Which of the following programs can only be taught by an Open Water Instructor with the applicable specialty instructor certification?
questions.explanation: Dry Suit Diving = Dedicated Equipment specialty with a serious equipment-risk profile (drysuit valve failure → uncontrolled ascent → DCS). The Standards require OWI authority for that risk band. (a) Marine Ecology is a knowledge specialty — AI eligible (mssi-inst-294). (c) Boat Diving is a low-risk Open Water specialty — also AI eligible. (d) all-correct sweeps in the AI-eligible programs.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.3 Review

questions.question: Why must a Dedicated Equipment specialty be taught by an Open Water Instructor with the Specialty Instructor certification?

options.a: These programs have higher risk levels and more complex equipment requirements
options.b: They can be taught by any SSI Professional
options.c: SSI does not want Assistant Instructors to teach everything
options.d: These programs have reduced risk levels and more complex equipment requirements

questions.explanation: Risk-based regulation: equipment failure modes (rebreather hypoxia, drysuit valve runaway ascent, sidemount entanglement, twinset valve isolations) all need an experienced OWI with the specific Specialty Instructor cert to teach. (b) opens it to any Pro, including DM and AI — too permissive. (c) frames the restriction as arbitrary, when it's safety-driven. (d) inverts risk — these specialties have HIGHER risk, not reduced.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.3 Review

questions.question: Which of the following instructor certifications can only be earned by an Open Water Instructor?
questions.explanation: Ice Diving = overhead environment (under ice — penetration risk, restricted egress) + extreme cold + entry/exit hazards. The combination puts it in the OWI-only band. (a) Science of Diving = knowledge specialty (mssi-inst-299), AI eligible. (b) Boat Diving = low-risk Open Water specialty, AI eligible. (d) Shark Ecology = knowledge specialty, AI eligible.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.3 Review

questions.question: To ensure student safety during training:

options.a: Have your students show each other how to complete the skill demonstration
options.b: Reduce ratios, and increase supervision levels
options.c: Eliminate the open water training dives

questions.explanation: Two well-established safety levers, consistent with mssi-inst-258 (lower ratios = more attention per student) and mssi-inst-254/256 (supervision intensity by environment). (a) uncertified students don't have teaching authority — peer demonstration risks propagating bad technique. (c) eliminating OW destroys the cert program. (d) skipping emergency skills creates a dangerous knowledge gap.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.3 Review

questions.question: As the subject matter expert, your understanding of the program content and personal abilities must be _____ your students' understanding and abilities.
questions.explanation: Subject-matter expert principle: the Pro must know AND perform clearly above the student level so they can teach, evaluate, anticipate problems, and recover situations. (b) and (c) leave the Pro at or below student level — can't teach what you don't know. (d) 'different' is vague — direction matters.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 8.3 Review

questions.question: The in-water sessions should:

options.a: Only focus on complex scenario management
options.b: Only cover skills the student has not already learned
options.c: Include as many repetitions of every skill as possible
options.d: Progress from basic review to complex scenario management

questions.explanation: Scaffolding pedagogy (callback mssi-inst-260): start with basic skill review to confirm baseline, then build into complex scenario management as confidence and competence rise. The progression is what consolidates skills under stress. (a) jumping straight to complex skips foundations — students drown cognitively. (b) skipping review misses the chance to catch decayed skills early. (c) max repetitions without structure is busy-work that doesn't grow capability.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.1 Review

questions.question: Before earning their certification, students must have:

options.a: A React Right certification or equivalent
options.c: The Advanced Open Water Diver recognition card
options.d: At least two additional specialty certifications

questions.explanation: Diver Stress & Rescue (and the wider rescue-tier programs that this lesson covers) requires a valid CPR + First Aid cert as a prerequisite. SSI's in-house program is React Right, and equivalents (EFR, EFAW, Red Cross First Aid) are accepted. (b) DM is a Pro rating, way past Stress & Rescue. (c) AOW recognition isn't a rescue prereq. (d) two specialties is the Specialty Diver requirement, different program.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.1 Review

questions.question: What is one benefit of the Diver Stress & Rescue program?

options.a: It refreshes and further develops emergency skills
options.c: It teaches divers how to manage decompression diving emergencies
options.d: It qualifies the diver to provide advanced medical care

questions.explanation: S&R extends the basic emergency skills introduced in OWD (CESA, panicked-diver assist, lost-diver search) into broader rescue scenarios — proactive stress recognition, in-water rescue tows, surface management of an unresponsive diver. (c) decompression emergencies live in Tec / Decompression Diving training. (d) advanced medical care is paramedic-level, far beyond diver rescue scope. (b) all-correct sweeps in the wrong claims.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following topics should be adapted to your local regulations and training center policies?

options.a: Managing the scene of an incident
options.d: Transporting victims to advanced medical care

questions.explanation: All three topics are jurisdiction-dependent: (a) scene management procedures vary by country / state regulation; (c) authorities to contact (coast guard, police, EMS) and how depend entirely on location; (d) transport to advanced medical care depends on local infrastructure (boat-to-shore + ambulance vs. medivac vs. hyperbaric chamber distance). The Pro must localise all three to their region — generic textbook answers can fail at the actual scene.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.1 Review

questions.question: There are ____ pool/confined water sessions, and _____ open water training dives in the Diver Stress & Rescue program.
questions.explanation: Diver Stress & Rescue = 3 + 3 structure: three pool/confined sessions (controlled stress recognition + rescue-scenario practice) + three OW training dives (real-environment scenarios). Higher dive count than the typical specialty (often 2 OW) because rescue scenarios need more repetitions to consolidate under pressure.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.1 Review

questions.question: The Diver Stress & Rescue program trains divers to deal with:
questions.explanation: S&R scope = recreational-diving emergencies: panic, out-of-air, lost diver, surface incidents, unresponsive diver, in-water tow. (b) 'personal only' excludes helping others — too narrow. (c) 'stressed divers only' covers just part of the syllabus (rescue + scene management aren't only stress-related). (d) 'any emergency' over-reaches — decompression emergencies belong to Tec training, not S&R.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.1 Review

questions.question: To begin training, students must have:
questions.explanation: Key distinction: 'to BEGIN training' is the entry gate, NOT the cert gate. Referral Diver (academic + all pool/confined complete, no OW dives yet — mssi-inst-325) is sufficient to start the S&R academic + pool content. They still need full OWD certification before earning the S&R cert (since rescue evaluation in OW requires OW competence). (d) OWD is required for FINISHING the cert (mssi-inst-351), but not for starting. (a) two specialties and (c) DM are too high a bar for entry.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following will improve your student's learning experience?

options.a: Acting as a mentor, and teaching them to evaluate their decisions
options.b: Only teaching them one correct way to deal with each scenario
options.c: Only focusing on the equipment configurations used at your training center
options.d: Teaching them every possible emergency scenario

questions.explanation: Mentorship + decision-evaluation skill (Lesson 1.2 / mssi-inst-109 callback — adults are autonomous and self-directed). Rescue work has too much variability for rote responses; the diver needs the judgement framework to read a situation and choose the right tool. (b) 'one correct way' breaks under any real-world variation. (c) home-TC-only equipment configs hurt transferability. (d) covering every possible scenario is impossible — there are infinite combinations.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following are prerequisites for starting the Divemaster program?

options.a: Have the Diver Stress & Rescue certification (or equivalent)
options.c: Have logged at least 40 open water dives

questions.explanation: All three DM entry requirements: (a) Diver Stress & Rescue cert (or equivalent) — rescue competence is non-negotiable before stepping into a Pro role; (c) at least 40 logged OW dives — minimum experience baseline; (d) age 18+ — legal/insurance threshold for Pro responsibility. Drop any one and DM enrolment is blocked.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.2 Review

questions.question: The Dive Guide program trains candidates to:

options.a: Lead certified divers on non-commercial recreational dives
options.b: Lead certified divers on extended range dives
options.c: Lead entry-level students on excursion dives

questions.explanation: Dive Guide = entry-tier Pro role focused on guiding ALREADY-certified rec divers around dive sites — site briefing, route planning, group control on sights. Not teaching authority. (b) extended-range dives are Tec territory, far beyond DG scope. (c) leading entry-level STUDENTS is teaching — Instructor authority, not DG. (d) all-correct sweeps in the wrong scopes.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.2 Review

questions.question: What is the purpose of a mid-program counseling session?

options.a: Identify which parts of the program can be eliminated
options.b: Scare the candidates into trying harder
options.c: Eliminate weak candidates from the program
options.d: Provide feedback and constructive criticism to your candidates

questions.explanation: Halfway formative review: tell the candidate what's working, where they need to improve, what's required before final eval. Catches issues while there's still time to fix them. (a) eliminating program parts breaches Standards (fixed content). (b) intimidation breaks SSI ethics. (c) 'eliminate weak' is the anti-SSI mindset (mssi-inst-266) — the methodology is supportive, not selective.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.2 Review

questions.question: What should your candidates complete before beginning their in-person training?

options.a: All of the Divemaster digital learning
options.b: Sections 1-3 of the Divemaster digital learning
options.c: The Diver Stress and Rescue and Open Water Diver digital learning
options.d: The Divemaster digital learning and the candidate water fitness evaluation

questions.explanation: Front-load all academic content via digital learning so in-person time is reserved for application + skills — that's where Pro time has the highest value. (b) Sections 1-3 is partial — they'd hit content gaps during practical sessions. (c) prereq-program materials aren't DM curriculum. (d) water fitness eval is itself part of in-person, not pre-work.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.2 Review

questions.question: What additional training materials are required for the Divemaster program?

options.c: Dive Briefing and Dive Guiding Evaluation forms

questions.explanation: DM core competencies = briefing certified divers and guiding them on dives (mssi-inst-359), so the program-specific evaluation forms cover exactly those skills. (a), (b) and (d) are teaching-eval instruments belonging to AI / Instructor levels — DM has no teaching authority, so teaching-eval forms aren't part of DM materials.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.2 Review

questions.question: Candidates gaining the Dive Guide certification may:

options.a: Guide friends and family on recreational dives
options.c: Work for a training center, dive resort, or dive boat

questions.explanation: Dive Guide scope is NON-COMMERCIAL recreational guiding (mssi-inst-359) — friends/family on local dives fits cleanly. (c) commercial work at a TC/resort/boat typically requires the Divemaster rating (which adds the commercial-guiding authority and the additional Pro responsibilities). (d) DG can be a stepping stone toward DM, but registration also depends on other prereqs (40 logged dives, 18+, S&R cert) — not automatic. (b) all-correct sweeps in the commercial-scope item.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.2 Review

questions.question: Candidates can earn the Divemaster rating by completing their training and:

options.b: Teaching at least five Scuba Skills Updates
options.c: Meeting the requirements for maintaining active status
options.d: Registering for an Instructor Training Course

questions.explanation: Two parts to earning ACTIVE DM rating: complete the training + meet active-status requirements (annual renewal fee, online renewal, TC affiliation, insurance where legally required — mssi-inst-107, 170, 172). Without active status the rating sits inactive and unusable. (a) Decompression Diving is Tec, not DM territory. (b) teaching SSUs requires DM authority already — chicken-and-egg. (d) ITC registration is the NEXT Pro-path step, not DM completion criterion.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.2 Review

questions.question: After completing their training, Divemasters must ____ before earning their certification card.

options.a: Complete the requirements for maintaining active status
options.d: Complete the Science of Diving program

questions.explanation: Science of Diving (a Diving Knowledge specialty — mssi-inst-299) is a SSI corequisite for the DM cert card — it provides the theoretical depth (physics, physiology, equipment, environment) that pure DM training doesn't fully cover, and that DM-level Pros need for downstream teaching prep. (a) active-status maintenance is the rating-activation requirement (mssi-inst-364), not the cert-card-issuance gate. (c) registering happens at the start of training. (b) all-correct sweeps in irrelevant items.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.2 Review

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Divemaster Practical Application Sessions?

options.b: Prepare a candidate to teach the Perfect Buoyancy program
options.c: Evaluate a candidate's abilities to complete the duties of a Divemaster
options.d: Introduce a candidate to the duties of a professional

questions.explanation: DM Practical Application Sessions = the evaluation phase: candidate has to PERFORM the DM duties (dive briefing, dive guiding, in-water scenario response, surface management) under assessment. (b) Perfect Buoyancy teaching is OWI authority, not DM. (d) introduction to Pro duties happens earlier in training; Application Sessions are the proof-of-competence stage, not intro. (a) all-correct sweeps in the wrong items.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.3 Review

questions.question: A candidate must demonstrate at least a _____ level of proficiency during the Open Water Diver Skills Workshop.
questions.explanation: DM candidate doing the OWD Skills Workshop must perform at EXPERT level — clean, slow, exaggerated, demonstration-quality. Consistent with mssi-inst-349 (Pro skill must be ABOVE student level). DMs are role models: students copy whatever they see in the water, so anything less than expert quality propagates errors. (a) recreational and (b) entry are student-target levels, too low. (d) 'professional' is vague and not the SSI verbatim.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.3 Review

questions.question: The Dive Guiding Evaluation:

options.a: Can be combined with the Dive Briefing Evaluation
options.c: Should only be conducted in the pool/confined water
options.d: Should be one hour long to increase training value

questions.explanation: Briefing + Guiding evals are a natural pair (briefing leads into guiding the same dive) — combining them is efficient and gives the evaluator a continuous view of the candidate's flow. (b) 'no problems' would defeat the purpose — evaluations need realistic scenarios. (c) pool-only contradicts the role: guiding mostly happens in OW. (d) one-hour is an arbitrary fixed length not in the standard.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.3 Review

questions.question: The Candidate Water Fitness Evaluation:

options.a: Must be completed after the Dive Guiding evaluation
options.b: Is optional, if the candidate has already completed the Student Water Fitness Evaluation
options.c: Is identical to the entry-level Student Water Fitness Evaluation
options.d: Evaluates a candidate's professional-level in-water comfort

questions.explanation: Pro-level fitness eval is tougher than the student version — DM candidates need extra reserves to manage rescues, towing, surface work over longer durations (swim distance, tread-water time, gear-tow, breath-hold). (a) ordering relative to Dive Guiding eval isn't fixed by standard. (b) is wrong — Pro fitness is separately required, not waivable by prior student-level test. (c) inverts difficulty — Pro level is harder by design.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.3 Review

questions.question: Divemaster candidates must successfully complete at least ____ Dive Briefing Evaluation, but should complete more if possible.
questions.explanation: Standard minimum = 1 successful Dive Briefing Evaluation. The 'should complete more if possible' wording flags it as a floor, not a target — more reps build the candidate's confidence and refine the briefing structure. (b), (c), (d) over-specify the minimum.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.3 Review

questions.question: What kind of scenarios should be included in the Dive Guiding Evaluation?
questions.explanation: DG Evaluation tests core competence: realistic enough to look like a real dive, but with SIMPLE problems so the evaluator sees the candidate's basic decision-making without overload. (a) and (b) over-stress with complex scenarios — that's not the entry-eval bar. (d) no problems at all leaves nothing to evaluate.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.3 Review

questions.question: The Diver Stress & Rescue Skills Workshop should be done:

options.b: Only if time and scheduling permits

questions.explanation: Workshop scenarios should mirror real rescue conditions — close enough that competence transfers to a real incident, but not contrived. (b) frames the Workshop as optional; it's a required part of DM training, not a bonus. (c) over-restricts environment; pool / confined work fits some scenarios. (d) intentionally cranking up stress beyond realistic crosses into hazing — defeats the learning purpose.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.3 Review

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Deep Dive Guiding Evaluation?

options.a: To prepare future Divemasters to guide certified divers on deep dives
options.b: Divemasters can qualify as Night & Limited Visibility or Deep Diving Specialty Instructors
options.c: Deep dives will be the primary responsibility of Divemasters worldwide
options.d: To increase the difficulty of the Divemaster program

questions.explanation: DMs frequently guide certified divers at the deeper end of recreational range (OWD 18 m, AOW 30 m), so the eval verifies the candidate can manage a group safely at depth — gas-monitoring discipline, narcosis awareness, ascent control. (b) Specialty Instructor ratings are a separate teaching pathway, not a DM-eval outcome. (c) over-claims — deep diving is one of many DM duties, not the primary one. (d) difficulty-for-its-own-sake is a non-rationale.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 9.3 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is a benefit of dive industry media?

options.a: Shares your experiences with friends and family
options.b: Attracts new customers to the sport
options.d: Informs divers and dive professionals about news, events, and technologies

questions.explanation: Three legitimate roles of dive industry media: (a) sharing experiences (social posts, blogs, photo galleries) reinforces the sport's culture and word-of-mouth; (b) acquisition — well-produced media reaches non-divers and pulls them in; (d) industry information — news, gear releases, conservation events keep Pros and active divers current. All three reinforce each other.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.1 Review

questions.question: Equipment manufacturers benefit the dive industry by:

options.d: Researching and creating new medical procedures

questions.explanation: Manufacturers' core industry contribution = R&D and innovation: lighter regulators, drysuit valve safety, dive computer decompression algorithms, BCD ergonomics. (c) destination promotion is the travel industry's lane (operators, tourism boards). (d) medical research lives with DAN, hyperbaric medicine groups, dive physiology academia — not equipment makers. (b) all-correct sweeps in out-of-scope items.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true?

options.a: The actions of a professional have little to no effect on a diver's career
options.b: Capable and confident divers are more likely to continue to dive
options.c: Divers with a poor training experience will continue to visit your training center

questions.explanation: Causality chain that drives the whole SSI ecosystem: quality training → competence + confidence → diver keeps diving → lifetime value for the diver and the industry. (a) inverts the truth — a Pro's behaviour is a major variable in whether a student becomes a lifelong diver (mssi-inst-115). (c) inverts the retention reality — poor experience = no return. (d) all-correct sweeps in the false claims.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is the best resource for a diver looking for additional training and experiences?
questions.explanation: TC = the whole ecosystem under one roof: certified instructors, equipment access, programs, social network, travel options, gear servicing. (a) unaffiliated Pros lack QMS oversight + standards backing — risk + no quality assurance. (b) non-diving people can't advise on training. (d) internet forums have wildly inconsistent quality and often pass on outdated or wrong information.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.1 Review

questions.question: What is the sequence of the Customer Loyalty Cycle?
questions.explanation: Logical funnel: (1) ACQUISITION — Try Scuba / intro programs pull non-divers in; (2) DEVELOPMENT — entry-level cert builds the foundational skills; (3) COMMITMENT — gear ownership, con-ed, emotional buy-in deepens engagement; (4) RETENTION — long-term relationship, repeat business, advocacy / referrals. Each phase feeds the next; the order is causal. The other options scramble the sequence and break the funnel logic.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.1 Review

questions.question: Travel agencies benefit the dive industry by:

options.b: Promoting dive industry news and new techniques
options.d: Connecting divers and training centers to new and exciting dive destinations

questions.explanation: Travel agencies bridge divers and TCs to destinations — booking trips to Egypt, Truk, Galapagos, Mediterranean liveaboards, group expeditions, package deals. (a) R&D is the manufacturers' lane (mssi-inst-375). (b) news / techniques is dive industry media (mssi-inst-374). (c) creating + certifying divers is the TC + Pro role, not travel agencies.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.1 Review

questions.question: Why do you need to earn your customer's trust and respect?

options.a: Because it is the only way to get them to spend money
options.b: Because they can always choose to take their business elsewhere
options.c: Because they will buy less equipment from you
options.d: Because it's required by the SSI Training Center Agreement

questions.explanation: Trust + respect = the foundation of retention. Divers have free choice across TCs and Pros — without trust they walk to a competitor at the next opportunity. (a) frames trust as a sales lever only — too transactional, not the whole picture. (c) inverts cause and effect. (d) trust isn't a contractual obligation under the TC Agreement; it's a relationship outcome.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.2 Review

questions.question: By completing the Acquisition phase, your customers:

options.a: Choose not to do business with you
options.c: Decide to do business with you
options.d: Choose to become a lifelong customer

questions.explanation: Acquisition = first stage of the Loyalty Cycle (mssi-inst-378). Output: the customer has decided to engage — signed up for Try Scuba, paid the OWD deposit, booked the first session. That's the WIN of acquisition. (a) is acquisition failing. (b) Pro path is many phases away. (d) lifelong customer is the Retention output — last stage, not first.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.2 Review

questions.question: Which part of the training center team should be aware of internal and external marketing efforts?
questions.explanation: Marketing alignment is a whole-team responsibility: (a) Pros mention current promos to students during classes; (b) owners set strategy and budget; (c) retail + travel staff are the brand's face in everyday interactions. If anyone on the team doesn't know about an active campaign, customers get mixed messaging — undermines conversion.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.2 Review

questions.question: What is the purpose of positioning yourself as an expert to your customers?

options.a: They will only ask you about training opportunities
options.b: They will learn that the internet is the best source of advice
options.c: They will come to you for advice on training, purchases, and travel
options.d: They will know to look somewhere else for advice

questions.explanation: Expert status = first-call status across the entire diving journey: training program selection, gear purchases, dive destination recommendations, equipment service. Trust + competence creates a multi-touchpoint relationship where the customer keeps coming back. (a) limits expertise to one slice. (b) and (d) describe what happens when you LOSE trust — customer goes elsewhere.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.2 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is part of a targeted marketing strategy?
questions.explanation: Targeted marketing = aim at a specific audience efficiently with a clear value proposition. Cost-effective + consistent messaging both reflect that discipline (spend lands where conversion is highest, every touchpoint reinforces the same brand promise). (a), (c) and (d) include mass-emailing or cold calling — both are spray-and-pray UN-targeted techniques that contradict the strategy.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.2 Review

questions.question: Which of the following applies to the Acquisition phase?
questions.explanation: Three pillars of effective Acquisition: (b) welcoming environment — the first impression has to feel inclusive (mssi-inst-206); (c) expert positioning (mssi-inst-383) — trust in competence converts interest into action; (d) targeted marketing (mssi-inst-384) — right message reaching the right audience efficiently. Together they make Acquisition work.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.2 Review

questions.question: Why is creating a welcoming atmosphere a key component of the Acquisition phase?

options.a: It is not a key component of the Acquisition phase
options.b: Customers who do not feel comfortable will seek out a different training center
options.c: It convinces new divers to register for continuing education programs
options.d: It makes experienced divers less likely to become loyal customers

questions.explanation: Free-choice market (mssi-inst-380): uncomfortable customers don't complain — they just go to a competitor. Welcoming atmosphere removes the entry-barrier (intimidation, judgement, gear-snobbery vibes) and lets the conversation about training actually start. (a) inverts — mssi-inst-385 already confirmed it's a pillar. (c) con-ed enrolment is the Commitment phase, not Acquisition. (d) is nonsense.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.2 Review

questions.question: What is the primary goal of the Development phase?

options.b: To convince divers to purchase equipment

questions.explanation: Development phase = need-creation, consistent with the Diver Diamond philosophy (mssi-inst-224): show the diver all six components and let them recognise their own gaps — that recognition motivates the next program, gear, or trip. (a) lifelong-diver outcome is the Retention phase output, downstream. (b) equipment purchases are one expression of Development but too narrow as the primary goal. (c) all-correct sweeps in the misframings.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.3 Review

questions.question: What is the purpose of an orientation session?

options.a: To show off the most expensive equipment available at the training center
options.b: To demonstrate how experienced the dive professionals are
options.c: To highlight the features of the training center and its resources to a customer
options.d: To show the customer how complicated diving can be

questions.explanation: Orientation = a guided welcome through the whole TC ecosystem — classroom, retail floor, equipment service, travel desk, social events. The customer leaves understanding the full value proposition and what's available beyond the first program. (a) is hard-sell on the priciest gear — kills trust. (b) Pro-experience name-dropping = arrogance. (d) intimidating new customers about complexity destroys confidence.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.3 Review

questions.question: Why is it important for a training center to maintain a professional appearance?

options.a: It is more attractive to the customer, and builds confidence
options.b: The SSI Service Center can revoke membership if the training center is not well-maintained
options.c: Customers will only visit training centers with the most modern features
options.d: Training centers do not need to maintain a professional appearance

questions.explanation: Visual polish is a silent quality signal — clean classroom, organised gear room, branded décor all tell the customer 'these people run a professional operation; my safety and training are in good hands'. (b) over-formalises with a Service Center revocation threat — appearance isn't enforced that way. (c) 'only most modern' is elitist and false (well-maintained ≠ flashy). (d) inverts the truth.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.3 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is a technique for developing a relationship with your customer?

options.a: Encourage them to provide feedback and reviews
options.b: Demonstrate professionalism in appearance and training center activities

questions.explanation: Three relationship-building moves working together: (a) feedback / reviews — signals that the customer's voice matters and gives the TC data to improve; (b) professional appearance + delivery (mssi-inst-389) — silent quality signal that reinforces trust; (c) orientation sessions (mssi-inst-388) — guided introduction to the full value proposition. Together they form active investment in the customer relationship.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.3 Review

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Development phase of the Customer Loyalty Cycle?

options.a: To seek out and find potential new customers
options.c: To encourage customers to become lifelong divers
options.d: To nurture and strengthen the relationship with your customer

questions.explanation: Development = middle phase of the Loyalty Cycle (mssi-inst-378). Acquisition is done — customer is already in. Now you nurture: deepen the relationship, build competence, surface their next-step needs (mssi-inst-387). (a) finding new customers belongs to Acquisition. (c) lifelong-diver outcome is Retention's payoff. (b) all-correct sweeps in out-of-phase activity.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.3 Review

questions.question: Reviews and training center/professional evaluations:

options.a: Help the training center create new travel and training opportunities
options.b: Make the customer feel like their opinion is valid

questions.explanation: Three concurrent functions of customer reviews + evaluations: (a) signal where to expand the offer (e.g. customers asking for drysuit specialty → run one); (b) customer voice validation — feeling heard builds engagement and trust; (c) blind-spot intelligence — surfaces operational issues the TC owners may not see. Together they form a continuous-improvement feedback loop.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.3 Review

questions.question: What is the most important consideration if you want to help a customer commit to your training center?

options.a: Only give them advice that creates more training opportunities for you to teach
options.b: Only give them advice that benefits your training center
options.c: Listen to their needs, and give them relevant advice
options.d: Listen to their needs, and advise them based on your opinions

questions.explanation: Customer-first approach: understand the customer's actual goals first, then steer them to programs / gear / trips that fit. Trust grows from 'this Pro listens to ME' rather than 'this Pro pushes their agenda'. (a) self-interested upselling. (b) manipulative TC-first framing. (d) opinion-based advice ignores the customer's specific needs — ego-driven instead of needs-driven.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.4 Review

questions.question: Why would a customer decide to travel with your training center?

options.a: They don't have any other options
options.b: They want an easy travel experience with a business they are already familiar with
options.c: They want you to sell them additional equipment and training
options.d: They don't know where else to find travel information

questions.explanation: Convenience + trust drive the decision: the customer already knows the Pros, the gear standards, the group dynamics — pre-trip stress and cognitive load drop dramatically vs. booking with a new operator. (a) frames the TC as a monopoly choice — false in any healthy market. (c) inverts motivation (customer's wish, not the TC's wish). (d) negative-reasoning framing — customers do their research; the TC has to actually be the better option.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.4 Review

questions.question: What should you consider when discussing equipment purchases with a customer?

options.a: You should recommend equipment that meets their needs and goals
options.b: They should buy the most-expensive equipment available in your training center
options.c: They should buy whatever brands you recommend

questions.explanation: Customer-first principle (consistent with mssi-inst-393): match gear to the student's actual diving plans — cold vs warm water, travel frequency, dive style, budget. Right-fit gear builds long-term trust + repeat purchases. (b) most-expensive hard-sell breaks trust on first transaction. (c) 'whatever brands YOU recommend' is arrogance + loss of customer agency. (d) all-correct sweeps in the manipulative claims.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.4 Review

questions.question: What is one way that you can connect continuing education to your customer's needs?

options.a: Register them for as many programs as possible
options.b: Emphasize that they should become an SSI Professional
options.c: Find programs that requires them to buy new equipment
options.d: Identify programs that will enhance their personal goals

questions.explanation: Same customer-first principle as mssi-inst-393, 395 — link education to the diver's own goals. They have a camera → recommend Photography; they're going on a Norway trip → Cold Water + Drysuit; they want to see wrecks → Wreck Diving. (a) 'as many as possible' is upsell anti-pattern. (b) pushing the Pro path is too aggressive on a recreational customer. (c) gear-tied programs as a sales tactic is manipulative bundling.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.4 Review

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Commitment phase of the Customer Loyalty Cycle?

options.b: To create need in your customers, and reinforce their connection to your training center
options.c: To convince customers to buy as much equipment as possible
options.d: To bring new customers into the training center

questions.explanation: Phase 3 of the Loyalty Cycle (mssi-inst-378). Customer is past Development (relationship nurtured, competence built); Commitment moves them into invested-state — gear ownership, con-ed enrolment, community participation. Need-creation continues, emotional bond strengthens. (a) Pro path is a separate (and rare) outcome. (c) 'as much as possible' is the manipulative read — actual goal is fitting commitment, not maximising it. (d) new customers belong to Acquisition (phase 1).
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.4 Review

questions.question: After completing the Retention phase of the Customer Loyalty Cycle, the customer:

options.a: Will be a customer for life
options.b: Restarts the cycle at one of the earlier phases
options.d: Does not require any additional effort from the training center

questions.explanation: Loyalty Cycle is a LOOP, not a linear endpoint (mssi-inst-378). Each new program triggers a fresh Development phase, each new specialty kicks off Commitment again — continuous engagement loop. (a) 'customer for life' over-states the static endpoint; the cycle has to keep moving or commitment fades. (d) 'no additional effort' is the inverted truth — Retention is the most active phase, requiring continuous attention. (c) all-correct sweeps in those static-endpoint claims.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.5 Review

questions.question: After passing the Instructor Evaluation, an Open Water Instructor can teach:

options.a: Open Water Diver, Enriched Air Nitrox, Diver Stress & Rescue, and Divemaster
options.b: Enriched Air Nitrox, Diver Stress & Rescue, and Divemaster
options.c: Open Water Diver, Perfect Buoyancy, Basic Diver and Science of Diving
options.d: Perfect Buoyancy, Open Water Diver, Dive Guide, and Deep Diving

questions.explanation: Comprehensive OWI scope post-IE in SSI's pathway: entry-level cert (OWD), gas-management specialty (EAN), rescue (S&R), and Pro-level training (Divemaster). The DM-teaching authority lands at OWI in SSI structure (vs other agencies where it sits higher, at AIT/IT). Distractors mix in specialties needing separate Specialty Instructor ratings (Perfect Buoyancy, Deep Diving — would need those SI certs) or programs outside this scope (Basic Diver, Dive Guide, Science of Diving programs aren't in this base bundle).
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.5 Review

questions.question: How can you support a diver who is looking for new travel opportunities?

options.a: Recommend trips that require the diver to take additional training
options.b: Recommend trips that meet their goals and budget
options.c: Only recommend trips that have the highest profit margin for the training center
options.d: Recommend trips that will require the diver to purchase new equipment

questions.explanation: Customer-first travel advice (consistent with mssi-inst-393, 395, 396): match the trip to goals AND financial reality. Right-fit recommendations build trust + repeat travel through the TC. (a) bundling training as a trip prereq is forced upsell. (c) highest-profit-first is the manipulative TC-first frame. (d) requiring gear purchases as a trip filter is bundling for the wrong reason.
questions.source: mySSI: ITC Lesson 10.5 Review

questions.question: People continue to dive because:
