options.c: The transfer of heat energy in a fluid
options.d: The change from a liquid to a vapor

questions.explanation: Radiation = heat transfer via electromagnetic waves, no medium required (sun warms space; infrared heat lamps work through air gaps). Diving relevance is limited compared to conduction and convection (water's main heat-loss paths) but sun radiation IS the engine that warms the surface layer, creating thermoclines. (b) is conduction (mssi-sod-021). (c) is convection (mssi-sod-026). (d) is evaporation, a phase change rather than a heat-transfer mode.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 1.5 Review

questions.question: How magnified do objects appear underwater compared to air?

options.a: No magnification, they appear the same

questions.explanation: Refraction at the water-air interface inside the mask makes objects appear ~33% LARGER and ~25% CLOSER. Light bends when crossing media of different densities (Snell's Law), so the angles entering the eye correspond to a wider field — the brain interprets the larger retinal image as a closer/bigger object. Practical: depth and distance perception underwater are skewed; new divers misjudge how far away big animals are (they're often further than they look).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 1.5 Review

questions.question: What is "evaporation" in terms of heat transfer?

options.a: The change from a liquid to a vapor
options.b: The transfer of heat energy through space by electromagnetic radiation
options.c: The transfer of heat energy in a fluid
options.d: The transfer of heat energy from molecule to molecule

questions.explanation: Evaporation = phase change liquid → vapour. It absorbs latent heat of vaporisation, which is why it cools (sweat is the primary human cooling mechanism on dry land). Diving relevance: minimal underwater (no evaporation while submerged), but topside after a dive a wet wetsuit cools fast as water evaporates from the suit's surface — significant hypothermia path between dives. Distractors match the other heat-transfer modes: (b) radiation, (c) convection, (d) conduction.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 1.5 Review

questions.question: What is a diver's SAC rate if they dive for 10 minutes at a depth of 10 meters, and consume 50 bar from a 10-liter cylinder?
questions.explanation: SAC (Surface Air Consumption) = breathing rate normalised to 1 ata. Step-by-step: gas consumed at depth = 50 bar × 10 L cylinder = 500 L. Ambient pressure at 10 m = 2 ata (1 atm surface + 1 bar hydrostatic). SAC = 500 L ÷ 10 min ÷ 2 ata = 25 L/min. The pressure division converts the depth-rate to the surface-equivalent rate (the diver actually inhaled gas at 50 L/min RMV at depth, but SAC normalises that back to surface so you can compare divers regardless of depth). Distractors: (a) ignores pressure factor; (d) is the RMV at depth, unnormalised.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 1.6 Review

questions.question: What is the minimum gas required for a buddy pair with a surface air consumption of 18 liters per minute, if they want to dive to 33 meters with 12-liter cylinders, and have a 2 minutes of troubleshooting?
questions.explanation: Min-gas = troubleshoot at depth + ascent gas, both divers sharing air. Pressure at 33 m ≈ 4.3 ata. Troubleshoot: 18 L/min × 4.3 × 2 min × 2 divers ≈ 310 L. Ascent + safety stop (33→0 m + 3 min stop, ~7 min average ~2.6 ata): 18 × 2.6 × 7 × 2 ≈ 655 L. Total ≈ 965 L ÷ 12 L cylinder ≈ 80 bar → matches 78 bar (rounding-dependent). The other options stop short: (b) 60 ≈ ascent only; (c) 53 ≈ troubleshoot with 1.5× stress factor; (d) 36 ≈ troubleshoot at depth only with no ascent reserve.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 1.6 Review

questions.question: Assuming they keep a 50 bar reserve, how long will a diver's cylinder last if they intend to dive to 10 meters with a 12-liter cylinder filled to 200 bar, and have a SAC rate of 20 liters per minute?
questions.explanation: Step-by-step: Total gas = 12 L × 200 bar = 2400 L. Reserve = 12 L × 50 bar = 600 L. Usable = 2400 − 600 = 1800 L. At 10 m the ambient pressure is 2 ata, so consumption at depth = 20 L/min × 2 = 40 L/min. Time = 1800 L ÷ 40 L/min = 45 minutes. Distractors: (a) 25 min would correspond to ~40 m / 5 ata; (c) 10 min is nonsensically short; (d) 50 min ignores the 50 bar reserve.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 1.6 Review

questions.question: What is the minimum gas required for a buddy pair with a surface air consumption of 20 liters per minute, if they want to dive to 27 meters with 12-liter cylinders, and have a 2 minutes of troubleshooting?
questions.explanation: Min-gas same approach as mssi-sod-031: troubleshoot at depth + ascent gas for both divers sharing air. Pressure at 27 m ≈ 3.7 ata. Troubleshoot: 20 L/min × 3.7 × 2 min × 2 divers ≈ 296 L. Ascent + safety stop (~6 min, average ~2.3 ata): 20 × 2.3 × 6 × 2 ≈ 552 L. Total ≈ 850 L ÷ 12 L cylinder ≈ 62-70 bar depending on the exact ascent / stop profile. Less than mssi-sod-031's 78 bar (which was for 33 m / 4.3 ata) — shallower dive = less min-gas, makes sense.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 1.6 Review

questions.question: Factors that might affect a diver's breathing gas consumption include:

options.c: Mental and physical exertion, experience, and confidence

questions.explanation: Three categories influence SAC simultaneously: (a) personal physiology — body mass, lung volume, age, gender, fitness; (b) environmental — depth (pressure × consumption), cold water (extra work breathing), currents (extra exertion); (c) psycho-physical — stress, workload, dive experience, confidence. A nervous new diver typically breathes 2-3× faster than a relaxed expert. SAC therefore is both individual and situational — measure your own at multiple sites/conditions, average over many dives.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 1.6 Review

questions.question: What is a diver's surface air consumption?

options.a: The amount of breathing gas they consume every minute
options.b: The rate per hour of their breathing gas consumed while at effort on the surface
options.c: The rate per minute of their breathing gas consumed while at rest on the surface
options.d: The rate per minute of their breathing gas consumed while at effort under the water

questions.explanation: SAC's three precise qualifiers: RATE PER MINUTE (not per hour), AT REST (baseline, not under exertion), ON THE SURFACE (1 ata, normalised). That standardisation is why SAC is comparable between divers and dives — anyone at any depth and workload can be back-calculated to this baseline (mssi-sod-030 formula). (a) is too vague, missing the rate / rest / surface qualifiers. (b) hour is wrong unit. (d) under-water + effort produces a depth-and-workload dependent number, not a baseline.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 1.6 Review

questions.question: What is the purpose of performing a safety stop during ascent?

options.a: To regulate off-gassing by making predetermined stop at a progressively shallower depth
options.b: To develop a dissolved nitrogen tension higher than the surrounding pressure
options.c: To maintain an ascent rate of 9 meters per minute
options.d: To prevent the formation of bubbles as the gases separate out of solution

questions.explanation: Safety stop = a predetermined pause (typically 3 min at 5 m) during ascent that REGULATES the off-gassing rate. By holding at a shallower depth before surfacing, the diver lets the largest gradient pressure-drop happen with extra time, allowing dissolved nitrogen to leave tissues smoothly through the lungs rather than coming out of solution as bubbles. (d) prevents-bubble-formation is the functional OUTCOME of regulated off-gassing, but (a) is the SoD's precise functional wording. (b) describes supersaturation (the state that drives off-gassing), not the stop's purpose. (c) ascent rate is a separate rule.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: What name do divers give the process of gas moving from a high-concentration area to a low-concentration area at the start of a dive?
questions.explanation: Decompression direction lexicon: ON-GASSING happens during descent / time at depth — ambient pressure is high, the gradient drives N₂ INTO tissues until equilibrium. OFF-GASSING is the reverse (ascent / surface interval) — N₂ leaves tissues. SUPERSATURATION is the STATE during ascent where tissue tension > ambient pressure (the driver of off-gassing). MICROBUBBLES are the silent inert-gas bubbles that always form during ascent — usually harmless, but if they grow / coalesce they become symptomatic DCS.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: Increased pressure on the body makes gases:
questions.explanation: Direct application of Henry's Law (mssi-sod-019): dissolved gas amount ∝ partial pressure. Higher pressure → more gas dissolves into the liquid (blood + tissues). This IS the mechanism for on-gassing during descent — every metre deeper raises N₂ partial pressure, increases solubility, drives more nitrogen into tissues. (a) is the opposite of Henry's. (c) contradicts the entire framework of decompression theory. (d) supersaturation is a state during ascent, not a direct effect of pressure on solubility.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: What does it mean if a body tissue is poorly-perfused?

options.a: It has a good blood supply

questions.explanation: Perfusion = blood flow rate through a tissue → controls how fast gases exchange. WELL-perfused tissues (heart, brain, lungs) = rapid on-gassing AND off-gassing. POORLY-perfused (fat, joints, ligaments, dense bone) = slow gas exchange in BOTH directions. The question asks about elimination, so 'more slowly' (c) is correct. (a), (b) and (d) all describe well-perfused tissue. Practical relevance: slow tissues are the limiting factor for fly-after-diving wait times — even after fast tissues have cleared, slow ones still have residual N₂.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: How do microbubbles increase the risks of decompression sickness?

options.b: They can grow or join together to form a mass
options.c: They can grow inside body tissues and cause nerve pain and damage
options.d: They can grow inside the cardiovascular system and block blood flow to organs

questions.explanation: Three pathways from harmless silent microbubbles to symptomatic DCS: (b) growth + coalescence forms bubbles big enough to cause mass effects; (c) tissue-bound bubbles compress nerves → pain (Type I 'bends') and can damage spinal cord (Type II); (d) intravascular bubbles can block circulation → tissue ischemia, CAGE, organ damage. All three are real mechanisms — controlled ascent + safety stops keep microbubbles small enough to be harmless.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: A diver becomes supersaturated with inert gas when:

options.c: The amount of gas in their blood exceeds equilibrium levels

questions.explanation: Three views of the same supersaturation state: (b) the DESATURATION gradient (tissue tension > ambient pressure) IS the supersaturation — it drives off-gassing flow outward; (c) tissue gas tension exceeding the equilibrium level (= ambient PP) is the textbook definition; (d) ASCENT is what triggers it — pressure drops below tissue tension. All three describe the same physical situation from different angles.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: What are microbubbles?

options.a: Small gas bubbles that form only in well-perfused tissues
options.b: Tiny inert gas bubbles that dissolve in a diver's bloodstream and tissues
options.c: Tiny inert gas bubbles that form in a diver's bloodstream and tissues
options.d: Small gas bubbles that a diver breathes out through their regulator

questions.explanation: Microbubbles = tiny INERT-GAS bubbles that FORM (the opposite of dissolve) in the bloodstream and tissues during ascent — silent, normally harmless if controlled by proper ascent rate + safety stop. (a) is too narrow — they form in any tissue with some blood supply, not only well-perfused. (b) inverts dissolve / form — when bubbles dissolve back, the threat is gone. (d) those are exhalation bubbles from the regulator, completely different — visible in the water column.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.1 Review

questions.question: What condition did early caisson workers experience that led to the identification of decompression sickness?
questions.explanation: 'Caisson disease' = the 19th-century original name for DCS. Workers in pressurised caissons (bridge piers, underwater tunnels) experienced joint pain — they hunched over to relieve it, hence 'the bends' — plus respiratory symptoms ('the chokes'). Paul Bert (1878) identified nitrogen as the cause, J.S. Haldane (1908) developed staged decompression — the foundation of all modern dive tables and computers. (b), (c) and (d) are unrelated symptoms.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: The Haldane model calculates decompression stops in increments of:
questions.explanation: Haldane (1908) used 10-foot increments — ~3 metres in metric. His algorithm allowed a maximum 2:1 supersaturation ratio (ascent halving the absolute pressure), and stops were placed every ~3 m to control off-gassing. Modern recreational tables and most Tec stops still use 3 m increments today, inherited directly from Haldane's framework. (c) 10 m would be a single Haldane stop spanning multiple compartments — too coarse. (a, d) don't match historical or modern practice.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: What is the main difference between models and algorithms?

options.a: Models and algorithms are both used to write code
options.b: Models and algorithms are the same thing
options.c: Models calculate future outcomes, algorithms predict them based on initial conditions
options.d: Models predict future outcomes, algorithms calculate them based on initial conditions

questions.explanation: In decompression context: a MODEL is the theoretical framework that predicts how gases behave in tissues (Bühlmann ZH-L16, RGBM, VPM-B, Haldane originals) — what it expects to happen physically. An ALGORITHM is the computational procedure that takes the model + initial conditions (current dive profile, breathing gas, tissue loadings) and calculates concrete outputs: NDL countdown, required stops, ascent ceiling. (c) inverts predict / calculate. (a, b) collapse the distinction.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: The M-value is the _____ value of nitrogen.
questions.explanation: M-value (from German Maximalwert) = the maximum nitrogen supersaturation a tissue compartment can tolerate without symptomatic bubble formation. Bühlmann ZH-L16 specifies 16 M-values per compartment, each a linear function of ambient pressure: M = M0 + ΔM·P. Crossing M-value means the compartment's tension exceeds safe supersaturation — DCS risk rises sharply. (a, b) 'minimum' inverts the concept. (c) 'saturation' (without 'super') means equilibrium, which is always tolerated — no decompression rule needed for that state.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true about flying after diving and altitude diving?

options.a: The diver is exposed to increased atmospheric pressure
options.b: The diver's tissues saturate and desaturate
options.d: A reduction in ambient pressure can cause nitrogen to come out of solution

questions.explanation: Both scenarios share the same physics: REDUCED ambient pressure vs sea-level surface. Flying = cabin altitude ~2400 m → ~0.75 ata; altitude lake diving = high elevation surface pressure already < 1 ata. For a diver carrying residual nitrogen, dropping to lower ambient pressure is effectively continuing the ascent → more off-gassing → bubble formation risk. That's why fly-after-diving wait times exist (typically 18-24 h depending on dive profile). (a) inverts the direction. (b) is vague. (c) all-correct sweeps in the wrong (a).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: Which type of model tracks bubbles as they grow or shrink during a dive?
questions.explanation: Two major decompression model families: DISSOLVED GAS models (Bühlmann ZH-L16, Haldane originals) track only the dissolved-gas tension in tissue compartments and check it against M-values (mssi-sod-046). BUBBLE models (VPM-B = Varying Permeability Model, RGBM = Reduced Gradient Bubble Model) track the bubbles themselves — their nucleation, growth and shrinkage through the dive — and tend to recommend deeper stops earlier in ascent. (c) SAC is a breathing rate, not a deco model. (d) Haldanian is a subset of the dissolved-gas family, so it doesn't track bubbles directly.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: Why is it important to maintain proper stop depth control during a dive?

options.a: To prevent oxygen toxicity during ascent
options.b: To increase the time required to reduce nitrogen in the tissues
options.c: To maximize the pressure gradient available for off-gassing and reduce the risk of decompression sickness
options.d: To allow the tissues to efficiently absorb nitrogen

questions.explanation: Stop depth = optimised balance: shallow enough for a strong off-gassing gradient (tissue tension >> ambient = fast nitrogen elimination), but not so shallow that supersaturation crosses the M-value (mssi-sod-046) and bubbles grow. Example at 5 m safety stop: ambient ~1.5 ata, fast tissues sit well above this → robust off-gassing flow. Going deeper (e.g., 8 m) reduces gradient → slower elimination. Going too shallow (e.g., 2 m or surfacing early) risks crossing M-value → DCS. (a) oxygen toxicity is a PPO₂ issue, not stop-depth; (b) and (d) invert direction.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: What must a diver do if their computer calculates that inert gas in a tissue has exceeded its M-value?

options.a: They must breathe oxygen immediately upon surfacing
options.b: They must make an extra long surface interval
options.c: They must make staged decompression stops
options.d: They must end the dive and surface immediately

questions.explanation: Crossing M-value means the dive is no longer no-decompression — the diver must perform MANDATORY staged decompression stops at progressively shallower depths (e.g., 9 m, 6 m, 3 m) so each compartment off-gasses below its M-value before continuing the ascent. (a) surface O₂ is first-aid for suspected DCS, not the normal procedure to avoid it. (b) surface intervals sit between dives, not within an ascent. (d) 'surface immediately' is the WORST choice — surfacing while supersaturated above M-value almost guarantees DCS.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: Haldane divided the body into compartments based on:

options.a: How quickly the tissues metabolize oxygen
options.b: How large the tissues in that area of the body are
options.c: How fast the tissue absorbs and releases gas, and how much pressure the tissue can handle
options.d: Where in the body the tissues are

questions.explanation: Haldane's compartment framework is a MATHEMATICAL abstraction — not anatomical. Each compartment is characterised by two numbers: (1) HALF-TIME = rate of gas uptake/release (Bühlmann ZH-L16 spans 4 to 635 minutes across 16 compartments — fast tissues like blood at one end, slow tissues like dense bone / fat at the other); (2) M-VALUE = max tolerated supersaturation, the pressure-tolerance ceiling (mssi-sod-046). (a) oxygen metabolism isn't directly tracked — compartments model inert-gas (N₂, He) loading. (b, d) inverts the abstraction — Haldane explicitly does NOT use anatomical size or location.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: What does a dissolved gas model calculate?

options.a: The amount of dissolved gas in the diver's brain at various depths and times during the dive
options.b: The amount of dissolved gas in each bubble at various depths and times during the dive
options.c: The tracking of one or more bubbles as they grow or shrink during the dive
options.d: The amount of dissolved gas in each compartment at various depths and times during the dive

questions.explanation: Dissolved-gas models (Bühlmann ZH-L16, Haldane) track inert-gas TENSION in each abstract compartment across the dive's depth-vs-time profile, then compare each compartment's tension to its M-value (mssi-sod-046, 051). (a) 'brain' is a single anatomical area — Haldane is mathematical, not anatomical (mssi-sod-051). (b) bubbles aren't 'dissolved' — bubbles imply gas has come out of solution. (c) describes the bubble-model family (VPM-B / RGBM, mssi-sod-048), not dissolved-gas.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.2 Review

questions.question: What factors should divers consider when choosing their conservatism level?

options.a: Type of marine life, water clarity, and number of divers
options.b: Predisposing factors, environmental conditions, and the number of planned dives
options.d: The size and number of cylinders they will use

questions.explanation: Conservatism = the safety margin added on top of the baseline algorithm. Three input dimensions: (1) PREDISPOSING FACTORS — age, fitness, hydration, fatigue, PFO, smoking, recent injuries (anything that shifts personal DCS susceptibility); (2) ENVIRONMENTAL CONDITIONS — cold water (slower perfusion), currents (workload), altitude (lower ambient); (3) NUMBER OF PLANNED DIVES — repetitive dive day means residual N₂ stacking, more conservatism warranted. (a, c, d) list factors that don't drive conservatism choice — they belong in dive planning instead.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: The low gradient factor (GF Low) is:

options.a: The minimum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann's original values
options.b: The percentage of the original M-value that defines the residual nitrogen at the surface at the end of the dive
options.c: The percentage of Bühlmann's original M-value that defines the beginning of the decompression stops

questions.explanation: Gradient Factor framework adds user-tunable conservatism on top of Bühlmann ZH-L16. GF LOW = the percentage of the M-value reached at the FIRST (deepest) stop — controls when decompression begins. Lower GF Low = deeper first stop = more time for fast compartments to off-gas before continuing ascent. GF HIGH controls the surface ceiling. Typical settings: 30/70 (conservative), 40/85 (moderate), 50/100 = pure Bühlmann. (a) M-value is MAX tolerated supersaturation, not min (mssi-sod-046). (b) describes GF High, not GF Low. (d) nonsensical.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: Gradient factors adjust the level of conservatism by:

options.a: Adjusting the diver's inert gas absorption
options.b: Adjusting the temperature gradient in water
options.c: Increasing the maximum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann's original values
options.d: Lowering the maximum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann's original values

questions.explanation: GF < 100% reduces the effective M-value below Bühlmann's pure original values = more conservative buffer. GF 30/70 means: start the first stop when fast compartments are at 30% of their original M-value gradient (deeper / earlier), and surface only when slow compartments sit at 70% (extra reserve at sea level). Lower GF = lower allowed supersaturation = more margin before bubble risk. (c) increasing M-value would mean less conservatism (and standard practice doesn't go above 100%). (a) absorption is depth/time-driven, not GF-driven. (b) temperature gradient is unrelated to GF.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: Dive computers with altitude settings allow divers to:

options.d: Dive as long as they like

questions.explanation: Altitude setting lets the diver tell the computer the current altitude (mountain lake, high-elevation site). The algorithm then uses the LOWER ambient surface pressure as the baseline — every depth measurement is referenced to that adjusted surface, and M-values scale accordingly. Without this input the computer would assume sea level (1 ata) and overshoot real NDL / under-prescribe stops. (b) the computer can't physically change pressure. (c) altitude ADDS conservatism, doesn't reduce it. (d) absurd.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: What is the high gradient factor (GF High)?

options.b: The percentage of the original M-value that defines the beginning of the decompression stops
options.c: The maximum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann's original values
options.d: The percentage of the original M-value that defines the residual nitrogen at the surface at the end of the dive

questions.explanation: Mirror to mssi-sod-054 (GF Low). GF HIGH = the percentage of Bühlmann's M-value reached at the SURFACE / end of ascent. Lower GF High = bigger buffer between tissue tension and M-value at the surface = safer surface margin. Example: GF High 70 means slow compartments may reach 70% of their M-value when surfacing, leaving a 30% buffer. (b) inverts to GF Low (deeper stops). (c) is the generic Bühlmann M-value definition without the Low/High distinction. (a) nonsensical.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: Which algorithm adjusts the level of conservatism on a dive computer?
questions.explanation: P0 / P1 / P2 are SSI / Suunto preset conservatism levels — all three are variants of the same underlying decompression algorithm with different safety margins layered on top. P0 (Standard) = baseline NDL, least conservative. P1 (Intermediate) = moderate buffer. P2 (Conservative) = strongest buffer (shorter NDL, earlier stops, deeper first stops). The diver picks based on predisposing factors and environmental conditions (mssi-sod-053).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: How do dive computers simplify pre-dive planning?

options.a: By providing real-time data on dive profiles and decompression limits
options.b: By monitoring dive parameters during the dive
options.c: By offering audible and visual alarms for potential risks
options.d: By automatically logging dive data for future analysis

questions.explanation: Logged dive history across many dives gives the diver real data to plan the NEXT dive: personal SAC pattern across conditions, how the computer responded to specific profiles, residual nitrogen carry-over after a dive day. Iterative improvement of dive planning. (a) 'real-time data' is primarily during-dive monitoring, not pre-dive planning. (b) and (c) are explicitly during-dive functions, not pre-dive. Distinction: pre-dive planning happens BEFORE the dive — using past dive data is how the computer helps with that.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: Options for adjusting conservatism levels on a dive computer might include:
questions.explanation: Three legitimate ways to tune conservatism: (a) ALGORITHM preset (P0 / P1 / P2 — mssi-sod-058); (c) DECOMPRESSION MODEL choice on computers that support multiple (Bühlmann ZH-L16 vs RGBM vs VPM-B); (d) GRADIENT FACTORS — custom GF Low / GF High pair on advanced computers (mssi-sod-054, 057). All three modify the buffer between tissue tension and the M-value, just at different levels of granularity.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: Which of these statements about dive computer advantages is true?

options.a: They provide valuable post-dive data for in-depth analysis
options.c: They display the required desaturation time for surface intervals
options.d: They accurately monitor and display real-time dive information

questions.explanation: Dive computers are an all-stages tool: (a) POST-dive — log every detail for later analysis, feeding into pre-dive planning improvement (mssi-sod-059); (c) BETWEEN dives — surface interval and desaturation / no-fly countdown; (d) DURING dive — real-time depth, NDL, ascent rate, time, gas pressure (in air-integrated computers), tissue compartment loading. All three together replace what used to be tables + watches + depth gauges, giving more accurate per-second tracking.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: What are gradient factors?

options.a: A device used to measure the water's temperature gradient
options.b: A pair of numbers entered into a dive computer to decrease the risks of decompression sickness
options.d: A method for measuring inert gas absorption

questions.explanation: Gradient Factors = a configurable PAIR of numbers (GF Low / GF High — mssi-sod-054, 057) entered into advanced dive computers (Shearwater, Garmin, etc.) to bias Bühlmann's algorithm toward more conservative decompression. Lower numbers = lower allowed supersaturation = lower DCS risk. (a) is nonsensical — GF isn't a thermometer. (c) BCD is buoyancy. (d) gas absorption is what the dive's profile + algorithm calculate continuously; GF only modifies the threshold against which absorption is checked.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 2.3 Review

questions.question: How does the heart circulate blood for oxygenation?

options.b: Deoxygenated blood is pumped to the lungs via the right atrium and ventricle
options.c: The heart pumps oxygenated blood from the lungs out to the body
options.d: Oxygenated blood enters the left atrium and ventricle from the lungs

questions.explanation: Dual cardiac circuit, all three statements correct: (b) PULMONARY circuit — deoxygenated blood from body returns to right atrium → right ventricle → pulmonary arteries → lungs for gas exchange; (d) PULMONARY VENOUS RETURN — freshly oxygenated blood from lungs enters left atrium → left ventricle; (c) SYSTEMIC circuit — left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood through aorta to the whole body. Right side handles low-pressure pulmonary loop, left side handles high-pressure systemic loop.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following are types of body tissue?
questions.explanation: Histology classifies all human tissue into four primary types: EPITHELIAL (skin, glands, organ linings), CONNECTIVE (bone, cartilage, blood, adipose, fibrous tendons), MUSCULAR (skeletal voluntary, cardiac, smooth), NERVOUS (brain, spinal cord, peripheral nerves). Together (b) + (c) + (d) cover all four — note (c) adipose is a SUBSET of connective tissue, not separate. All three answer options describe valid tissue categories.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following are parts of the heart?
questions.explanation: Heart has 4 chambers — 2 atria (right + left, top, RECEIVERS taking blood from veins) + 2 ventricles (right + left, bottom, PUMPS pushing blood into arteries). Right pair handles pulmonary circuit (low pressure, blood to lungs); left pair handles systemic circuit (high pressure, blood to body). Consistent with mssi-sod-063 cardiac flow.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following are parts of the lungs?
questions.explanation: Respiratory tree branches progressively smaller: TRACHEA → BRONCHI (one main per lung, subdivide into lobar and segmental) → BRONCHIOLES (smaller airways, ~1 mm) → ALVEOLI (terminal grape-like sacs where gas exchange happens, ~300 million per pair of lungs). Alveoli are the critical interface for diving: O₂ in / CO₂ out, AND the failure point for lung overexpansion injuries when ascending while breath-holding (mssi-sod relevant to 026, OWD pneumothorax).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: What are blood vessels in the cardiovascular system?
questions.explanation: Three blood vessel types: ARTERIES carry blood AWAY from heart (high pressure, thick walls — e.g. aorta, pulmonary artery); CAPILLARIES are microscopic (~5-10 µm) where gas / nutrient exchange happens at tissue level; VEINS return blood TO heart (low pressure, thinner walls, valves to prevent backflow). (a) and (b) wrongly include alveoli, which are LUNG structures (mssi-sod-066), not blood vessels. (d) drops veins — incomplete.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about alveoli is true?

options.b: They can hold two or more lung volumes
options.c: They are strong and can take a lot of pressure
options.d: They are delicate and can rupture with too much pressure

questions.explanation: Alveolar membrane is just one cell thick (~0.5 µm) — that thinness is REQUIRED for efficient O₂/CO₂ diffusion across the gas-blood barrier. Trade-off: extreme fragility. Pressure differentials beyond ~0.1 bar can rupture them, causing the lung overexpansion family of injuries: arterial gas embolism (AGE), pneumothorax, mediastinal emphysema, subcutaneous emphysema. That's why divers MUST NEVER hold their breath while ascending — even from shallow depth. (b, c) invert reality; (a) all-correct includes the wrong claims.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: During inhalation, the diaphragm:
questions.explanation: Inhalation mechanics: diaphragm CONTRACTS → moves DOWN like a piston → chest cavity expands → intra-thoracic pressure drops below ambient → air flows in (Boyle's Law in living form). Exhalation reverses: diaphragm relaxes → moves up → cavity shrinks → pressure rises → air flows out. (a) and (c) muddle the up/down direction. (b) rib-cage expansion is secondary (intercostal muscles); diaphragm is the primary driver of breathing.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: What is vasoconstriction?

options.a: The way that gas perfuses through the body
options.b: The constriction of blood vessels, which increases blood pressure
options.c: The dilation of blood vessels, which decreases blood pressure
options.d: A physical process which makes the body very cold

questions.explanation: Vaso- = vessel, constriction = narrowing. Smaller vessel diameter means higher resistance to flow (Poiseuille's Law: R ∝ 1/r⁴), so for the same cardiac output, blood pressure rises. Diving relevance: cold water triggers PERIPHERAL VASOCONSTRICTION — the body shunts blood from skin/extremities to the core to preserve heat. The core fills with extra volume → kidneys read this as 'too much fluid' → cold-induced diuresis (need to pee on every dive). Side effects: dehydration, raised DCS risk. (c) is the OPPOSITE process (vasodilation). (a) describes perfusion. (d) confuses cause/effect — cold causes vasoconstriction, not the other way around.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: Where does gas exchange take place in the lungs?

options.b: Through pulmonary capillaries in the trachea
options.c: Through arteries in the vena cava
options.d: Through pulmonary capillaries in the alveoli

questions.explanation: Gas exchange happens ONLY at the alveolar-capillary interface: alveoli (~300 million in adult lungs, ~70 m² total surface) are wrapped in a dense mesh of pulmonary capillaries. The barrier is just two cells thick (alveolar epithelium + capillary endothelium, ~0.5 µm) — O₂ diffuses in, CO₂ diffuses out, driven by partial pressure gradients (Henry's Law / Dalton's Law). (b) Trachea = conducting airway only, no exchange (it's part of 'anatomical dead space'). (c) Vena cava is a systemic vein returning deoxygenated blood to the right heart, no lung tissue. (a) wrongly includes the wrong sites.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: How does blood transport oxygen around the body?

options.a: Red blood cells bind with white blood cells
options.b: Red blood cells dilate to absorb oxygen
options.c: Red blood cells bind oxygen to a protein called hemoglobin
options.d: White blood cells bind oxygen to a protein called hemoglobin

questions.explanation: About 98% of O₂ in blood is bound to HEMOGLOBIN — an iron-containing protein packed inside RED blood cells (erythrocytes). Each Hb molecule has 4 heme groups, each binds 1 O₂ → 4 O₂ per Hb, ~250 million Hb per RBC. The remaining ~2% O₂ travels dissolved in plasma. WHITE blood cells (leukocytes) handle immunity, NOT gas transport. (a) RBCs and WBCs don't 'bind' to each other. (b) RBCs don't dilate — they passively let O₂ diffuse onto Hb. (d) wrong cell type.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: Which blood vessels are used for gas exchange in the body?
questions.explanation: CAPILLARIES are the ONLY site of gas/nutrient/waste exchange between blood and tissues. Their walls are a single endothelial cell thick (~1 µm), and blood flow slows dramatically (huge total cross-section) — both factors maximize diffusion time. (a) Aorta is the largest artery, just a high-pressure conduit, walls too thick for exchange. (b) Vena cava is the largest vein, also a conduit returning blood to the heart. (c) Arteries in general carry blood AWAY from the heart but their muscular walls preclude exchange — they branch into arterioles, then capillaries (where exchange happens), then venules, then veins.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.1 Review

questions.question: What is immersion pulmonary edema?

options.a: A condition that occurs when the lung alveoli fill with fluid
options.b: A condition that occurs when an unconscious diver swallows water
options.c: A condition that occurs when the respiratory rate slows down

questions.explanation: Immersion Pulmonary Edema (IPE) — also Swimming-Induced PE (SIPE) — is fluid (a transudate) leaking from pulmonary capillaries INTO the alveoli during immersion. Mechanism: cold + immersion + tight exposure suit → peripheral vasoconstriction shifts ~700 mL of blood from extremities into the thoracic cavity → pulmonary capillary hydrostatic pressure rises → plasma seeps across the alveolar barrier. Symptoms: cough (sometimes pink-frothy sputum), shortness of breath, hypoxia, chest tightness, sometimes hemoptysis. Predisposing factors: hypertension, heart disease, cold water, exertion, tight wetsuit. Treatment: end dive, oxygen, medical evac. (b) is aspiration / near-drowning. (c) is hypoventilation. (d) drowning requires submersion + asphyxiation, not just alveolar fluid (and is distinct medically).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: What is the mammalian dive reflex?

options.a: A series of physiological changes that result from tachypnea during a dive
options.b: A reflex that makes humans want to dive
options.c: A series of psychological changes that result from seeing mammals in the water
options.d: A series of physiological changes that result from breath-holds, especially during immersion

questions.explanation: The Mammalian Dive Reflex (MDR) is a set of automatic physiological responses triggered by BREATH-HOLD + face/forehead contact with (cold) water — afferent signal via the trigeminal nerve. Four main effects: (1) BRADYCARDIA — heart rate drops 10–30% to conserve O₂; (2) PERIPHERAL VASOCONSTRICTION — blood shunted from limbs to brain and heart; (3) BLOOD SHIFT — at depth, plasma fills the thoracic cavity to keep lungs from compressing below residual volume (lets freedivers go past the once-thought 'crush depth'); (4) SPLEEN CONTRACTION — releases stored RBCs, boosting O₂ carrying capacity ~5–10%. Powerful in marine mammals (seals can dive 60+ min); vestigial but real in humans, foundational to freediving physiology. (a) Tachypnea (rapid breathing) is the OPPOSITE — MDR involves apnea/bradycardia. (b) nonsense. (c) physiological, not psychological — and not triggered by 'seeing mammals'.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: Physical responses to a perceived danger include:

options.a: Increased adrenaline production, increased heart rate, increased breathing, and increased energy demand
options.d: Decreased adrenaline production, decreased heart rate, decreased breathing, and decreased energy demand

questions.explanation: Classic FIGHT-OR-FLIGHT response — sympathetic nervous system activation. Adrenal medulla pumps out epinephrine (adrenaline) and norepinephrine, which drive: ↑ heart rate (β1 receptors), ↑ contractility, ↑ ventilation rate (bronchodilation via β2), ↑ blood pressure (peripheral α1 vasoconstriction in non-essential beds), ↑ glucose mobilization (glycogenolysis in liver), pupillary dilation, sweating. Net effect: more O₂ + fuel to muscles, ready for action. Diving relevance: panic dramatically inflates SAC rate, accelerates CO₂ buildup, can cascade into rapid ascent / lung overexpansion injuries. Hence 'Stop, Breathe, Think, Act'. (b) dehydration is a chronic state, not an acute response. (c) cardiac arrest is the OPPOSITE of mobilization. (d) describes the parasympathetic 'rest and digest' state — opposite of acute stress.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: What are triggers of the mammalian dive reflex?

options.a: Seeing a mammal that is diving
options.b: Breath-holding, facial immersion, and increased ambient pressure
options.c: Rapid breathing, facial immersion, and decreased ambient pressure
options.d: Breath-holding, facial immersion, and decreased ambient pressure

questions.explanation: Three triggers per the SSI SoD curriculum: (1) BREATH-HOLDING (apnea) — central trigger, activates chemoreceptor-driven O₂ conservation; (2) FACIAL IMMERSION — cold water on the forehead/cheeks/orbits stimulates the trigeminal nerve, which is the strongest afferent driver of the bradycardia component; (3) INCREASED AMBIENT PRESSURE — depth intensifies the response, particularly the blood-shift component (plasma fills thoracic cavity to compensate for compressed lung volume below residual volume). (a) absurd — visual stimulus alone doesn't trigger autonomic reflex. (c) Rapid breathing (tachypnea) is the OPPOSITE of apnea, and ascending (decreased pressure) ends a dive rather than starts the reflex. (d) Two correct triggers but flips the pressure direction.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: What might a diver experience if they do not breathe adequately?

options.a: An overwhelming sense of relaxation and rest
options.b: A sharp increase in oxygen levels and red skin
options.d: Fatigue, breathlessness, and a feeling of suffocation

questions.explanation: Inadequate breathing — skip-breathing, shallow ventilation, or breathing into a too-large dead space — causes CO₂ to accumulate (hypercapnia) and O₂ delivery to lag (relative hypoxia). Symptoms: shortness of breath / 'air hunger', fatigue, headache, sweating, tunnel vision, suffocation feeling, and at the extreme, unconsciousness. Diving consequence: SAC rate spikes when the diver finally tries to catch up, panic risk rises, and rapid uncontrolled ascents are common downstream events. The fix is slow, deep, RHYTHMIC breathing — never skip-breathe to save gas. (a) Opposite — proper breathing brings calm; under-breathing brings distress. (b) Hypoventilation REDUCES O₂ exchange; doesn't raise levels. (c) Buoyancy is governed by lung volume averaging and BCD trim, unrelated to ventilation adequacy.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: How does the body's cardiovascular system respond to immersion in water?

options.a: The heart rate lowers as less effort is required to pump blood
options.c: Blood vessels constrict to prevent unnecessary oxygen consumption
options.d: More blood shifts towards the body's core

questions.explanation: All three statements are accurate descriptions of the immersion response. (a) IMMERSION BRADYCARDIA — hydrostatic pressure assists venous return + neutral buoyancy reduces gravitational load on the heart, so HR settles lower than on land. (c) PERIPHERAL VASOCONSTRICTION — cold + sympathetic / dive-reflex tone narrows extremity vessels, conserving O₂ for core organs. (d) BLOOD SHIFT — roughly 700 mL of blood is displaced from the limbs into the thoracic cavity, which raises pulmonary capillary pressure (and is the mechanism behind IMMERSION PULMONARY EDEMA — see mssi-sod-074). These three responses overlap with the Mammalian Dive Reflex (075/077) but operate even without breath-holding, just from being in the water.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: What actions can a diver take if they experience an involuntary stress response?

options.a: Breathe faster to get rid of the stress
options.b: Stop activity, rest, focus on breathing, and try to alleviate the stressors
options.c: Keep going until the stress passes
options.d: Go for a run and burn off the stress

questions.explanation: Classic SSI 'STOP — BREATHE — THINK — ACT' protocol for in-water stress: (1) STOP the activity so it doesn't compound the problem; (2) Establish neutral buoyancy / hold a stable position to REST; (3) Focus on SLOW, DEEP, RHYTHMIC BREATHING — this activates the parasympathetic system, breaks the panic-feedback loop, and restores normal CO₂; (4) IDENTIFY the stressor (entanglement, low gas, freeflow, lost buddy, vertigo) and address it. Resume only when calm. (a) Hyperventilating washes out CO₂ → respiratory alkalosis → tingling, dizziness, tetany, even loss of consciousness — and on a freedive precedes shallow-water blackout. (c) Pushing through stress is the textbook ramp-up to full panic and uncontrolled ascent (lung overexpansion / DCS risk). (d) Exertion is impossible underwater and adds sympathetic load.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.2 Review

questions.question: The primary symptom of ear barotrauma in its initial phase is:

options.b: A pulling sensation in the chest
options.d: Pain or discomfort that intensifies with depth changes

questions.explanation: Middle-ear squeeze on descent: as ambient pressure rises faster than the diver can equalize through the Eustachian tube, a pressure differential builds across the tympanic membrane → first FULLNESS, then DISCOMFORT, then sharp PAIN that intensifies with every meter of further descent (more ΔP). Teed scale of middle-ear barotrauma: 0 = symptoms only, 1 = TM injection, 2 = barotitis with hemorrhage, 3 = blood in middle ear, 4 = TM rupture (sudden cold-water inflow → vertigo + hearing loss), 5 = round/oval window damage. Fix: stop descent, ascend a meter or two, equalize gently (Frenzel/Valsalva), descend slowly. NEVER force equalization — that drives the round-window-rupture risk. (a) Tachypnea is a breathing pattern, unrelated. (b) Chest 'pulling' is thoracic / lung squeeze (rare in scuba, more in freediving). (c) Joint pain is DCS Type I, not barotrauma.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: Tooth barotrauma is a condition caused by:

options.b: Expanding air in the lungs during ascent
options.c: Expanding air in the teeth during ascent
options.d: Pressure changes in the ear canal during ascent

questions.explanation: Barodontalgia ('tooth squeeze') = gas trapped in dental defects — under fillings, in cracked teeth, abscesses, decay pockets, recent root canals. Boyle's Law in action: on DESCENT compressed gas can cause vacuum-pain or pull a filling inward; on ASCENT trapped gas EXPANDS sharply, can fracture the tooth or shoot a filling out — usually the most painful and damaging direction. Sometimes severe enough to abort a dive or split a tooth in half. Prevention: pre-trip dental check, NO diving for 24 h after fillings/extractions/root canals (let the cement cure, let voids settle). (a) Dehydration affects DCS risk, not tooth barotrauma. (b) Expanding lung gas during ascent = PULMONARY barotrauma (lung overexpansion / AGE), different organ. (d) Ear-canal pressure change = external/middle-ear barotrauma (mssi-sod-081).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: What are places that barotrauma could occur in the body?
questions.explanation: Every gas-filled body space is a potential barotrauma site (Boyle's Law: a closed gas volume changes inversely with pressure). Full SSI inventory: (1) EARS — external (hooded squeeze, plugged earwax), middle (Eustachian-tube blockage), inner (round/oval-window rupture from forced Valsalva); (2) SINUSES — frontal, maxillary, ethmoid (squeeze on descent, reverse block on ascent); (3) LUNGS — pulmonary overinflation injuries: arterial gas embolism (AGE), pneumothorax, mediastinal & subcutaneous emphysema; (4) TEETH — barodontalgia (mssi-sod-082); (5) GI TRACT — aerogastralgia from swallowed air, carbonation, or gas-producing meals expanding on ascent; (6) MASK — face squeeze (vacuum on descent if not exhaled into); (7) DRYSUIT — skin pinch in folded areas. So 'all answers are correct'.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: What is a type of barotrauma that can occur during descents and ascents?
questions.explanation: Ear barotrauma is bidirectional: (1) DESCENT — middle-ear SQUEEZE: ambient pressure rises faster than the diver can clear through the Eustachian tube → TM pushed inward → pain → at extreme, rupture (cold-water vertigo); (2) ASCENT — REVERSE BLOCK: gas expanding in the middle ear can't vent out through a now-swollen / mucus-blocked Eustachian tube → TM bulges outward → pain, sometimes ALTERNOBARIC VERTIGO from asymmetric pressure between left and right ears. Both phases of every dive are at risk if equalization isn't working cleanly. (a) Hyperoxia = O₂ toxicity (high PpO₂ → CNS or pulmonary tox), a gas-toxicity issue, not barotrauma. (b) Narcosis = inert-gas neuropharmacology (N₂ at depth), not mechanical. (d) 'Spinal barotrauma' isn't a recognized category — spinal DCS exists but it's decompression illness (bubbles), not a gas-space mechanical injury.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: Permanent hearing loss, vertigo and ringing in the ear can be caused by a ruptured:
questions.explanation: INNER-EAR BAROTRAUMA (IEBt) — rupture of the ROUND WINDOW and/or OVAL WINDOW. These thin membranes separate the gas-filled middle ear from the fluid-filled inner ear (cochlea + vestibular apparatus). Trigger: forced Valsalva or untreated middle-ear squeeze, where the diver pushes a sudden pressure spike across the window. Result: a PERILYMPH LEAK (perilymphatic fistula) — fluid escapes from the inner ear into the middle ear → SENSORINEURAL hearing loss (often permanent), severe VERTIGO, and TINNITUS (ringing). Worst-grade ear barotrauma (Teed grade 5). Treatment: stop diving immediately, head-up bed rest, NO STRAINING (no Valsalva, no nose-blowing, no heavy lifting), urgent ENT — sometimes surgical patch. Recovery is often incomplete and a contraindication to further diving. (a) Eardrum rupture → conductive (usually temporary) loss + transient cold-water vertigo, typically heals in weeks. (c) Eustachian tube doesn't 'rupture' — it can block, swell, dysfunction. (d) Semicircular canals are bony channels in the temporal bone; the membranous components inside can be damaged, but the classic IEBt mechanism is at the round/oval window membrane.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: What is barotrauma?

options.a: An infection caused by waterborne bacteria
options.b: A condition where bubbles form in the tissues and bloodstream
options.c: A condition where the pressure in internal air spaces equals the external pressure
options.d: Damage to the body's tissues and organs due to excessive gas volume changes in closed spaces

questions.explanation: BAROTRAUMA = mechanical injury to body tissues caused by pressure-driven gas-volume changes (Boyle's Law) in closed gas-filled spaces. Three required ingredients: (1) a gas-filled space inside the body (ear, sinus, lung, tooth, GI, mask, suit); (2) inability to equalize that space with ambient; (3) a change in ambient pressure (descent or ascent). Volume change from Boyle's Law (V₁P₁ = V₂P₂) generates a ΔP across membranes / tissues, which then strain, bleed, or rupture. Critical contrast — DECOMPRESSION SICKNESS (DCS) is a totally different mechanism: dissolved inert gas (mostly N₂) coming OUT of solution as bubbles when ambient pressure drops too fast (Henry's Law). (a) Infection is microbial. (b) That's the definition of DCS — important not to mix the two. (c) Equalized pressure = the PROTECTIVE state, not the injury — barotrauma is what happens when equalization FAILS.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: What preventative measures can help prevent barotrauma?

options.b: Equalize the ears, sinuses and lungs frequently

questions.explanation: All three rules are real SSI barotrauma-prevention pillars. (b) EQUALIZE FREQUENTLY — start equalizing at the surface, then every meter or so on descent (Frenzel preferred to Valsalva — gentler, lower round-window-rupture risk). Sinuses equalize passively if mucosa is healthy (avoid diving with cold/allergies). 'Lungs equalize' through normal breathing — never close the glottis. (c) AVOID RAPID PRESSURE CHANGES — slow controlled descents (stop if you feel pressure, ascend a meter, equalize, continue), slow ascents (≤9 m/min, even slower in last 10 m), include safety stops. Rapid ΔP outpaces the body's compensatory mechanisms. (d) NEVER HOLD YOUR BREATH — the #1 SSI scuba rule. Breath-hold + ascent = expanding alveolar gas → lung overexpansion injuries: AGE (arterial gas embolism — most lethal), pneumothorax, mediastinal/subcutaneous emphysema. Continuous breathing keeps lungs equalized with ambient. Together these three habits cover essentially all barotrauma mechanisms.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: How many pairs of sinuses does a person have?
questions.explanation: Humans have 4 PAIRS of paranasal sinuses (8 individual air-filled cavities, one on each side): (1) FRONTAL — in the forehead, just above the eyebrows; most commonly painful in sinus barotrauma. (2) MAXILLARY — in the cheekbones, under the eyes; the largest pair, often gives 'cheek pressure' on descent. (3) ETHMOID — between the eyes, behind the bridge of the nose; technically a honeycomb of small cells but grouped as one pair. (4) SPHENOID — deep behind the nose, near the base of the skull; rarely symptomatic. All connect to the nasal cavity via small ostia (drainage holes); when those clog with mucus or mucosal swelling (cold, allergy, sinusitis), gas can't equalize → squeeze on descent or reverse block on ascent. So the answer is 4 pairs = 8 cavities total.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: What type of barotrauma can occur on descents due to congestion?
questions.explanation: SINUS SQUEEZE on descent: congestion from a cold, allergies, sinusitis, or anatomical narrowing blocks the sinus ostia (the small holes that drain each sinus into the nasal cavity). As ambient pressure rises, gas inside the sinus stays at lower pressure → relative vacuum pulls the sinus mucosa away from the bony walls → capillaries rupture → blood seeps into the sinus → throbbing pain (cheek/forehead) and often a 'bloody mask' of nasal discharge on ascent. Most often the frontal (forehead) and maxillary (cheek) pairs. Prevention: do NOT dive with congestion; decongestants are a stopgap with risks (rebound congestion mid-dive, dive-window timing). (a) Hypoxia = low blood O₂, a gas-toxicity / breathing-pattern issue, not a mechanical pressure injury. (b) Hypercapnia = CO₂ retention, also gas-toxicity, not barotrauma. (d) 'Neck barotrauma' is not a recognized SSI category — subcutaneous emphysema can track into neck tissue but originates as a pulmonary overinflation injury, not a primary neck cavity.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.3 Review

questions.question: What method can be used to prevent decompression sickness during dives?
questions.explanation: All three are core SSI prevention layers — they stack, none alone is sufficient: (a) SAFETY STOP — 3–5 min at 5 m / 15 ft on every dive (SSI Total Diving System standard), provides margin against asymptomatic microbubbles and lets near-fast tissues (5–20 min compartments) keep off-gassing under modest hydrostatic pressure. (c) SLOW ASCENT RATES — ≤9 m/min (30 ft/min) per SSI; some computers slow further in the last 10 m. Slow ascent keeps tissue supersaturation below the M-value (Bühlmann) so bubbles don't nucleate. The classic 'dirty' violation: rocketing to the surface chasing a buddy or running out of gas. (d) FOLLOW THE DIVE COMPUTER — modern algorithms (Bühlmann ZH-L16 + GF, RGBM, VPM-B) track all 16 tissue compartments in real time across multi-level profiles — much more accurate than square-profile tables. Computer enforces NDL, ascent rate, mandatory stops, surface intervals.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: How might gas bubbles enter arterial blood vessels?

options.a: Through a cut in the diver's skin
options.b: Pulmonary barotrauma or shunting through a patent foramen ovale

questions.explanation: Two recognized pathways for ARTERIALIZATION of bubbles. (1) PULMONARY BAROTRAUMA → ARTERIAL GAS EMBOLISM (AGE): a breath-hold on ascent or air trapping ruptures alveoli; gas crosses the alveolar–capillary barrier into pulmonary VEINS → left atrium → systemic arterial circulation, often lodging in cerebral arteries → 'stroke-like' symptoms within minutes of surfacing (most lethal dive injury, demands immediate O₂ + recompression). (2) PATENT FORAMEN OVALE (PFO) SHUNTING: ~25% of adults have a probe-patent flap between right and left atria. Under raised right-atrial pressure (Valsalva, coughing, heavy gear-strain, lifting tanks immediately post-dive), VENOUS bubbles — which would normally be filtered by the pulmonary capillary bed — can shunt directly into systemic arteries → cerebral, spinal, or cutaneous DCS even on conservative profiles. PFO screening is routine for divers with unexplained or repeated DCS. (a) Skin cuts don't introduce bubbles. (c) Tight wetsuit restricts venous return / contributes to IPE but doesn't 'arterialize' bubbles. (d) Vasoconstriction narrows vessels — doesn't move bubbles into arteries.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: What skin or lymphatic symptoms are indicative of decompression sickness?

options.a: Lack of motor control, personality changes, and disturbed vision
options.d: Amnesia, hearing loss, headache, and nausea

questions.explanation: Cutaneous (Type I) DCS — 'skin bends': (1) ITCHING — often the earliest sign, especially after chamber or saturation exposures; (2) RASHES — classic 'CUTIS MARMORATA': mottled, marbled, blue-red blotchy patches on torso, shoulders, abdomen (caused by venous gas in subcutaneous capillaries; often a marker of an underlying PFO and may herald more serious neurological DCS); (3) SWELLING — lymphatic obstruction by bubbles, gives 'peau d'orange' / lymphedema in groin, neck, axillae; (4) CREPITUS — palpable crackling under the skin = subcutaneous emphysema (free gas in tissue planes). Treatment: 100% surface O₂, oral hydration, evac to a chamber if cutis marmorata, spreading rash, or any neurological signs. (a) Motor / personality / vision changes are CEREBRAL Type II DCS. (b) Mixes skin (rashes) with CNS (confusion, unconsciousness). (d) Amnesia, hearing loss, headache, nausea = INNER-EAR / CEREBRAL Type II DCS.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: What is pulmonary barotrauma?

options.b: Lung damage caused by a traumatic brain injury
options.c: Tissue damage and possible gas leakage caused by an excessive pressure gradient
options.d: Tissue damage caused by oxygen toxicity

questions.explanation: PULMONARY BAROTRAUMA = lung tissue damage caused by an excessive TRANS-ALVEOLAR pressure gradient. Mechanism: ascent with closed glottis / breath-hold / locally trapped air (asthma plug, mucus, broncho-spasm) → alveolar gas expands per Boyle's Law → as little as ~80–100 mbar of ΔP across the alveolar wall ruptures it. Four resulting injuries (the 'lung overexpansion family'), increasing severity: (1) MEDIASTINAL EMPHYSEMA — gas in the mediastinum: chest tightness, voice change, neck fullness; (2) SUBCUTANEOUS EMPHYSEMA — gas tracking up to neck tissue, palpable CREPITUS under the skin; (3) PNEUMOTHORAX — gas in pleural space, lung collapses: sharp unilateral chest pain, dyspnea, decreased breath sounds (tension pneumothorax = surgical emergency); (4) ARTERIAL GAS EMBOLISM (AGE) — gas enters pulmonary veins → left heart → systemic arteries → brain (stroke-like symptoms within minutes of surfacing — most lethal dive injury). This is exactly why the #1 SSI rule is NEVER HOLD YOUR BREATH. (a) Confuses two unrelated findings — crepitus comes from subQ emphysema; cutis marmorata is cutaneous DCS. (b) Wrong causal direction. (d) Oxygen toxicity = gas-tox issue (CNS hit / pulmonary tox at high PpO₂), not mechanical.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: What are some of the symptoms of lung barotrauma?

options.c: Numbness in the hands and feet and ringing in the ears

questions.explanation: Hallmark presentations of pulmonary barotrauma — both elements in (a) are classic: HEMOPTYSIS (coughing up blood, sometimes pink-frothy sputum) from alveolar rupture and bleeding into the airways; LIGHTHEADEDNESS / DIZZINESS / NEAR-SYNCOPE arises when AGE has embolized cerebral circulation, or when a tension pneumothorax compromises cardiac output. Other markers in the same picture (so you recognize the full pattern): sharp chest pain on/after ascent, shortness of breath, voice change (mediastinal emphysema), neck fullness with palpable crepitus (subQ emphysema), and the dramatic AGE presentation — 'stroke-like' focal weakness, slurred speech, vision changes, loss of consciousness — within minutes of surfacing. Onset timing is the key clue: AGE shows up almost immediately, DCS more often 30 min – 24 h post-dive. (b) Bradycardia + vertigo is more dive-reflex / inner-ear, not lung injury. (c) Numbness + tinnitus suggests Type II DCS / inner-ear barotrauma, not pulmonary. (d) Dry mouth + nausea are non-specific post-dive (dehydration, seasickness).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: What is an arterial gas embolism?

options.a: The blockage of an arterial blood vessel by bubbles
options.c: The blockage of a vein by bubbles
options.d: Blue lips caused by low oxygen

questions.explanation: ARTERIAL GAS EMBOLISM (AGE) = blockage of an ARTERIAL blood vessel by gas bubbles. Pathway: pulmonary barotrauma (breath-hold ascent / air trapping) → ruptured alveoli → gas crosses into pulmonary VEINS → left atrium → left ventricle → pumped into the systemic ARTERIAL circulation → embolizes the organ wherever a bubble lodges (most often cerebral arteries → CEREBRAL AGE / CAGE). Symptoms onset is FAST — usually within minutes (often within seconds) of surfacing: focal weakness, slurred speech, vision loss, confusion, seizures, loss of consciousness. The most LETHAL diving injury. Treatment: lay supine (NOT head-down — that's outdated and worsens cerebral edema), 100% O₂, fluids if conscious, immediate evac to a recompression chamber. (b) A 'brain bleed' is a hemorrhagic stroke / SAH — vessel RUPTURE, different mechanism. (c) Bubbles in VEINS = VENOUS gas embolism (VGE) — commonly produced by every dive in tiny amounts, usually filtered out by the pulmonary capillary bed; bypass via PFO leads to arterialization (see mssi-sod-091). (d) Cyanosis is a SIGN of hypoxemia (could accompany severe AGE) but isn't the DEFINITION.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: Which predisposing factors can increase the risk of decompression sickness?
questions.explanation: All three are recognized SSI / DAN risk factors. (a) DEHYDRATION — reduces plasma volume, slows microcirculation, thickens blood, impairs N₂ elimination. The single biggest CONTROLLABLE risk factor (drink before / between / after dives, urine should run pale yellow). (c) OBESITY — adipose tissue has roughly 5× the nitrogen solubility of lean tissue (N₂ is lipid-soluble) AND fat is poorly perfused, so it loads slowly but unloads even slower → silent reservoir of dissolved gas that liberates during ascent and the surface interval. (d) EXTREME HEAT OR COLD — both disrupt the on-gas / off-gas balance: COLD during the bottom phase causes peripheral vasoconstriction (slows on-gassing — sounds protective), but late re-warming after surfacing causes peripheral vasodilation precisely when supersaturation is at its peak → bubble nucleation; HEAT post-dive (hot shower, hot tub, sauna, sun-baked deck) accelerates off-gas exactly when bubbles want to grow. Other classic risk factors not in this question: alcohol, fatigue, age, low fitness, hard exertion at depth, repeat-day diving, fast ascent, recent musculoskeletal injury, PFO, asthma.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: Which type of decompression illness involves the central nervous system?
questions.explanation: DCS is graded by severity: TYPE I (mild) covers MUSCULOSKELETAL 'bends' (joint/limb pain — most common presentation), CUTANEOUS (itching, cutis marmorata, rashes), and LYMPHATIC symptoms (peau d'orange swelling). TYPE II (serious / life-threatening) covers NEUROLOGICAL involvement — CEREBRAL (confusion, personality changes, vision/speech disturbance), SPINAL CORD (girdle pain, paraplegia, urinary retention — most-feared form, often misattributed to back strain initially), VESTIBULAR / INNER-EAR (vertigo, tinnitus, nystagmus, nausea — 'staggers'), and PULMONARY ('chokes' — right-heart bubble overload, retrosternal pain, dyspnea, cough). All Type II requires immediate O₂ + chamber. (b) Pneumothorax is a pulmonary BAROTRAUMA, not DCS. (c) Type I excludes CNS by definition. (d) ARTERIAL GAS EMBOLISM does affect the brain but is a SEPARATE DCI entity — barotrauma-driven, not bubbles formed from dissolved gas. DCI umbrella = DCS (this question) + AGE; this question specifically asks about DCS.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: Which condition can occur when gas enters the lining of the lungs?
questions.explanation: MEDIASTINAL EMPHYSEMA — a sub-injury of pulmonary barotrauma. When alveoli rupture, escaping gas tracks along the bronchovascular sheaths up into the MEDIASTINUM (the central chest cavity between the lungs that holds the heart, trachea, esophagus, great vessels). Symptoms: chest tightness, dyspnea, voice change, sometimes 'Hamman's crunch' (crackling synchronized with the heartbeat on auscultation), neck fullness as gas tracks further upward into subcutaneous tissue (subQ emphysema, palpable crepitus). Treatment: 100% O₂ (washes out N₂, accelerates reabsorption), rest, observation; rarely needs invasive intervention unless tension develops. Strictly speaking 'lining of the lungs' = pleura, and gas in the pleural space is PNEUMOTHORAX — but that's not on the option list, so mediastinal emphysema (gas in the adjacent compartment) is the intended answer. (a) Edema = FLUID accumulation in tissue, not gas. (c) 'Hypoxic myopia' is not a recognized term — myopia is short-sightedness, unrelated to lung gas. (d) Hypercapnia = CO₂ retention in BLOOD, a gas-toxicity issue, not gas in tissue.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.4 Review

questions.question: The first recreational divers in the 1950s used:
questions.explanation: Per the SSI SoD curriculum: 1950s recreational scuba was dominated by TWIN-HOSE regulators — both inhalation and exhalation corrugated hoses routed from a back-mounted regulator box atop the cylinder valve. The iconic Cousteau-Gagnan AQUA-LUNG (patented 1943, mass-produced and mainstream by the 1950s through US Divers / La Spirotechnique) is the archetype. Note: the Aqua-Lung itself was actually a two-stage demand valve — Gagnan adapted it from car-engine gas regulators — but SSI simplifies the era as 'single-stage twin-hose' for the curriculum and that is what mySSI grades correct. (a) Jacket-style BCDs are MUCH later: Watergill ATPAC 1971, Scubapro Stab Jacket 1976. (b) Two-stage SINGLE-hose = the MODERN config, popularized only from the late 1950s onward (Sportsways Waterlung 1958, then the Scubapro Mk-5 era). (c) Bluetooth — obvious modern distractor.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: How often should equipment be serviced?

options.a: Once a week and according to manufacturer instructions
options.b: At least annually and according to manufacturer instructions

questions.explanation: Two-part SSI standard: (1) AT LEAST ANNUALLY — calendar baseline, regardless of how few dives done that year. O-rings dry out and harden, silicone grease degrades, salt creep migrates into tight-tolerance regulator components even with thorough rinsing. (2) AND per MANUFACTURER INSTRUCTIONS — many manufacturers specify additional thresholds that may trigger service sooner: a DIVE-COUNT (e.g., Scubapro / Atomic recommend service at 100 dives OR 1 year, whichever comes first), a HEAVY-USE schedule (every 6 months for instructors / working divers / liveaboard regulars), specific O-ring or seat-replacement kits, and sometimes a STORED-INACTIVE clause (regulator stored unused 2+ years should be inspected before redeployment). (a) Weekly is absurdly over-frequent and uneconomical. (c) Wrong — (b) is right. (d) 'Once a year' alone is INCOMPLETE — it misses the manufacturer-instructions clause, which is the part that picks up dive-count and heavy-use thresholds. (b) is the most complete answer.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: What is the recommended lubricant to use for equipment maintenance?
questions.explanation: Always use the MANUFACTURER-SPECIFIED lubricant. The right grease depends on the service type: SILICONE GREASE — standard for general (air-only) regulator and valve servicing, compatible with rubber/silicone seals. CHRISTO-LUBE MCG-111 or TRIBOLUBE 71 — perfluoropolyether (PFPE) greases that are OXYGEN-COMPATIBLE: mandatory for any O₂-clean equipment (≥40% O₂ nitrox/trimix, full-O₂ deco rigs, partial-pressure blending stations). KRYTOX — high-temp PFPE alternative. Why it matters: (b) WATER is corrosive (especially salt) and provides no lubrication. (c) MOTOR OIL is petroleum-based — degrades silicone and rubber seals over weeks; far more dangerous, petroleum products will AUTO-IGNITE under high PpO₂ (oxygen fires inside the regulator are catastrophic — that's why O₂-clean is a discipline). (d) 'WD-20' is likely a typo for WD-40 — also wrong: WD-40 is a petroleum solvent / water displacer, not a lubricant; same seal damage + combustion risks. Mixing the wrong grease into O₂ service has caused real-world regulator fires.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: Using a lubricant on equipment components helps to:

options.a: Reduce friction, maintain flexibility, and prevent corrosion
options.b: Reduce friction, avoid motor failure, and stop compartments from drying out
options.c: Make the propeller spin faster, stop o-rings from leaking, and prevent battery discharge
options.d: Prevent corrosion, keep the body tube clean, and reduce friction

questions.explanation: Three textbook benefits of proper lubrication on dive gear: (1) REDUCE FRICTION — regulator pistons, valve stems, port plugs, BCD inflator buttons, swivel hose joints all move smoothly without binding or grinding wear; (2) MAINTAIN FLEXIBILITY — keeps O-rings and rubber/silicone seals pliable and resistant to COMPRESSION SET (the permanent deformation that develops when a seal stays squashed for too long, the leading cause of seal failures); (3) PREVENT CORROSION — creates a barrier between metal surfaces and water/salt/oxygen, especially in valve threads, cylinder neck-o-ring seats, DIN/yoke fittings. (b) 'Motor failure' — recreational dive gear has no motors (only DPVs/scooters do, specialty equipment outside this lesson). (c) Propellers, batteries — clearly off-topic for scuba regulator/BCD/cylinder maintenance, and lubricant doesn't 'stop o-rings from leaking' — it lets them seat properly. (d) Lubricant doesn't 'keep the body tube clean' — that's what RINSING and proper drying do; lubricant is about friction/seals/corrosion, not cleanliness.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: How often should equipment be inspected for signs of wear and tear?
questions.explanation: Equipment inspection for wear and tear is a USER-LEVEL routine done EVERY DIVE — both before and after. PRE-DIVE check: visual scan of hoses (cracks, splits, bulging — signs of imminent rupture), cylinder valve o-ring intact and threads clean, LP and HP hose connections snug with no creep-leaks, BCD inflator and dump valves cycle freely and don't free-flow, computer battery state and settings, mask strap and fin straps not dry-rotted. POST-DIVE check: thorough rinse (regulator 1st stage in fresh water with DUST CAP firmly on; press the inflator during BCD rinse to flush salt out of the inflator mechanism), drain any water that found its way into the BCD bladder, look for new damage (scrapes, cuts, lost or loose hardware, hose wear at swivel points), hang to dry away from direct sunlight (UV degrades rubber/neoprene). (b) and (c) describe the SERVICE intervals — that's technician-level work, separate from the visual wear-and-tear check the user does every dive. (d) Clearly wrong. The before/after habit catches the small issues (a tiny hose crack, a loose port plug, a sticky inflator button) BEFORE they become incidents.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: Why must o-rings be properly maintained and replaced if necessary?

options.a: They provide additional buoyancy to the equipment
options.b: They regulate the speed breathing gas
options.c: They prevent water from entering equipment and keep the internal parts dry
options.d: They help the equipment move through the water

questions.explanation: O-rings are the primary STATIC and DYNAMIC SEALS in pressurized dive equipment — small toroidal rubber/silicone gaskets that compress between two surfaces to block fluid passage. Their function: PREVENT WATER ENTRY (and keep gas in / contaminants out). Critical locations: (1) CYLINDER VALVE O-RING (DIN or yoke) — seals the tank-to-regulator joint at full HP (200–300 bar); a damaged or missing one is the most common cause of failed pre-dive pressure-up. (2) REGULATOR 1st-STAGE PORT O-RINGS — seal each HP and LP port against gas leakage. (3) 1st-STAGE PISTON or DIAPHRAGM O-RINGS — separate the spring chamber from the water; if the seal fails, salt water floods the spring → corrosion → regulator killed. (4) BCD INFLATOR O-RINGS — seal LP gas into the bladder. (5) DIVE COMPUTER / HOUSING BULKHEAD O-RINGS — keep water out of the electronics (failure = flood = total loss). When o-rings dry out, harden, or take COMPRESSION SET, the result is free-flows, gas leaks, water intrusion, or floods. Hence the protocol: inspect, lubricate with manufacturer-spec grease (see mssi-sod-101), replace at any sign of damage or as part of annual service. (a) Tiny rubber rings provide no meaningful buoyancy. (b) Gas-flow regulation is done by springs / diaphragms / poppets / orifices — o-rings just SEAL. (d) Hydrodynamics is determined by gear shape, not o-rings.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about repairs is true?

options.a: Divers can repair any component themselves
options.b: Divers can take components apart if they have a buddy to help them
options.c: Repairs can be left until just before the dive
options.d: Some components may require a specialist technician

questions.explanation: Equipment repair has tiers — and choosing the wrong tier voids warranty, can fail at depth, and is professionally catastrophic for an instructor. (1) USER-LEVEL (DIY OK): rinsing, drying, mouthpiece zip-tie change, fin/mask strap replacement, user-replaceable battery on some computers, basic LP hose o-ring swap, BCD inflator lubrication. (2) TECHNICIAN-LEVEL (factory-trained, manufacturer-certified): regulator 1st/2nd-stage rebuilds, BCD inflator overhaul, tank valve service, annual VIP (Visual Inspection of cylinder interior), 5-year HYDROSTATIC TEST of cylinders (pressure-test to 5/3 of working pressure). (3) SPECIALIST-LEVEL: rebreather electronics, OXYGEN-CLEAN service (anything ≥40% O₂), mixed-gas regulators, dive computer mainboard work. (a) Wrong — pressurized components require trained techs. (b) A buddy doesn't grant tech credentials. (c) NEVER dive with known unrepaired gear; 'fix it just before the dive' is the worst possible timing — no test cycle, no leak-down monitoring of the rebuilt regulator. If a student is injured on gear you serviced without authorization, your liability insurance will likely not cover you.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: Things that divers should consider when choosing equipment include whether it will be:

options.a: Suitable and reliable for their planned dive

questions.explanation: Three valid considerations all listed: (a) SUITABLE AND RELIABLE FOR THE PLANNED DIVE — biggest single factor. Match the rig to the conditions: cold water demands environmentally-sealed regulators, drysuit, hooded undersuit; tropical lets you go lighter; depth/decompression dives need redundancy (pony bottle or sidemount, dual computers, redundant masks/lights/cutters); cave/wreck diving demands long-hose primary + bungeed backup, etc. A rec-rated regulator fine for 30 m doesn't belong on 60 m trimix. (b) OVERCOMPLICATED — phrased awkwardly but real: match equipment complexity to your skill level and dive type. A new OWD diver doesn't need a 7-button air-integrated trimix computer with five gas slots — too many menus, too many ways to misconfigure mid-dive, and the diver loses confidence using kit they don't fully understand. (d) USED CONSISTENTLY — gear sitting unused 10 months a year develops more problems than gear in regular service: o-rings dry out, batteries corrode, neoprene cracks, springs lose calibration. Buy what you'll actually use; if you only dive twice a year on holidays, rental kit may beat ownership for both reliability AND budget.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 4.1 Review

questions.question: What additional features might a diver look for in a snorkel?

options.b: A semi-dry or dry top to keep water out
options.d: A mouth strap to keep it in the mouth

questions.explanation: Three snorkel-design tiers with the higher-tier features being the upgrade many divers buy: (1) WET — basic open tube, old-school simple, no failure points; user must purge water after every duck-dive or wave splash. Preferred by spearos / freedivers for low drag. (2) SEMI-DRY — a SPLASH GUARD / BAFFLE at the top deflects waves and surface chop without fully sealing — reduces water entry but airflow is unrestricted. (3) DRY — a FLOAT-ACTUATED VALVE at the top: when submerged the float lifts and closes the airway → zero water entry; on surfacing the float drops and the valve re-opens. Ideal for new divers, surface swims in chop, conditions where water entry would startle a diver. Most snorkels also have a PURGE VALVE at the lower bend (one-way exhaust to clear water with a sharp puff) and a FLEX section so the mouthpiece hangs naturally when not in the mouth. Snorkels attach to the LEFT side of the mask strap via a snorkel keeper / clip. (a) 'Really complex design' is not a specific feature, just an adjective.
