BEYOND THESE THREE, bony fish also include DETRITIVORES (catfish, mullets cleaning organic debris on bottom); PARASITE-EATERS (cleaner wrasses set up stations where larger fish queue for cleaning); SABER-TOOTHED BLENNIES that MIMIC cleaner wrasses then bite chunks off the suckered larger fish (aggressive mimicry, opposite of Batesian); FILTER FEEDERS (paddlefish, some herring) though rarer among bony fish; SYMBIOTIC FEEDERS (anemonefish/clownfish eating parasites + scraps from their anemone home); SPECIALIZED PREDATORS (anglerfish with bioluminescent lures, gulper eels with expandable mouths). The diversity of bony fish feeding strategies is a major reason they're the most ecologically successful vertebrate group on Earth.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 5.6 Review

questions.question: Which venomous fish are well-camouflaged and could cause serious injury?
questions.explanation: Family SCORPAENIDAE — masters of marine camouflage AND venom. STONEFISH (Synanceia, including reef stonefish S. verrucosa and estuarine S. horrida) are the MOST VENOMOUS FISH IN THE WORLD. Skin texture mimics encrusted rock or coral rubble so perfectly that divers and waders step on them without seeing. 13 dorsal spines deliver venom causing excruciating pain (often described as 'the worst pain imaginable'), tissue necrosis, cardiovascular collapse, occasional fatalities. Treatment: HOT-WATER IMMERSION at 45 °C for 30–90 min (denatures heat-labile venom proteins) + stonefish antivenom for severe envenomations. SCORPIONFISH (Scorpaena and related genera): SCORPIONFISH PROPER — incredible camouflage on reef rubble, weed, and algae-covered rocks, venomous spines (less potent than stonefish but still very painful); LIONFISH (Pterois volitans, P. miles) use the OPPOSITE strategy — bright APOSEMATIC warning colors (red-white striped fan-like fins), not camouflaged but venomous; invasive in Caribbean / Atlantic from accidental Florida releases in the 1980s; DEVIL SCORPIONFISH (Inimicus) — camouflaged ambush predator. DIVER SAFETY: never put hands where you can't see (don't grip rocks/ledges), wear booties on reef walks, scan the bottom carefully before settling, watch for 'the rock that breathes'. (a) Barracudas + groupers are predators, not venomous. (b) Sharks bite (not venomous); some rays have venomous tail barbs but aren't 'well-camouflaged' in the sit-still scorpionfish sense. (c) Blue tang has a sharp caudal spine but no venom; moray eels have nasty bites but are not venomous — the lacerations get infected from oral bacteria.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 5.6 Review

questions.question: How can divers minimize negative interactions with marine life?

options.b: Observe, but do not touch or bait animals
options.c: Wear appropriate exposure suits and follow local regulations
options.d: Move cautiously, avoid sudden movements, maintain buoyancy, and avoid covered areas

questions.explanation: All three principles cover the responsible marine-life interaction code. (b) OBSERVE but DON'T TOUCH or BAIT: don't touch — your skin oils harm coral and fish slime layers, touching stresses animals, many species are venomous (cone snails, fire coral, scorpionfish — see mssi-sod-206), some species are legally protected; don't bait/feed — alters natural behavior, creates dependency, conditions sharks/rays to associate divers with food (dangerous), introduces pollutants, harms species (bread to fish causes digestive issues). (c) APPROPRIATE EXPOSURE SUIT + LOCAL REGULATIONS: suit protects you from stings, scrapes, abrasions, and protects animals from your skin chemistry contacting their slime layer; local regs vary widely — many areas prohibit gloves to discourage touching coral, restrict night diving, ban shark feeding, require permits for protected sites; ignorance is not an excuse — research before diving. (d) CAUTIOUS MOVEMENT + GOOD BUOYANCY + AVOIDING HIDDEN AREAS: slow deliberate movement (sudden moves trigger fight/flight responses); good buoyancy keeps you off the bottom (preventing reef damage from fin kicks, knees, gear drag — bad buoyancy is the leading cause of diver-induced reef damage); avoid covered areas (don't reach blindly into holes/crevices, don't stick fingers in shells, don't grab kelp/seagrass for purchase). Other principles worth knowing: MAINTAIN RESPECTFUL DISTANCE (varies — sharks 2–3 m, mantas 3–5 m, dolphins follow their lead, never chase); NO FLASH PHOTOGRAPHY at close range with delicate species; DON'T BLOCK ESCAPE ROUTES; remove trash, don't introduce gear that could be lost on the reef.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 5.6 Review

questions.question: What are the three main groups of fishes?

options.a: Bony fishes, cartilaginous fishes, and orca
options.b: Bony fishes, cartilaginous fishes, and jawless fishes
options.c: Cartilaginous fishes, bony fishes, and carnivorous fishes

questions.explanation: Three classes of fish in modern taxonomy: (1) AGNATHA / CYCLOSTOMATA — JAWLESS fish: lampreys (parasitic, attach to host via sucker mouth, rasp blood/tissue) and hagfish (deep-sea scavengers; produce massive amounts of slime as defense). Most ancient fish lineage; ~120 surviving species; no paired fins, no jaws, cartilaginous skeleton, no true scales. (2) CHONDRICHTHYES — CARTILAGINOUS fish: sharks (~500 species), rays (manta, stingray, eagle, electric), skates, chimaeras (ratfish, ghost sharks). ~1200 species; cartilage skeleton (no bones), placoid scales (skin like sandpaper), 5–7 gill slits visible externally, internal fertilization. (3) OSTEICHTHYES — BONY fish: ~30,000 species, the largest vertebrate class. Bone skeleton, single gill cover (operculum), swim bladder for buoyancy. Two subclasses: RAY-FINNED (most living fish) + LOBE-FINNED (coelacanths, lungfish — ancestors of tetrapods). Includes everything from goldfish to tuna, all reef fish, all teleosts. (a) ORCA is a MAMMAL (cetacean) — common confusion because of 'killer WHALE' name; not a fish. (c) 'CARNIVOROUS FISH' is a FEEDING strategy (mssi-sod-205), not a taxonomic class — categories must be parallel (all feeding categories or all taxonomic categories, not mixed). (d) Wrong because (a) and (c) are wrong.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 5.6 Review

questions.question: What have marine birds developed for life in a marine environment?

options.a: Webbed feet, waterproofed feathers, adapted beaks, and salt glands
options.b: Waterproofed feathers, salt glands, and fins
options.c: Webbed feet, waterproofed feathers, and gills

questions.explanation: Marine birds evolved four key adaptations for saltwater life. (1) WEBBED FEET — skin between toes acts as a paddle for surface paddling (gulls, ducks) or underwater propulsion (penguins, cormorants). (2) WATERPROOFED FEATHERS — preening with oil from the UROPYGIAL / PREEN GLAND (at base of tail) coats feathers, sealing out water. Notable exception: CORMORANTS lack waterproofed feathers (you see them sun-drying with wings spread after fishing) — this lets them be less buoyant for deeper dives. (3) ADAPTED BEAKS — diet specialists: plunge-divers (gannets, terns) have sharp pointed beaks for spearing fish; filter feeders (flamingos, some petrels) have lamellae-filled beaks; crushers (oystercatchers) have strong beaks for shellfish; hook-tipped (gulls, albatrosses) have multi-purpose. (4) SALT GLANDS — supraorbital (above-eye) glands that secrete concentrated salt solution from drinking seawater + salty prey. Salt droplets visible at the bill tip in many seabirds (albatross, petrels, gulls). Penguins, marine iguanas, sea turtles all have analogous salt glands. Without these, marine birds would dehydrate from osmotic imbalance. Other adaptations: air-filled bones for flight (most species); counter-shading camouflage (dark above + light below); ability to drink seawater directly thanks to those salt glands. (b) Marine birds don't have FINS — penguins have FLIPPERS, which evolved from wings, not fins. (c) Birds breathe AIR with LUNGS, not gills. (d) Wrong because (a) is correct.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 5.6 Review

questions.question: What are the major groups of marine mammals?

options.b: Pinnipeds (seals, sea lions, and walruses) and sirenians (dugongs and manatees)

questions.explanation: All three groupings include real marine mammal lineages. ~130+ marine mammal species worldwide, all SECONDARILY AQUATIC (descended from terrestrial ancestors that returned to the sea at different times). (d) CETACEANS — whales, dolphins, porpoises: MYSTICETES (baleen whales — blue, humpback, fin, minke, gray, right; filter feeders, the largest); ODONTOCETES (toothed whales — orcas, sperm whales, dolphins, porpoises, beaked whales, belugas, narwhals; predators). Fully aquatic, never come ashore; descended from artiodactyl ancestors (closest living relatives are hippos). (b) PINNIPEDS ('fin-feet') + SIRENIANS: PINNIPEDS — true seals (Phocidae), eared seals + sea lions (Otariidae), walruses (Odobenidae); hauled-out breeders, fully aquatic foragers; descended from caniform carnivores (related to bears). SIRENIANS — manatees (3 species, Atlantic basin) + dugongs (1 species, Indo-Pacific); fully aquatic HERBIVORES grazing seagrass; descended from afrotherian ancestors (closest living relatives are elephants). (a) MUSTELIDS + URSIDAE — SEMI-AQUATIC groups: SEA OTTER (Enhydra lutris) is fully marine, marine otter (Lontra felina) coastal South America; densest fur of any mammal (substitutes for blubber); POLAR BEAR (Ursus maritimus) recognized as marine mammal because of dependence on sea ice for hunting seals — ecologically marine but not fully aquatic. So (c) all answers correct.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 5.6 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about vertebrates is true?

options.c: They have a complex nervous system

questions.explanation: All three describe defining vertebrate features. (d) BACKBONE — the namesake feature: a series of articulated VERTEBRAE forming the spinal column that protects the spinal cord. This is the single defining synapomorphy of subphylum VERTEBRATA. From hagfish (rudimentary vertebral elements) to blue whales (largest), all vertebrates share this body plan. (a) PROTECTIVE SKULL (cranium) — bony or cartilaginous structure encasing the brain. Allows large, complex brains to evolve safely. Distinguishes vertebrates from other chordates (lancelets, tunicates) which have notochord but no skull. (c) COMPLEX NERVOUS SYSTEM — centralized BRAIN + SPINAL CORD + peripheral nerves; bilaterally symmetric body plan; CEPHALIZATION (concentration of sensory organs and nervous tissue at the front end). Enables sophisticated behavior, learning, sensory processing — what makes vertebrates the dominant predators in most ecosystems. Other vertebrate features beyond this question: CLOSED CIRCULATORY SYSTEM with a chambered heart (2 chambers in fish, 3 in amphibians, 4 in birds/mammals); ENDOSKELETON of bone or cartilage (allows growth without molting); PHARYNGEAL SLITS / POUCHES (gills in aquatic; develop into ear/throat structures in terrestrial); NOTOCHORD during development (replaced by vertebral column in adults); POST-ANAL TAIL (varies in adult expression). Marine vertebrates: fish (jawless, cartilaginous, bony), reptiles (sea turtles, sea snakes, marine iguanas, saltwater crocodile), birds (penguins, seabirds), mammals (cetaceans, pinnipeds, sirenians, sea otters).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 5.6 Review

questions.question: What are the "ampullae of Lorenzini"?

options.c: Sensory organs that help Chondrichthyan fishes to hunt

questions.explanation: AMPULLAE OF LORENZINI are ELECTROSENSORY ORGANS — a network of jelly-filled pores around the heads of cartilaginous fish (sharks, rays, skates, chimaeras) — that detect BIOELECTRIC FIELDS generated by living organisms. ANATOMY + FUNCTION: visible as small black dots (pores) around shark snouts and ray heads; each pore connects via a jelly-filled canal to a sensory bulb (the AMPULLA proper) lined with electroreceptor cells; the jelly conducts electric fields from surrounding water to receptors. SENSITIVITY IS EXTRAORDINARY — sharks can detect electric fields as weak as 5 NANOVOLTS PER CENTIMETER (5×10⁻⁹ V/cm) — like feeling the heartbeat of a fish from a meter away. DETECT: BIOELECTRIC SIGNALS from prey muscle contractions, heartbeats, gill movements (allows hunting prey BURIED IN SAND, rays do this routinely); EARTH'S MAGNETIC FIELD for long-distance navigation in some species; TEMPERATURE gradients (1 millikelvin sensitivity). DISCOVERY: described by Stefano LORENZINI in 1678 but their function (electroreception) wasn't understood until the 1960s. WHY THIS MATTERS FOR DIVERS: sharks can detect you in zero visibility — your heart beats, your gills move, slight muscle tremors create detectable fields. Diver gear with metal/batteries (cameras with strobes, dive computers, regulator metals in seawater) can occasionally trigger curious investigation. Shark deterrents (Sharkbanz, Shark Shield) work by overwhelming ampullae with strong electric pulses, causing the shark to turn away. (a) Filter-feeding plates = baleen in whales or gill rakers in fish, totally different. (b) Stinging tentacles = cnidarian nematocysts (mssi-sod-198/199). (d) Nerve nets = simple decentralized nervous systems in cnidarians and a few other invertebrates; ampullae are highly specialized organs, not a nerve net.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 5.6 Review

questions.question: Gases with a fraction of oxygen greater than 0.21 are:
questions.explanation: Gas classifications by oxygen fraction (FO₂), benchmarked against atmospheric air (21% O₂). HYPOXIC: FO₂ < 0.21 — examples include trimix for deep dives (e.g. 10/70 = 10% O₂ + 70% He + 20% N₂); CANNOT be breathed safely on the surface (PpO₂ too low: 0.10 bar at 1 ATA causes blackout); travel gas / bottom gas only at depths where pressure raises PpO₂ above ~0.18 bar minimum. NORMOXIC: FO₂ ≈ 0.21 — air itself; 'normoxic trimix' (e.g. 21/35 = 21% O₂ + 35% He + 44% N₂, breathable at the surface). HYPEROXIC: FO₂ > 0.21 — NITROX blends (EAN32, EAN36, EAN40, EAN50, EAN80) and pure O₂ (100%). Etymology: HYPO- (Greek 'under') / NORMO- (normal) / HYPER- ('over') + -OXIC. WHY DISTINCTIONS MATTER: HYPOXIC mixes are unbreathable near surface, must be switched to deeper. HYPEROXIC mixes extend NDL by reducing inert-gas loading, but have a MAXIMUM OPERATING DEPTH (MOD) dictated by PpO₂ limit (1.4 bar working / 1.6 bar contingency only): EAN32 MOD ~33 m at 1.4; EAN36 ~28 m; EAN40 ~22 m; pure O₂ ~6 m. EXCEEDING MOD risks CNS OXYGEN TOXICITY — convulsions underwater = drowning, the ultimate non-recoverable error. Lesson 3.5 context: gas-mix nomenclature anchors the nitrox theory (mssi-sod-127..133), MOD calculations, and partial-pressure physics that govern technical and recreational nitrox diving. (b) NORMOXIC = 21%, same as air. (c) 'HighOx' isn't a recognized term. (d) HYPOXIC = LESS oxygen than air, opposite direction.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of central nervous system oxygen toxicity include:

options.d: Coughing and a burning sensation in the lungs

questions.explanation: CNS OXYGEN TOXICITY (the Paul Bert effect) is triggered when PpO₂ exceeds ~1.4–1.6 bar (working / contingency limits) for too long → CNS hyperexcitability → seizure. Classic mnemonic VENTID-C: V Vision changes (tunnel vision); E Ear changes (tinnitus); N Nausea; T TWITCHING (often facial / lips first — 'lip twitches'); I Irritability; D Dizziness; C CONVULSIONS (grand mal seizure — most dangerous, often hits without warning prodrome). The CONVULSION phase is the catastrophic stage: diver loses regulator → drowns. Recovery from convulsion at depth is rarely survivable without a buddy who can hold the regulator in place until the postictal phase passes (then ascend slowly). PREVENTION: respect MOD limits, log CNS clock % (cumulative percentage of CNS exposure based on PpO₂ × time), keep PpO₂ ≤ 1.4 bar working / ≤ 1.6 bar contingency only. Never breathe rich nitrox below its MOD even briefly. INDIVIDUAL VARIABILITY is huge — the same person can convulse one day at 1.3 bar and tolerate 1.6 bar another day; CO₂ retention, exertion, immersion, cold all lower threshold. (b) CHERRY-RED LIPS = sign of CO POISONING (carboxyhemoglobin's bright red color). (c) Numbness / tingling has many causes (DCS, hyperventilation, neuropraxia, hypoglycemia) — not specific to O₂ tox. (d) Coughing + burning lungs = PULMONARY O₂ TOXICITY (Lorrain Smith effect), different system, slow-onset over days of sustained high PpO₂; tracked by OTU (Oxygen Tolerance Units), affects technical / saturation divers more than recreational.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Histotoxic hypoxia occurs when:

options.a: Toxic substances cause problems with red blood cell function
options.b: Oxygenated blood cannot reach tissues and organs
options.c: The body cannot absorb enough oxygen from the air
options.d: Low hemoglobin levels stop oxygen from binding to red blood cells

questions.explanation: ⚠️ NEEDS VERIFICATION — mySSI grades (a) correct, but the strict medical/physiology definition of histotoxic hypoxia describes a different mechanism. The four classical hypoxia types: (1) HYPOXIC HYPOXIA — insufficient O₂ in inspired gas (altitude, drowning, hypoxic mix near surface). (2) ANEMIC HYPOXIA — insufficient or dysfunctional HEMOGLOBIN to carry O₂ (anemia, blood loss, CO POISONING where carbon monoxide binds Hb 250× more avidly than O₂). (3) STAGNANT / CIRCULATORY HYPOXIA — inadequate blood FLOW to tissues (shock, heart failure, ischemia). (4) HISTOTOXIC HYPOXIA — TISSUES / CELLS CAN'T USE the oxygen even though it's being delivered. Caused by cellular toxins like CYANIDE, hydrogen sulfide, sodium azide that block MITOCHONDRIAL ELECTRON TRANSPORT (specifically cytochrome c oxidase / Complex IV). Cell respiration halts despite normal PaO₂ and normal Hb. Etymology: HISTO- (tissue) + TOXIC = 'tissue-poisoning' — the toxin acts at the tissue/cellular level, NOT at the RBC level. mySSI's wording in option (a) — 'toxic substances cause problems with red blood cell function' — actually describes ANEMIC HYPOXIA more accurately (CO poisoning is the classic dive-relevant case affecting RBC function). The textbook histotoxic answer would be closer to 'tissues can't utilize delivered oxygen due to cellular poisoning (cyanide etc.)'. Going with mySSI's grading since that's what the exam tests, but be aware the medically precise definition differs. If you see this question in DAN or dive-medicine textbook context, expect the cellular-utilization-block answer instead. (b) STAGNANT hypoxia. (c) HYPOXIC hypoxia. (d) ANEMIC hypoxia.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: What condition is caused by increased exposures to elevated partial pressure of oxygen levels?
questions.explanation: OXYGEN TOXICITY — see mssi-sod-214 for full breakdown. Two forms: (1) CNS / CENTRAL OXYGEN TOXICITY (Paul Bert effect) — acute, triggered by PpO₂ exceeding ~1.4–1.6 bar; symptoms VENTID-C (vision, ear, nausea, twitching, irritability, dizziness, convulsions); convulsion underwater = drowning; this is the limiting factor that sets MOD for nitrox/trimix dives. (2) PULMONARY / WHOLE-BODY OXYGEN TOXICITY (Lorrain Smith effect) — slow-onset over days of sustained moderate-high PpO₂; symptoms cough, burning chest, reduced vital capacity; tracked by OTU (Oxygen Tolerance Units), affects technical / saturation / hyperbaric-medicine patients more than recreational. PREVENTION: respect MOD (max 1.4 bar working / 1.6 contingency), track CNS clock and OTU on multi-day dives, never breathe rich nitrox below its MOD. (b) NITROGEN NARCOSIS comes from elevated PpN₂, not O₂. (c) DECOMPRESSION ILLNESS is from off-gassing dissolved inert gas too fast on ascent (mostly N₂ bubbles), not from high PpO₂. (d) HYPERCAPNIA is CO₂ retention — separate gas-toxicity issue (mssi-sod-078).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: How does skip breathing possibly cause hypoxia?

options.a: It allows oxygen levels to drop and carbon dioxide levels to rise
options.c: It allows carbon dioxide levels to drop and oxygen levels to rise
options.d: It increases central nervous system oxygen toxicity

questions.explanation: SKIP BREATHING is a bad habit some divers develop to 'save gas' by inserting pauses between breaths or briefly holding breath between inhale and exhale. It DOESN'T save gas, and it's dangerous. PHYSIOLOGY DURING THE PAUSE: metabolism continues — cells consume O₂ and produce CO₂ at the normal rate; CO₂ ACCUMULATES in blood → hypercapnia → respiratory acidosis → HEADACHE, breathlessness, narcosis-like effects, eventual PANIC; O₂ LEVELS DROP in lungs and blood → mild hypoxia (worse at altitude or in deeper dives). The diver feels increasingly bad, then breathes HARDER when finally inhaling → SAC rate eventually goes UP, not down. THE RIGHT PATTERN: slow, deep, rhythmic breathing — long exhales (longer than inhales, e.g. 3 sec inhale, 5 sec exhale), full lung-volume use to recruit dead-space air, continuous flow, NEVER hold breath. Result: lower SAC AND no CO₂ buildup AND no panic. WHY THIS IS DANGEROUS BEYOND DISCOMFORT: (1) Hypercapnia POTENTIATES nitrogen narcosis at depth (can turn a tolerable 30 m dive into severe narcosis); (2) Elevated CO₂ predisposes to CNS oxygen toxicity (lowers seizure threshold); (3) Eventually leads to dyspnea / panic which can cascade into uncontrolled ascent. (b) Skip breathing doesn't reduce blood volume. (c) OPPOSITE — skip breathing RAISES CO₂ and DROPS O₂. (d) Skip breathing actually increases narcosis risk via hypercapnia, but doesn't directly cause CNS O₂ toxicity (though hypercapnia lowers tox threshold).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of hypoxia include:
questions.explanation: All three are real signs of HYPOXIA (low blood/tissue O₂). (a) CRAWLING SENSATION / PARESTHESIAS — 'pins and needles,' tingling on skin, especially extremities; from peripheral nerve dysfunction as O₂ availability drops. (b) CYANOSIS — bluish-purple discoloration of lips, fingertips, mucous membranes. Classic LATE sign — happens when deoxygenated hemoglobin >5 g/dL in capillaries. Visible cyanosis = significant hypoxia (SpO₂ typically <85% before noticeable). (c) NUMBNESS — loss of sensation in extremities; brain doesn't get enough O₂ to maintain normal nerve function. OTHER HYPOXIA SIGNS not in the question: HEADACHE (early); CONFUSION / IMPAIRED JUDGMENT (insidious — diver doesn't realize they're impaired); VISUAL DISTURBANCES (tunnel vision, dimming); EUPHORIA ('happy hypoxia') — particularly dangerous because diver feels FINE; TACHYPNEA + TACHYCARDIA (compensatory); LOSS OF CONSCIOUSNESS at severe stage; CARDIAC ARREST at extreme. DIVING RELEVANCE: SHALLOW-WATER BLACKOUT in freediving = hypoxia at end of long breath-hold ascent (PpO₂ falling as ambient pressure drops) = unconscious diver who looks calm. Why solo freediving = solo dying. CO POISONING from contaminated fill produces hypoxia by binding Hb. HYPOXIC MIX at surface (technical diving error) hits within seconds. The insidious 'happy hypoxia' makes hypoxia uniquely dangerous — by the time you notice something's wrong, judgment is already impaired and decisions become bad.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: A lack of oxygen in the body can cause:
questions.explanation: Severe hypoxia produces a cascade of failures, all listed. (b) ASPHYXIA AND DEATH — at the extreme. Brain irreversible damage at ~4–6 minutes of zero O₂. Cardiac muscle next. Drowning, severe CO poisoning, suffocation, hypoxic-mix surface incidents are diving-relevant pathways. (c) IMPAIRMENT OF METABOLIC FUNCTION — cells need O₂ for AEROBIC RESPIRATION (electron transport chain in mitochondria → ATP production, ~36 ATP per glucose). Without enough O₂, cells switch to ANAEROBIC GLYCOLYSIS → only ~2 ATP per glucose + lactic acid waste → metabolic acidosis → tissue dysfunction. Sustainable for minutes at most before cellular damage. (d) UNCONSCIOUSNESS — the BRAIN consumes ~20% of body's O₂ despite being only 2% of body mass; the most O₂-sensitive organ by far. Drops in PaO₂ produce loss of consciousness within seconds — 10–20 sec for sudden cessation, longer for gradual hypoxia. ORDER OF ORGAN FAILURE as O₂ deprivation worsens: (1) BRAIN first (consciousness loss in 10–20 sec of complete cessation; cellular damage at 4–6 min; permanent damage at 6–10 min — basis of CPR's '5 minute window'). (2) HEART — myocardial ischemia, arrhythmia, eventual asystole. (3) OTHER ORGANS (kidneys, liver) more tolerant, fail at longer durations. DIVING RELEVANCE: shallow-water blackout (freediving hypoxia on ascent), CO poisoning from contaminated fill, hypoxic mix at surface (technical diving error), drowning (water in lungs = no O₂ uptake) — all can produce this lethal cascade.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: How does hyperventilation possibly cause hypoxia?

options.b: It decreases blood carbon monoxide levels
options.c: It massively increases the amount of oxygen a diver inhales
options.d: It causes an imbalance between oxygen intake and carbon dioxide levels

questions.explanation: This is the classic FREEDIVING SHALLOW-WATER BLACKOUT mechanism. SEQUENCE: (1) Pre-dive HYPERVENTILATION — diver breathes deeply and rapidly hoping to 'load up oxygen' (a myth — blood Hb is already saturated at sea level, you can't pre-stuff extra). (2) EFFECT: CO₂ blown off → HYPOCAPNIA (low CO₂); O₂ levels barely change. (3) Diver descends, holds breath; metabolism continues. (4) As metabolism progresses, CO₂ builds back up AND O₂ drops. (5) Normally rising CO₂ triggers urgent need to breathe — but starting from artificially LOW CO₂, the breathing-urge trigger is DELAYED far past safe O₂ levels. (6) On ASCENT, ambient pressure drops, PpO₂ in lungs drops further → O₂ FALLS BELOW THE CONSCIOUSNESS THRESHOLD BEFORE THE BREATHING URGE HITS → SHALLOW-WATER BLACKOUT (often within meters of the surface). (7) Unconscious diver underwater drowns unless rescued by a trained buddy. The 'IMBALANCE BETWEEN O₂ INTAKE AND CO₂ LEVELS' (d) describes exactly this: the body's natural alarm system (CO₂-driven urge to breathe) is MUTED while O₂ actually drops dangerously. Why NEVER hyperventilate before freediving, ALWAYS dive with a trained buddy who can rescue. Modern freediving training explicitly forbids hyperventilation. (a) Hyperventilation doesn't reduce Hb levels. (b) Hyperventilation doesn't significantly affect CO levels. (c) You can't massively increase inspired O₂ by breathing harder — already saturated.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: The minimum partial pressure of oxygen required to sustain life is:
questions.explanation: The absolute physiological floor for sustaining consciousness / life is around 0.10 bar PpO₂ — below this, arterial O₂ saturation collapses rapidly and unconsciousness/death follow within seconds to minutes. PpO₂ BENCHMARKS FOR DIVERS: <0.10 bar = LETHAL HYPOXIA (immediate unconsciousness, this is the question's answer); 0.16 bar = minimum sustainable (significant impairment, tunnel vision, judgment compromised); 0.18 bar = PRACTICAL SAFE MINIMUM (technical diving standard for travel/bottom gas; below this you wouldn't deliberately design a breathable mix); 0.21 bar = sea-level air (normoxic baseline); 1.4 bar = recommended WORKING MAXIMUM (CNS-tox safety standard for nitrox/trimix); 1.6 bar = CONTINGENCY MAXIMUM (deco / emergency use only); >1.6 bar = escalating CNS oxygen-toxicity risk. TECH-DIVING DESIGN RULE: hypoxic mixes must be designed so PpO₂ ≥ 0.18 bar at the SHALLOWEST depth they'll be breathed — otherwise you have a 'blackout depth zone' you can't transit safely. The difference between 0.10 (the absolute survival floor) and 0.18 (the safe operational minimum) gives a margin so a brief regulator failure or accidental ascent doesn't drop you into unconsciousness territory immediately. (a) 1.40 bar is the working MAXIMUM for nitrox CNS safety, not a minimum. (c) 0.21 bar is normal sea-level air, not the minimum to survive. (d) 0.18 bar is the OPERATIONAL safe minimum (DIR / tech standard) but technically life can persist briefly down to ~0.10 bar.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Gases with a fraction of oxygen less than 0.18 are:
questions.explanation: HYPOXIC mixes have FO₂ < 0.18 by the strict tech-diving cutoff used in this Lesson 3.5. Note: SSI uses two different cutoffs in different contexts — mssi-sod-213 uses the looser '< 0.21' definition (anything below normal air), while this question uses the stricter '< 0.18' definition (the surface-unbreathable threshold). Both definitions appear in SSI texts. The 0.18 cutoff is the OPERATIONALLY meaningful one because that's where surface breathability fails: at 1 ATA, FO₂ < 0.18 means PpO₂ < 0.18 bar, below the practical safe minimum (mssi-sod-221). HYPOXIC MIX EXAMPLES: trimix 10/70 = 10% O₂ + 70% He + 20% N₂ (used for very deep dives, breathable only at depth where pressure raises PpO₂); trimix 12/65 = 12% O₂ used for extreme depths. THESE MIXES MUST BE SWITCHED ABOVE THEIR MINIMUM DEPTH (where PpO₂ ≥ 0.18 bar minimum) — typically with a TRAVEL GAS (often EAN32 or air) for the upper portion of the dive. Procedure error in switching back to hypoxic mix above its safe depth = unconsciousness within seconds. (a) HYPEROXIC = above 0.21 (nitrox, pure O₂). (c) NORMOXIC = ~0.21 (air, normoxic trimix). (d) ANOXIC = literally ZERO oxygen, more extreme than hypoxic; practically not used as a category in diving (you'd never breathe an anoxic mix).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Hypoxemic hypoxia occurs when:

options.a: The partial pressure of oxygen in arterial blood is too low
options.b: Low hemoglobin levels stop oxygen from binding to red blood cells
options.c: Oxygenated blood cannot reach tissues and organs
options.d: Toxic substances cause problems with red blood cell function

questions.explanation: ⚠️ NEEDS VERIFICATION — recording the medically correct answer (a). HYPOXEMIC HYPOXIA literally means 'low blood oxygen' (hypo + oxy + -emia = blood) and refers to LOW PaO₂ (partial pressure of O₂ in ARTERIAL BLOOD). This is option (a). CAUSES OF HYPOXEMIC HYPOXIA: low FO₂ in inspired gas (hypoxic mix breathed at surface, high altitude); V/Q mismatch in lungs (pulmonary disease); diffusion defects (pulmonary edema, fibrosis); hypoventilation (drug-induced, mechanical chest wall issue). Diving-relevant scenarios: hypoxic mix at surface, drowning (water in lungs blocks O₂ uptake), severe IPE. THE FOUR CLASSIC HYPOXIA TYPES MAP CLEANLY TO THE OPTIONS: HYPOXEMIC / HYPOXIC = (a) low PaO₂; ANEMIC = (b) Hb dysfunction (CO poisoning, anemia); STAGNANT / CIRCULATORY = (c) inadequate blood flow; HISTOTOXIC = (d) cellular toxin (cyanide blocks cytochrome oxidase) — though SSI's mssi-sod-215 mapped histotoxic to 'RBC function' which is actually anemic territory. CONSISTENT MEDICAL TAXONOMY: (a)=Hypoxemic, (b)=Anemic, (c)=Stagnant, (d)=Histotoxic. The user noted uncertainty about answering (b) — the textbook answer is clearly (a). If mySSI actually grades (b), this is mySSI's wording inconsistency between Q215 (histotoxic mapped to RBC) and the correct hypoxemic definition; flag as exam-quirk to memorize per mySSI versus dive-medicine standard.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Recreational divers conducting no-decompression dives must use breathing gases with a partial pressure of oxygen:
questions.explanation: Recreational no-decompression diving requires PpO₂ to stay in the safe operational window: 0.21 bar (atmospheric air, lower bound — recreational divers don't use hypoxic mixes) up to 1.4 bar (working CNS-tox safety limit, upper bound). Above 1.4 bar is CONTINGENCY-only territory (1.6 bar maximum reserved for emergency/decompression scenarios in technical diving) — never planned for recreational profiles. This range governs MOD CALCULATIONS for nitrox dives: MOD (m) = (PpO₂_max / FO₂ − 1) × 10. EAN32: MOD = (1.4 / 0.32 − 1) × 10 = 33.75 m; EAN36: MOD = 28.9 m; EAN40: MOD = 25 m; EAN50 (deco gas): MOD = 18 m; pure O₂: MOD = 4 m. Recreational divers diving on air stay safely under PpO₂ 1.4 because air's PpO₂ at 60 m would still only be 1.47 bar — but recreational depth limit is 40 m anyway, so air = max ~1.05 PpO₂. The 0.21–1.4 range is the practical envelope every recreational nitrox certified diver must stay within. (a) 0.18–0.21 is the hypoxic-to-air narrow zone, far too restrictive (this would forbid all nitrox). (b) 1.4–1.6 is contingency only, no time spent there electively. (d) 'Up to 1.6' allows contingency depth, not the recreational standard.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: At what partial pressure of oxygen can hypoxia occur?
questions.explanation: HYPOXIA symptoms typically begin at PpO₂ ≤ 0.16 bar — first signs are impaired judgment, tunnel vision, mild euphoria/confusion (the dangerous 'happy hypoxia' where the diver feels fine but cognition has degraded). Below this threshold, brain cells start running out of usable O₂ for normal metabolism. PpO₂ HYPOXIA SCALE: 0.21 bar = normal sea-level air (fully oxygenated, no hypoxia); 0.18 bar = OPERATIONAL SAFE MINIMUM (just above hypoxia threshold, used as design floor in tech diving); 0.16 bar = HYPOXIA ONSET — symptomatic; 0.10 bar = LETHAL floor (immediate unconsciousness, mssi-sod-221). The gap between 0.18 (safe minimum) and 0.16 (hypoxia onset) is the safety margin built into tech-diving gas planning. WHY 0.16 MATTERS in practice: hypoxic-mix surface incidents (technical diver accidentally breathes 10/70 at 1 ATA → PpO₂ = 0.10, immediate unconsciousness — but warning starts already at 0.16); altitude diving (effective FO₂ at altitude is reduced; high-altitude airfields can put divers at risk); freediving shallow-water blackout (ascent reduces PpO₂ as ambient pressure drops, can hit 0.16 just before surface). (a) 0.21 = normal air, no hypoxia. (c) 1.6 = HYPEROXIC range (CNS-tox risk), opposite direction entirely. (d) 0.18 = right at the safe-minimum threshold, just above hypoxia onset; SSI text uses 0.16 as the explicit hypoxia threshold.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: At what partial pressure of oxygen does central nervous system toxicity typically occur at?
questions.explanation: CNS OXYGEN TOXICITY (Paul Bert effect) typically manifests at PpO₂ EXCEEDING 1.6 bar — the contingency maximum that recreational and technical divers should never electively cross. RISK ZONES: 0.21–1.4 bar = recreational safe range; 1.4 bar = WORKING MAXIMUM (planned exposure stays at or below this for normal nitrox/trimix profiles); 1.4–1.6 bar = CONTINGENCY zone (emergency / decompression / brief deviation from plan, never planned bottom time); >1.6 bar = HIGH RISK, where CNS tox typically manifests acutely with VENTID-C symptoms (vision, ear, nausea, twitching, irritability, dizziness, CONVULSIONS — see mssi-sod-214). The 1.4-bar conservative working limit is set well below 1.6 because CNS-tox risk rises NON-LINEARLY with PpO₂ × time exposure, tracked as CNS CLOCK percentage (e.g., 1.6 bar = 100% CNS clock at 45 min exposure; 1.5 bar = 100% at 120 min; 1.4 bar = 100% at 150 min). INDIVIDUAL VARIABILITY is huge — the same diver may convulse at 1.3 bar one day and tolerate 1.6 bar another (CO₂ retention, exertion, immersion, cold all lower seizure threshold). RECREATIONAL PROFILES: rarely approach 1.4 (most recreational diving on air at 30 m gives PpO₂ ~0.84; even EAN40 at 25 m gives 1.4 — the planned MOD). TECHNICAL PROFILES: deco stops on 50% nitrox at 21 m = PpO₂ 1.55 bar (within 1.6 contingency); 100% O₂ at 6 m = 1.6 bar (decompression standard). (a) <0.18 = HYPOXIC range, opposite mechanism. (c) <1.20 = within safe operational range. (d) >0.21 = anything above air would include the entire safe range, far too inclusive.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.5 Review

questions.question: Hypercapnia may occur due to:
questions.explanation: HYPERCAPNIA = elevated CO₂ in arterial blood (PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg / 6 kPa). All three listed mechanisms are real causes. (b) HYPOVENTILATION — inadequate ventilation can't clear CO₂ as fast as cells produce it; happens consciously (slow breathing to 'save gas') or unconsciously when narcotized, cold, sedated, or fatigued. (c) SKIP-BREATHING — inserting breath-pauses between cycles lets CO₂ accumulate (the same misguided 'save gas' technique covered in mssi-sod-217 — backfires badly). (d) POOR EQUIPMENT MAINTENANCE — multiple pathways: INCREASED WORK OF BREATHING (WoB) in a poorly-serviced regulator → diver subconsciously hypoventilates to reduce effort → CO₂ builds up; WORN 2nd-STAGE EXHAUST CHECK VALVE → exhaled CO₂ leaks back during inhalation → re-breathing your own CO₂; DEAD-SPACE INCREASE from poorly fitting or oversized mouthpiece; on REBREATHERS the FAILED CO₂ SCRUBBER (sofnolime exhausted, channeling, water flooding) is the SILENT KILLER — caustic cocktail or runaway hypercapnia happens with no warning except subtle headache that the diver may dismiss until convulsion. Combined effects: all three pathways stack — a poorly maintained regulator + skip-breathing + heavy workload at depth produces severe hypercapnia very quickly.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: Hypercapnia can be prevented:

options.c: By breathing in a controlled manner

questions.explanation: All three are textbook preventive measures for hypercapnia. (a) PROPER EQUIPMENT MAINTENANCE — annual service of regulators (especially exhaust valves and 2nd-stage cracking effort calibration), clean filters; for rebreathers: scrubber pre-pack quality, sensor calibration, plumbing integrity, leak checks. A poorly-maintained regulator forces hypoventilation and traps CO₂. (b) MINIMIZE WORKLOAD AT DEPTH — exertion produces CO₂ proportional to metabolic rate; coupled with DENSER GAS at depth (higher gas density = higher mechanical work to move air through the regulator and into lungs), CO₂ can spike fast. Practical: rest after entry before descending fast, swim slowly, avoid fighting current (drift with it instead), avoid finning hard against surge, plan profiles so you're not exhausted at depth. (c) CONTROLLED BREATHING — slow, deep, rhythmic. Long exhales (longer than inhales — 3 sec in, 5 sec out) flush CO₂ effectively. NO skip-breathing, NO breath-holds, NO frantic shallow breaths during stress. The 'STOP — BREATHE — THINK — ACT' protocol explicitly emphasizes breathing because controlled breathing breaks both panic and CO₂-buildup cascades. WHY HYPERCAPNIA IS DANGEROUS BEYOND DISCOMFORT: (1) potentiates nitrogen narcosis (you get narced harder at any given depth); (2) lowers CNS oxygen-toxicity seizure threshold (so a normally safe PpO₂ becomes dangerous); (3) headache → confusion → impaired judgment → bad decisions cascade; (4) at extreme, convulsions and unconsciousness underwater = drowning.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: Scientists think nitrogen narcosis is caused by:
questions.explanation: The MEYER-OVERTON HYPOTHESIS (1900s) and modern refinements: DISSOLVED INERT GAS (mostly N₂) under pressure disrupts nerve-membrane function — likely by dissolving into the LIPID BILAYER of neurons and altering ion-channel kinetics, plus possibly binding to specific neuronal protein receptors (similar to general anesthetics like xenon, isoflurane, halothane). The NARCOTIC POTENCY of a gas correlates with its LIPID SOLUBILITY (the Meyer-Overton rule). Narcotic ranking from least to most: He < H₂ < Ne < N₂ < Ar < Kr < Xe (xenon is so narcotic it's used as a clinical anesthetic at 1 ATA). HELIUM is essentially non-narcotic at recreational depths — that's why HELIOX and TRIMIX exist for deep technical diving. DIVING RELEVANCE: onset around 30 m / 4 ATA for most divers (the 'MARTINI LAW' rule of thumb — each 10 m of depth on air ≈ one martini's worth of impairment). CO₂ RETENTION POTENTIATES narcosis (the multiplier effect — a hypercapnic diver gets narced harder at any given depth). Narcosis reverses immediately on ascent (no residual after the dive). Builds some tolerance with frequent deep diving (the 'deep adaptation' effect). (a) Oxygen toxicity is a SEPARATE gas-toxicity issue (Paul Bert effect at high PpO₂). (b) INCREASED CO₂ potentiates narcosis, not decreased — the option is exactly backwards. (d) Wrong because (a) and (b) are wrong.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: How can divers potentially reduce the risk of narcosis?
questions.explanation: All three help mitigate nitrogen narcosis risk. (a) CONTROLLED DESCENTS — slow descent allows time for inert gas to load gradually rather than dumping a sudden bolus into nerves; many divers report less acute narcosis impact with deliberately paced descents (especially below 30 m). Some agencies recommend stopping every 10 m on deeper rec descents to assess yourself. (c) PHYSICAL AND MENTAL FITNESS — fit divers retain less CO₂ during exertion (better cardiovascular efficiency, more efficient gas exchange), and CO₂ potentiates narcosis; mental fitness means task focus, calm response, recognizing impairment early, not letting narcosis cascade into poor judgment. (d) REDUCING EXERTION AND STRESS — both elevate CO₂ → CO₂ potentiates narcosis. Cold water ALSO worsens narcosis (hypothermia + N₂ loading interact). OTHER MITIGATION STRATEGIES not in this question: SWITCH TO TRIMIX below ~30 m (replace some N₂ with He → less narcotic load — standard tech procedure); AVOID ALCOHOL / SEDATIVES 24h pre-dive (additive CNS effects); HYDRATE WELL (dehydration thickens blood, slows perfusion, worsens gas dynamics); plan DEEP DIVES EARLY in the day (less fatigue); DEEP ADAPTATION via frequent deep diving within a training period reportedly raises tolerance.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: What is high-pressure nervous syndrome?

options.a: A psychotic disorder associated with carbon monoxide
options.c: A central nervous system disorder associated with deep heliox dives
options.d: A disorder that recreational divers get when they are stressed

questions.explanation: HIGH-PRESSURE NERVOUS SYNDROME (HPNS) is a CNS disorder occurring at EXTREME depths breathing HELIUM-BASED MIXES (heliox / heliox-rich trimix), typically below ~150 m / 16 ATA. SYMPTOMS develop progressively with depth and compression rate: TREMORS (hands, lips, eyelids — most characteristic, distinguishes HPNS from narcosis); DIZZINESS, NAUSEA; COGNITIVE SLOWING, MICRO-SLEEP EPISODES; PSYCHOMOTOR COORDINATION LOSS; EEG changes (theta waves, increased slow-wave activity); at extreme depths, CONVULSIONS. MECHANISM: poorly understood but theorized as a direct PRESSURE EFFECT on neuronal membrane proteins — compression physically deforms ion channels — distinct from gas-narcosis mechanism. Importantly, COMPRESSION RATE matters more than absolute depth — slow descents (taking days, 'saturation' profiles) reduce HPNS markedly. MITIGATION in commercial / saturation diving: SLOW COMPRESSION (saturation profiles take 24+ hours to reach 300+ m); ADD NITROGEN BACK ('trimix' with small N₂ %) — N₂'s narcotic effect counterbalances HPNS, the 'NARCOTIC COUNTERBALANCE' principle; HYDROGEN-HELIUM mixes (HYDRELIOX) for very deep — H₂ has narcotic effect too. FOR RECREATIONAL DIVERS: HPNS isn't a real concern — you'd need ~150 m+ on heliox to see it, far beyond rec/tec recreational limits (rec stops at 40 m, tec rec at 60 m, full tec trimix at 100 m, where HPNS hasn't fully manifested). Mentioned in SoD curriculum for completeness on inert-gas physiology. (a) CO psychosis is unrelated (CO causes hypoxia + tissue damage, not 'psychosis'). (b) DCS is bubble formation from off-gassing inert gas, totally separate mechanism. (d) HPNS is a real physical/CNS phenomenon at extreme depth, not stress-induced psychogenic.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: Severe hypercapnia can increase surface air consumption rates to around:
questions.explanation: Normal SAC (Surface Air Consumption) scales with workload and stress: RESTING (calm, neutral, slow swim) ≈ 10–15 L/min; LIGHT WORK (gentle current, photography) ≈ 15–25 L/min; MODERATE WORK (current, camera, navigation) ≈ 25–40 L/min; HEAVY EXERTION (fighting current, swim sprints) ≈ 40–60 L/min; SEVERE STRESS / PANIC / HYPERCAPNIA ≈ 100–150+ L/min. A panicked or hypercapnic diver can hit 150 L/min — that's 10–15× resting consumption. At 30 m (4 bar absolute), that's 600 L/min from the cylinder → an AL80 (~2,400 L) drained in 4 minutes. THE VICIOUS CYCLE: hypercapnia → dyspnea (air hunger) → panic-breathing → MORE CO₂ retained per breath (shallow rapid breathing has high dead-space ratio so most of the volume just shuttles in and out without alveolar exchange) → worse hypercapnia → faster gas burn → low-on-gas emergency → more panic. WHY THIS MATTERS for gas planning: gas plans MUST account for STRESS SAC, not just calm SAC. Rule of thirds, rock-bottom gas, minimum-gas calculations all factor an elevated 'emergency SAC' (typically 25–30 L/min, sometimes higher for tech divers in extreme scenarios). A diver running 15 L/min calmly can be in serious trouble if their SAC suddenly jumps to 150 L/min on a deep dive — without rock-bottom margin you simply can't make the surface. (a) 20 = light work, normal calm rate. (b) 200 = unrealistic, exceeds normal physiological respiratory minute volume even at maximum exertion. (c) 50 = heavy work, not severe hypercapnia.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: Carbon dioxide narcosis is:

options.a: Physical and mental disturbance caused by nitrogen narcosis
options.b: Physical and mental disturbance caused by hyperventilation
options.c: Physical and mental disturbance caused by hypercapnia
options.d: Caused by breathing in too much carbon dioxide

questions.explanation: CARBON DIOXIDE NARCOSIS = physical and mental disturbance caused by HYPERCAPNIA (elevated PaCO₂). Onset around PaCO₂ > 60 mmHg / 8 kPa (vs. normal ~40 mmHg). Symptoms parallel nitrogen narcosis: confusion, disorientation, impaired judgment, headache, lethargy → eventually loss of consciousness. Distinct from N₂ narcosis in mechanism (acidosis + direct CO₂ effects on neurons) but with similar end-state appearance — and the two COMPOUND: a hypercapnic diver gets N₂-narced harder than the depth alone would predict (the multiplier effect). DIVING SCENARIOS: skip-breathing, poor regulator maintenance with high WoB, dense gas at extreme depth (gas density above ~6 g/L makes adequate ventilation nearly impossible — narcosis-like impairment kicks in around 50–60 m on air just from gas density), rebreather scrubber failure (the silent killer in CCR diving — diver feels increasingly fuzzy without realizing why). Recovery: ascend to lower pressure / better gas exchange, controlled breathing, surface and recover. (a) Nitrogen narcosis is a SEPARATE gas-mechanism (Meyer-Overton, lipid solubility — see mssi-sod-229). (b) HYPERVENTILATION produces HYPOCAPNIA (LOW CO₂), the opposite — and that causes its own problems (respiratory alkalosis, tetany, shallow-water blackout in freediving) but isn't called 'narcosis'. (d) Loose phrasing — divers don't usually 'breathe in too much CO₂' (compressor air is filtered, scrubbed); the issue is the BODY RETAINING too much CO₂ from inadequate clearance.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: Increased blood carbon dioxide levels is known as:
questions.explanation: HYPERCAPNIA (also 'hypercarbia') = elevated CO₂ in blood (PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg / 6 kPa, normal ~40). ETYMOLOGY CHEAT SHEET for the distractors: prefixes — HYPER- (above normal) / HYPO- (below normal); roots — CAPN- (smoke / CO₂) / THERM- (heat) / VOL- (volume) / OXY- (oxygen) / GLYC- (sugar). So: HYPO-THERMIA = low body temp; HYPER-THERMIA = high body temp (overheating); HYPO-VOLEMIA = low blood volume (dehydration, hemorrhage); HYPER-VOLEMIA = excess blood volume; HYPER-CAPNIA = high CO₂ (this question); HYPO-CAPNIA = low CO₂ (from hyperventilation — the freediving danger that causes shallow-water blackout); HYPOXIA = low O₂; HYPEROXIA = high O₂; HYPOGLYCEMIA = low blood sugar. The pattern is consistent — once you learn the prefix/root system, every term is decodable. Diving-relevant set to know cold: hypoxia, hyperoxia, hypercapnia, hypocapnia, hypothermia, hyperthermia, hypovolemia.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: Excessive blood carbon dioxide levels:

options.a: Make the blood acidic and increase breathing rates
options.b: Make the blood acidic and decrease breathing rates
options.c: Have no effect on blood pH
options.d: Make the blood alkaline and increase breathing rates

questions.explanation: Two effects, both captured in option (a). (1) ACIDIC BLOOD (RESPIRATORY ACIDOSIS) — chemistry: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ (carbonic acid) ⇌ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻ (bicarbonate + hydrogen ion). More CO₂ → equilibrium shifts right → more H⁺ → lower pH → acidic. Normal arterial pH = 7.35–7.45; respiratory acidosis pulls it below 7.35. (2) INCREASED BREATHING RATE — body's response: CENTRAL CHEMORECEPTORS in the brainstem medulla detect rising H⁺ in cerebrospinal fluid (proxied via CO₂); PERIPHERAL CHEMORECEPTORS in carotid + aortic bodies detect rising arterial PaCO₂; both signal the medulla's respiratory center → INCREASE respiratory rate AND tidal volume → more alveolar ventilation → blow off CO₂ → restore pH. This is why hypercapnia gives the AIR-HUNGER / DYSPNEA sensation — the body is screaming at you to breathe harder. KEY PHYSIOLOGICAL FACT: respiratory drive is primarily CO₂-DRIVEN, not O₂-driven, in normal physiology. The body protects pH first, oxygenation second. This is why HYPERVENTILATING BEFORE FREEDIVING is dangerous (mssi-sod-220) — flushing out CO₂ removes the primary breathing trigger, letting O₂ drop dangerously before the diver feels the need to surface. WHEN THIS COMPENSATION FAILS (mechanical limit on ventilation, dense gas at depth, drug-suppressed breathing, deep narcosis): CO₂ keeps rising → CO₂ narcosis → unconsciousness. (b) Wrong — high CO₂ INCREASES breathing, not decreases. (c) Wrong — pH definitively changes. (d) Wrong — high CO₂ → ACIDIC, not alkaline (alkaline = hypocapnia from hyperventilation).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: As a diver descends, the narcotic effect of nitrogen becomes:
questions.explanation: HENRY'S LAW: at higher pressure, more gas dissolves into a liquid (proportional to partial pressure). Deeper diving = higher ambient pressure = higher PpN₂ = more dissolved N₂ in blood and tissues = stronger narcotic effect. QUANTITATIVE: at the surface, PpN₂ = 0.79 × 1 = 0.79 bar; at 30 m / 4 bar, PpN₂ = 0.79 × 4 = 3.16 bar (4× surface); at 60 m / 7 bar, PpN₂ = 5.53 bar (7× surface). So narcosis intensifies linearly-to-non-linearly with depth (perceived effect ramps up faster than the linear PpN₂ rise as cognitive thresholds get crossed). CLASSICAL 'MARTINI LAW' rule of thumb: 10 m ≈ 1 martini's worth of impairment, 20 m ≈ 2 martinis, 30 m ≈ 3 (noticeable for many), 40 m = max recreational ≈ 4 (significant for some), 50–60 m ≈ severely narced on air for most divers, 70 m+ = unsafe on air for ANYONE. WHY TECH DIVERS SWITCH TO TRIMIX: helium replaces some N₂. At 60 m on trimix 18/45 (18% O₂ + 45% He + 37% N₂), PpN₂ = 0.37 × 7 = 2.6 bar (≈ equivalent narcotic depth of 23 m on air). The 'EQUIVALENT NARCOTIC DEPTH (END)' calculation tells you what depth your trimix feels like — keeping END below 30 m is standard tech practice for cognitive function. (a) Wrong — only (c) is correct. (b) Less — opposite of physics, narcosis intensifies with depth. (d) No change — physically impossible per Henry's Law.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.6 Review

questions.question: What are physiological responses to increased body temperature?
questions.explanation: All three are textbook thermoregulation responses to HYPERTHERMIA (body too hot). (a) PERSPIRATION (sweating) — sweat glands secrete water onto skin → EVAPORATIVE COOLING (each gram of sweat that evaporates removes ~580 calories of heat). Most powerful body-cooling mechanism. Limited underwater because sweat doesn't evaporate efficiently in water (hence pre-dive overheating risk in wetsuit on hot deck — you produce sweat but it can't cool you, you just dehydrate). (b) METABOLIC HEAT REDUCTION — body downregulates metabolic rate slightly to reduce internal heat production (slows non-essential processes). Less prominent than active cooling responses but real. (c) BEHAVIORAL CHANGES — seek shade, remove layers (unzip wetsuit between dives, get out of direct sun, dive deeper to cooler thermocline water), drink water (hydration), reduce activity, change posture. Often the most EFFECTIVE thermoregulation because it changes the environment, not just the body. OTHER PHYSIOLOGICAL RESPONSES not listed: PERIPHERAL VASODILATION (blood routed to skin surface → radiative heat loss → flushed appearance); INCREASED HEART RATE (supports skin perfusion); TACHYPNEA (faster breathing loses heat via warm exhaled air). DIVING RELEVANCE: pre-dive hyperthermia in wetsuit on tropical boats causes dehydration before entering water → DCS risk increase. Cool down + hydrate between dives. Topside thermal management is part of dive planning in hot climates.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Which of the following statements about adipose (fatty) tissue is true?

options.a: It makes a diver more resistant to heat loss
options.b: It acts as a natural layer of insulation
options.d: It has detrimental effects on a diver's health

questions.explanation: All three describe real properties of body fat — adipose is a DOUBLE-EDGED trait for divers. (a) MORE RESISTANT TO HEAT LOSS — fat has lower thermal conductivity (~0.2 W/m·K) than muscle (~0.5 W/m·K), water (~0.6 W/m·K), or skin. A 1 cm subcutaneous fat layer insulates roughly 3× better than the same thickness of muscle. (b) NATURAL INSULATION LAYER — subcutaneous adipose acts as the body's own neoprene wetsuit. Larger/heavier divers with more subQ fat often tolerate cold water better in equivalent exposure suits than lean divers; free-divers, channel swimmers, and Antarctic explorers historically benefit from extra body fat for cold tolerance. (d) DETRIMENTAL HEALTH EFFECTS — the flip side: excess adipose carries real risks: DCS RISK FACTOR (mssi-sod-096) — adipose has ~5× the nitrogen solubility of lean tissue (N₂ is lipid-soluble) and is poorly perfused → loads slowly, unloads even slower → silent reservoir of dissolved gas through ascent; CARDIOVASCULAR RISK — obesity correlates with hypertension, atherosclerosis, increased cardiac work, all bad for diving stress; REDUCED FITNESS — lower aerobic capacity, faster CO₂ buildup at exertion, higher SAC, harder to manage current; BUOYANCY/WEIGHTING — positively-buoyant fat needs more lead to descend, increasing drag and gas consumption; PARADOXICAL HEAT RETENTION in warm water — fat that helps in cold can cause overheating in tropical conditions. So fat is a real insulator BUT also a metabolic and DCS liability — both sides true.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Why does hypothermia increase the risk of decompression sickness?

options.a: The dilation of blood vessels reduces circulation
options.b: The constriction of blood vessels increases blood flow
options.c: The dilation of blood vessels increases heat absorption
options.d: The constriction of blood vessels reduces circulation

questions.explanation: Cold causes PERIPHERAL VASOCONSTRICTION → reduced blood flow to extremities and skin → slower NITROGEN OFF-GASSING during ascent and surface interval → increased DCS risk. THE MECHANISM IN DETAIL: (1) DURING THE DIVE (cold) — peripheral vasoconstriction means less blood flow to fat-rich tissues (limbs, skin); those tissues load N₂ more slowly than they normally would (sounds protective). (2) DURING ASCENT AND SURFACE INTERVAL — still cold, still vasoconstricted → those same tissues now OFF-GAS slowly too → at the moment supersaturation peaks (right after surfacing), the dissolved N₂ in cold/poorly-perfused tissues isn't getting cleared. (3) POST-DIVE RE-WARMING (taking off wetsuit, hot shower, hot tub, hot deck) — peripheral VASODILATION suddenly opens up perfusion to those gas-loaded tissues → released N₂ floods into the blood when ambient pressure is already at minimum → BUBBLE NUCLEATION CASCADE. (4) CLASSIC CLINICAL PATTERN: DCS strikes 30 min – several hours post-surfacing during re-warming. THIS IS WHY post-dive hot showers, hot tubs, sauna are strongly discouraged within ~12–24 h after diving (especially deep/long profiles). Cold during the dive isn't always bad if you stay cold — but the warming TRANSITION is dangerous. Heated drysuits, hot-water suits in commercial diving avoid this by keeping perfusion stable throughout. (a) DILATION INCREASES local blood flow, doesn't reduce. (b) CONSTRICTION DECREASES flow, not increases. (c) Dilation increases heat LOSS to environment, not absorption.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: What actions can divers take to reduce the risks of hypothermia?

options.b: Plan their dive according to the conditions
options.c: Make sure they have appropriate training and experience
options.d: Use the right equipment and have appropriate surface supplies

questions.explanation: All three are SSI standard practices for cold-water dive safety. (b) PLAN DIVE ACCORDING TO CONDITIONS — match profile to thermal limits: cold water (<15 °C) → drysuit + thick undergarment; limit bottom time as water gets colder (cold + long dive = compounding risk); don't push depth where thermocline drops temperature 5+ °C without thermal contingency; plan surface-interval thermal recovery (warm drinks, dry layer between dives). (c) TRAINING AND EXPERIENCE — drysuit certification, ice diving specialty, cold-water specialty courses; experience with cold-water gear management (frozen regulator handling, gloved-hand drills, drysuit valve operation); recognizing early hypothermia signs in self AND buddy (early signs: shivering, slowed thinking, clumsy hands; later signs paradoxically include shivering STOPPING, which is the dangerous transition to severe hypothermia). (d) RIGHT EQUIPMENT + SURFACE SUPPLIES — IN-WATER: appropriate exposure suit (drysuit + thick undergarment for cold; hood + gloves + booties always for cold), heated vest (Santi BZ-400, Thermalution) for extreme cold, ARGON for drysuit insulation (~30% better than air); TOPSIDE: dry change of clothes, towel, hot drinks (tea, soup), wind shelter, warming blankets, dry bag for wet gear. OTHER PRACTICES: dive in EARLY SHIFT before fatigue, eat carb-rich food before diving (fuel for shivering metabolism), avoid alcohol pre-dive (impairs thermoregulation).
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: What actions can divers take to reduce the risks of hyperthermia?

options.b: Don wetsuits just before the dive

questions.explanation: All three address overheating, particularly in tropical / sun-exposed dive operations. (a) DRINK PLENTY OF FLUIDS — hyperthermia + sweating without evaporation (you're in a wetsuit, sweat can't cool you) = rapid dehydration; dehydration also separately raises DCS risk. Hydrate continuously: water + electrolyte drinks; avoid heavy caffeine and alcohol (diuretics). Practical target: pale yellow urine pre-dive. (b) DON WETSUITS JUST BEFORE THE DIVE — don't suit up 30 min in advance on a hot deck. Wetsuits TRAP heat outside water (especially black neoprene in direct sun → can hit 50 °C+ surface temp). Carry the suit to the entry point and put it on at the last moment, or sit in shade with suit half-on. (d) MINIMIZE EFFORT AND EXERTION topside in heat — let crew load gear, avoid long walks in full kit on tropical sand, avoid carrying heavy doubles to entry, on liveaboards stay in shaded areas between dives. OTHER MEASURES not in this question: GET IN THE WATER for cooling between dives (skin sheds heat to surrounding cooler water); WET DOWN THE WETSUIT with cool water before the dive (pre-cool the diver); SHADE STRUCTURES on dive boats (canopies, umbrellas, awnings); LIGHT-COLORED exposure suit if available (less solar absorption); COOL drinks (hydration + body cooling combined). The cumulative effect: pre-dive hyperthermia → dehydration + elevated metabolic state → significantly higher post-dive DCS risk in tropical waters, sometimes counter-intuitive to the warm-water 'easy diving' assumption.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of mild hypothermia can include:

options.a: Uncontrollable shivering, slurred speech, and unconsciousness
options.b: Shivering, decreased heart rate, and hallucinations
options.c: Shivering, increased heart rate, and a core temperature of less than 28°C
options.d: Shivering, increased heart rate, and loss of dexterity

questions.explanation: Hypothermia is staged by CORE BODY TEMPERATURE with distinct symptom patterns. MILD (32–35 °C / 90–95 °F): SHIVERING, INCREASED HR, increased breathing, LOSS OF DEXTERITY, mild confusion, the 'UMBLES' (mumbles, fumbles, stumbles) — body is actively defending core temperature. MODERATE (28–32 °C / 82–90 °F): violent shivering → then SHIVERING STOPS (paradoxical, dangerous transition), slurred speech, slowed reflexes, confusion, uncoordinated movement, possible hallucinations. SEVERE (<28 °C / <82 °F): UNCONSCIOUSNESS, weak/slow pulse, low BP, severe cardiac arrhythmia risk, eventual cardiac arrest — looks dead but can sometimes be revived ('not dead until warm and dead' — cold preserves brain function during arrest). MILD HYPOTHERMIA underwater: increased HR + cardiac output to maintain perfusion against vasoconstriction; shivering generates ~5× resting metabolic rate of heat; LOSS OF FINE MOTOR CONTROL — extremities cool fastest, hands fumble first (hard to fasten BCD straps, struggle with masks, fumble drysuit dump valve). UNDERWATER WARNING SIGNS: buddy hands moving stiffly / dropping things, slow response to signals, misreading dive computer/SPG, shortened attention span, skin cyanosis (blue lips, white extremities). RESPONSE TO ONSET: end dive immediately, surface controlled, dry off + REWARM PASSIVELY (insulating layers, warm shelter, hot drinks if alert). NEVER hot-shower or vigorous-activity rewarming — peripheral vasodilation pulls cold blood from extremities to core, can drop core temp further (AFTERDROP) and trigger arrhythmia. (a) Slurred speech + unconsciousness = MODERATE-TO-SEVERE, not mild. (b) DECREASED HR is wrong direction for mild — HR rises in mild then falls in severe. (c) Core temp <28 °C is SEVERE, not mild — at that temp the diver is unconscious, not just losing dexterity.
questions.source: mySSI: SoD Lesson 3.7 Review

questions.question: Assistant Instructors may teach which programs?

options.b: Try Scuba and Scuba Skills Update
options.c: Open Water Diver and Basic Diver

questions.explanation: AI may teach Try Scuba and Scuba Skills Update.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: The Instructor Evaluation must be conducted within ______ months of the completion of the Instructor Training Course.
questions.explanation: IE within 6 months of ITC completion.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: Regarding supervision, what may an active status Assistant Instructor do?

options.a: Indirectly supervise and evaluate surface skills during open water training dives for the Open Water Diver program under the direct supervision of an active status Open Water Instructor
options.b: Directly supervise and evaluate surface skills during open water training dives for the Open Water Diver program under the indirect supervision of an active status Open Water Instructor
options.c: Directly supervise and evaluate all skills during open water training dives for the Open Water Diver program under the indirect supervision of an active status Open Water Instructor
options.d: Directly supervise all skills during pool/confined water sessions for the Open Water Diver program

questions.explanation: AI directly supervises and evaluates surface skills on OW dives, under indirect OWI supervision.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: A candidate must hold his _____ certification for at least ____ months prior to start the Instructor Training Course
questions.explanation: Hold DM for 6 months before starting ITC.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following is a prerequisite for registering for the Instructor Training Course?

options.d: Navigation, Deep Diving and Night & Limited Visibility

questions.explanation: Specialty trio (Nav + Deep + Night/Limited Vis) is required for ITC.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: Candidate(s) for an Instructor Evaluation must be registered in MySSI at least _____ weeks prior to the scheduled Instructor Evaluation start date.
questions.explanation: MySSI registration at least 6 weeks before IE start.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the in-water ratio for the Assistant Instructor Training Course?
questions.explanation: AITC has no fixed in-water ratio.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: What specialty instructor certifications can an Assistant Instructor qualify for?

options.c: Perfect Buoyancy and Advanced Wreck Diving

questions.explanation: AI qualifies for Boat Diving and Navigation specialty instructor.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: To become a Divemaster, the candidate must complete the requirements for maintaining active status:

options.a: With the responsible SSI Training Center
options.b: With the responsible SSI Service Center
options.d: With SSI as outlined in the General Training Standards

questions.explanation: Per General Training Standards.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: A Divemaster can:

options.a: Qualify as a Specialty Instructor for all Diving Knowledge specialty programs
options.b: Take up to three (3) students at a time on excursion dives
options.d: Enroll in an SSI Instructor Training Course

questions.explanation: DM can enroll in ITC.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: How can an Assistant Instructor qualify to teach the SSI Explorer program?

options.a: This certification is given automatically with the Assistant Instructor certification
options.b: Completing the SSI Explorer Instructor Online Upgrade
options.d: This certification is given automatically with the Divemaster certification

questions.explanation: Online Upgrade is the path.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: What are the requirements for completing the Instructor Training Course?

options.a: Read and complete all sections, including reviews and assessments, from the Instructor Training Course digital learning
options.c: Complete all academic sessions, requirements and evaluations as outlined in the instructor manual for the Instructor Training Course
options.d: Complete and pass the Instructor Training Course final exam

questions.explanation: All three required.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: To qualify as a Master Instructor the candidate may apply completed Try Scuba, programs toward the minimum requirements for Open Water Diver certifications.

options.a: Six (6) Try Scuba recognitions are equivalent to one (1) Open Water Diver certification
options.b: Two (2) Try Scuba recognitions are equivalent to one (1) Open Water Diver certification
options.c: Twelve (12) Try Scuba recognitions are equivalent to one (1) Open Water Diver certification
options.d: Five (5) Basic Diver certifications are equivalent to one (1) Open Water Diver certification

questions.explanation: 12 Try Scuba = 1 OW for MI minimums.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the minimum instructor rating to conduct the Assistant Instructor Training Course?
questions.explanation: AIT (Assistant Instructor Trainer) is the minimum.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: A Divemaster is a:

options.a: Professional who may teach Diving Knowledge specialty programs
options.b: Professional who can teach entry-level students

questions.explanation: DM is a certified assistant role.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: The Divemaster card is a(an):
questions.explanation: DM card is the entry-level Pro certification card.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: How many logged open water dives are required to start the Instructor Training Course?
questions.explanation: 60 dives + 40 hours.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following is a prerequisite for registering for the Instructor Training Course?

options.b: Specialty Instructor in Navigation, Deep Diving, and Night & Limited Visibility

questions.explanation: DM is the prerequisite.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: A Divemaster candidate must have _____ logged dives prior to certification.
questions.explanation: DM = 60 logged dives.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: The Candidate Water Fitness Evaluation is valid for:
questions.explanation: Validity = 6 months.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: The Divemaster Instructor card is a(an):
questions.explanation: DM Instructor = Pro certification.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: Before beginning training, how can professionals from other training agencies earn an equivalent SSI Professional certification?

options.a: Complete an SSI training course from the beginning, regardless of prior certification
options.c: Verify certification equivalency, register for the certification, purchase required materials, and upload necessary documents to MySSI
options.d: Purchase the required materials without registering or uploading documents

questions.explanation: Verify equivalency, register, purchase, upload to MySSI.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the minimum depth for open water training during the Instructor Training Course?
questions.explanation: ITC OW training min depth = 5 m.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the minimum number of certifications required from any recreational scuba program to qualify as a Master Instructor?
questions.explanation: MI minimum = 150 recreational certs.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: Who must directly supervise all academic sessions and in-water activities during the Assistant Instructor Training Course?

options.b: An active status Instructor Trainer or higher
options.c: An active status Assistant Instructor Trainer
options.d: An active status Assistant Instructor Trainer can indirectly supervise all academic sessions, but must direct supervise all in-water activities

questions.explanation: Active AIT directly supervises AITC academics + in-water.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: An active status Assistant Instructor Trainer may teach, supervise and issue certifications for the following programs:

options.d: Specialty Instructor Seminar (if they have the applicable Specialty Instructor certification)

questions.explanation: AIT can teach ITC, AIT seminars, and Specialty Instructor Seminars (with applicable cert).
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: What parts of the Instructor Training Course must be completed by Assistant Instructor candidates?

options.a: Academic Sections 1-5, the final exam, and Practical Applications 1-6
options.b: Academic Sections 1-7, the final exam, and Practical Applications 1-6
options.c: Academic Sections 1-5, the final exam, and Practical Applications 1-5
options.d: Academic Sections 1-6, the final exam, and Practical Applications 1-4

questions.explanation: Sections 1-6 + final exam + PA 1-4.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: Is the SSI Divemaster certification ISO-certified?
questions.explanation: DM is ISO-certified.
questions.source: Personal: AIT Exam Form B

questions.question: Dives Guides can:

options.a: Act as a dive instructor for certified divers
options.b: Lead certified divers, like their friends and family
options.c: Issue certifications for SSI Scuba Diver programs

questions.explanation: Dive Guides are qualified to lead certified divers (e.g., friends and family) on dives, but cannot teach or issue certifications.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the first part of the Responsible Diver Code?

options.a: Evaluate the conditions before every dive, and make sure they fit my personal capabilities
options.b: Accept responsibility for my personal well-being on every dive
options.c: Be environmentally conscious on every dive
options.d: Dive within the limits of my ability and training

questions.explanation: The Responsible Diver Code begins with diving within the limits of one's ability and training.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What should you do before visiting a well-known dive site?

options.a: Only ask the boat captain what to expect
options.b: Nothing if you know the site well
options.c: Ask your divers what to expect at the site
options.d: Review any available information on the site

questions.explanation: Always review any available information about the site beforehand, even if it is well-known, because conditions change.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Visibility can be affected by:
questions.explanation: Visibility is influenced by weather, currents/tides, and other divers stirring sediment.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Weather conditions:

options.a: Always remain constant throughout the day
options.b: May change quickly and with little warning
options.c: Only change quickly in the tropical ocean regions

questions.explanation: Weather can change rapidly with little warning, which is why ongoing monitoring is part of the Dive Guide's responsibility.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Divemasters are responsible for:
questions.explanation: The Divemaster's core responsibility is the diver's well-being and safety; logbook updates and equipment assembly remain the diver's own responsibility.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What are the three components to establishing group control?

options.a: Be an authority figure, be prepared, and choose an appropriate group size
options.b: Be strict, uncompromising, and choose an appropriate group size
options.c: Be prepared, uncompromising, and let the divers determine the group size
options.d: Be an authority figure, be prepared, let the group decide what is best for themselves

questions.explanation: Group control rests on being an authority figure, being prepared, and choosing an appropriate group size.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What is a check dive primarily conducted for?
questions.explanation: A check dive is used to assess the diver's skills and comfort before more challenging dives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following dive parameters can only be determined at the dive site?
questions.explanation: Local weather and visibility can only be assessed accurately at the dive site itself on the day of the dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: If you or your divers are doing repetitive dives, you should:

options.a: Add an extra margin of conservatism for safety
options.b: Dive to the no-decompression limit on each dive
options.c: Plan decompression stops using your computer so you can continue diving
options.d: Schedule a rest day at least one every two weeks

questions.explanation: Repetitive diving accumulates nitrogen, so adding conservatism (shallower depths, shorter times, longer surface intervals) is the standard practice.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: What equipment should be included on every dive?

options.a: First aid kit, save-a-dive kit, and backup Total Diving System
options.b: First aid kit, emergency action plan, save-a-dive kit
options.c: Emergency action plan, save-a-dive kit, and personal lights
options.d: Save-a-dive kit, backup Total Diving System, and personal lights

questions.explanation: A first aid kit, emergency action plan, and save-a-dive kit should accompany every dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Safety cylinders are used:

options.a: Only at the end of deep or wreck dives
options.b: Only in low-on-air situations or out-of-air emergencies
options.d: Only during difficult or high-current dives

questions.explanation: Safety cylinders are reserved exclusively for low-on-air or out-of-air emergencies.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: The purpose of a dive briefing is to:

options.a: Share information about the dive with your divers
options.b: Show your divers how experienced and capable you are
options.c: Ask your divers questions about their expectations

questions.explanation: The dive briefing shares essential information about the dive with the divers.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: During the dive briefing, you should:

options.a: Describe maximum and minimum depths only
options.b: Describe maximum depth limits and maximum dive time
options.c: Let the divers determine their maximum depths
options.d: Let the divers determine their depth limits and dive time

questions.explanation: The Dive Guide sets the maximum depth and maximum dive time during the briefing.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: To avoid overheating or fatigue, your diver should:

options.a: Put their equipment on at the same time
options.b: Only put their wetsuit on halfway
options.c: Leave their wetsuit unzipped during the briefing

questions.explanation: All listed practices help reduce overheating and fatigue before the dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: Why should you conduct a pre-dive check?

options.a: It will prevent you from entering the water with faulty equipment
options.c: It ensures your cylinder valve is properly opened
options.d: It ensures your buddy understands your equipment configuration

questions.explanation: Pre-dive checks catch faulty equipment, confirm valves are open, and familiarize buddies with each other's gear.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Dive Guide Exam Form A

questions.question: If a diver has an equipment failure:

options.a: Do whatever is necessary to get them in the water
options.b: Give them your equipment, and use theirs during the dive
