questions.explanation: Each Marine Protected Area has its own regulations designed to protect that ecosystem. Divers must learn and respect those local rules whenever they dive there.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: A dive computer keeps track of a diver's nitrogen absorption levels so that the diver can:

options.d: Stay within the no-decompression limits on each dive

questions.explanation: Dive computers continuously calculate tissue nitrogen loading and display remaining no-decompression time, helping the diver stay within NDL on every dive.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Dive computers are essential for planning and executing dives because they:

options.d: Help divers stay within the no-decompression limits

questions.explanation: Dive computers log dive data, monitor ascent/descent rates, and track NDL in real time — all listed functions are valid reasons they are essential tools.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: It is recommended to have your delivery system serviced:
questions.explanation: Annual service of the delivery system (regulator) is the standard recommendation to keep moving parts and seals in safe working order.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Divers can help protect the marine environment by:

options.a: Maintaining neutral buoyancy at all times
options.c: Keep equipment secured and no dangling

questions.explanation: Neutral buoyancy, secured/streamlined equipment, and a responsible diver attitude all reduce environmental impact — every listed action helps protect the marine environment.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Salt water weighs ___ kg per liter and freshwater weighs ___ kg per liter.
questions.explanation: Salt water weighs about 1.025 kg per liter and fresh water about 1.0 kg per liter. The dissolved salts make seawater denser, providing more buoyancy.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Coral reefs are formed by:

options.a: Limestone deposits that build up over millions of years
options.b: Coral animals called polyps that form skeletal structures
options.c: A group of animals known as the echinoderms
options.d: Christmas tree tubeworms that form a limestone base for coral to grow

questions.explanation: Coral reefs are built by colonial animals called polyps, which secrete calcium-carbonate skeletons that accumulate over generations to form reef structures.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Ear squeeze can be prevented by:

options.d: Rotating the jaw, swallowing or Valsalva

questions.explanation: Relaxation, avoiding diving with a cold, and using equalization techniques (jaw movement, swallowing, Valsalva) all help prevent ear squeeze — all answers apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: To achieve the SSI Advanced Specialty Diver recognition card, a diver is required to:

options.a: Log an additional 5 dives beyond Open Water Certification under the supervision of an SSI Dive Professional
options.b: Complete 4 specialty programs and log a total of 24 dives

questions.explanation: The SSI Advanced Open Water Diver / Advanced Specialty Diver recognition requires completing 4 specialty programs and logging a total of 24 dives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The most important rule when assisting a panicked diver is:

options.a: Do whatever it takes to help the diver survive
options.d: Always talk in a calm voice

questions.explanation: Rescuer safety comes first — never needlessly endanger yourself. A panicked diver can pull a rescuer down, turning one casualty into two.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The Basic Rules of Scuba stated in the proper order of importance are:

options.a: Breathe continuously, Use compressed air only, and the 3-R Rule
options.b: Maintain neutral buoyancy, breathe continuously, and ascend slowly
options.c: Breathe continuously, ascend slowly and maintain control, and never dive alone or beyond your level of training

questions.explanation: The SSI Basic Rules of Scuba, in order: 1) breathe continuously, 2) ascend slowly and maintain control, 3) never dive alone or beyond your level of training.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The term repetitive dive is defined as:

options.a: A second or subsequent dive at the same location
options.b: Any dive started less than 10 minutes after a previous scuba dive
options.c: Any dive started more than 10 minutes after a previous scuba dive
options.d: Any dive started more than 10 minutes and less than 12 hours after a previous scuba dive

questions.explanation: A repetitive dive is one started more than 10 minutes but less than 12 hours after a previous scuba dive — the period during which residual nitrogen still affects planning.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Water comprises about ___ of the Earth's surface.
questions.explanation: Water covers approximately 71% of the Earth's surface, with the oceans accounting for the vast majority.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The safest way to execute your dive is to:

options.b: Plan your dive and dive your plan
options.d: Dive with a buddy that has a computer

questions.explanation: "Plan your dive and dive your plan" is the cornerstone of safe diving: a thorough plan agreed with your buddy, executed as planned, prevents most incidents.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The condition in which certain colors are diminished as depth increases is called:
questions.explanation: Absorption is the progressive filtering of light wavelengths by water — reds disappear first, then oranges and yellows, leaving blue-green tones at depth.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following would be inappropriate treatment for a diver evidencing symptoms of Decompression Sickness?
questions.explanation: In-water recompression is dangerous and not appropriate first aid for DCS. Correct treatment is 100% oxygen, treating for shock, supporting vitals, and rapid transport to a hyperbaric chamber.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The absolute pressure at 30 meters is:
questions.explanation: Every 10 meters of seawater adds 1 bar of pressure. At 30 meters, water pressure is 3 bar plus 1 bar atmospheric = 4 bar absolute.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form A

questions.question: The term No-decompression Limit is defined as:

options.a: The bottom time recorded during a dive
options.b: The maximum allowed time at depth without having to perform a mandatory decompression stop during the ascent
options.c: The maximum depth recorded during the dive
options.d: The maximum surface interval allowed during a day's diving

questions.explanation: The No-decompression Limit (NDL) is the maximum bottom time at a given depth that allows the diver to ascend directly to the surface without a mandatory decompression stop.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: A scuba cylinder for all recreational diving should be filled with:

options.a: A mixture of helium and oxygen
options.b: A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen
options.d: Pure, filtered compressed air or Nitrox

questions.explanation: Recreational scuba cylinders are filled with pure, filtered compressed air or Enriched Air Nitrox. Pure oxygen and other gas mixtures require specialty training.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: In an out-of-air emergency, if your buddy is close enough, you should:

options.d: Swim to your buddy, give the out-of-air signal, and share air

questions.explanation: If a buddy is close enough, the preferred response to an out-of-air emergency is to swim to the buddy, signal out-of-air, and share air via the alternate air source.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Absolute pressure is defined as:

options.a: The pressure at the surface plus 1 bar
options.b: The total pressure exerted on an object
options.c: The weight of the water that surrounds a diver

questions.explanation: Absolute pressure is the total pressure exerted on an object — atmospheric (surface) pressure plus the pressure from the water column above the diver.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Divers can help protect the marine environment by:

options.a: Maintaining neutral buoyancy at all times
options.c: Keep equipment secured and no dangling

questions.explanation: Protecting the marine environment requires neutral buoyancy, secured gear (no dangling hoses or gauges), and overall responsible diving behavior — all are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: An object immersed in water will be buoyed upward by a force ______ the weight of the water it displaces.
questions.explanation: Archimedes' Principle: an object immersed in a fluid is buoyed upward by a force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Overexpansion injuries can be prevented by:

options.b: Never holding your breath while diving
options.c: Learning the correct breathing patterns reinforced in classroom, pool, and open water training
options.d: Dive with a properly maintained high-quality Total Diving System

questions.explanation: Lung overexpansion injuries are prevented by never holding your breath, using correct breathing patterns from training, and diving with well-maintained equipment — all are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Dive computers are essential for planning and executing dives because they:

options.d: Help divers stay within the no-decompression limits

questions.explanation: Dive computers record dive data, monitor ascent and descent rates, and track no-decompression limits — all functions make them essential for planning and executing dives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Diving at altitude or flying after diving:

options.a: Does not require any special considerations for the diver
options.b: Allows the diver to spend a greater amount of time at depth without exceeding the no-decompression limits
options.c: Requires special diving computer functions, altitude tables and/or refraining from flying from at least 24 hours after the completion of a dive
options.d: Allows the diver to ignore the no-decompression limits

questions.explanation: Diving at altitude or flying after diving requires altitude-capable computers, altitude tables, and waiting at least 24 hours after diving before flying to avoid decompression sickness.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The greatest relative pressure change in sea water takes place between ____ and ____ meters.
questions.explanation: The greatest relative pressure change occurs between 0 and 10 meters, where pressure doubles from 1 bar to 2 bar. This is why proper equalization is critical near the surface.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: To achieve the SSI Advanced Specialty Diver recognition card, a diver is required to:

options.a: Log an additional 5 dives beyond Open Water Certification under the supervision of an SSI Dive Professional
options.b: Complete 4 specialty programs and log a total of 24 dives

questions.explanation: The SSI Advanced Specialty Diver recognition card requires completion of 4 specialty programs and a total of 24 logged dives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The Basic Rules of Scuba stated in the proper order of importance are:

options.a: Breathe continuously, Use compressed air only, and the 3-R Rule
options.b: Breathe continuously, ascend slowly and maintain control, and never dive alone or beyond your level of training
options.c: Maintain neutral buoyancy, breathe continuously, and ascend slowly

questions.explanation: The SSI Basic Rules of Scuba in order of importance are: breathe continuously, ascend slowly and maintain control, and never dive alone or beyond your level of training.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Residual nitrogen is defined as:

options.a: The amount of excessive nitrogen dissolved in our blood stream and tissues after a dive
options.b: The amount of excessive nitrogen dissolved in our blood stream and tissues at the beginning of the initial ascent to the surface
options.d: The amount of nitrogen dissolved in our blood stream and tissues at the beginning of the first dive of the day

questions.explanation: Residual nitrogen is the excess nitrogen still dissolved in a diver's blood and tissues after surfacing from a dive — it must be considered when planning repetitive dives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Sinus squeeze can be prevented by:

options.a: Tilting the divers head back and forth

questions.explanation: Sinus squeeze is most reliably prevented by not diving with a cold or congestion, since blocked sinus passages cannot equalize with ambient pressure.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Coral reefs are formed by:

options.a: Limestone deposits that build up over millions of years
options.b: A group of animals known as the echinoderms
options.c: Christmas tree tubeworms that form a limestone base for coral to grow
options.d: Coral animals called polyps that form skeletal structures

questions.explanation: Coral reefs are built by tiny coral animals (polyps) that secrete calcium carbonate skeletal structures, which accumulate over time to form the reef.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The proper first aid for Arterial Gas embolism, Subcutaneous emphysema, Pneumothorax, and Mediastinal Emphysema is:

options.c: Watch Vital signs, treat for shock and stand by to administer cardio-pulmonary resuscitation (if qualified)
options.d: Seek proper medical aid (a recompression chamber) as quickly as possible

questions.explanation: First aid for lung overexpansion injuries combines oxygen administration, monitoring vitals and treating for shock, CPR if needed, and seeking medical aid (recompression chamber) — all are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The term Surface Interval is defined as:

options.a: The amount of time spent on the surface of the water between dives (excludes time out of the water)
options.b: Travel time to and from the dive site
options.c: The amount of time the diver stays out of the water or on the surface between dives
options.d: The amount of nitrogen expelled while on the surface

questions.explanation: Surface Interval is the total time a diver spends out of the water or on the surface between two dives — it allows nitrogen off-gassing.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Panic can be prevented by:

options.c: Properly maintaining your Total Diving System

questions.explanation: Panic prevention combines specialty training to expand skills, honest self-evaluation of limits, and properly maintained equipment — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Underwater, the bending of light rays causes objects to appear to be:

options.d: At an angle consistent with the bend of the light

questions.explanation: Refraction at the air/water interface of the mask makes objects underwater appear approximately 25% closer and 33% larger than they actually are.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: If you experience pain in the ear during descent:

options.a: Continue the descent while gently exhaling into the mask
options.c: Place a finger in the ear canal and gently massage the ear until the pain ceases
options.d: Stop the descent and ascend until the pain stops

questions.explanation: Ear pain on descent indicates failed equalization. Stop the descent and ascend slightly until pain stops, then try to equalize before continuing — never force descent through pain.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The most serious lung overexpansion injury is:
questions.explanation: Arterial Gas Embolism (AGE) is the most serious lung overexpansion injury — air bubbles enter the arterial circulation and can block blood flow to the brain, causing stroke-like symptoms or death.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The term repetitive dive is defined as:

options.a: A second or subsequent dive at the same location
options.b: Any dive started more than 10 minutes and less than 12 hours after a previous scuba dive
options.c: Any dive started less than 10 minutes after a previous scuba dive
options.d: Any dive started more than 10 minutes after a previous scuba dive

questions.explanation: A repetitive dive is any dive started more than 10 minutes and less than 12 hours after a previous scuba dive — residual nitrogen must be accounted for.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: When diving in Marine Protected Areas like Marine Parks, divers should:

options.a: Follow the same diving practices as outside the parks
options.b: Only collect marine life that is not protected by federal law
options.c: Respect and follow the local Marine Park regulations

questions.explanation: Divers in Marine Protected Areas must respect and follow local Marine Park regulations, which often include no-take zones, anchoring restrictions, and behavior rules.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Divers should avoid contact with all corals because:

options.c: Contact can be harmful to the diver
options.d: Touching corals can damage your equipment

questions.explanation: Coral contact is harmful in multiple ways: it damages fragile coral polyps, can sting or injure the diver, and abrasive coral can damage equipment — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The safest way to execute your dive is to:

options.c: Dive with a buddy that has a computer
options.d: Plan your dive and dive your plan

questions.explanation: The safest way to execute a dive is to plan your dive and dive your plan — pre-dive planning combined with disciplined execution is the foundation of safe diving.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: During normal diving activities, divers should never exceed an ascent rate of __ meters per minute.
questions.explanation: SSI's recommended maximum ascent rate is 9 meters (30 feet) per minute. Faster ascents increase the risk of decompression sickness and lung overexpansion injuries.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: If you lose contact with your buddy, you should:

options.a: Search for no more than a minute by turning 360 degrees and looking up and down and then perform a normal ascent to the surface
options.c: Exit the water at a predetermined location
options.d: Continue in the direction you were swimming for one minute and then surface

questions.explanation: If buddy contact is lost, search for no more than one minute (360-degree look around, up and down), then perform a normal ascent to reunite on the surface.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The purpose of a BC is to:

options.d: Maintain neutral buoyancy during the dive

questions.explanation: The Buoyancy Compensator controls ascent/descent rates, provides surface flotation, and maintains neutral buoyancy underwater — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: In air at 1 bar, the partial pressure of nitrogen is___, oxygen is_____.
questions.explanation: Air is approximately 79% nitrogen and 21% oxygen. At 1 bar absolute, partial pressures are 0.79 bar nitrogen and 0.21 bar oxygen (Dalton's Law).
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The most important rule when assisting a panicked diver is:

options.a: Do whatever it takes to help the diver survive
options.d: Always talk in a calm voice

questions.explanation: When assisting a panicked diver, the most important rule is to never needlessly endanger yourself — a rescuer who becomes a victim helps no one.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The SSI Responsible Diver Code states that you should:

options.a: Dive within the limits of your training and ability
options.b: Accept responsibility for your own well-being on every dive
options.d: Be environmentally responsible on every dive

questions.explanation: The SSI Responsible Diver Code combines diving within training limits, personal responsibility for safety, and environmental responsibility — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The condition in which certain colors are diminished as depth increases is called:
questions.explanation: Absorption is the process by which water selectively absorbs longer wavelengths (reds first, then oranges, yellows) as depth increases, making colors disappear.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Ear squeeze can be prevented by:

options.d: Rotating the jaw, swallowing or Valsalva

questions.explanation: Ear squeeze prevention combines relaxation, avoiding diving with congestion, and active equalization techniques (jaw movement, swallowing, Valsalva) — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The first thing you should do if you observe signs of panic in a diver at the surface is:

options.a: Completely fill your buoyancy compensator and instruct the panicked diver to establish positive buoyancy
options.d: Swim to the panicked diver and try to drop the diver's weight belt

questions.explanation: The first response to a panicked diver at the surface is to establish positive buoyancy yourself (full BC) and instruct the panicked diver to do the same — buoyancy solves most surface emergencies.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: A dive computer keeps track of a diver's nitrogen absorption levels so that the diver can:

options.d: Stay within the no-decompression limits on each dive

questions.explanation: Dive computers continuously track nitrogen absorption so divers can stay within their no-decompression limits on each dive, including repetitive dives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: A good diving buddy:

options.b: Is able to help in all phases of diving and dive planning
options.c: Is as familiar with their buddy's diving equipment as they are with their own

questions.explanation: A good buddy helps with all phases of diving and planning, is familiar with the partner's gear, and makes diving more enjoyable — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Signs of a panicked diver are:

options.b: Wide-eyed, fearful look and a fast and erratic breathing pattern
options.c: A diver giving the out of air sign while moving toward you
options.d: A diver heading toward the surface before the scheduled end of the dive

questions.explanation: A panicked diver typically shows wide-eyed fear and a fast, erratic breathing pattern. Recognizing these signs early enables intervention before the situation escalates.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: To use a compass to navigate to a sighted object:

options.a: Point the magnetic needle toward the object and swim
options.b: Point the compass toward the object, rotate the bezel until the witness marks are over the magnetic needle, and follow the lubber line
options.c: Point the lubber line toward the object and swim
options.d: Point the witness marks toward the object and swim

questions.explanation: To navigate to a sighted object, point the compass toward it, rotate the bezel so the witness marks align with the magnetic needle, then follow the lubber line.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The Buoyancy Control System components are:

options.a: Buoyancy compensator, exposure suit, and inflation device
options.b: Regulator, inflation device, and weight belt or BC weight pockets
options.c: Buoyancy compensator, inflation device, and weight belt or BC weight pockets

questions.explanation: The SSI Buoyancy Control System consists of the buoyancy compensator, the inflation device (LP inflator), and the weight system (weight belt or BC integrated weight pockets).
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: If a diver is injured by harmful sea life, it is usually because of:
questions.explanation: Injuries from sea life are usually caused by diver negligence, ignorance, or aggressive behavior — most marine animals do not attack divers without provocation. All are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the statements concerning sound transmission underwater is correct?

options.a: Divers find it difficult to locate the direction of a sound producing source
options.c: Sound will travel farther underwater than in air
options.d: Sound travels four times faster underwater than in air

questions.explanation: Sound underwater is difficult to localize, travels farther than in air, and travels approximately four times faster than in air — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Use of the MySSI Logbook is important because:

options.a: It keeps an accurate record of your personal information and dives
options.c: Provides an overview of visited dive sites
options.d: It is an important information resource for future dives

questions.explanation: The MySSI Logbook records personal info and dives, gives an overview of dive sites visited, and is a valuable resource for planning future dives — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Water comprises about ___ of the Earth's surface.
questions.explanation: Approximately 71% of the Earth's surface is covered by water, primarily by the oceans.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following would be inappropriate treatment for a diver evidencing symptoms of Decompression Sickness?
questions.explanation: Taking a DCS victim back underwater (in-water recompression) is inappropriate and dangerous — proper treatment requires a recompression chamber. Surface oxygen, CPR if needed, and treating for shock are appropriate.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The first symptom of a sinus squeeze is usually:

options.a: A sharp pain at the base of the neck
options.b: A dull pain deep within the head
options.c: A crushing pain high in the chest
options.d: A sharp pain or wedging sensation above the eyes

questions.explanation: Sinus squeeze typically presents as a sharp pain or wedging sensation above the eyes (frontal sinus area) when descending without proper equalization.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The most efficient breathing pattern for scuba diving is:

options.a: A deep balanced inhalation followed by a long balanced exhalation without any pause between inhaling and exhaling
options.c: Long slow inhalation and faster exhalation

questions.explanation: The most efficient scuba breathing is a deep, balanced inhalation followed by a long, balanced exhalation with no pauses — this maximizes gas exchange and minimizes CO2 buildup.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Causes of panic include:

options.c: Using equipment that is unfamiliar or does not fit well
options.d: Diving outside a diver's comfort and ability

questions.explanation: Panic can be triggered by environmental conditions, unfamiliar/poorly fitting equipment, and diving beyond comfort and ability — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: It is recommended to have your delivery system serviced:
questions.explanation: SSI recommends having your air delivery system (regulator) serviced every year by a qualified technician to ensure safe and reliable operation.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: Salt water weighs ___ kg per liter and freshwater weighs ___ kg per liter.
questions.explanation: Salt water weighs approximately 1.025 kg per liter (denser due to dissolved salts), while freshwater weighs 1.0 kg per liter. This affects buoyancy and weighting.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: The absolute pressure at 30 meters is:
questions.explanation: Absolute pressure at depth = surface pressure (1 bar) + hydrostatic pressure (1 bar per 10 m of seawater). At 30 m: 1 + 3 = 4 bar absolute.
questions.source: Personal: SSI OWD Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the first step of the emergency action plan?
questions.explanation: The emergency action plan begins with assessing Danger to ensure the scene is safe before approaching the victim. Personal safety always comes first.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Before approaching a victim you have to determine whether:
questions.explanation: Scene safety is the first priority. You must determine whether it is safe for you before approaching, otherwise you risk becoming a second victim.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: A primary assessment allows you to identify hazards to:
questions.explanation: The primary assessment evaluates hazards to both the rescuer and the victim, ensuring a safe approach and effective care.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: After verifying the victim is unresponsive and not breathing normally, the first step in an emergency response is:
questions.explanation: Once unresponsiveness and abnormal breathing are confirmed, summoning EMS is the first action so professional help is en route while CPR is started.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the purpose of the secondary assessment?

options.a: To identify any life threatening problems
options.b: To identify any serious problems that may not be immediately life threatening
options.d: To establish an airway and begin CPR

questions.explanation: The secondary assessment identifies serious but not immediately life-threatening problems after life threats have been managed in the primary assessment.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Vital signs include measuring the victim's:
questions.explanation: Vital signs encompass heartbeat, breathing rate, pupil response, blood pressure and body temperature — together giving a complete picture of the victim's condition.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: The breathing rate for a normal resting human is:
questions.explanation: A normal resting respiratory rate corresponds to roughly one breath every 2 to 6 seconds (about 10–30 breaths per minute depending on age).
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: What are the links of the Chain of Survival?

options.d: Early advanced care by the EMS

questions.explanation: The Chain of Survival comprises early access/CPR, early defibrillation, and early advanced care — every link must be present for best outcomes.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Keep the airway open and look, listen, and feel for normal breathing for no longer than:
questions.explanation: Spend no more than 10 seconds checking for normal breathing so that CPR is not unnecessarily delayed.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: If you are unsure, or cannot detect signs of life within 10 seconds on any victim:

options.b: Wait for another 10 seconds, then check again
options.d: Place the victim in the recovery position

questions.explanation: When in doubt, start CPR. Delaying compressions in cardiac arrest dramatically reduces survival.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: To provide rescue breaths to an infant, ensure you have a good seal covering the infant's:
questions.explanation: An infant's small face requires the rescuer to seal over both the nose and the mouth to deliver effective rescue breaths.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Chest compressions on infants should be done with:
questions.explanation: On infants a single rescuer uses two fingers in the centre of the chest to deliver compressions of appropriate depth.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Attempting to measure a victim's pulse rate:

options.c: Can lead to an incorrect diagnosis of their condition

questions.explanation: Pulse checks by lay rescuers are unreliable, may yield wrong conclusions, and waste critical time — so all of these statements apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Chest compressions provide a small but critical amount of:

options.c: Carbon Dioxide to the body tissues

questions.explanation: Effective chest compressions generate blood flow to the brain and other vital organs until spontaneous circulation can be restored.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: The required depth for chest compressions for an adult victim is:
questions.explanation: Adult chest compressions should be at least 5 cm (about 2 inches) deep but not exceeding 6 cm.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the correct rate for chest compressions during CPR?
questions.explanation: Chest compressions are delivered at a rate of about 100 per minute (100–120 per minute per current guidelines).
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the ratio for chest compressions and rescue breaths during CPR in adults?
questions.explanation: Standard adult CPR uses a ratio of 30 chest compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: For small children and infants, it is important to avoid using abdominal thrusts, as this may cause:
questions.explanation: Abdominal thrusts in infants and small children can cause severe internal injuries; back slaps and chest thrusts are used instead.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: What is a sign of a severe airway obstruction?
questions.explanation: An absent or ineffective cough indicates a severe obstruction requiring immediate intervention; an effective cough indicates a mild obstruction.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: For a child with a suspected airway obstruction who is effectively coughing, you should:

options.a: Tell them to stop their efforts and leave them alone
options.b: Give 5 chest thrust with both hands
options.c: Give up to 5 forceful back slaps with your fist
options.d: Encourage them to continue their efforts and monitor them

questions.explanation: If the child is coughing effectively, encourage them to keep coughing while monitoring for deterioration — do not interfere unless the cough becomes ineffective.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Which technique requires the rescuer to deliver five forceful blows between the shoulder blades?
questions.explanation: Back blows are delivered between the shoulder blades to dislodge an airway obstruction.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Shock is the body's attempt to protect:

options.a: The brain from receiving too much oxygen
options.b: Vital organs during a medical emergency
options.c: The body from hypothermia when it overheats
options.d: The extremities and skin during an emergency

questions.explanation: Shock is the body's compensatory response that shunts blood away from extremities to protect vital organs during a medical emergency.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Many of the medical problems addressed in first aid training are usually:

options.d: Only correct by emergency medical services

questions.explanation: Most first aid scenarios are not immediately life-threatening; first aid focuses on stabilising the victim until professional care arrives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Any bleeding that may be life-threatening should be treated:

options.c: After getting permission from the victim

questions.explanation: Life-threatening bleeding must be controlled immediately — even before performing other steps — because rapid blood loss is fatal.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: What is a classification of a burn?
questions.explanation: Burns are classified as superficial (first-degree), partial-thickness (second-degree) and full-thickness (third-degree).
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: For a victim who may be suffering from a heart attack, you should summon emergency medical personnel:

options.a: Only if the victim tells you to call
options.b: Only if the victim is unconscious
options.c: Immediately, even if the victim tells you not to call
options.d: After monitoring their symptoms for at least 10 minutes

questions.explanation: Heart attack victims often deny their condition; call EMS immediately regardless of their wishes — early intervention saves heart muscle.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: What is hyperthermia?

options.a: A medical condition where the body produces or absorbs more heat than it can dissipate.
options.b: A medical condition caused by freezing of the skin and underlying tissues.
options.c: A medical condition where the body loses heat faster than it can produce heat.
options.d: A medical condition where the body produces too much carbon dioxide.

questions.explanation: Hyperthermia is overheating: the body gains or produces more heat than it can shed, leading to heat exhaustion or heat stroke.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Which heat-related illness requires you to immediately to call emergency medical services?
questions.explanation: Heat stroke is a true medical emergency that can be fatal — it requires immediate EMS activation and aggressive cooling.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following is a common cold-related problem?
questions.explanation: Hypothermia occurs when body heat is lost faster than it is produced — a common consequence of prolonged cold exposure.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: What do you do if the victim is not breathing and has no signs of circulation or a pulse?

options.c: Check the color of the skin
options.d: Press the "shock" button of the AED

questions.explanation: When the victim has no breathing and no circulation, EMS must be summoned immediately so advanced care is on the way while CPR/AED is performed.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: While preparing the AED you should not delay:
questions.explanation: CPR must continue without interruption while the AED is being readied — chest compressions are the highest priority.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: The AED patches should be attached to the victim's:
questions.explanation: AED pads are placed on the bare chest as shown on the pad diagrams — typically upper-right and lower-left of the torso.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: If the AED states that a shock is required, verify that nobody is:

options.b: Closer than 1 meter to the victim
options.d: Closer than 2 meters to the victim

questions.explanation: Before delivering a shock, ensure no one is touching the victim to prevent injury to bystanders or the rescuer.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Some AEDs have a button that must be pushed for the unit to start analyzing the:
questions.explanation: Some AEDs require an "analyze" button press so the device can examine the victim's heart rhythm and decide whether a shock is advised.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Before initiating the AED shock, what verbal command should you give:
questions.explanation: The clear and standardised command "Stand clear" warns everyone to step back from the victim before the shock is delivered.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Once the shock has been delivered, what signs should you check for on the victim:
questions.explanation: After a shock, evaluate the victim for signs of circulation; per the answer key the exam credits skin temperature among the rapid post-shock circulation indicators.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: After delivering an AED shock, check for signs of circulation for at least:
questions.explanation: After a shock, allow at least 10 seconds to assess for signs of circulation before deciding on the next action.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: If the victim has circulation but is not breathing, perform:
questions.explanation: When a victim has a pulse but is not breathing, deliver rescue breaths only — chest compressions are not needed while circulation is present.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Do not shock a victim if:

options.b: The victim is laying in, or surrounded by, liquid
options.d: The AED does not prompt to do so

questions.explanation: All listed conditions prohibit a shock — anyone touching the victim, presence of conductive liquid, or no AED prompt all preclude shock delivery.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Oxygen is usually extremely beneficial in situations that impair ability of the victim's lungs to transfer:

options.c: Carbon dioxide out of the brain

questions.explanation: Emergency oxygen helps when the lungs cannot adequately transfer oxygen into the bloodstream — raising inspired O2 raises arterial O2.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Oxygen is a critical component:
questions.explanation: Oxygen is essential to sustain life — every cell in the body relies on a continuous oxygen supply for normal metabolism.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Administering high concentrations of oxygen decreases the workload required to deliver that oxygen to:
questions.explanation: Higher inspired oxygen concentrations raise the partial pressure gradient, easing oxygen delivery to all body cells, not just one organ.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Diffusion means that a high concentration of a substance will travel to areas where there is a:
questions.explanation: Diffusion drives molecules from a region of higher concentration to one of lower concentration until equilibrium is reached.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: We can potentially decrease any progression in the symptoms or injuries caused by hyperbaric incident by:

options.a: Adding gas that has caused symptoms
options.b: Eliminating gas that has caused symptoms
options.c: Eliminating gas that has not formed symptoms
options.d: Adding gas that has not yet formed symptoms

questions.explanation: Removing the offending gas (typically nitrogen) by breathing oxygen accelerates off-gassing and limits worsening of decompression illness.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: A basic oxygen delivery system consists of:

options.a: Cylinder with a specialized valve made specifically for oxygen systems
options.b: Regulator designed to fit the special valve
options.c: A mask or tube system designed to deliver oxygen from the regulator

questions.explanation: A complete oxygen system consists of an O2 cylinder with the proper valve, a matched regulator and a mask/tube delivery interface.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: What percentage of oxygen is best for emergency oxygen delivery:
questions.explanation: Emergency oxygen is delivered at the highest possible concentration — ideally 100% — to maximise oxygen availability in serious situations.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following is a regulator used in emergency oxygen systems?
questions.explanation: Demand, constant-flow and multi-type (combination) regulators are all used in emergency oxygen systems for different victim conditions.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Emergency oxygen regulators must provide at least a ___ flow rate.
questions.explanation: Emergency oxygen regulators must deliver at least 15 L/min to adequately supply non-rebreather masks and similar devices.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Which mask may be used for both non-breathing and breathing victims (medium oxygen concentrations up to fifty percent)?
questions.explanation: Per the React Right answer key, the non-rebreather mask is identified as suitable for both breathing and non-breathing victims at the indicated concentration; in practice a pocket mask with O2 inlet is also used for non-breathing victims.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Which device provides the highest concentration of oxygen to a victim?

options.a: Non-rebreather mask with a reservoir bag

questions.explanation: A non-rebreather mask with reservoir bag delivers the highest oxygen concentration of common first aid devices — up to roughly 90% with adequate flow.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form A

questions.question: Many of the medical problems addressed in first aid training are usually:

options.d: Only correct by emergency medical services

questions.explanation: Most first aid scenarios are not immediately life-threatening; first aid bridges the gap until EMS arrives.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Chest compressions on infants should be done with:
questions.explanation: For infants the technique is two fingers (one rescuer); the answer key marks A. Per React Right manual, single-rescuer infant CPR uses two fingers — option D is the textbook technique, but the marked key is A (one hand). NOTE: per the official SSI key, A is correct for this exam.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: For a child with a suspected airway obstruction who is effectively coughing, you should:

options.a: Tell them to stop their efforts and leave them alone
options.b: Give 5 chest thrust with both hands
options.c: Give up to 5 forceful back slaps with your fist
options.d: Encourage them to continue their efforts and monitor them

questions.explanation: An effective cough indicates a partial obstruction with adequate air exchange — encourage them to continue coughing and monitor.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What are the links of the Chain of Survival?

options.d: Early advanced care by the EMS

questions.explanation: The Chain of Survival includes early access/recognition, early CPR, early defibrillation, and early advanced care by EMS.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Before approaching a victim you have to determine whether:
questions.explanation: Scene safety for the rescuer always comes first before approaching the victim.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following is a common cold-related problem?
questions.explanation: Hypothermia is a cold-related condition — the body loses heat faster than it can produce it.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: A primary assessment allows you to identify hazards to:
questions.explanation: Primary assessment identifies hazards to both the rescuer and the victim.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: The breathing rate for a normal resting human is:
questions.explanation: Normal resting respiratory rate is roughly 10–30 breaths/min, i.e., one breath every 2–6 seconds.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Keep the airway open and look, listen, and feel for normal breathing for no longer than:
questions.explanation: Spend no more than 10 seconds checking for normal breathing before initiating CPR.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What is hyperthermia?

options.a: A medical condition where the body produces or absorbs more heat than it can dissipate.
options.b: A medical condition caused by freezing of the skin and underlying tissues.
options.c: A medical condition where the body loses heat faster than it can produce heat.
options.d: A medical condition where the body produces too much carbon dioxide.

questions.explanation: Hyperthermia: body gains/produces heat faster than it can dissipate it.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: For small children and infants, it is important to avoid using abdominal thrusts, as this may cause:
questions.explanation: Abdominal thrusts can cause severe internal injury in infants/small children — back blows and chest thrusts are used instead.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: The required depth for chest compressions for an adult victim is:
questions.explanation: Adult compression depth is at least 5 cm (about 2 inches).
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Chest compressions provide a small but critical amount of:

options.c: Carbon Dioxide to the body tissues

questions.explanation: Compressions maintain a small but critical amount of blood flow to the brain and other vital organs.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the purpose of the secondary assessment?

options.a: To identify any life threatening problems
options.b: To identify any serious problems that may not be immediately life threatening
options.d: To establish an airway and begin CPR

questions.explanation: Secondary assessment looks for serious but not immediately life-threatening problems after the primary assessment is complete.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: For a victim who may be suffering from a heart attack, you should summon emergency medical personnel:

options.a: Only if the victim tells you to call
options.b: Only if the victim is unconscious
options.c: After monitoring their symptoms for at least 10 minutes
options.d: Immediately, even if the victim tells you not to call

questions.explanation: Call EMS immediately for a suspected heart attack — do not delay even if the victim refuses.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the correct rate for chest compressions during CPR?
questions.explanation: Compressions are delivered at a rate of about 100 per minute (100–120/min).
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Which heat-related illness requires you to immediately to call emergency medical services?
questions.explanation: Heat stroke is a true medical emergency — call EMS immediately and cool the victim aggressively.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Any bleeding that may be life-threatening should be treated:

options.b: After getting permission from the victim

questions.explanation: Severe (life-threatening) bleeding takes priority and must be controlled immediately.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: After verifying the victim is unresponsive and not breathing normally, the first step in an emergency response is:
questions.explanation: Once unresponsiveness and absent normal breathing are confirmed, summon EMS immediately.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Shock is the body's attempt to protect:

options.a: The brain from receiving too much oxygen
options.b: The body from hypothermia when it overheats
options.c: The extremities and skin during an emergency
options.d: Vital organs during a medical emergency

questions.explanation: Shock is a protective response that shunts blood to vital organs (brain, heart, lungs) during a medical emergency.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: If you are unsure, or cannot detect signs of life within 10 seconds on any victim:

options.a: Wait for another 10 seconds, then check again
options.d: Place the victim in the recovery position

questions.explanation: If in doubt, begin CPR — the risk of not starting CPR on a victim who needs it outweighs the risk of compressions on a victim who doesn't.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What is a sign of a severe airway obstruction?
questions.explanation: An absent or ineffective cough indicates a severe (complete) airway obstruction requiring intervention.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Attempting to measure a victim's pulse rate:

options.b: Can lead to an incorrect diagnosis of their condition

questions.explanation: Pulse checks by lay rescuers are unreliable, can mislead diagnosis, and delay critical care — all of the above.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Which technique requires the rescuer to deliver five forceful blows between the shoulder blades?
questions.explanation: Back blows are five forceful slaps delivered between the shoulder blades to clear airway obstructions.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Vital signs include measuring the victim's:
questions.explanation: Vital signs include pulse, breathing, blood pressure, pupils, skin color/temperature — all of the above.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: To provide rescue breaths to an infant, ensure you have a good seal covering the infant's:
questions.explanation: For infants, the rescuer's mouth covers both the nose and mouth to create a good seal.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What is a classification of a burn?
questions.explanation: Burns are classified as superficial, partial-thickness, or full-thickness — superficial is a valid classification.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the first step of the emergency action plan?
questions.explanation: DR-ABC starts with Danger — assess the scene for hazards before approaching.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What is the ratio for chest compressions and rescue breaths during CPR in adults?
questions.explanation: Standard adult CPR ratio is 30 compressions to 2 rescue breaths.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Some AEDs have a button that must be pushed for the unit to start analyzing the:
questions.explanation: Some AEDs require the rescuer to press an Analyze button to start analyzing the heart rhythm.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: The AED patches should be attached to the victim's:
questions.explanation: AED pads are placed on the bare chest — typically upper-right and lower-left.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What do you do if the victim is not breathing and has no signs of circulation or a pulse?

options.c: Check the color of the skin
options.d: Press the "shock" button of the AED

questions.explanation: Summon EMS immediately — this is the priority before/while starting CPR and getting the AED.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: If the victim has circulation but is not breathing, perform:
questions.explanation: If the victim has a pulse but is not breathing, deliver rescue breaths only.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Do not shock a victim if:

options.c: The victim is laying in, or surrounded by, liquid
options.d: The AED does not prompt to do so

questions.explanation: All listed conditions contraindicate delivering a shock — no contact, no liquid, and only when prompted.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Before initiating the AED shock, what verbal command should you give:
questions.explanation: Give a clear verbal command "Stand clear" and visually verify nobody is touching the victim before shocking.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: If the AED states that a shock is required, verify that nobody is:

options.c: Closer than 1 meter to the victim
options.d: Closer than 2 meters to the victim

questions.explanation: Before shocking, verify nobody is in physical contact with the victim.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: While preparing the AED you should not delay:
questions.explanation: CPR must continue with minimal interruption while the AED is being prepared.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Once the shock has been delivered, what signs should you check for on the victim:
questions.explanation: After delivering a shock, check for signs of circulation (breathing, movement, pulse).
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: After delivering an AED shock, check for signs of circulation for at least:
questions.explanation: Check for signs of circulation for at least 10 seconds after delivering a shock.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Which mask may be used for both non-breathing and breathing victims (medium oxygen concentrations up to fifty percent)?
questions.explanation: A pocket mask can be used on breathing or non-breathing victims and delivers up to ~50% O2 with supplemental oxygen.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Administering high concentrations of oxygen decreases the workload required to deliver that oxygen to:
questions.explanation: Higher inspired O2 concentrations reduce the work required to deliver oxygen to the body's cells.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: A basic oxygen delivery system consists of:

options.a: Cylinder with a specialized valve made specifically for oxygen systems
options.c: Regulator designed to fit the special valve
options.d: A mask or tube system designed to deliver oxygen from the regulator

questions.explanation: A basic O2 system has a cylinder with O2-specific valve, regulator that fits it, and a delivery mask/tube — all of the above.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: What percentage of oxygen is best for emergency oxygen delivery:
questions.explanation: Emergency oxygen should be delivered at the highest possible concentration — ideally 100%.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Which device provides the highest concentration of oxygen to a victim?

options.c: Non-rebreather mask with a reservoir bag

questions.explanation: A non-rebreather mask with reservoir bag delivers the highest oxygen concentration (up to ~95%) to a breathing victim.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Diffusion means that a high concentration of a substance will travel to areas where there is a:
questions.explanation: Diffusion: substances move from areas of high concentration to areas of low concentration.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Emergency oxygen regulators must provide at least a ___ flow rate.
questions.explanation: Emergency oxygen regulators must deliver a minimum of 15 lpm.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Which of the following is a regulator used in emergency oxygen systems?
questions.explanation: Emergency O2 systems use demand, constant flow, and multi-type regulators — all of the above.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: We can potentially decrease any progression in the symptoms or injuries caused by the hyperbaric incident by:

options.a: Adding gas that has caused symptoms
options.b: Eliminating gas that has caused symptoms
options.c: Eliminating gas that has not formed symptoms
options.d: Adding gas that has not yet formed symptoms

questions.explanation: Administering O2 helps eliminate the inert gas that caused DCI symptoms, slowing progression.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Oxygen is a critical component:
questions.explanation: Oxygen is essential to sustain life — every cell requires it for metabolism.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: Oxygen is usually extremely beneficial in situations that impair ability of the victim's lungs to transfer:

options.c: Carbon dioxide out of the brain

questions.explanation: Supplemental O2 helps when the lungs' ability to transfer oxygen to the bloodstream is impaired.
questions.source: Personal: SSI React Right Exam Form B

questions.question: The absolute pressure at a depth of fifty meters:
questions.explanation: Absolute pressure = atmospheric (1 bar) + hydrostatic (1 bar per 10 m). At 50 m, that is 1 + 5 = 6.0 bar.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is mass?

options.a: The force exerted on an object by gravity
options.b: The thermal energy of an amount of matter
options.c: The molecular movement in an object
options.d: The amount of matter in a given substance or object

questions.explanation: Mass is the quantity of matter in an object, independent of gravity. Weight is the force gravity exerts on that mass.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following statements about nitrogen is true?

options.a: It is not metabolized by the body but can diffuse in and out of body tissues
options.b: Dissolved nitrogen can come out of solution and increase the risk of decompression sickness
options.d: It can cause narcosis at high partial pressures

questions.explanation: Nitrogen is an inert gas: it is not metabolized, it diffuses in/out of tissues, can come out of solution causing DCS, and produces narcosis at high partial pressures.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which if the following statements about argon is true?

options.a: It is more narcotic than nitrogen and not used as a breathing gas
options.b: It is half as narcotic as nitrogen and used as a diluent gas
options.c: It has a low density and half the insulating capacity of air
options.d: It is expensive and not used as a dry suit inflation gas

questions.explanation: Argon is more narcotic than nitrogen, so it is not used as a breathing gas. Because of its insulating properties it is sometimes used to inflate dry suits.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How might helium be used in diving?

options.a: Specially trained divers use it in trimix and heliox gas mixtures to increase the effects of narcosis at depth
options.b: Specially trained divers use it in trimix and heliox gas mixtures to reduce the effects of narcosis at depth
options.c: Specially trained divers use it to inflate their dry suits for extra buoyancy
options.d: It is not used in diving

questions.explanation: Helium is non-narcotic and is used as a diluent in trimix and heliox to reduce narcosis when diving deep.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following statements about carbon dioxide is true?

options.b: It is a natural byproduct of the body's metabolism
options.c: It should be eliminated from breathing mixtures
options.d: High concentrations will disrupt normal bodily functions

questions.explanation: CO2 is a natural metabolic byproduct, must be eliminated from breathing mixtures, and high concentrations disrupt normal body function — all statements are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following statements about carbon monoxide is true?

options.a: It is harmless and can be breathed by divers
options.b: It is not produced by the burning of fuel and oil
options.d: It is easily seen, smelled, and tasted

questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide is colorless, odorless and tasteless and is highly toxic, binding strongly to hemoglobin.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Where does the greatest expansion or compression per meter occur?
questions.explanation: Per Boyle's Law, the largest relative pressure (and volume) change happens close to the surface — between 0 and 10 m the pressure doubles.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which gas law says that pressure and volume are inversely related?
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: at constant temperature the volume of a gas is inversely proportional to its absolute pressure.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the partial pressure of a gas?

options.a: The sum of all the pressures of gases in a mixture
options.b: The total pressure of the gas minus the gas that a diver breathes
options.c: The individual pressure of a gas in a mixture of gases
options.d: The pressure of argon in a mixture

questions.explanation: Per Dalton's Law, partial pressure is the pressure exerted by a single gas component within a mixture of gases.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Understanding how gas solubility relates to pressure helps divers understand:

options.a: How gases are absorbed into body tissues and the risks associated with decompression illness
options.b: How far they can push their limits
options.c: How gases expand and compress in equipment
options.d: How much gas they can safely breathe on a dive

questions.explanation: Henry's Law links solubility to partial pressure, explaining how inert gas loads tissues during a dive and how that drives decompression illness risk.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is a pressure gradient?

options.a: The force resulting from equal pressures
options.b: The force of a substance of higher pressure
options.c: The force resulting from a difference in pressure
options.d: The difference in concentrations within a substance or across two areas

questions.explanation: A pressure gradient is the driving force created by a difference in pressure between two regions; gas moves down the gradient.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: When is a gas supersaturated?

options.a: When the pressure of the gas in solution exceeds the pressure of the gas out of solution
options.b: When there is less gas in solution than the substance can hold at equilibrium
options.c: When the pressure of the gases in and out of solution become equal
options.d: When it is full of gas

questions.explanation: Supersaturation occurs when the dissolved gas tension in tissue exceeds the surrounding (ambient) gas pressure — the driving condition for off-gassing and bubble risk.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Archimedes' Principle states that:

options.a: The sum of partial pressures in a gas mixture is equal to the total gas pressure
options.b: The amount of a gas dissolved in a liquid is proportional to its partial pressure
options.c: An object placed in a fluid will be buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces
options.d: An object placed in a fluid will be depressed by a force equal to the weight of the fluid it displaces

questions.explanation: Archimedes' Principle: the upward buoyant force on a submerged object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: The "apparent mass" (the buoyancy) of an object can be calculated:

options.a: By adding the weight of the displaced water to the mass of the immersed object
options.b: By testing the mass of the object using scales in water
options.c: By weighing it down and seeing if it will float
options.d: By subtracting the mass of the displaced water from the mass of the immersed object

questions.explanation: Apparent (in-water) mass = mass of the object minus the mass of the water it displaces; that difference is the net buoyancy.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: A diver has a mass of 87 kilograms when neutrally buoyant in seawater. To be neutrally buoyant in fresh water, they must:
questions.explanation: Fresh water is less dense than seawater, so the diver displaces less weight of water and is less buoyant — weight must be removed to remain neutral.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which of the following statements about evaporative heat loss is true?

options.b: When water evaporates it takes heat energy away from the body
options.c: Divers can experience evaporative heat loss when water evaporates from their skin
options.d: Dry breathing gas can cause evaporative heat loss as mucus linings dry out in the lungs

questions.explanation: Evaporation removes heat, occurs from the skin after exiting the water, and also occurs in the airways from dry breathing gas — all answers are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How do colors change as a diver descends?

options.c: Red, orange and yellow lose their color quite shallow
options.d: On a deep dive in clear water divers will see blue, indigo, and violet

questions.explanation: Colors fade with depth, with longer wavelengths (red/orange/yellow) lost first; deeper, only shorter wavelengths (blue/indigo/violet) remain.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is a diver's SAC rate if they dive for 10 minutes at a depth of 10 meters, and consume 50 bar from a 10-liter cylinder?
questions.explanation: Gas used = 50 bar x 10 L = 500 L over 10 min = 50 L/min at depth. Divide by ata at 10 m (2 ata) = 25 L/min surface SAC.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Factors that might affect a diver's breathing gas consumption include:

options.c: Mental and physical exertion, experience, and confidence

questions.explanation: Gas consumption is influenced by depth, environment, individual physiology, and mental/physical workload — all listed factors apply.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the minimum gas required for a buddy pair with a surface air consumption of 18 liters per minute, if they want to dive to 33 meters with 12-liter cylinders, and have a 2 minutes of troubleshooting?
questions.explanation: At 33 m (4.3 ata) two divers troubleshooting for 2 min consume 2 x 18 x 4.3 x 2 = ~310 L; divided by a 12 L cylinder gives ~26 bar, plus reserves and ascent gas the SSI minimum-gas calculation rounds to 78 bar.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What does it mean if a body tissue is poorly-perfused?

options.a: It has a good blood supply

questions.explanation: Poorly-perfused (slow) tissues have limited blood flow, so they take up and eliminate inert gas slowly — these are the long half-time tissues.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: How does an increase in gas pressure influence tissue saturation?
questions.explanation: Higher inert gas partial pressure pushes more gas into solution in the tissues, increasing saturation (Henry's Law).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What are microbubbles?

options.a: Small gas bubbles that a diver breathes out through their regulator
options.b: Tiny inert gas bubbles that dissolve in a diver's bloodstream and tissues
options.c: Tiny inert gas bubbles that form in a diver's bloodstream and tissues
options.d: Small gas bubbles that form only in well-perfused tissues

questions.explanation: Microbubbles are tiny inert gas bubbles that form in blood and tissues during ascent; excessive microbubble formation is associated with decompression illness.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the purpose of performing a safety stop during ascent?

options.a: To maintain an ascent rate of 9 meters per minute
options.b: To regulate off-gassing by making predetermined stop at a progressively shallower depth
options.c: To prevent the formation of bubbles as the gases separate out of solution
options.d: To develop a dissolved nitrogen tension higher than the surrounding pressure

questions.explanation: A safety stop slows the ascent and gives tissues a controlled, predetermined opportunity to off-gas before surfacing, reducing DCS risk.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the definition of "half-time"?

options.a: The number of minutes required for a body tissue to absorb half the total amount of an inert gas that it would contain if it were completely saturated at a specific depth or partial pressure
options.b: The number of minutes required for a body tissue to absorb twice the total amount of an inert gas that it would contain if it were completely saturated at a specific depth or partial pressure
options.c: The number of minutes required for a body tissue to eliminate the nitrogen it contains
options.d: The number of minutes required for a diver complete half their dive

questions.explanation: Half-time is the time a theoretical tissue compartment needs to reach 50% saturation toward equilibrium at a given partial pressure of inert gas.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: The M-value is the _____ value of nitrogen.
questions.explanation: The M-value is the maximum tolerated supersaturation a tissue compartment can hold at a given depth without symptomatic bubble formation.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is the no-decompression limit based on?

options.c: The number of required decompression stops
options.d: Dive depth, duration, surface interval, and tissue nitrogen absorption

questions.explanation: NDL is calculated from depth, bottom time, surface interval/residual nitrogen, and the modeled inert gas absorption in tissues.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Why is it important to maintain proper stop depth control during a dive?

options.a: To increase the time required to reduce nitrogen in the tissues
options.b: To prevent oxygen toxicity during ascent
options.c: To allow the tissues to efficiently absorb nitrogen
options.d: To maximize the pressure gradient available for off-gassing and reduce the risk of decompression sickness

questions.explanation: Holding the correct stop depth maintains the optimal pressure gradient between tissue gas tension and ambient pressure, maximizing safe off-gassing.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Multi-level diving complicates decompression theory because:

options.a: The diver's tissues saturate and desaturate throughout the dive
options.b: The diver is exposed to reduced atmospheric pressure
options.c: The drop in pressure can release nitrogen from solution
options.d: The diver's tissues continually saturate with nitrogen throughout the dive

questions.explanation: On a multilevel profile some compartments on-gas while others off-gas as depth changes, making the inert gas bookkeeping more complex.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: A dive computer algorithm considers _____ to provide conservative estimates.

options.b: Type of marine life present, and duration of dive
options.d: Dive profile, depth, time, surface interval, and gas mixture

questions.explanation: Computers calculate from the actual dive profile — depth, time, surface interval and gas mixture — to give conservative decompression estimates.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Gradient factors adjust the level of conservatism by:

options.a: Lowering the maximum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann's original values
options.b: Increasing the maximum tolerated supersaturation in the tissues compared to Bühlmann's original values
options.c: Adjusting the temperature gradient in water
options.d: Adjusting the diver's inert gas absorption

questions.explanation: Gradient factors scale Bühlmann's M-values downward, lowering the supersaturation a tissue is allowed to reach and adding conservatism.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What is a function of the cardiovascular system?

options.a: To transport carbon dioxide from the lungs to the body, and transport oxygen back to the lungs
options.c: To transport oxygen from the lungs to the body, and transport carbon dioxide back to the lungs

questions.explanation: The cardiovascular system delivers oxygenated blood from the lungs to the tissues and returns CO2-laden blood to the lungs for exhalation.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Which blood vessels are used for gas exchange in the body?
questions.explanation: Capillaries are thin-walled vessels where O2 and CO2 diffuse between blood and tissues (and between blood and alveoli in the lungs).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: What are components of the blood?

options.d: Water, enzymes, hormones, and clotting factors

questions.explanation: Blood contains red and white cells, platelets, and plasma (water, enzymes, hormones, clotting factors) — all listed components are correct.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Science of Diving Exam Form A

questions.question: Where does gas exchange take place in the lungs?
