questions.question: Preparation in which area may be the life-saving difference in a panic or rescue situation?
questions.explanation: Accident management training teaches you how to coordinate the entire response — assessment, EMS activation, first aid, and evacuation — and is often the life-saving differentiator in a real emergency.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: The main reason people run out of breathing gas is:

options.c: Failure to make visual inspection every year.
options.d: Failure to monitor the pressure gauge or air integrated dive computer.

questions.explanation: Out-of-gas situations are almost always caused by failing to monitor the SPG or air-integrated computer. Regular gas checks are the simplest and most effective prevention.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: All divers should get used to the idea that it's okay to make a no-go diving decision without fear of embarrassment, at which point?

options.a: Before setting up the Total Diving System
options.d: After entering the water but before descent

questions.explanation: A no-go decision is acceptable at any time — before setup, on the surface, or even at depth. Anyone can call any dive at any time without embarrassment.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Your personal preparedness to stay a step ahead of stress includes:

options.a: Wearing a isolation system, dive without a buddy and not deeper than allowed
options.b: Being healthy, having the proper skills, and using good quality and well-maintained equipment
options.c: Being healthy, having expensive equipment and using Nitrox 40 only
options.d: Being comfortable, having the number of dives needed and using rental equipment

questions.explanation: Staying ahead of stress means being healthy, properly skilled, and using good-quality, well-maintained equipment. These three pillars reduce the chance that surprises overwhelm your capacity to respond.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: When you detect stress in your buddy, you need to?:

options.a: Ignore it, all certified divers can solve any stress situation by themselves
options.b: Keep your distance to not endanger youself if he/she starts panicking
options.c: Immediately assess the source of that stress and deal with it appropriately

questions.explanation: Detecting stress early gives you a chance to intervene before it escalates to panic. Immediately assess the cause and deal with it appropriately at the lowest level of intervention needed.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: What is stress?

options.a: Always the result of pressure at depth and demands to the Total Diving System
options.b: The result of pressures or demands which outweigh a person's ability or capacity to respond to them
options.c: Stress is always the result of going diving

questions.explanation: Stress is defined as pressures or demands that outweigh a person's ability or capacity to respond. It is the imbalance between demand and capability, not depth or diving itself.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Hypothermia is generally defined as:

options.a: The condition of having one's body temperature rising above normal.
options.b: The condition of having one's body temperature normal
options.c: The condition of too much Oxygen at depth
options.d: The condition of having one's body temperature fall below normal

questions.explanation: Hypothermia is defined as core body temperature falling below normal. Rising body temperature is hyperthermia; oxygen excess at depth is oxygen toxicity.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form A

questions.question: Even when no-decompression limits are strictly adhered to, the following factors can cause some divers to be more susceptible to decompression sickness:
questions.explanation: Fatigue, body weight, and dehydration are all predisposing factors for DCS even when staying within NDL.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Signs and symptoms of lung overexpansion injuries include:
questions.explanation: Breathing difficulty and chest pain are classic signs of lung overexpansion injury (e.g., AGE/pneumothorax).
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: When you begin to tire, lack the ability to make decisions, or lend support, it is time to:
questions.explanation: Recognize your limits and call for additional help — never compromise your own safety.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Which level of stress is considered to be healthy?

options.a: A consistent high level of Stress.
options.b: A consistent low level of Stress, because it keeps us alert and warns us of danger.
options.c: A consistent medium level of Stress outside the water.

questions.explanation: A low level of stress keeps us alert and acts as an early warning system.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: The following are causes of psychological stress:

options.a: Lack of fitness, fatigue, constriction of movement and hypothermia
options.b: Lack of mental preparedness, mental control, and mental coping
options.c: Equipment loss, equipment failure and hyperthermia
options.d: Headache, fitness and a well maintained Total Diving System

questions.explanation: Psychological stress stems from inadequate mental preparedness, control, and coping skills.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: The main reason people run out of breathing gas is:

options.c: Failure to make visual inspection every year.
options.d: Failure to monitor the pressure gauge or air integrated dive computer.

questions.explanation: Out-of-air situations are almost always due to failing to monitor the SPG/air-integrated DC.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: The two most common dive maladies are:
questions.explanation: DCS and lung overexpansion injuries are the two most common pressure-related dive maladies.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: For mild and serious symptoms of DCS and Overexpansion injuries the appropriate first aid is:
questions.explanation: First aid for DCS/AGE includes 100% O2, activating EMS, and monitoring vitals — all of the above.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: In a buddy assist situation, which person do you have to take care first:
questions.explanation: Always secure your own safety first — you cannot help anyone if you become a second victim.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Each and every safe dive starts with?

options.c: Entering the water with an giant stride.
options.d: Entering the water with a back-roll

questions.explanation: Every safe dive begins with thorough pre-dive planning.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Physical causes of stress include:

options.a: Headache, fitness and a well maintained Total Diving System
options.b: Lack of fitness, fatigue, constriction of movement and hypothermia
options.c: Poor judgment, buddy system failure and warm water
options.d: Equipment loss, equipment failure and hyperthermia

questions.explanation: Physical stressors include poor fitness, fatigue, restricted movement, and hypothermia.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Each diver will have slight variances in their dive plan that will affect their diving profile, especially during an entire week of diving. This is why:

options.a: You have to stay very close together if you use one computer with you buddy
options.b: Each diver must dive with their own computer
options.c: Each diver should drink more or less water
options.d: Each diver should use Nitrox 40 at the end of the week

questions.explanation: Each diver's profile differs slightly, so each must use their own personal dive computer.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: If the victim is not breathing but has a pulse, your decision on your level of care will be based on:

options.a: Your distance from the shore or boat
options.d: Your distance from the next group of divers

questions.explanation: Distance to safety drives whether to provide rescue breaths in water or tow first then ventilate.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Your personal preparedness to stay a step ahead of stress includes:

options.a: Wearing a isolation system, dive without a buddy and not deeper than allowed
options.b: Being healthy, having the proper skills, and using good quality and well-maintained equipment
options.c: Being healthy, having expensive equipment and using Nitrox 40 only
options.d: Being comfortable, having the number of dives needed and using rental equipment

questions.explanation: Health, skills, and quality well-maintained gear are the foundations of stress prevention.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Preparation in which area may be the life-saving difference in a panic or rescue situation?
questions.explanation: Accident management training ties together all rescue skills and is the life-saving differentiator.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: When you detect stress in your buddy, you need to?:

options.a: Ignore it, all certified divers can solve any stress situation by themselves
options.b: Immediately assess the source of that stress and deal with it appropriately
options.c: Keep your distance to not endanger youself if he/she starts panicking

questions.explanation: Identify the cause of stress immediately and address it before it escalates to panic.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: One of the best arguments in favor of always using the buddy system is:

options.a: In a stress situation there will almost always be at least two Dive Professionals which maintain control and are able to lend aid.
options.b: You will always have someone which can help you setting up your Total Diving System.
options.c: In a stress situation there will almost always be at least one diver who maintains control and is able to lend aid
options.d: You always can share your diving experiences after the dive.

questions.explanation: The buddy system ensures at least one diver remains in control to render aid in a crisis.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: If you are separated from you dive buddy, you should:

options.c: Start searching until your breathing gas gets low, then surface
options.d: Surface after making two 360 degree visual sweeps (1 Minute)

questions.explanation: Standard buddy-separation procedure: search no more than ~1 minute (two 360-degree sweeps), then surface.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: What is stress?

options.a: Always the result of pressure at depth and demands to the Total Diving System
options.b: The result of pressures or demands which outweigh a person's ability or capacity to respond to them
options.c: Stress is always the result of going diving

questions.explanation: Stress occurs when demands exceed a person's capacity or ability to respond.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: All divers should get used to the idea that it's okay to make a no-go diving decision without fear of embarrassment, at which point?

options.a: Before setting up the Total Diving System
options.c: After entering the water but before descent

questions.explanation: Any diver can call any dive at any time for any reason — no questions asked.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Decisions about the best method of transportation should be based on the distance to the facility and:

options.d: The type of oxygen regulator used for first aid

questions.explanation: Severity of injury combined with distance determines the appropriate evacuation method.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Hypothermia is generally defined as:

options.a: The condition of having one's body temperature rising above normal.
options.b: The condition of having one's body temperature fall below normal
options.c: The condition of having one's body temperature normal
options.d: The condition of too much Oxygen at depth

questions.explanation: Hypothermia = core body temperature falling below normal.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Common skills for self-aid are:

options.b: Using alternate fin kicks and relieving leg cramps
options.d: Switching to alternate air sources and regulator retrieval

questions.explanation: All listed skills (buoyancy, emergency ascent, fin kicks, cramp relief, alternate air, reg retrieval) are self-aid skills.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Factors which can impair movement and complicate the rescue process include:
questions.explanation: Entanglement and entrapment are the principal factors that impair movement during a rescue.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Before attempting to lift an unconscious diver into a boat or onto shore, you should:

options.d: Remove and ditch the victim's equipment

questions.explanation: Ditch the victim's gear before extraction so the load is manageable and the airway/chest are accessible.
questions.source: Personal: SSI Diver Stress & Rescue Exam Form B

questions.question: Which physical law states that, if temperature remains constant, the pressure and volume of a gas are inversely related?
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: at constant temperature, P x V = constant, so pressure and volume are inversely proportional.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/601446887/ssi-assistant-instructor-pre-test-flash-cards/

questions.question: What is the absolute pressure (in atmospheres absolute) at 33 feet of seawater?
questions.explanation: Every 33 ft of seawater adds 1 ATA. Absolute pressure = 1 ATA (surface) + 1 ATA (water column) = 2 ATA.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/113032957/ssi-open-water-diver-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: What is the absolute pressure (in bar) at 10 metres of seawater?
questions.explanation: Each 10 m of seawater = 1 bar. Absolute pressure = 1 bar (atmosphere) + 1 bar (water) = 2 bar.
questions.source: https://cozumelscuba.com/wp-content/uploads/Exam-English-7.pdf

questions.question: What is the absolute pressure (in bar) at 30 metres of seawater?
questions.explanation: 30 m of seawater = 3 bar of water pressure + 1 bar atmospheric = 4 bar absolute.
questions.source: https://cozumelscuba.com/wp-content/uploads/Exam-English-7.pdf

questions.question: If a flexible container of air is taken from the surface to 30 m / 99 ft, its volume will be:
questions.explanation: Pressure goes from 1 ATA to 4 ATA. By Boyle's Law V2 = V1 x P1/P2 = 1 x 1/4.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A flexible container of air is taken from 30 m / 99 ft to the surface. Its volume will:
questions.explanation: Pressure drops from 4 ATA to 1 ATA, so volume increases 4x by Boyle's Law.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.subarea: Physics & Chemistry — Buoyancy change with depth
questions.question: If 2 litres of air are needed in your BCD to maintain neutral buoyancy at 10 m in the ocean, what will happen if you ascend 3 m without venting?

options.a: You will become more negatively buoyant

questions.explanation: As ambient pressure drops, the gas in the BCD expands, increasing displacement and therefore positive buoyancy.
questions.source: https://www.proscubadiver.net/padi-course-director-joey-ridge/gas-laws-physics/

questions.question: Henry's Law primarily describes:

options.a: The relationship between volume and temperature of a gas
options.b: The amount of gas dissolved in a liquid as a function of partial pressure
options.c: The buoyant force on a submerged object
options.d: The total pressure of a mixed gas

questions.explanation: Henry's Law: at constant temperature the amount of a given gas dissolved in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of that gas above the liquid.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Dalton's Law of partial pressures states that:

options.a: Gases dissolve into liquids in proportion to their pressure
options.b: Pressure and volume are inversely related
options.c: Total pressure of a mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of each component
options.d: An object displaces fluid equal to its weight

questions.explanation: Dalton: P_total = P1 + P2 + ... where each Pn is the partial pressure of each gas in the mixture.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Archimedes' Principle states that an object immersed in a fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the:
questions.explanation: The buoyant force equals the weight of the displaced fluid.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Compared to fresh water, salt water is:

options.d: Less dense at depth, more dense at surface

questions.explanation: Seawater (~1.03 kg/L) is denser than fresh water (1.00 kg/L), generating more buoyant force.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A diver is correctly weighted in salt water. Using the same equipment in fresh water, the diver should:
questions.explanation: Fresh water provides less buoyancy than salt water, so less ballast is needed.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/601446887/ssi-assistant-instructor-pre-test-flash-cards/

questions.question: How much does one cubic foot of sea water weigh?
questions.explanation: Sea water weighs about 64 lb/ft^3; fresh water about 62.4 lb/ft^3.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/84015479/ssi-open-water-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: Underwater, the bending of light rays causes objects to appear:

options.a: The same as on the surface

questions.explanation: Refraction at the mask air-water interface makes objects appear about 25% closer and 33% larger.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/113032957/ssi-open-water-diver-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: The condition in which certain colors are diminished as depth increases is called:
questions.explanation: Water selectively absorbs warm wavelengths (red first, then orange, yellow). This is called absorption.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/113032957/ssi-open-water-diver-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: Which color is the first to disappear as a diver descends?
questions.explanation: Long-wavelength colors are absorbed first; red typically vanishes by ~5 m.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Suspended particles that limit underwater visibility are known as:
questions.explanation: Turbidity refers to the cloudiness of water caused by suspended particulate matter.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Compared to air, sound travels through water:

options.d: It does not travel through water

questions.explanation: Sound travels ~1500 m/s in water vs ~340 m/s in air — roughly four times faster.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/113032957/ssi-open-water-diver-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: Underwater, divers find it difficult to:

options.b: Determine the direction of a sound source

questions.explanation: Because sound travels so fast in water, the time difference between ears is too small for the brain to localize the source.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/113032957/ssi-open-water-diver-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: Water conducts heat away from the body approximately how many times faster than air?
questions.explanation: Water's thermal conductivity is roughly 25x that of air, which is why divers can become cold even in warm water.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: An early sign of mild hypothermia in a diver is:
questions.explanation: Mild hypothermia presents with shivering and mental confusion. When shivering stops, hypothermia is becoming severe.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: If a diver experiences pain in the ear during descent, the correct action is to:

options.b: Stop the descent and ascend until the pain stops

questions.explanation: Ascending slightly relieves the pressure differential; equalize then continue. Never push past pain.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/84015479/ssi-open-water-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: The purpose of equalization is to:

options.a: Equalize the pressure between dive buddies
options.b: Introduce additional air into body air spaces in response to increasing external pressure
options.c: Remove excess nitrogen from the lungs

questions.explanation: Equalization adds gas to airspaces (middle ear, sinuses, mask) so internal pressure matches ambient water pressure.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/84015479/ssi-open-water-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: Ear squeeze can be prevented by:

options.a: Relaxing and never diving with a cold
options.b: Equalizing early and often (e.g., Valsalva, swallowing, jaw movement)

questions.explanation: All listed techniques help prevent middle-ear barotrauma during descent.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/84015479/ssi-open-water-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: The first symptom of sinus squeeze is usually:

options.b: A sharp pain or wedging sensation above the eyes

questions.explanation: Frontal-sinus squeeze typically causes pain over the forehead/behind the eyes during descent.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: If a diver experiences ear pain during ascent, the correct action is to:

options.c: Stop the ascent and descend slightly until the pain stops

questions.explanation: A reverse block traps expanding gas. Descending a bit lets the trapped gas vent, then continue ascent slowly.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The most serious lung overexpansion injury is:
questions.explanation: AGE introduces gas bubbles into the arterial circulation, potentially causing stroke-like symptoms or death.
questions.source: https://brainly.com/question/43943372

questions.question: The most important rule to prevent lung overexpansion injuries is:

options.b: Breathe continuously and never hold your breath while ascending

questions.explanation: Holding the breath while ascending allows expanding gas to rupture alveoli — the cause of AGE and other lung overexpansion injuries.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Nitrogen narcosis typically begins to affect the average diver at a depth of approximately:
questions.explanation: Most divers begin to feel narcosis around 30 m / 100 ft, although individual susceptibility varies.
questions.source: https://scubaboard.com/community/threads/nitrogen-narcosis-according-to-ssi.493656/

questions.question: Symptoms of nitrogen narcosis are most similar to:
questions.explanation: Narcosis impairs judgment, reasoning and short-term memory and feels much like mild alcohol intoxication.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The best response if a diver shows signs of narcosis is to:

options.a: Continue the dive at the same depth
options.b: Descend a bit deeper to test the effect
options.d: Take several deep breaths and continue

questions.explanation: Ascending to a shallower depth quickly resolves narcosis as the partial pressure of nitrogen drops.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: CNS oxygen toxicity becomes substantially more likely above a partial pressure of oxygen of:
questions.explanation: Risk of CNS toxicity rises sharply above ppO2 of 1.6 bar; recreational EAN diving uses a 1.4 bar working limit.
questions.source: https://cozumelscuba.com/wp-content/uploads/Nitrox-Exams.pdf

questions.question: Symptoms of CNS oxygen toxicity can be remembered with the acronym ConVENTID. The 'C' stands for:
questions.explanation: ConVENTID: Convulsions, Vision changes, Ear ringing, Nausea, Twitching, Irritability, Dizziness.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: An excess buildup of carbon dioxide (hypercapnia) in a diver is most often caused by:

options.a: Skip breathing or shallow rapid breathing
options.c: Diving with a pure oxygen mixture

questions.explanation: Skip-breathing or under-ventilation traps CO2 and triggers headache, shortness of breath, even unconsciousness.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The most likely source of carbon monoxide contamination in a scuba cylinder is:

options.b: A compressor intake located near engine exhaust

questions.explanation: CO contamination usually comes from combustion gases drawn into a poorly placed compressor intake.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Decompression sickness is caused by:

options.b: Inert gas (typically nitrogen) coming out of solution and forming bubbles in tissues or blood
options.c: Low CO2 in the blood after a long dive

questions.explanation: When ambient pressure drops faster than the body can off-gas dissolved nitrogen, bubbles form in tissues and blood.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The recommended ascent rate that recreational divers should not exceed is:
questions.explanation: SSI recommends not exceeding 30 ft / 9 m per minute on ascent for recreational diving.
questions.source: https://cozumelscuba.com/wp-content/uploads/Exam-English-7.pdf

questions.question: A safety stop should be performed at:
questions.explanation: SSI recommends a 3-5 minute safety stop at 5 m / 15 ft on every dive.
questions.source: https://cozumelscuba.com/wp-content/uploads/Exam-English-7.pdf

questions.question: In dive-table theory, a 'tissue half-time' refers to the time required for a tissue compartment to:

options.a: Reach 50% saturation or desaturation under a constant pressure differential

questions.explanation: Half-time is the time for a compartment to reach 50% of the way toward equilibrium with the new pressure.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/516127466/ssi-science-of-diving-flash-cards/

questions.question: The definitive treatment for decompression sickness is:

options.c: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy in a recompression chamber

questions.explanation: Recompression in a hyperbaric chamber, breathing 100% O2, is the definitive treatment. Surface 100% O2 is critical first aid.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The most important first-aid measure for a suspected DCS case at the surface is:

options.a: Have the diver re-enter the water to recompress
options.b: Administer 100% oxygen and contact emergency services
options.d: Lay the diver in head-down position

questions.explanation: Surface 100% O2 reduces the pressure gradient driving inert gas out of bubbles. In-water recompression is contraindicated for recreational divers.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Which of the following increases the risk of decompression sickness?
questions.explanation: Dehydration, fatigue, obesity, alcohol, age and cold all elevate DCS risk.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: After a single no-decompression dive, the recommended minimum surface interval before flying is:
questions.explanation: DAN recommends a minimum 12-hour surface interval after a single no-stop dive (18+ hours after multi-day repetitive diving).
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: After multiple days of repetitive diving, the recommended preflight surface interval is at least:
questions.explanation: DAN flying-after-diving guidelines recommend at least 18 hours after multiple repetitive dives.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Compared with a first dive, the no-decompression limit for a repetitive dive (within a few hours) at the same depth will be:
questions.explanation: Residual nitrogen from the previous dive reduces available NDL on the next dive.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Compared to a steel cylinder of similar capacity, an aluminum cylinder is generally:
questions.explanation: Aluminum cylinders are more buoyant; many become positively buoyant when nearly empty.
questions.source: https://thedivingblog.com/aluminum-steel-scuba-cylinders/

questions.question: A scuba cylinder marked '3AA' indicates it is made of:
questions.explanation: DOT 3AA marking indicates a chrome-moly steel cylinder; 3AL indicates aluminum alloy.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The function of a scuba regulator's first stage is to:

options.a: Deliver air on demand at ambient pressure
options.b: Reduce cylinder pressure to an intermediate pressure
options.c: Filter the air from the tank

questions.explanation: First stage reduces high cylinder pressure (200+ bar) to an intermediate pressure (~9-10 bar above ambient). Second stage delivers it on demand.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The function of a scuba regulator's second stage is to:

options.a: Reduce cylinder pressure to intermediate pressure
options.b: Deliver air to the diver on demand at ambient pressure

questions.explanation: Second stage takes intermediate-pressure air and delivers it to the diver at ambient water pressure when the diver inhales.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The primary purpose of a BCD (buoyancy compensator) is to:

options.b: Control buoyancy at the surface and at depth

questions.explanation: A BCD provides surface flotation, lets you control buoyancy underwater, and supports the cylinder.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/113032957/ssi-open-water-diver-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: A correctly-weighted diver at the surface, with an empty BCD and holding a normal breath, should:

options.c: Float with the head completely out of water

questions.explanation: Correct weighting: at the surface, eye-level float with full breath, empty BCD; sinks slowly when exhaling.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A diver wearing a 7 mm wetsuit will typically need:

options.a: Less weight than in a 3 mm suit
options.b: More weight than in a 3 mm suit
options.c: The same weight as in a 3 mm suit

questions.explanation: Thicker neoprene contains more closed-cell gas, providing more buoyancy and requiring more lead.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Mask squeeze is prevented by:

options.b: Periodically exhaling air through the nose into the mask during descent
options.c: Holding the mask hard against the face

questions.explanation: Adding air through the nose equalizes the airspace inside the mask with ambient pressure.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A snorkel is primarily useful to:

options.b: Conserve cylinder gas while breathing at the surface
options.c: Provide an emergency reserve at depth

questions.explanation: Snorkels conserve cylinder air for surface breathing during long swims at the surface.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A dive computer continuously calculates and displays:

options.b: Depth, time, and remaining no-decompression time

questions.explanation: Dive computers track depth, elapsed time and a tissue-compartment model to compute remaining NDL.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: When diving in a current, divers should generally:

options.b: Begin the dive heading into the current
options.d: Avoid all currents at all costs

questions.explanation: Begin into the current so the easier downcurrent leg occurs after fatigue and air consumption have built up.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Slack tide is defined as:

options.b: Period of minimal current between tide changes
options.c: The deepest point of low tide
options.d: The highest point of high tide

questions.explanation: Slack water occurs at the turn between tides when current speed is at its minimum.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Most marine animal injuries to divers are the result of:

options.a: Aggressive predatory behavior of the animal
options.b: Diver negligence, ignorance, or aggressive behavior

questions.explanation: Statistically, divers are usually injured because they touched, harassed or surprised marine life.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/113032957/ssi-open-water-diver-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: First aid for a venomous fish (lionfish/scorpionfish/stonefish) puncture wound includes:

options.a: Immerse the wound in hot (non-scalding) water

questions.explanation: Heat denatures the protein-based venom; soak in water as hot as the patient can tolerate (~45 C / 113 F) for 30-90 min.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Which two flags signal divers in the water?
questions.explanation: The 'sport diver' (red with diagonal white stripe) flag and the international Alpha flag both warn that divers are below.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/84015479/ssi-open-water-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: Divers can help protect the marine environment by:

options.c: Acting as a responsible diver and not touching marine life

questions.explanation: All listed actions reduce diver impact on fragile reef ecosystems.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/113032957/ssi-open-water-diver-final-exam-flash-cards/

questions.question: Surface Air Consumption (SAC) is best defined as:

options.a: Air consumed per minute at depth
options.b: Air consumed per minute normalized to surface pressure (1 ATA)
options.c: Total air consumed during a dive

questions.explanation: SAC normalizes consumption to 1 ATA so it can be compared across different depths and dives.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: If a diver uses 50 bar at 10 m in 10 minutes from a 10-litre cylinder, what is their SAC rate?
questions.explanation: Volume used = 50 bar x 10 L = 500 L over 10 min = 50 L/min at 2 ATA. Normalized to 1 ATA = 25 L/min SAC.
questions.source: https://www.proscubadiver.net/padi-course-director-joey-ridge/gas-laws-physics/

questions.question: What is the absolute pressure at 99 ft (30 m) of seawater?
questions.explanation: 99 ft / 33 ft per ATA = 3 ATA water + 1 ATA atmospheric = 4 ATA absolute.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Each 10 m of seawater adds approximately how much pressure?
questions.explanation: Saltwater: 10 m equals approximately 1 bar gauge pressure.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Each 10.3 m of fresh water adds approximately how much pressure?
questions.explanation: Freshwater is slightly less dense than salt; 10.3 m of fresh water = 1 bar.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A typical aluminum 80 cu ft cylinder has a buoyancy characteristic of approximately:

options.a: Negative full and full positive empty
options.c: Slightly negative full, slightly positive when empty

questions.explanation: AL80s typically start ~1.5 kg negative full and become ~0.9 kg positive empty.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Atmospheric air is approximately:
questions.explanation: Air is roughly 78% N2, 21% O2, 0.93% argon and trace CO2/other gases.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Hyperventilation before a breath-hold dive primarily increases breath-hold time by:

options.b: Decreasing blood CO2 levels and delaying the urge to breathe

questions.explanation: Hyperventilation lowers CO2 (the main breathing trigger) and can lead to shallow-water blackout from hypoxia of ascent.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Shallow water blackout in breath-hold divers is caused by:

options.b: Hypoxia (low O2) due to dropping ppO2 on ascent after hyperventilation

questions.explanation: Hyperventilation suppresses CO2 drive; oxygen is consumed at depth; on ascent ppO2 drops below the level needed for consciousness.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Near-drowning differs from drowning in that:

options.b: The victim survived the initial submersion incident
options.c: It only occurs during cold-water dives
options.d: It is treated with hot water immersion

questions.explanation: Near-drowning: the victim survived the immediate submersion. They still need urgent medical assessment.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: If you double the absolute pressure on a fixed mass of gas at constant temperature, the volume will:
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P1V1 = P2V2; doubling pressure halves volume.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: If a balloon contains 6 L of air at 4 ATA, what is its volume at 1 ATA (surface)?
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: At 30 m / 99 ft (4 ATA), the partial pressure of oxygen in air is approximately:
questions.explanation: ppO2 = FO2 x ambient = 0.21 x 4 = 0.84 bar.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: At 40 m on EAN32, the partial pressure of oxygen is:
questions.explanation: ppO2 = 0.32 x 5 ATA = 1.6 bar — the recreational MOD limit for EAN32.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: What is the maximum operating depth (MOD) for EAN32 at a 1.4 bar ppO2 limit?
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: What is the MOD for EAN36 at a 1.4 bar ppO2 limit?
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The primary benefit of recreational nitrox compared to air is:

options.b: Extended no-decompression limits at recreational depths
options.c: Eliminates the need for safety stops

questions.explanation: Lower nitrogen fraction means less inert-gas loading, giving longer NDL — but MOD is shallower because of higher O2.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Before every nitrox dive, the diver should personally:

options.a: Trust the fill station's label only
options.b: Analyze the cylinder's oxygen content and log it
options.c: Test the regulator on land first

questions.explanation: The diver who breathes the gas is responsible for analyzing and logging FO2 prior to every nitrox dive.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: In-water recompression for a recreational diver suspected of DCS is:

options.b: Only acceptable in remote locations with proper gas supply and trained personnel
options.d: The same as a safety stop

questions.explanation: In-water recompression is highly risky for recreational divers and is not standard practice; surface O2 and evacuation to a chamber is the protocol.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Gases may separate out of solution to form bubbles, leading to DCS, when a diver:

options.c: Exceeds the ascent rate or makes a rapid uncontrolled ascent

questions.explanation: Rapid ascent doesn't allow nitrogen to off-gas safely, allowing bubbles to form.
questions.source: https://cozumelscuba.com/wp-content/uploads/Exam-English-7.pdf

questions.question: Type I (mild) decompression sickness typically presents with:

options.a: Joint pain (the bends), skin rashes, itching

questions.explanation: Type I DCS = pain-only or skin/lymphatic; Type II = neurological/cardiopulmonary, more serious.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Type II (serious) decompression sickness involves:
questions.explanation: Type II includes paralysis, numbness, vertigo, breathing difficulty — life-threatening.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Most cases of DCS show symptoms within:

options.b: 1-3 hours of surfacing (over 80% within the first hour)

questions.explanation: Symptoms onset is most commonly within 1-3 hours; >80% within the first hour.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Basic components of a diver's compass include:
questions.explanation: All listed parts are essential to a standard navigational diver compass.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/225226263/dive-ssi-specialty-course-navigation-sc-nv-flash-cards/

questions.question: To navigate to a sighted object using a compass, the correct procedure is to:

options.a: Aim and follow the needle directly
options.b: Point the compass at the object, rotate the bezel so the witness marks line up with the magnetic needle, then follow the lubber line
options.d: Swim until the needle reads zero

questions.explanation: This procedure stores the bearing on the bezel so you can re-acquire course if you drift.
questions.source: https://quizlet.com/225226263/dive-ssi-specialty-course-navigation-sc-nv-flash-cards/

questions.question: When a metal object comes close to a compass, the compass will:

options.b: Be deviated and give a false reading

questions.explanation: Ferrous metals deflect the compass needle. Keep dive tools and tank valves away from the compass when reading bearings.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Tides are caused primarily by:

options.c: Gravitational pull of the moon and sun on the oceans

questions.explanation: Lunar (and solar) gravitational forces drive tidal cycles; earth rotation modulates timing.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A rip current is best described as:

options.a: A strong, narrow current flowing seaward from a beach

questions.explanation: Rip currents form when water piled on the beach by waves drains back through a gap in sandbars.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: If caught in a rip current, the proper response is to:

options.a: Swim straight back to shore against it
options.b: Swim parallel to shore until out of the current, then back to shore
options.d: Float and wait for the tide

questions.explanation: Swim parallel to the beach to escape the narrow rip channel, then come ashore.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Waves are mainly caused by:

options.b: Wind blowing across the surface of the water

questions.explanation: Wind transferring energy to the water surface forms wind waves; tsunamis come from seismic events but normal waves are wind-generated.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A thermocline is:

options.a: A zone of rapid temperature change with depth

questions.explanation: Thermocline = a layer where water temperature drops sharply with depth. Halocline = sharp salinity change.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A halocline is:

options.a: A layer of rapid temperature change
options.b: A layer of rapid salinity change with depth
options.c: An underwater fog of suspended sediment
options.d: A sharp visibility boundary at the surface

questions.explanation: Haloclines occur where fresh and salt water meet (e.g., cenotes, fjords) and can blur vision at the boundary.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Charles's Law describes the relationship between:

options.b: Volume and temperature at constant pressure

questions.explanation: At constant pressure, volume of a fixed gas mass is proportional to absolute temperature.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Gay-Lussac's Law says that, at constant volume, the pressure of a gas:
questions.explanation: P/T = constant. This is why a hot scuba cylinder shows higher pressure than the same cylinder when cool.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.subarea: Physics & Chemistry — Cylinder pressure and temperature
questions.question: If a scuba cylinder filled to 200 bar at 20 C is left in hot sun until it reaches 50 C, the pressure (assuming sealed and rigid) will:
questions.explanation: Gay-Lussac: P is proportional to T (kelvin). Heating raises pressure; do not leave full cylinders in hot sun.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Immersion pulmonary edema (IPE) presents with:

options.b: Coughing up frothy fluid, shortness of breath, sometimes blood-tinged sputum

questions.explanation: IPE is fluid in the lungs caused by immersion stress. Get the diver out of the water and seek medical care.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A patent foramen ovale (PFO) is:

options.a: A small opening between the heart's atria that can allow venous bubbles to bypass the lungs

questions.explanation: A PFO can shunt venous bubbles into arterial circulation, increasing risk of unprovoked DCS.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: When ascending in a dry suit, the diver must:

options.a: Vent expanding gas from the suit to prevent uncontrolled ascent
options.b: Add gas to the suit to maintain warmth
options.d: Remove the suit at the safety stop

questions.explanation: Drysuit gas expands on ascent (Boyle's Law) and must be vented to maintain control.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The most common cause of an uncontrolled ascent in a dry suit is:

options.b: Failing to vent gas as you ascend (especially if inverted)

questions.explanation: Trapped gas in feet/legs of a dry suit can cause a feet-up runaway ascent; keep ankles below chest and vent.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The 'fight-or-flight' response in divers can lead to:

options.b: Rapid breathing, tunnel vision, panic, and poor decision-making

questions.explanation: Acute stress narrows attention, increases breathing rate and impairs problem solving — a key hazard underwater.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Most scuba cylinders require visual inspection at intervals of:
questions.explanation: Annual visual inspection is industry standard; hydrostatic testing typically every 5 years (varies by jurisdiction).
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A hydrostatic (pressure) test on a scuba cylinder is typically required:
questions.explanation: Most jurisdictions: hydrostatic test every 5 years. Some (e.g., Australia) annual.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: At 20 m / 66 ft (3 ATA), a diver will breathe air from the cylinder approximately how many times faster than at the surface?
questions.explanation: Density (and thus volume of air consumed per breath at surface-equivalent) scales with absolute pressure: 3 ATA = 3x.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: 1 bar is approximately equal to:
questions.explanation: 1 bar ~ 14.5 psi ~ 0.987 atm. Useful for cross-converting tank pressures.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The recommended minimum reserve pressure to begin returning to the exit is typically:
questions.explanation: Plan to be at the exit/safety stop with around 50 bar / 700 psi remaining as a reserve.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Diving at high altitude requires adjusted procedures because:

options.a: Atmospheric pressure is lower than at sea level

questions.explanation: Lower surface pressure changes the pressure ratio for off-gassing; depth gauges and tables must be corrected.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: The submersible pressure gauge (SPG) measures:
questions.explanation: SPG reads remaining cylinder pressure; modern computers often integrate this wirelessly.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Alternobaric vertigo is most often caused by:

options.b: Unequal middle-ear pressure between right and left ears

questions.explanation: Asymmetric equalization causes unequal pressure between the two middle ears, leading to dizziness.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Nitrogen is eliminated from the body primarily through:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen off-gassing happens through the pulmonary circulation and is exhaled.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: On dive tables, a pressure group letter represents:

options.b: The relative amount of residual nitrogen in the body

questions.explanation: Pressure-group letters quantify residual nitrogen for repetitive-dive planning.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Increasing the surface interval between dives generally:

options.b: Decreases residual nitrogen, allowing longer NDL on the next dive

questions.explanation: Off-gassing during the surface interval reduces residual N2, increasing available NDL on the next dive.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: An object weighing 30 kg in air with a volume of 25 L is fully immersed in seawater (density 1.03 kg/L). It will be:
questions.explanation: Buoyant force = 25 x 1.03 = 25.75 kg. Weight 30 kg > buoyant force 25.75, so net 4.25 kg negative.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: How many liters of lift are needed to make a 5 kg negative object neutrally buoyant in seawater?

options.b: About 5 L (since 1 L of seawater displacement provides about 1 kg of lift)

questions.explanation: Each liter of seawater displacement gives ~1.03 kg of lift; ~5 L offsets 5 kg negative.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A mask should fit such that:

options.a: It seals on the face by suction without the strap
options.b: It is loosely fitted and held by the strap
options.c: It covers the mouth and nose
options.d: It fits only over the eyes

questions.explanation: A properly-sized mask seals via gentle suction even before the strap is tightened.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Pulmonary oxygen toxicity differs from CNS oxygen toxicity in that pulmonary toxicity:

options.b: Develops over long exposures to elevated ppO2 (e.g., long chamber treatments)

questions.explanation: Pulmonary (whole-body) toxicity is dose-dependent over hours; CNS toxicity is acute and depends on ppO2.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Most body heat is lost during a dive through:

options.b: The head and torso (large surface area, blood flow)

questions.explanation: The head and torso are major heat-loss areas; a hood significantly reduces heat loss.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: A reverse-profile dive (deeper after shallower) is generally:

options.b: Allowed for recreational diving but with appropriate planning

questions.explanation: Modern guidance accepts reverse profiles with conservative planning, but the traditional advice is deepest first.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Which equalization technique uses the throat muscles, similar to swallowing?
questions.explanation: Frenzel uses tongue/throat muscles to push air into the Eustachian tubes; gentler than Valsalva.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Forceful Valsalva equalization can cause:

options.a: Round window rupture or inner-ear barotrauma

questions.explanation: Forceful Valsalva spikes intracranial and middle-ear pressures and can rupture round-window membrane.
questions.source: compiled_from_dive_theory

questions.question: Which factor determines whether stress is positive or negative for you?

options.b: Your ability to anticipate the consequences

questions.explanation: SSI teaches that whether stress is positive (helpful, alerting) or negative (overwhelming) depends on three interrelated factors: your sense of choice, your ability to anticipate consequences, and your degree of control over the situation. When all three are present, the same stimulus produces useful arousal that keeps a diver alert; when they are missing, that arousal becomes a threat to safety. Recognizing this is why SSI emphasizes good planning, training within limits, and the buddy system — they preserve choice, anticipation, and control.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: The tolerance for stress for everyone is:
questions.explanation: Stress tolerance is individual — it varies from diver to diver and even from day to day for the same diver. Personality, training, experience, fitness, fatigue, and mood all shape how much demand a person can absorb before stress becomes negative. This is why SSI instructors must observe each student individually and not assume that what is comfortable for one diver is comfortable for another. Recognizing differences in tolerance is the foundation for matching buddy teams and adjusting dive plans to the weakest link.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: If a demand overpowers your ability to maintain control over the situation:
questions.explanation: When a demand exceeds the diver's capacity to respond, stress does not stay constant — it multiplies. One stressor compounds with another in a cycle that, if unchecked, can escalate toward panic. SSI teaches that the way to break this cycle is to recognize the early signs and act on them: stop, breathe, rest, think, then act. Aborting the dive may eventually be the right call, but the immediate priority is regaining control before stress overwhelms you.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: Stress can be detected by you or another who is close by at which point?

options.b: At any point as it develops

questions.explanation: SSI doctrine is clear: stress can be detected at any point as it develops — before, during, or after the dive. A diver who is unusually quiet at the briefing, who fumbles gear at the entry, who breathes rapidly underwater, or who is shaken on the boat afterward is showing signs that you or a buddy can act on. Because stress can appear at any moment, vigilance is continuous; the buddy system works precisely because there is almost always at least one diver in the team who maintains control and can lend aid.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: It is easier to respond to stress:
questions.explanation: Early detection is the single most important factor in managing stress. While stress is still mild, the diver retains choice, control, and the ability to anticipate — exactly the qualities that keep stress positive. Once stress has multiplied and approached panic, those resources are gone and intervention becomes far harder and riskier. This is why SSI trains divers to know their own early warning signs (rapid heartbeat, shortness of breath, muscle tension) and to watch buddies for the same, so action can be taken at the earliest possible moment.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: You can consciously recognize a new demand before it arises and respond to it fast. Right or wrong?
questions.explanation: Right. A trained diver who anticipates conditions, rehearses skills, and stays mentally engaged can recognize a developing demand before it becomes a crisis and respond quickly. This is exactly what SSI's emphasis on preparation, dive planning, and skill mastery builds: the ability to see a problem coming — running low on gas, current picking up, a buddy slowing down — and act early while choice and control are still intact. Anticipation is one of the three factors that keeps stress positive rather than negative.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: The positive stress of diving can turn into negative stress: Right or wrong?
questions.explanation: Right. The same arousal that makes diving exciting and alerting can flip into negative stress if demands begin to outweigh the diver's capacity to respond. A drop in visibility, unexpected current, equipment trouble, or simply accumulating fatigue can push a diver from comfortably alert to overwhelmed. Recognizing this transition early — before it multiplies toward panic — is the diver's job and the buddy's job. Healthy diving stress is a consistent low level; the moment it begins climbing, it is time to act.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: The sudden stimulus we feel when frightened, startled, or threatened is called?
questions.explanation: The fight-or-flight syndrome is the body's automatic response when we are frightened, startled, or threatened. Adrenaline rises, heart rate and breathing accelerate, blood is shunted to large muscles, and senses sharpen — preparing us to either confront or escape the threat. In diving, this primitive reaction can be useful in small doses but dangerous when uncontrolled, because the urge to flee underwater (e.g., bolting to the surface) can cause lung overexpansion injury or DCS. Recognizing the response is the first step to managing it through stop-breathe-think-act.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: The first step in learning to control stress and respond to it logically is:

options.d: Ignore signs of stress if you are out of the water

questions.explanation: Recognizing the signs of stress is always the first step. You cannot manage what you cannot see. Once a diver — or a buddy, or an instructor — can identify the early warning signs (rapid heartbeat, shortness of breath, muscle tension, wide eyes, unusual quiet, fumbling gear), logical responses become possible: stop, breathe slowly, rest, assess, alleviate. SSI builds this awareness throughout the Stress & Rescue program because every later skill depends on it.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: Early warning signs for stress include:

options.a: Rapid heartbeat, Shortness of breath, Muscle tension
options.b: Pain in the chest, fullness in the neck

questions.explanation: The classic early warning signs of stress are rapid heartbeat, shortness of breath, and muscle tension. They are the first physiological hints that the fight-or-flight system is engaging and that demand is approaching the diver's tolerance. Catching these signs early — in yourself or a buddy — gives you the widest window to break the stress cycle before it multiplies. Chest pain, slurred speech, and similar symptoms suggest medical emergencies, not early stress; comfort and relaxed breathing are the opposite of warning signs.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: A big step toward being able to manage stress in a social situation is to realize?

options.a: That no one is immune to fear
options.b: That everyone is immune to fear
options.c: That stress in social situation cannot be solved.
options.d: To ignore the fears of others and take care of yourself

questions.explanation: Realizing that no one is immune to fear removes the social pressure to hide stress. Divers — including very experienced ones — feel anxiety, and pretending otherwise (to fit in, to look brave, to avoid letting a buddy down) only drives stress underground where it cannot be addressed. SSI teaches divers to admit anxiety, talk about it, and treat it as useful information rather than weakness. This openness is what allows buddy teams to support each other and what allows instructors to manage classes safely.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: Scuba diving is an activity that provides pleasure and enjoyment to the participant, therefore it is:

options.a: A challenge to be won at all costs
options.b: A challenge for all ages to be won
options.c: NOT a challenge to be won or lost
options.d: A challenge to be won or lost

questions.explanation: Scuba diving is NOT a challenge to be won or lost. It is a recreational activity meant to provide pleasure and enjoyment. Treating a dive as something to be won — pushing through warning signs, refusing to abort, ignoring limits to save face — is one of the surest ways to convert positive stress into negative stress and ultimately into an accident. Any dive can be safely called off at any time, by any diver, for any reason, with no loss of standing. This mindset is central to SSI's safety culture.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: It is the combinations of stressors that can threaten your control. Right or wrong?
questions.explanation: Right. A single stressor — slight current, mild cold, a sticky inflator — is usually well within a trained diver's tolerance. Danger arises when several minor stressors combine: cold plus current plus low visibility plus a fogging mask plus a slow buddy. Each item alone is trivial; together they can overwhelm. SSI teaches divers to inventory stressors before the dive, watch for accumulation during the dive, and act early when several are stacking up, because it is the combination — not any one factor — that most often threatens control.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: Stress can be quite useful because:

options.a: It is a warning sign that you can`t do anything about it.
options.b: It can be a simple warning sign which will alert you to do nothing about a particular demand.
options.c: It can be a simple warning sign which will alert you to do something about a particular demand
options.d: It keeps your heart beating faster

questions.explanation: Stress is useful precisely because it is a warning sign that alerts you to DO SOMETHING about a particular demand. Healthy stress is a consistent low level that keeps a diver alert and tells you when conditions are changing or capacity is being approached. Treating that signal as information — not as something to suppress or ignore — lets you stop, assess, and take action while you still have control. A diver who never feels any stress is not paying attention; a diver who feels it and acts on it is diving safely.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: Panic is an overpowering fear caused by?

options.a: A real or imagined control over a situation
options.b: A real or imagined control over a dive plan
options.c: A real or imagined loss of breathing gas due to a leak
options.d: A real or imagined loss of control over a situation

questions.explanation: Panic is overpowering fear caused by a real or imagined loss of control over a situation. The trigger does not have to be objectively dangerous — the perception of helplessness is enough. This is why SSI focuses so heavily on building skills and preserving choice, anticipation, and control: a diver who feels capable, even in a difficult situation, is far less likely to panic than one who feels trapped. Once panic begins, rational thought collapses and behavior becomes dangerous (bolting, fighting the regulator, ditching gear), so the entire stress-management chain is built around preventing it.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: You can begin to understand how stress can multiply into panic and how to stop it, if you are aware of:

options.a: The air consumption of your Buddy
options.b: The common sequence of events that occurs prior to the onset of panic
options.c: The common depth in which panic occurs

questions.explanation: Awareness of the common sequence of events leading to panic — minor stressor, accumulation, rising heart rate and breathing, narrowing focus, loss of control — is what allows you to interrupt the chain before it completes. SSI teaches this sequence so divers can spot themselves or a buddy on the path toward panic early, while intervention is still simple. Without this map, the warning signs look random; with it, they look like steps on a recognizable ladder you can step off at any time.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: If you are aware of the cycle of stress you can beat it at any time, by:

options.b: Simply continue, ignore the causes, and take actions to abort the dive
options.c: Swimming as fast as you can to the surface if stress occurs
options.d: Simply stop, rest, assess the causes, and take actions to alleviate them 17 ©

questions.explanation: The SSI response to mounting stress is: stop, rest, assess the causes, and take actions to alleviate them. Stopping breaks momentum; resting and slow breathing reset the physiology; assessing identifies which stressor is driving the spiral; alleviating addresses it (vent some gas, ditch a weight, signal the buddy, ascend slowly, end the dive). Relying solely on a buddy gives up your own control; ignoring causes lets them grow; bolting to the surface invites lung overexpansion or DCS. The stop-rest-assess-alleviate cycle is the standard answer at any depth, at any time.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 1 Review

questions.question: Stress usually originates in divers because of five reasons:

options.a: Comfort, self-confidence, well maintained equipment, environmental conditions, or lack of skill.
options.b: Dive plan, psychological causes, equipment, environmental conditions, or lack of water
options.c: Physical causes, psychological causes, equipment problems, environmental conditions, or mastering of skills
options.d: Physical causes, psychological causes, equipment problems, environmental conditions, or lack of skill.

questions.explanation: SSI groups the origins of diver stress into five categories: physical causes (fitness, fatigue, cold), psychological causes (lack of mental preparation, fear, overconfidence), equipment problems (misuse, poor fit, poor maintenance), environmental conditions (current, visibility, depth, marine life), and lack of skill (inadequate training or rusty practice). Knowing these categories lets a diver inventory potential stressors before a dive and address each one through preparation. The other answer choices either replace key categories with their opposites (comfort, well-maintained equipment, mastering of skills) or list irrelevant items.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: As a general rule, a diver should be fit enough to respond to an emergency without becoming:
questions.explanation: SSI's general fitness standard is that a diver must be fit enough to handle an emergency — towing a buddy, swimming against current, climbing back aboard — without becoming exhausted or risking injury. Diving puts physical demands on the body even on calm dives, and emergencies multiply those demands suddenly. A diver who reaches exhaustion mid-rescue becomes a second victim. Maintaining cardiovascular fitness, strength, and flexibility is therefore a core part of personal preparedness, alongside good skills and well-maintained equipment.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: Having a buddy and being a buddy are closely related to another major objective of any dive:

options.a: Being prepared and, consequently, avoiding stress
options.b: Being not alone and, consequently, seeking thrills
options.c: Being unprepared and, consequently, relying on my buddy
options.d: Being dressed in a Total Diving System and, consequently, diving shallow

questions.explanation: The buddy system is part of being prepared, and being prepared is what avoids stress. A good buddy doubles your eyes, hands, and air supply; checks your gear and is checked by you; and provides reassurance throughout the dive. But the buddy system does not replace personal preparation — it complements it. The wrong attitude is using a buddy as an excuse to be unprepared (option c). The right attitude is two prepared divers who together make a still-safer team and consequently avoid the stressors that come from feeling alone or under-equipped.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: If you want to dive in a dry suit, it is recommended that you:

options.a: Try different brands before buying one
options.b: Do at least 100 dives in wetsuit before
options.c: Participate in an SSI Dry Suit specialty program
options.d: Buy the most expensive dry suit, because high price is always high quality

questions.explanation: Drysuits handle very differently from wetsuits — buoyancy is controlled by adding and venting air through a separate suit valve, and a drysuit can become its own runaway buoyancy device or trap air in the feet. SSI's strong recommendation is therefore to take the SSI Dry Suit specialty program before diving one. The course covers buoyancy, valve use, undergarments, fit, emergency procedures, and maintenance. Trying brands and avoiding cheap suits are reasonable shopping advice, but the safety-critical step is formal training under an instructor.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: You should be able to avoid stress and have a gratifying experience if:

options.a: You have a good buddy and can rely on him/her
options.b: You use expensive equipment, dive shallow, and are prepared to abort the dive at any time
options.c: You use good judgment, dive within your limits, and are prepared with the proper equipment
options.d: You use good equipment, dive beyond your limits, and are prepared to abort the dive at any time

questions.explanation: The SSI recipe for a stress-free, gratifying dive is: good judgment, diving within your limits, and being prepared with the proper equipment. These three pillars cover the psychological, training, and equipment categories of stress in one stroke. Relying solely on a buddy outsources your safety; expensive gear does not substitute for skill; and diving beyond your limits with the intention of bailing out if needed is exactly the mindset that produces accidents. Limits, judgment, and proper gear keep choice, anticipation, and control on your side.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: A good buddy team depends on proper matching, except for a pair of divers with:
questions.explanation: A good buddy team must be matched in skills, fitness, equipment, and air consumption — but the one thing buddies must share, not differ on, is dive objectives. The question asks for the exception to matching, i.e., the one factor that must NOT be different. If one diver wants to photograph a wreck while the other wants to do a high-speed drift, the team will split underwater no matter how well they match in other ways. Differences in confidence, experience, or carrying a camera are workable; differences in objective are not.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: Which are the basic things buddies can do while diving to assure confidence?
questions.explanation: All answers are correct. Confident buddy teams do three things continuously: they work as a team and constantly communicate (signals, eye contact, planning); they provide support when needed (help with gear, share gas, assist with skills); and they monitor each other's actions (gas, depth, body language, positioning). Each behavior reinforces the others, and together they keep both divers in the team aware of the situation and ready to intervene early — which is exactly the moment stress is easiest to manage.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: Even if a diver may be nervous for a variety of reasons, there is one thing that can make for a safe and enjoyable dive for both parties.

options.b: The reassurance of a full diving tank
options.d: The reassurance of a caring buddy

questions.explanation: The reassurance of a caring buddy is the single biggest factor that can turn a nervous dive into a safe and enjoyable one for both divers. Knowing that another diver is genuinely watching out for you — checking on you, ready to help, willing to call the dive without judgment — restores choice and control, the two ingredients that keep stress positive. This is why SSI emphasizes the human side of the buddy system, not just the procedural side: buddies are not just safety partners but emotional anchors.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: The use of controlled substances (drugs, medication)is discouraged because of?

options.a: Their ability to help you relaxing during the dive.
options.b: Their inability to impair judgment and affect alertness.
options.c: Their ability to impair judgment and affect alertness and other mental faculties

questions.explanation: Drugs and medications — including many over-the-counter ones — are discouraged before diving because of their ability to impair judgment, reduce alertness, and affect other mental faculties. The same depth, narcosis, and cold that a sober diver can handle may overwhelm a medicated one, and the interaction of medications with pressure is often unstudied. Divers on prescription medication should consult a diving physician. The principle is simple: scuba demands clear thinking, and anything that clouds it raises stress and risk.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: Alcohol should be:

options.a: Not be abused the night before a dive, should not be used between dives, and should be used in moderation at all other times
options.b: Used only if you dive to a maximum depth of 30 meters
options.c: Abused the night before and can be used between dives
options.d: Used between dives to warm up

questions.explanation: SSI's guidance on alcohol is straightforward: do not abuse it the night before a dive, do not use it between dives, and use it in moderation at all other times. Alcohol dehydrates the body, impairs judgment, disturbs sleep, and increases susceptibility to decompression sickness — all of which compound diving stress. Using it between dives is particularly dangerous because the diver is still off-gassing and has more dives planned. Moderation outside of diving days respects both health and the next time you put a regulator in your mouth.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: If you should decide to indulge in alcohol the night before, you should:

options.b: Do deep dives to enjoy the increased nitrogen narcosis

questions.explanation: If you have indulged in alcohol the night before, the SSI recommendation is to avoid diving the following day. Residual dehydration, fatigue, impaired judgment, and increased DCS susceptibility do not vanish overnight, and they combine with the normal physiological load of diving to magnify every stressor. Switching to Nitrox or limiting depth does not address the core problem. Take a surface day, hydrate, rest, and dive when you are fully fit — the dive site will still be there.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: The single largest contributor to stress , which is a much more common problem than running out of air, is?
questions.explanation: The single largest contributor to diver stress — and far more common than running out of air — is equipment misuse. This includes ill-fitting gear, gear the diver is not familiar with, gear that is poorly maintained, and gear used outside its design. Each of these creates problems mid-dive that consume attention, raise breathing rate, and stack stressors. The good news is that equipment-related stress is largely preventable: properly selected, well-fitting, well-maintained, and familiar gear virtually eliminates this category. SSI's Equipment Service Program supports this directly.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: The unexpected loss of equipment during a dive can contribute to a loss of control? Right or wrong?
questions.explanation: Right. Unexpectedly losing a piece of equipment during a dive — a fin slipping off, a mask kicked off, a weight pocket falling out, a regulator knocked from the mouth — is a sudden, unanticipated demand and therefore a textbook trigger for loss of control. The diver loses one of the three factors that keeps stress positive (anticipation), often loses a second (control over the situation), and sometimes a third (sense of choice). This is why SSI drills mask removal, regulator recovery, weight retrieval, and similar skills until they are automatic responses rather than crises.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: Equipment-related stress can involve:
questions.explanation: SSI identifies three main equipment-related stress sources, all of which can compromise comfort and safety. Not having the right equipment for the environment (e.g., insufficient exposure protection) creates physical stress and distraction. Ill-fitting equipment chafes, leaks, restricts movement and continuously demands attention underwater. Poorly maintained gear is unreliable and can fail at the worst moment, triggering acute stress or panic. Because each option describes a legitimate equipment-related stress source, the comprehensive answer is correct: divers prevent these issues by choosing properly sized gear suited to the environment and keeping their Total Diving System on the SSI Equipment Service Program.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: Simply making sure your equipment fits well, is correctly adjusted, and you know how to use it properly will?

options.b: Allows you to dive with Nitrox.
options.c: Allows you to dive in buddy teams.

questions.explanation: SSI teaches that the simplest and most powerful prevention against equipment-related stress is owning gear that fits, is correctly adjusted, and that the diver knows how to operate confidently. When the Total Diving System feels like a natural extension of the body, the diver can focus on enjoyment, buddy contact and the environment instead of fighting straps, leaks, or unfamiliar buttons. Nitrox, buddy diving and depth privileges depend on training and certification — not on fit and familiarity. Equipment ownership combined with regular use through Scuba Skills Updates and the Equipment Service Program virtually eliminates this category of stress.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: The Equipment Service Program, which is available through your SSI Training Center, is a complete maintenance program designed to:

options.a: Keep your regulator working at depth better than in shallow water
options.b: Perform hydrostatic tests on your dive tank every 10 years
options.c: Keep the components of your Total Diving System performing to the best of their potential
