options.d: Make complete service and maintenance of all your dive gear by yourself

questions.explanation: The SSI Equipment Service Program is a comprehensive, manufacturer-aligned maintenance program offered through SSI Training Centers that keeps every component of the diver's Total Diving System (regulator, BC, instruments, exposure suit, cylinder, etc.) performing to the best of its potential. Regulators must function equally well at all depths, not better at depth. Hydrostatic testing is a separate cylinder requirement, not the program's purpose. Self-service is explicitly discouraged because most dive equipment requires factory-trained technicians and proprietary parts. Regular professional service through the Equipment Service Program is the SSI-prescribed route to reliable, stress-free gear.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: You should cancel, postpone or abort a dive whenever necessary if a dive involves:
questions.explanation: Current, waves and cold water are routine environmental factors that trained divers regularly handle when they have appropriate equipment, training and experience. SSI's no-go criterion is broader: any potentially hazardous environmental condition — meaning conditions that exceed your training, equipment, fitness, or comfort level on that day. This could be excessive current, dangerous surf, very low visibility, electrical storms, or any combination that elevates risk. The decision to cancel, postpone or abort can and should be made at any time without embarrassment. Diving must always be optional; surviving is mandatory.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: If you are uncomfortable with the conditions:

options.a: Tell your buddy and continue to dive.
options.b: Don`t tell anyone, the feeling will ease as soon as you are diving
options.c: Choose not to dive, or change your diving location
options.d: Choose to dive with an extra alternate air source

questions.explanation: SSI doctrine is unambiguous: if you are uncomfortable with the conditions, you choose not to dive or change to a more suitable location. Diving while uncomfortable amplifies stress, narrows attention and dramatically increases the chance of panic underwater. Pushing on hoping the feeling will pass, or hiding it from a buddy, is exactly how preventable accidents happen. Adding redundant gear does not address the psychological source of the discomfort. Any diver, at any experience level, can call a dive at any moment without embarrassment — this principle is fundamental to safe diving and to the Stress & Rescue mindset.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: To get the chance to practice under supervision of an instructor, break bad habits, and catch up on new skills and pieces of equipment because you dive only once a year you should get?.

options.a: An annual Scuba Skills Update at your local training center
options.b: A new Open Water Diver program every two years
options.c: A new Total Diving System every year
options.d: Nitrox fills for the first 3 dives

questions.explanation: SSI prescribes an annual Scuba Skills Update at a local SSI Training Center for any diver whose diving is infrequent — typically once a year on vacation. The session is conducted under instructor supervision, lets the diver rehearse core skills (mask clearing, regulator recovery, weight checks, buoyancy, ascents), break any bad habits that have developed, and become familiar with new equipment or procedures introduced since the last dive. Repeating the Open Water program is unnecessary, replacing the Total Diving System annually is wasteful, and Nitrox fills do not address skill decay. The Scuba Skills Update is the SSI tool for maintaining proficiency.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: Added buoyancy will increase your rate of ascent as you near 9 meters, and make safety stops at 5 meters more challenging if you use:

options.a: Full aluminum cylinders on safety stop
options.b: Too much weight on your weight belt
options.c: Aluminum cylinders which are close to empty

questions.explanation: Aluminum cylinders become positively buoyant as they approach reserve pressure: the gas mass leaves the tank but the aluminum shell stays the same volume, so the rig gets lighter as the dive ends. Near 9 m the diver is already ascending against expanding wetsuit and BC bubbles, and the now-positive aluminum cylinder adds even more lift, accelerating ascent and making the 5 m safety stop hard to hold. Full aluminum cylinders are negatively buoyant, full steel cylinders even more so, and excess lead always makes the diver sink, not rise. Proper weighting must account for an empty aluminum cylinder so safety stops remain controlled.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: While diving, a perfectly weighted diver will require only small buoyancy adjustments of the?
questions.explanation: A perfectly weighted diver carries just enough lead to be neutrally buoyant at the safety stop with a near-empty cylinder and a vented BC. During the dive, only small additions or releases of air in the buoyancy compensator (BC) are needed to compensate for depth changes and wetsuit compression. The weight system is set up before the dive, not adjusted underwater. The Delivery System (regulator/cylinder) and the Information System (computer/SPG/compass) are not buoyancy tools. SSI emphasizes correct weighting because it directly reduces gas consumption, exertion and stress while improving trim and control.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: Each time you dive you should mentally rehearse:
questions.explanation: SSI teaches divers to mentally rehearse emergency ascent procedures before every dive. Visualizing the steps — signal buddy, share air on the alternate air source and ascend together; or, if alone, perform a controlled emergency swimming ascent — keeps the response automatic when stress would otherwise narrow thinking. Recreational sport divers do not plan decompression stops, and Trimix is technical-level diving outside the recreational scope. Mentally walking through what you would do in the unlikely event of a problem is a low-cost, high-value habit that prevents stress from escalating to panic and is one of SSI's core stress-prevention tools.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 2 Review

questions.question: There are certain behavioral signs that signal when stress is present in your buddy. These signs can occur:
questions.explanation: Stress in a buddy can appear at any of the three SSI-recognized stages: before the dive (Stage 1 — apprehension, equipment fumbling, irritability), during the dive (Stage 2 — rapid breathing, wide eyes, jerky movements, refusal to descend or continue), and after the dive (Stage 3 — failure to establish positive buoyancy, mask off, regulator out, exhaustion, signs of drowning). A good buddy stays observant in all three phases because intervention is much easier and safer the earlier stress is recognized. Therefore the comprehensive answer that covers before, during and after is correct.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: The key to detect Stress in Stage 1 before the dive, is:

options.b: To check if everyone has high quality , expensive dive gear
options.d: To mind just your own business

questions.explanation: In Stage 1 — before the dive — the most powerful tool for detecting stress is communication. Talking openly with your buddy about objectives, parameters, conditions, equipment and personal feelings reveals apprehension, fatigue, illness or doubts that would otherwise stay hidden until they become a problem underwater. Expensive gear has nothing to do with detecting stress. Ignoring problems or staying out of a buddy's business directly contradicts the buddy concept and is exactly how Stage 1 stress turns into Stage 2 panic underwater. Honest pre-dive communication is the foundation of a safe dive.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: Signs for stress in stage 1 before the dive could be:
questions.explanation: All three are classic Stage 1 stress indicators. Unusual behaviors — being unusually quiet, withdrawn, joking nervously, or showing changes from a person's normal pre-dive demeanor — signal something is wrong. Irritability and frustration with normal preparation tasks indicate elevated emotional arousal. Difficulty with equipment a diver normally handles easily (struggling with straps, fins, mask, weights) often reflects nerves rather than mechanical problems. A vigilant buddy or instructor recognizes any of these and uses communication to assess the source and address it before the dive begins, when intervention is safest and easiest.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: To eliminate fear of the unknown by answering questions up front, it is critical to make a:

options.c: Scuba Skills Update before each vacation

questions.explanation: A good dive plan eliminates fear of the unknown by answering all the important questions up front: what is the objective, what are the depth and time parameters, what is the entry and exit, what are the expected conditions, what signals will we use, what is our gas-management rule, what do we do if separated or in an emergency. With these decisions made on land, both buddies enter the water confident and aligned, which dramatically reduces Stage 1 stress. A Navigation specialty, an in-water buddy check or a Scuba Skills Update are useful tools but not the primary answer to pre-dive uncertainty — a thorough dive plan is.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: Elements of a good dive plan include, that both buddies agree on:

options.b: They will continue the dive even if they get separated and meet at the end
options.c: The objective and Parameters (depth time) of the dive
options.d: Equipment misuse and decompression stop procedures

questions.explanation: Two of the core elements every dive plan must contain are the objective of the dive (what we are going to do — sightseeing, photography, navigation practice, etc.) and the parameters: maximum depth, maximum bottom time, turnaround pressure, and ascent rate. Both buddies must explicitly agree on these limits so neither feels pressured to exceed them underwater. Equipment color is irrelevant. Continuing solo after separation contradicts the SSI lost-buddy procedure (search for one minute, then surface). Decompression stops and equipment misuse are not part of recreational dive planning. Agreement on objective and parameters is fundamental.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: A good buddy will create a sense of trust and ease through:

options.a: Buddy system failure during the dive
options.b: Making a buddy feel intimidated or inferior by boasting about knowledge and experience
options.d: Pressure a buddy to dive beyond his her limits

questions.explanation: SSI describes the qualities of a good buddy explicitly: communication, encouragement, support and respect. Open communication keeps both divers informed and aligned; encouragement helps a less confident partner relax; support — physical, mental and logistical — means being there when needed; respect means honoring the other's limits, comfort level and right to call any dive at any time. Boasting, intimidation, abandoning the buddy system or pressuring a partner to exceed personal limits are exactly the behaviors that create stress, undermine trust and lead to accidents. A good buddy makes diving safer and more enjoyable for both.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: A major source of stress is feeling that you must go ahead with a dive when you know you?

options.a: Have a high quality Total Dive System
options.b: Aren't able to or do not wish to
options.c: Are able to or wish to

questions.explanation: SSI identifies as a major stress source the feeling that you must dive even though you know you are not able to (illness, fatigue, injury, insufficient training for the conditions, equipment problems) or simply do not wish to. Peer pressure, sunk-cost thinking, fear of disappointing a group or paid trip can all push a diver into the water against their better judgment, which creates immediate, intense stress that often escalates underwater. SSI's principle is unambiguous: a no-go decision can be made at any time without embarrassment. Owning quality gear, having gas, or being capable are not stress sources — being coerced into diving is.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: Factors that justify a no-go diving decision include?

options.b: Poor environmental conditions or improper training for this conditions

questions.explanation: All three categories independently justify calling off a dive. Personal physical state — sickness, fatigue, hangover, dehydration, recent injury — directly increases risk of medical incidents and panic underwater. Environmental factors that exceed the diver's training (strong current, low visibility, cold, surf, depth, overhead environment) push the dive beyond the safety envelope. Equipment that is missing, malfunctioning or unfamiliar removes the redundancy and reliability the Total Diving System is designed to provide. Any one of these alone is enough; together the case is overwhelming. SSI emphasizes that a no-go decision can be made at any time, by any diver, without embarrassment.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: If anything out-of-the-ordinary happens in the planning phase:

options.a: Don`t worry if your buddy can handle it
options.c: Continue, because you are trained for all conditions
options.d: Exchange you dive cylinder to a bigger one

questions.explanation: SSI teaches that anything out-of-the-ordinary during planning — unexpected change in conditions, a buddy feeling unwell, missing or malfunctioning equipment, last-minute changes to objective or site, or simply a gut feeling that something is off — should trigger a full reassessment of the dive decision. The reassessment may conclude that the dive can proceed with modifications, that it should be postponed, or that it should be cancelled. Assuming a buddy will cope, pretending all conditions are within training, or swapping to a bigger cylinder all dodge the real question. Honest reassessment when something feels wrong is a hallmark of safe, mature diving.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: Once you and your buddy are geared up, you should:

options.a: Make a last minute check of your equipment and your dive plan
options.c: Rinse you masks with fresh water

questions.explanation: SSI's pre-entry routine is a last-minute check of both equipment and dive plan after the buddy team is fully geared up. The buddy check confirms air on, weight system in place and releasable, releases on the BC located, final equipment check (computer on, SPG reading, alternate air source secured), and a quick verbal review of the agreed objective, depth, time and signals. Ditching weights or fully deflating the BC before entry is dangerous — divers should enter with positive buoyancy. Mask rinsing happens before donning. The geared-up buddy check is the final filter that catches problems while still on the surface.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: If your buddy feels apprehensive, allow him or her to:
questions.explanation: If a buddy feels apprehensive at any point, SSI doctrine is to allow them to abort the dive without embarrassment or pressure. Apprehension is Stage 1 or Stage 2 stress; forcing the dive to continue almost guarantees the stress will escalate, often into panic. A genuine buddy supports the no-go decision and surfaces with their partner. Going deeper compounds the problem by adding narcosis, gas density and distance from safety. An emergency swimming ascent and ditching weights are emergency procedures used only in actual emergencies, not as responses to discomfort. The safest, most respectful response is simply: end the dive together.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: A sign of rapid, shallow breathing is an almost continuous exhalation of a large amount of bubbles. Normal breathing should appear as a:

options.a: Fast inhalation followed by a fast exhalation.
options.b: Fast deep inhalation followed by a fast deep exhalation.
options.c: Slow, steady inhalation followed by 2 seconds of breath holding and fast deep exhalation.
options.d: Slow, steady inhalation followed by a slow, steady exhalation.

questions.explanation: Normal, relaxed diver breathing is a slow, steady inhalation followed by a slow, steady exhalation, producing distinct, evenly spaced bubble streams. This pattern maximizes gas exchange, minimizes CO2 retention and conserves breathing gas. Fast or shallow breathing produces a near-continuous bubble stream and is one of the earliest, most visible stress indicators. Holding the breath at any point during scuba is dangerous because of pulmonary overexpansion risk and CO2 buildup. Monitoring the breathing pattern of yourself and your buddy is, per SSI, one of the most important observations on every dive — it changes long before other stress signs appear.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: If your fellow diver is unable to recognize you or react to your presence, beware, it may be caused by:

options.a: Severe stress may be present and panic could result
options.b: His/her vision might be disturbed by a foggy mask
options.c: Your buddy is relaxed and might fall asleep
options.d: This is the first sign for decompression sickness

questions.explanation: Tunnel vision and unresponsiveness — a buddy who looks straight through you, fails to acknowledge signals, and seems frozen or detached — are classic signs of severe Stage 2 stress. Adrenaline narrows attention onto a single perceived threat; the diver may genuinely not see or process anything outside that focus. Without intervention this state can rapidly tip into panic, which is the leading precursor to recreational diving fatalities. A foggy mask blocks vision but does not eliminate response to physical signals. Divers do not fall asleep underwater. DCS does not present this way. Recognize the unresponsiveness, get face-to-face contact, calm the diver and end the dive.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: Throughout your dive occasionally stop, rest, and?

options.b: Hold your breath to save breathing gas

questions.explanation: SSI recommends that throughout every dive divers occasionally stop, rest and honestly evaluate their physical condition: breathing rate, gas remaining, exertion, thermal comfort, any unusual sensations, and overall well-being. These short check-ins catch developing problems — overexertion, cold, equipment niggles, gas-management issues — while they are still small and easy to fix. Breath-holding on scuba is never acceptable because of pulmonary overexpansion risk. Inflating the BC randomly disrupts buoyancy. Watching corals is enjoyable but is not a self-assessment. Brief, deliberate self-checks are a cornerstone of stress prevention during the dive.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: Never feel the need to rush when diving, but move in a slow, relaxed manner. When diving with a group, move at the pace of?

options.c: The strongest group member, because it is called "sports diving".
options.d: The slowest group member to keep them from becoming fatigued.

questions.explanation: When diving as a group, SSI's rule is to move at the pace of the slowest member. This prevents fatigue, overexertion, elevated CO2 and the cascade of stress that follows when a struggling diver tries to keep up. Age and physical strength are poor proxies; what matters is the actual swimming pace each diver can sustain comfortably. Calling diving a sport does not justify pushing weaker partners. Slowing the whole group keeps everyone relaxed, reduces gas consumption, extends bottom time, and means everybody enjoys the dive — which is the goal of recreational diving.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: To keep in constant contact with your buddy you should communicate:
questions.explanation: Constant buddy contact uses every available channel. Hand signals exchange information clearly: OK, problem, low on gas, up, level off, look at this. Eye contact, even without signals, confirms presence, awareness and emotional state — wide eyes or a fixed stare are early stress flags. Physical contact (a touch on the arm, holding hands in low visibility or current, guiding a stressed buddy) communicates reassurance and keeps the team together when other signals are impractical. Each method covers situations where the others fail, so the comprehensive answer is correct. Good buddies cycle through all three throughout the dive.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: You always have to be alert and observant, but one of the most important things to monitor in yourself and others is:

options.a: The amount of fish close by

questions.explanation: Of all the things to watch on a dive, breathing pattern is the single most important indicator of stress in yourself and your buddy. A normal slow, steady inhalation/exhalation cycle reflects calm and good gas exchange. Rapid, shallow breathing — visible as an almost continuous stream of bubbles — is the earliest, most reliable warning that stress is building, gas is being burned faster than expected, and CO2 is climbing. Catching that change early lets you stop, signal, slow down and recover before stress escalates to panic. Fish, surface distance and lead weight are situational; breathing is universal.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: To avoid and deal with stress as divers enter the water, you should always:

options.c: Use the easiest methods of entry and descent
options.d: Keep in constant contact with your buddy and be aware of physical stress

questions.explanation: Stress management during entry and descent uses all three SSI tools together. Use the easiest entry and descent method appropriate for the site (controlled seated entry, giant stride, line descent) so the transition into the water environment is smooth and relaxed. Monitor instruments — depth, time, computer, gas — from the start so any issue is caught immediately. Keep constant buddy contact and watch for signs of physical stress (rapid breathing, jerky movement, wide eyes, refusal to descend) so you can intervene early. Each measure independently reduces stress; combined they make the entry phase, statistically a high-risk part of the dive, much safer.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: The drowning victim usually does not scream for help and wave energetically on the surface, but rather drops silently below the surface unseen and unheard.
questions.explanation: Right. Real drowning almost never resembles the dramatic Hollywood version of screaming and waving for help. The Instinctive Drowning Response described by SSI is silent: the victim's mouth bobs at the waterline, arms press laterally to push the body up, breathing takes priority over speech, the body remains vertical with no kicking, and within 20-60 seconds the victim slips silently below the surface unseen and unheard. This is why surface watch by buddies, instructors and boat crew must be active and continuous — by the time someone yells, it is usually already too late. Recognizing the silent presentation of drowning is fundamental to rescue.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: Which of the following is not a sign of drowning?

options.a: Arms attempting to push out of the water
options.b: Able to inflate buoyancy compensator and relax
options.c: Head held back and out of water

questions.explanation: Being able to inflate the BC and relax is the opposite of drowning — it is the controlled response of an aware diver who has solved the problem. The other three are textbook Instinctive Drowning Response signs: arms pressing laterally on the water trying to push the body up (not waving), head tilted back with the mouth fighting to stay above the waterline, and inability to speak because every breath is being used for survival rather than vocalization. Recognizing these silent signs and ruling out the calm, BC-inflated diver lets a rescuer correctly identify who actually needs immediate help on the surface.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: You should be aware of the following signs of stress at the end of your dive:

options.a: Mask off, regulator removed, failure to achieve positive buoyancy, shortness of breath, signs of drowning.
options.b: None, because the dive is almost over
options.c: BC inflated, regulator or snorkel in mouth
options.d: Mask on, alternate air source removed, failure to achieve negative buoyancy, breathing normally

questions.explanation: End-of-dive stress is real and dangerous, often striking exactly when divers think they are safe. SSI lists the warning signs as: mask off (premature, often to gulp air), regulator removed before reliable positive buoyancy is established, failure to achieve positive buoyancy on the surface, shortness of breath, and any signs of drowning. The other choices either describe a safe, controlled diver (BC inflated, regulator/snorkel in mouth) or are nonsensical. Stage 3 — at the surface and exiting — has historically produced many recreational fatalities, so attentive surface watch and quick intervention here are as important as any other phase.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: Intelligent ascent and surface procedures can help you prevent stress at the end of the dive. These are:

options.a: Ditch any hand held extra equipment that is creating drag
options.c: Plan to end your dive with 50 bar
options.d: Control your ascents and stop at 5 meters for 3 to 5 minutes

questions.explanation: All listed actions are SSI-recommended end-of-dive procedures to prevent stress. Planning to surface with 50 bar reserve guarantees adequate gas for any unexpected delay. Controlled ascents with a 3–5 minute safety stop at 5 meters reduce DCS risk and let you check the surface before exiting. Ditching hand-held equipment that creates drag conserves energy when you may already be cold or tired. Together these intelligent ascent and surface habits prevent the fatigue-and-low-air combination that leads to incidents.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: At the end of the dive if you are cold, tired, and low on air -- you could be a prime candidate for:
questions.explanation: SSI identifies the combination of cold, fatigue, and low gas at the end of a dive as the classic prime-candidate profile for stress and an incident. Each factor on its own raises workload and reduces reserve capacity; together they overwhelm a diver's ability to manage problems and trigger panic. Recognising this state is the cue to abort, signal for help, or switch to alternate procedures rather than push through. Prevention through intelligent ascent procedures and gas planning is always preferable to reacting once stress has compounded.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: To rest comfortably on the surface without having to tread water, you always have to:

options.a: Establish negative buoyancy at the surface
options.b: Establish neutral buoyancy at the surface
options.c: Establish positive buoyancy at the surface
options.d: Ditch your weight system before doing the safety stop 33 ©

questions.explanation: Positive buoyancy is the only state that lets a diver rest on the surface without expending energy. Neutral buoyancy still requires constant finning to keep the airway clear, and negative buoyancy will pull the diver under. SSI teaches divers to fully inflate the BC (and ditch weights if needed) immediately upon reaching the surface, especially when tired or stressed. This conserves energy, frees the hands for signalling, and prevents the fatigue spiral that leads to surface panic and drowning.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 3 Review

questions.question: What will carry both you and the victim through the incident until professional medical assistance arrives.
questions.explanation: SSI emphasises mental stamina as the decisive factor that carries rescuer and victim through an incident until EMS arrives. Physical skill, strength, and CPR are tools, but a rescue may require sustained effort, clear thinking, and emotional control over many minutes — towing, ventilating, coordinating help, and managing bystanders. Without mental stamina the rescuer fatigues, makes errors, or quits. Training, visualisation, and the Stop-Breathe-Think-Act routine build the mental discipline needed to keep functioning under stress until professional medical help takes over.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 4 Review

questions.question: The number one priority when diving will be always:
questions.explanation: Prevention of stress is SSI's number-one diving priority because virtually every dive incident is preceded by a stress trigger that went unmanaged. By eliminating or controlling stressors — proper gear, fitness, training, planning, communication, and intelligent ascent procedures — divers prevent the chain of events that leads to panic and accidents. Reacting to a problem in the water is always harder, more dangerous, and more uncertain than preventing it. Water temperature and gear price are conditions of the dive, not safety priorities.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 4 Review

questions.question: If you are ever in a rescue situation by yourself, you will need to weigh?:

options.a: The benefits against the risks of the rescue attempt
options.b: If the amount of weights to carry is too much
options.c: If you should call for help or not
options.d: If it is ok to drop the expensive camera to help my buddy

questions.explanation: When rescuing alone, SSI requires the rescuer to weigh the benefits against the risks of the attempt. A solo rescuer who becomes a second victim helps no one and doubles the workload of arriving responders. Factors include conditions, distance, the rescuer's training and fitness, the victim's likely state, and whether help is en route. If risks outweigh benefits, the correct choice is to summon help and stand by rather than attempt heroics. This benefit-versus-risk analysis is the foundation of every rescue decision.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 4 Review

questions.question: The dive team, will be able to justify a certain amount of risk with a potentially high benefit if they are responding in:
questions.explanation: Only in rescue mode — when there is a realistic chance of saving a life — can a dive team justify accepting elevated risk for a potentially high benefit. In search and recovery modes the victim is presumed deceased, so the operation must be conducted with conservative profiles and zero rescuer risk because no benefit can outweigh another casualty. Recognising which mode applies (rescue vs. search vs. recovery) governs the speed, aggressiveness, and risk tolerance of the response, and is a core accident-management decision.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 4 Review

questions.question: Checking vital signs on a victim consists of:

options.a: Checking the heart for cardiac arrhythmias
options.b: Checking to see if the diver's airways are open, they are breathing, and they have circulation
options.c: Checking the pulse with thick gloves at the legs

questions.explanation: Vital-sign assessment in dive accident management follows the ABC sequence: Airway open, Breathing present, Circulation (pulse) detectable. This is a rapid lay-rescuer evaluation that drives the next intervention — open the airway, give rescue breaths if not breathing, start CPR if no pulse. Diagnosing arrhythmias requires medical equipment, pulse checks through thick gloves at the legs are unreliable, and dive-profile review comes later for medical staff. ABC is the universal first-responder standard taught in every SSI rescue and first-aid programme.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 4 Review

questions.question: Decompression sickness symptoms usually appear:

options.a: As you descend to begin your dive

questions.explanation: SSI teaches that DCS symptoms typically appear within 15 minutes to 12 hours after surfacing, with the majority manifesting in the first hour. This window is why divers must remain observable and reachable post-dive and why post-dive flying restrictions exist. Symptoms appearing during descent or before surfacing are not DCS, and onset beyond 24 hours is uncommon though possible. Knowing this timeframe lets rescuers correlate post-dive complaints with the recent profile and act fast: vital signs, 100% oxygen, EMS activation, and evacuation.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 4 Review

questions.question: Which is not a sign for Decompression Sickness:

options.b: Local pain in arms, legs and joints
options.d: Tingling and itching of the skin

questions.explanation: Bright red (cherry-red) skin coloration is not a DCS sign — it is a classic indicator of carbon monoxide poisoning, often from contaminated breathing gas. True DCS signs include localised joint and limb pain (the bends), skin tingling and itching (skin bends), rashes, fatigue, neurological deficits, and in severe cases collapse or unconsciousness. Recognising the difference matters because CO poisoning requires immediate fresh air and oxygen, while DCS additionally requires recompression. Mistaking one for the other delays correct treatment.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 4 Review

questions.question: Which type of transportation is the most common and the safest in case of a diving incident?

options.b: None of the answers are correct

questions.explanation: Ground transport is the most common and safest evacuation method for dive incidents. It is widely available, can be summoned through standard EMS, allows continuous patient care en route, and avoids the altitude exposure of air evacuation that can worsen DCS by reducing ambient pressure. Water transport is slow, exposed, and limited in care capability. Air transport is reserved for cases where distance or terrain makes ground impossible, and it should be flown low and pressurised when DCS is suspected. Distance and severity guide the final choice.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 4 Review

questions.question: If there are enough people on location, you may want someone to sit with emotional witnesses to keep them:
questions.explanation: Emotional witnesses should be kept comforted by a dedicated person whenever resources allow. Bystanders distressed by what they have seen can interfere with the rescue, become secondary medical concerns themselves, or spread panic. A calm companion reassures them, keeps them out of the responder's way, and gathers useful information for EMS. The goal is care and support, not silencing or detaining them. Effective accident management treats the scene as a whole, including the psychological wellbeing of everyone present.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 4 Review

questions.question: Basically the same skills learned in an Open Water Diver program are needed for:
questions.explanation: Self-aid uses essentially the same skills already taught in the Open Water Diver course: alternate-air-source use and regulator retrieval, buoyancy control and emergency ascents, alternate fin kicks, and cramp release. The Stress & Rescue programme reframes those familiar skills as the diver's first line of defence against personal problems underwater. CPR, search patterns, and first aid are additional disciplines requiring separate training. Recognising that OWD competencies already form a self-rescue toolkit reinforces why mastery of the basics matters.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: You should use alternate fin kicks if you:
questions.explanation: Alternate fin kicks (frog kick, modified flutter, etc.) are used when a diver is tired, because they recruit different muscle groups and reduce fatigue compared to the standard flutter kick. Switching kicks lets the diver continue moving while resting overworked muscles, preventing the cramps and exhaustion that often trigger stress at the end of a dive. Kick choice does not change depth direction — that is controlled by buoyancy and body position. Alternate kicks are a self-aid skill every diver should master.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: Before acting in an emergency situation you have to:
questions.explanation: Regaining control is the mandatory first step before any other action in an emergency. A diver acting from panic makes poor decisions, wastes gas, and risks lung overexpansion or rapid ascent. SSI's Stop-Breathe-Think-Act routine restores composure: stop movement, breathe slowly through the regulator, think through the problem, then act with a plan. Inflating the BC, locating the alternate, or other physical responses come only after mental control is established. Holding the breath is never correct underwater.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: The best way to regain control of a situation is to immediately Stop- Breathe- Think-Act!
questions.explanation: True. Stop-Breathe-Think-Act is the SSI-endorsed sequence for regaining control in any underwater or surface emergency. Stopping interrupts the panic cycle; slow, deep breathing restores oxygen and calms the nervous system; thinking lets the diver identify the actual problem and the right tool from training; acting then follows with deliberate, measured movements. This four-step routine works for self-aid, buddy assist, and full rescues, and it is the foundation of every other Stress & Rescue skill.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: A Buddy assist is used:

options.a: If your Buddy is relaxed and comfortable
options.b: If your Buddy is experiencing stress but still under control
options.d: If your Buddy is experiencing stress and out of control

questions.explanation: A buddy assist is the appropriate response when a buddy shows signs of stress but is still in control of themselves and able to cooperate. The assisting diver helps solve the problem — sharing gas, signalling, slowing the ascent, calming — before stress escalates to panic. Once control is lost, the situation becomes a rescue with very different procedures. A relaxed buddy needs no assist, and an unconscious buddy requires full rescue protocols. Recognising the early stress signs is what makes the buddy assist possible.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: Always remember to take care of yourself first, because both of your lives become endangered without your:
questions.explanation: Without your own safety, both lives are endangered. SSI's rule is unambiguous: secure yourself first, then help. A rescuer who runs out of gas, loses buoyancy, or panics becomes a second victim and removes the only chance of help. Self-first means controlled breathing, positive buoyancy, awareness of surroundings, and adequate gas before extending aid. Only from a position of personal safety can a diver provide effective buddy assist or rescue. This principle governs every contact with a stressed or impaired diver.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: To dominate the situation when conducting a rescue, it is vital you keep:
questions.explanation: Mental control is what lets a rescuer dominate a chaotic, high-stress scene. By staying calm and thinking clearly, the rescuer makes correct sequencing decisions, projects authority that calms the victim and bystanders, and avoids the errors that turn rescues into double tragedies. Physical distance and equipment retention serve specific tactical purposes but neither defines control. Dominating the situation flows from the same Stop-Breathe-Think-Act discipline used in self-aid, scaled up to manage a victim and the surrounding environment.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: Rescue breathing in water:

options.c: May force water into the lungs

questions.explanation: All three statements are accurate. In-water rescue breathing may delay exit because each breath stops forward progress. It may force water into the victim's lungs since waves, technique error, or improper head position can drive water past the airway. And it is extremely difficult — wet faces, masks, regulators, towing, and surface chop all work against an effective seal and rhythm. SSI therefore recommends rescue breaths only when distance to safety is significant and the victim has a pulse but is not breathing.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: If you are separated from your dive buddy, you should:

options.a: Start searching until your breathing gas gets low, then surface
options.d: Surface after making two 360 degree visual sweeps (1 Minute)

questions.explanation: SSI's standard buddy-separation procedure is to make two 360-degree visual sweeps — about one minute total — and then surface. Brief structured search lets buddies reunite at depth if they are nearby, while the one-minute limit prevents prolonged searches that compound the separation and waste gas. Surfacing immediately skips the chance to reunite quickly; continuing the dive abandons the buddy; searching until low on gas creates a low-air emergency. Reunion happens at the surface, where both divers can see and signal each other.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: To help your buddy without endangering yourself, you must be able to:
questions.explanation: All three conditions must be met simultaneously to assist a buddy safely. Seeing the victim, surroundings, and your own gauges lets you assess and act without surprises. Breathing — meaning a working air supply and a calm, regulated breath cycle — keeps you conscious and capable. Positive buoyancy frees your hands, prevents fatigue, and keeps both heads above water on the surface. If any one of these is missing, helping the buddy turns into a second emergency. Self-safety always precedes assistance.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: When bringing an unconscious buddy to the surface, you need to do it:

options.c: In a controlled manner, without endangering yourself

questions.explanation: An unconscious diver must be brought up in a controlled manner that does not endanger the rescuer. Controlled means a steady ascent rate that minimises lung-overexpansion and DCS risk for the victim while letting the rescuer manage buoyancy, airway position, and equipment. "As slow as possible" can be wrong if the victim is not breathing — getting them to surface care quickly matters. The decisive constraint is the rescuer's own safety: a lost rescuer cannot help anyone. SMB and alternate air are tools, not the principle.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: If the victim on the surface has no pulse, you should start in-water resuscitation immediately.
questions.explanation: False. Effective CPR — the only intervention for a pulseless victim — requires a firm, flat surface and cannot be delivered in the water. Compressions on a floating body are useless. The correct response is to get the victim out of the water as quickly as possible (boat, shore, platform) and start full CPR there. In-water rescue breathing applies only when the victim has a pulse but is not breathing and shore is far away. Time spent attempting in-water CPR is wasted time the victim cannot afford.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 5 Review

questions.question: It is important that you have good skills, knowledge, equipment, and experience to perform:
questions.explanation: Skills, knowledge, equipment, and experience are required for every level of response — self aid, buddy assist, and full rescues or recoveries. Self-aid demands mastery of basic OWD skills; buddy assist adds communication and shared problem-solving; rescues and recoveries add tow techniques, water exits, CPR, oxygen, and accident management. Underlying all three is the diver's experience and judgement to choose the right action and recognise their own limits. SSI's continuing-education ladder (training, gear, dive logs) is designed to build all four pillars together.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 6 Review

questions.question: If you are stranded on the surface, which precautions will make you easier to recognize?
questions.explanation: All three precautions improve recognition of a stranded surface diver. Brightly coloured wetsuits or BCs (yellow, orange, red) stand out against waves and contrast with grey or blue water. A whistle carries far further than the human voice and works without wind. An inflatable SMB rises three to two metres above the surface, becoming visible at long range and to passing boats. Combined, they cover sight and sound, day and night-into-distance, and form the standard SSI surface-signalling kit every open-water diver should carry.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 6 Review

questions.question: Environmental conditions, which make rescues more difficult include:
questions.explanation: Poor visibility and cold water are the two environmental conditions SSI flags as principal complicators of any rescue. Poor visibility hides the victim, slows search patterns, and isolates rescuer from buddy. Cold water sucks energy and dexterity from both rescuer and victim, accelerates exhaustion, and raises the risk of hypothermia after the rescue. Warm water with good visibility is ideal for rescues; low tide on a sandy beach actually eases extraction; tide and moon do not by themselves complicate rescues.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 6 Review

questions.question: SSI does not recommend diving in either ice or caves without:
questions.explanation: SSI explicitly does not recommend ice or cave diving without proper training. Both environments are overhead environments — direct ascent to the surface is impossible — and entrapment, disorientation, gas-management, and visibility hazards are far beyond recreational open-water skills. Specialist programmes (Ice Diver, Cavern Diver, full Cave training) build the procedures, redundancy, and judgement required. Equipment such as double cylinders, dry suit, and lights is necessary but useless without the training to use them. Training is the prerequisite that gates equipment choices.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 6 Review

questions.question: If you or any diver is ever experiencing shortness of breath or chest pain on the surface, the dive should be:
questions.explanation: Shortness of breath or chest pain on the surface are red-flag symptoms that mandate cancelling the dive. They can signal cardiac problems, lung overexpansion injury, immersion pulmonary oedema, or significant exertion stress — all of which become far more dangerous underwater where a developing emergency cannot be aborted instantly. Postponing by an hour or starting/continuing the dive ignores a clear warning. The diver should rest, be observed, and seek medical evaluation. Future diving resumes only after a doctor familiar with diving clears the cause.
questions.source: SSI: Stress & Rescue Manual — Section 6 Review
